topics.title: Advantages of Using Composites in High Performance Structures; Application Trends; Composite Applications in Aircraft
Space
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT744 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Space

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Composites in "Space"** (Space Applications of Composite Materials) refers to the engineering discipline concerning the design, selection, fabrication, and deployment of **fiber-reinforced polymer (FRP)**, **metal matrix composites (MMC)**, and **ceramic matrix composites (CMC)** for use in **launch vehicles, satellites, space stations, deep-space probes, and re-entry vehicles**. It is one of the most demanding application areas in the entire field of materials engineering because the operational envelope combines **extreme thermal cycling** ($\Delta T \approx \pm 150^\circ\text{C}$ in LEO), **high vacuum**, **micro-meteoroid/orbital-debris (MMOD) impact**, **UV radiation**, **outgassing constraints**, and **near-zero gravity structural loads**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (Module 1):** Space is identified as a flagship *application-driven* topic. The expected cognitive learning outcome is: *"Identify and justify the use of specific composite systems for space-grade structural, thermal, and shielding functions."* This is a **CO1–CO2 level** outcome (Understand → Apply) on the Revised Bloom's Taxonomy ladder.

## 1.2 Why Composites Dominate Space Engineering

Conventional monolithic aerospace alloys such as **Al 7075-T6** ($\rho \approx 2.81\text{ g/cc}$) and **Ti-6Al-4V** ($\rho \approx 4.43\text{ g/cc}$) have been progressively replaced by composites because **launch cost is directly proportional to payload mass** — historically, lifting **1 kg of mass to Low Earth Orbit (LEO)** costs between **\$2,000 and \$25,000** depending on the launch vehicle (e.g., Falcon 9 vs. legacy Space Shuttle).

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Rocket Equation" Context:**
> The **Tsiolkovsky rocket equation** states that the velocity change of a rocket is:
> $$\Delta v = I_{sp} \cdot g_0 \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{m_0}{m_f}\right)$$
> where $m_0$ is the initial (wet) mass and $m_f$ is the final (dry) mass. A **10 % reduction in structural mass** can therefore translate into a **15–25 % increase** in useful payload — a financially transformative gain in the launch industry.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are packing a **glass water bottle** for a long mountain trek. The glass is strong but heavy. Now, suppose you could replace it with a bottle made of:
- A **paper-thin polymer shell** (the **matrix**), which holds everything together and is light.
- Reinforced with **woven carbon threads** (the **reinforcement/fiber**), which carry all the load.

This hybrid bottle would be **5× lighter, equally strong**, and would not shatter if dropped. **This is exactly what a carbon/epoxy composite does for a satellite.** The fibers carry the mechanical load (like the steel skeleton of a building), while the epoxy matrix distributes stress and protects the fibers (like the concrete wrapping the rebar).

---

## 1.4 Key Performance Metrics Used in Space-Grade Composite Selection

| Performance Metric | Symbol | Why it Matters in Space |
|---|---|---|
| **Specific Strength** | $\sigma_u / \rho$ | Every gram saved = dollars saved at launch |
| **Specific Modulus (Stiffness)** | $E / \rho$ | Prevents resonance & buckling under thrust |
| **Coefficient of Thermal Expansion (CTE)** | $\alpha$ | Must match adjacent metals to avoid delamination |
| **Outgassing (TML / CVCM)** | % mass loss | Contaminates optics & sensitive instruments |
| **Glass Transition Temperature** | $T_g$ | Determines upper service temperature |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Remembering Space-Composite Requirements:**
> **"S.T.A.R. L.I.G.H.T."** — **S**trength, **T**hermal stability, **A**tmospheric (vacuum) resistance, **R**adiation tolerance, **L**ow density, **I**mpact resistance, **G**as barrier (low outgassing), **H**ygrothermal stability, **T**oughness.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Logic of Why Composites are Selected for Space

The selection of a composite for a space structural element follows a strict five-step engineering rationale:

1. **Identify the dominant load case** — axial (rocket body), bending (satellite panel), torsional (antenna boom), or pressure (pressurized module).
2. **Compute the mass budget** — Every subsystem is allocated a maximum allowable mass (e.g., a satellite bus: 50–200 kg).
3. **Compute stiffness/strength demand** — Using the **Euler–Bernoulli beam equation** for booms or **plate-bending theory** for panels.
4. **Compare specific properties of candidate materials** — Aluminum, titanium, CFRP, GFRP, CFRP/Al hybrids.
5. **Verify environmental compatibility** — CTE mismatch, outgassing per **ECSS-Q-ST-70-02C** standard, thermal cycling endurance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Rule:** When asked *"Why are composites preferred over metals in space?"* — always structure your answer around the **specific property table** (below). Examiners award marks for **quantitative justification**, not just qualitative phrases.

## 2.2 Common Composite Systems Used in Space

| Composite System | Density $\rho$ (g/cc) | Tensile Strength $\sigma_u$ (MPa) | Tensile Modulus $E$ (GPa) | Typical Space Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **CFRP (Carbon/Epoxy)** — T700/2510 | 1.55 | 2550 | 132 | Satellite panels, rocket fairings |
| **CFRP (IM7/8552)** | 1.58 | 2800 | 165 | Primary structures, cryogenic tanks |
| **GFRP (E-Glass/Epoxy)** | 1.85 | 2400 | 45 | Low-cost antenna reflectors |
| **KFRP (Kevlar/Epoxy)** | 1.40 | 1400 | 76 | MMOD impact shields (Whipple shields) |
| **C/C (Carbon/Carbon)** | 1.65 | 600–1000 | 70–150 | Nozzle throats, re-entry leading edges |
| **SiC/SiC (Ceramic Composite)** | 2.5–3.0 | 250–400 | 200–300 | Hot structures, turbine engines |
| **Al 7075-T6** (Reference Metal) | 2.81 | 572 | 71 | Legacy baseline comparison |
| **Ti-6Al-4V** (Reference Metal) | 4.43 | 950 | 113 | Legacy baseline comparison |

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** Carbon/Epoxy has nearly **2× the strength** and **2× the stiffness** of Al 7075 at **half the density**. This is the quantitative justification examiners want.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Exam-Critical)

| # | Formula / Equation | Description & Units |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\displaystyle \sigma_u / \rho$ | Specific Strength — $\text{MPa} / (\text{g/cc})$ |
| 2 | $\displaystyle E / \rho$ | Specific Modulus — $\text{GPa} / (\text{g/cc})$ |
| 3 | $\displaystyle \sigma_c = \frac{P}{A}$ | Axial compressive stress (rocket body) |
| 4 | $\displaystyle \sigma_b = \frac{M \cdot c}{I}$ | Bending stress in beam (panel rib) |
| 5 | $\displaystyle P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 E I}{(KL)^2}$ | Euler buckling load for slender boom |
| 6 | $\displaystyle \Delta L = \alpha \cdot L_0 \cdot \Delta T$ | Thermal expansion of structural member |
| 7 | $\displaystyle V_f \cdot E_f + V_m \cdot E_m = E_c$ | Rule of mixtures (longitudinal modulus) |
| 8 | $\displaystyle \frac{1}{E_c} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m}$ | Inverse rule of mixtures (transverse modulus) |
| 9 | $\displaystyle \sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$ | Rule of mixtures for tensile strength |
| 10 | $\displaystyle \Delta v = I_{sp}\,g_0\,\ln\!\left(\frac{m_0}{m_f}\right)$ | Tsiolkovsky rocket equation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Note on Notation:** In the tables above, the vertical bar symbol `\|` has been deliberately avoided inside equations (e.g., absolute values are written as $\vert x \vert$, not as `\|x\|` inside table cells) to keep the markdown table syntax intact.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The logic above is used by organizations such as **ISRO**, **NASA**, **ESA**, and **SpaceX** when selecting materials for:
- **Payload fairings** (CFRP honeycomb sandwich)
- **Cryogenic propellant tanks** (CFRP overwrapped LIN/LOX tanks — Boeing's 2007 patent)
- **Solar array substrates** (KFRP/foam sandwich)
- **Antenna reflectors** (CFRP skin with honeycomb core, $<\!\pm 0.1$ mm surface accuracy)
- **Re-entry Thermal Protection Systems (TPS)** — C/C and SiC/SiC composites used on Space Shuttle leading edges and NASA X-38 vehicle.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Specific Strength Comparison: CFRP vs. Al 7075-T6

**Problem Statement:** A satellite solar-array substrate is currently designed in **Al 7075-T6** ($\rho_{Al} = 2.81\text{ g/cc}$, $\sigma_{Al} = 572\text{ MPa}$). A design review proposes switching to **IM7/8552 CFRP** ($\rho_c = 1.58\text{ g/cc}$, $\sigma_c = 2800\text{ MPa}$). **Calculate the mass savings (%) if the substrate is redesigned to carry the same axial tensile load.**

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Set up the equality of axial tensile load.**

For the two materials to carry the **same tensile load $P$**, we require:
$$P = \sigma_{Al} \cdot A_{Al} = \sigma_c \cdot A_c$$

**Step 2 — Express the area ratio.**
$$\frac{A_{Al}}{A_c} = \frac{\sigma_c}{\sigma_{Al}}$$

Substitute the numerical values:
$$\frac{A_{Al}}{A_c} = \frac{2800\text{ MPa}}{572\text{ MPa}} = 4.895$$

So the **aluminum section requires 4.895 times more cross-sectional area** than the CFRP section to carry the same load.

**Step 3 — Express the masses.**
$$m_{Al} = \rho_{Al} \cdot A_{Al} \cdot L$$
$$m_c = \rho_c \cdot A_c \cdot L$$

where $L$ is the common length of the substrate.

**Step 4 — Form the mass ratio.**
$$\frac{m_{Al}}{m_c} = \frac{\rho_{Al} \cdot A_{Al}}{\rho_c \cdot A_c} = \frac{\rho_{Al}}{\rho_c} \cdot \frac{\sigma_c}{\sigma_{Al}}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute numerical values.**
$$\frac{m_{Al}}{m_c} = \frac{2.81}{1.58} \times \frac{2800}{572}$$

Evaluate the numerator and denominator step-by-step:
$$= 1.7785 \times 4.8951$$
$$= 8.706$$

**Step 6 — Compute mass savings percentage.**
$$\text{Mass Savings (\%)} = \left(1 - \frac{m_c}{m_{Al}}\right) \times 100$$

$$\frac{m_c}{m_{Al}} = \frac{1}{8.706} = 0.1149$$

$$\text{Mass Savings} = (1 - 0.1149) \times 100 = 88.51\%$$

### Final Result (boxed for exam)
$$\boxed{\text{Mass Savings} \approx 88.5\%}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Examiner's Note:** Always carry units throughout and write the **boxed final answer with units (%)**. A missing percentage sign is a common 1-mark deduction.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Composite Longitudinal Modulus using Rule of Mixtures

**Problem Statement:** A unidirectional **Carbon/Epoxy** composite has a fiber volume fraction $V_f = 0.60$, fiber modulus $E_f = 230\text{ GPa}$, and matrix modulus $E_m = 3.5\text{ GPa}$. Calculate the **longitudinal Young's modulus** $E_{c,L}$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — State the governing equation (isostrain / Voigt model).**

Under longitudinal loading, fiber and matrix experience the **same strain** $\varepsilon_L$. Hence, the total stress is the volume-weighted sum of the two phase stresses:
$$\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$$

**Step 2 — Divide both sides by $\varepsilon_L$ (Hooke's law).**
$$\frac{\sigma_c}{\varepsilon_L} = \frac{\sigma_f}{\varepsilon_L} V_f + \frac{\sigma_m}{\varepsilon_L} V_m$$

This yields the **rule of mixtures**:
$$E_{c,L} = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$$

**Step 3 — Use the constraint $V_f + V_m = 1$, so $V_m = 1 - V_f$.**
$$E_{c,L} = E_f V_f + E_m (1 - V_f)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values.**
$$E_{c,L} = (230)(0.60) + (3.5)(1 - 0.60)$$

$$= (230)(0.60) + (3.5)(0.40)$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate each term separately.**
$$= 138.0 + 1.40$$

**Step 6 — Add the terms.**
$$= 139.4\text{ GPa}$$

### Final Result
$$\boxed{E_{c,L} \approx 139.4\text{ GPa}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Thermal Expansion Mismatch in a Bimetallic Space Joint

**Problem Statement:** A **CFRP boom** ($L_0 = 1.500\text{ m}$, $\alpha_c = -0.5 \times 10^{-6}\text{ /K}$, since carbon fibers have *negative* axial CTE) is bonded at one end to an **Al 7075 bracket** ($\alpha_{Al} = 23 \times 10^{-6}\text{ /K}$) of equal length. The joint is cured at $T_{cure} = 25^\circ\text{C}$ and is exposed to orbital eclipse cooling down to $T_{cold} = -150^\circ\text{C}$. Calculate the **differential expansion** $\Delta(\Delta L)$ that the adhesive must accommodate.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute the temperature drop.**
$$\Delta T = T_{cold} - T_{cure} = -150 - 25 = -175\text{ K}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the thermal expansion law $\Delta L = \alpha L_0 \Delta T$ for each material.**

For the CFRP boom:
$$\Delta L_c = \alpha_c L_0 \Delta T = (-0.5 \times 10^{-6})(1.500)(-175)$$

$$= (-0.5 \times 10^{-6})(-262.5)$$

$$= +1.3125 \times 10^{-4}\text{ m} = +0.131\text{ mm}$$

(The boom *contracts* very little — it actually *elongates* slightly because $\alpha_c$ is negative and $\Delta T$ is also negative.)

For the Al bracket:
$$\Delta L_{Al} = \alpha_{Al} L_0 \Delta T = (23 \times 10^{-6})(1.500)(-175)$$

$$= (23 \times 10^{-6})(-262.5)$$

$$= -6.0375 \times 10^{-3}\text{ m} = -6.038\text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the differential expansion.**
$$\Delta(\Delta L) = \Delta L_c - \Delta L_{Al}$$

$$= (+0.131\text{ mm}) - (-6.038\text{ mm})$$

$$= +6.169\text{ mm}$$

### Final Result
$$\boxed{\Delta(\Delta L) \approx +6.17\text{ mm differential expansion across the joint}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Engineering Consequence:** This 6 mm differential across 1.5 m is enough to **delaminate** a poorly designed joint. This is why space engineers use *CTE-matched hybrids* (e.g., CFRP with $\alpha$ tuned via fiber/matrix selection to match Invar at $\approx 1.2 \times 10^{-6}\text{ /K}$).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Specific Strength vs. Specific Modulus

| Material | $\rho$ (g/cc) | $\sigma_u$ (MPa) | $E$ (GPa) | $\sigma_u / \rho$ | $E / \rho$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Al 7075-T6 | 2.81 | 572 | 71 | 203.6 | 25.3 |
| Ti-6Al-4V | 4.43 | 950 | 113 | 214.4 | 25.5 |
| E-Glass/Epoxy | 1.85 | 2400 | 45 | 1297.3 | 24.3 |
| CFRP (T700) | 1.55 | 2550 | 132 | 1645.2 | 85.2 |
| CFRP (IM7) | 1.58 | 2800 | 165 | 1772.2 | 104.4 |

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the matrix:** CFRP offers **~8× the specific strength** and **~4× the specific stiffness** of aluminum — this single statement is worth 3 marks in a typical KTU Module 1 answer.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Space-Composite Selection Logic

The Mermaid diagram below maps the **decision flow** used by space-systems engineers when selecting a composite for a structural or thermal application. It replaces a physical schematic that would otherwise require complex multi-layer drawings.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Define Space Mission: LEO GEO Interplanetary] --> B[Identify Load Case]
    B --> B1[Axial Thrust]
    B --> B2[Bending Panel]
    B --> B3[Pressure Vessel]
    B --> B4[Thermal Shield]

    B1 --> C[Compute Specific Strength Requirement]
    B2 --> D[Compute Specific Stiffness Requirement]
    B3 --> E[Compute Burst Pressure from Hoop Stress]
    B4 --> F[Compute Re-entry Heat Flux]

    C --> G{Compare Candidate Composite Systems}
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G

    G --> G1[CFRP: High Specific Strength]
    G --> G2[GFRP: Low Cost, Low Stiffness]
    G --> G3[KFRP: High Impact Resistance]
    G --> G4[CMC SiC SiC: High Temperature]
    G --> G5[CC Composite: Ablative Thermal]

    G1 --> H[Verify Environmental Compatibility]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    G4 --> H
    G5 --> H

    H --> H1[CTE Mismatch Check]
    H --> H2[Outgassing Test ECSS-Q-ST-70-02C]
    H --> H3[Thermal Cycling Endurance]
    H --> H4[Radiation and UV Resistance]

    H1 --> I{Final Composite Selected}
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    H4 --> I

    I --> J[Fabrication Process Selection]
    J --> J1[Autoclave Cure]
    J --> J2[Resin Transfer Molding RTM]
    J --> J3[Filament Winding]
    J --> J4[Chemical Vapor Infiltration for CMC]
```

## 4.2 Sandwich Composite Schematic (used in satellite panels & fairings)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Top Skin: CFRP Laminate] --> C[Core: Aluminum Honeycomb]
    C --> P2[Bottom Skin: CFRP Laminate]
    P1 --> A[Adhesive Film: FM 300]
    A --> C
    C --> B[Adhesive Film: FM 300]
    B --> P2
    P1 --> L1[Load Path Axial]
    C --> L1
    P2 --> L1
    P1 --> L2[Load Path Bending]
    P2 --> L2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Diagram Interpretation:** In a sandwich structure, the **CFRP skins** carry the in-plane and bending loads (analogous to the flanges of an I-beam), while the **honeycomb core** carries the transverse shear and stabilizes the skins against buckling. The adhesive films transfer shear between the skins and core.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define "specific strength" and "specific modulus" of a composite material. Why are these parameters particularly important in the selection of materials for space applications?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
- **Specific Strength** is the ratio of a material's ultimate tensile strength $\sigma_u$ to its density $\rho$, with units of $\text{MPa}/(\text{g/cc})$.
- **Specific Modulus** is the ratio of the Young's modulus $E$ to the density $\rho$, with units of $\text{GPa}/(\text{g/cc})$.

**Importance in Space (1.5 marks):**
In space applications, launch cost is directly proportional to payload mass. A composite with **higher specific strength** allows the structure to carry a given load with less mass, while **higher specific modulus** prevents elastic deformation and resonance under launch vibration. For example, CFRP IM7/8552 has $\sigma_u/\rho \approx 1772$ — roughly **8× that of Al 7075-T6**.

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**List any three space-grade composite systems and state one specific application for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Composite System | Specific Space Application |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **CFRP (Carbon/Epoxy)** | Satellite solar-array substrates, rocket fairings |
| 2 | **C/C (Carbon/Carbon)** | Rocket nozzle throats, Space Shuttle leading edges |
| 3 | **KFRP (Kevlar/Epoxy)** | MMOD Whipple shields on the ISS |

*(A fourth optional entry: SiC/SiC CMC for hot-section turbine shrouds of advanced space-plane engines.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]` — Choice A (14 Marks)

**"Composite materials have revolutionized space structural engineering." Justify this statement with reference to (a) quantitative mass savings, and (b) specific applications in launch vehicles and satellites.**

#### Part (a) — Quantitative Mass Savings (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Specific strength comparison.**

For an axial tensile member carrying load $P$ with length $L$:
$$\frac{m_{Al}}{m_{CFRP}} = \frac{\rho_{Al}}{\rho_{CFRP}} \cdot \frac{\sigma_{CFRP}}{\sigma_{Al}}$$

Substituting representative values:
$$= \frac{2.81}{1.58} \times \frac{2800}{572} = 1.778 \times 4.895 = 8.71$$

**Step 2 — Interpretation.**

The CFRP structure is **8.71× lighter** than the equivalent aluminum structure. **Mass savings ≈ 88.5 %.** For a 500 kg satellite bus, this translates to **≈ 442 kg saved**.

**Step 3 — Cost linkage.**

Using the conservative launch cost of **\$10,000 / kg to LEO**:
$$\text{Cost saving} = 442 \text{ kg} \times \$10{,}000/\text{kg} = \$4.42 \text{ million per launch}$$

This is the quantitative justification that CFRP is **economically essential** despite higher raw-material cost.

#### Part (b) — Specific Applications (7 Marks)

**Application 1 — Rocket Payload Fairings (3 marks):**
CFRP honeycomb sandwich panels protect satellites during atmospheric ascent. Outer skin uses high-temperature-resistant cyanate ester / carbon composites; inner skin uses standard CFRP. The sandwich reduces mass by **40 %** over equivalent aluminum skin while providing the same bending stiffness.

**Application 2 — Cryogenic Propellant Tanks (2 marks):**
Boeing's **Composite Overwrapped Pressure Vessel (COPV)** uses a thin Al or Invar liner overwrapped with T1000 CFRP. These tanks hold liquid hydrogen/liquid oxygen at $-253^\circ\text{C}$ while being 30 % lighter than all-metal tanks.

**Application 3 — Satellite Reflector Antennas (2 marks):**
CFRP-skinned honeycomb reflector panels maintain surface accuracy **below $\pm 0.1$ mm** across a $\pm 150^\circ\text{C}$ thermal swing — a performance impossible with metals whose CTE is **40× higher**.

---

### Question B. `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]` — Choice B (14 Marks)

**Explain (a) the rule of mixtures for predicting the longitudinal modulus of a unidirectional composite, and (b) why CTE (Coefficient of Thermal Expansion) matching is critical in space joints, with a numerical example.**

#### Part (a) — Rule of Mixtures Derivation (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — State the isostrain assumption.**

Under axial loading parallel to the fibers, both fiber and matrix undergo the **same longitudinal strain**:
$$\varepsilon_L = \varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m$$

**Step 2 — Total force balance.**

For a composite cross-section of area $A_c$ with fibers of area $A_f$ and matrix of area $A_m$:
$$F_c = F_f + F_m$$
$$\sigma_c A_c = \sigma_f A_f + \sigma_m A_m$$

**Step 3 — Convert to volume fractions.**
$$\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$$

**Step 4 — Apply Hooke's Law.**
$$E_{c,L}\,\varepsilon_L = E_f\,\varepsilon_L\,V_f + E_m\,\varepsilon_L\,V_m$$
$$\therefore E_{c,L} = E_f V_f + E_m(1 - V_f)$$

**Step 5 — Numerical substitution.**

For $V_f = 0.55$, $E_f = 230\text{ GPa}$, $E_m = 3.5\text{ GPa}$:
$$E_{c,L} = 230 \times 0.55 + 3.5 \times 0.45 = 126.5 + 1.575 = 128.08\text{ GPa}$$

#### Part (b) — CTE Matching in Space Joints (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Why CTE matters.**

In space, structures experience thermal swings from $-150^\circ\text{C}$ (eclipse) to $+150^\circ\text{C}$ (sunlit). A joint between two materials with mismatched CTE will generate **thermal stresses**, leading to **delamination, cracking, or fastener fatigue**.

**Step 2 — Numerical example.**

A 1.5 m long CFRP boom ($\alpha_c = -0.5 \times 10^{-6}\text{ /K}$) joined to an Al 7075 bracket ($\alpha_{Al} = 23 \times 10^{-6}\text{ /K}$) is cooled from $+25^\circ\text{C}$ to $-150^\circ\text{C}$.

$$\Delta L_c = \alpha_c L_0 \Delta T = (-0.5 \times 10^{-6})(1.5)(-175) = +0.131\text{ mm}$$
$$\Delta L_{Al} = \alpha_{Al} L_0 \Delta T = (23 \times 10^{-6})(1.5)(-175) = -6.04\text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Differential expansion.**
$$\Delta(\Delta L) = 0.131 - (-6.04) = 6.17\text{ mm}$$

This **6.17 mm differential** across the joint must be absorbed by the adhesive layer. Without CTE engineering, the joint fails within 5–10 thermal cycles.

**Step 4 — Engineering solution.**

Use a **CTE-tailored hybrid** such as a CFRP/Invar joint, or select a fiber/matrix combination that gives a near-zero axial CTE matching the metal counterpart.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Mistake 1:** Writing "CFRP is used because it is light" — examiners deduct 2 marks for *qualitative* answers without *quantitative* specific-strength comparison.
> 2. **Mistake 2:** Forgetting the **outgassing** requirement for space composites — the matrix must meet **ECSS-Q-ST-70-02C** with **TML < 1.0 %** and **CVCM < 0.1 %**.
> 3. **Mistake 3:** Mixing up **specific strength ($\sigma/\rho$)** with **specific modulus ($E/\rho$)**. Always label which one you are computing.
> 4. **Mistake 4:** In CTE problems, students often forget to convert temperatures to Kelvin deltas or omit the **negative CTE** characteristic of carbon fibers along their axial direction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

- [ ] **Specific Strength** $\sigma_u / \rho$ — primary metric for **load-carrying mass efficiency** in launch vehicles.
- [ ] **Specific Modulus** $E / \rho$ — primary metric for **stiffness-limited structures** like satellite booms and reflectors.
- [ ] **CFRP (Carbon/Epoxy)** — the **workhorse** space composite; uses T700, IM7, T1000 fibers with epoxy, cyanate ester, or BMI matrices.
- [ ] **C/C (Carbon/Carbon)** — used in **rocket nozzles and re-entry leading edges** owing to extreme-temperature capability ($>2000^\circ\text{C}$).
- [ ] **KFRP (Kevlar/Epoxy)** — used in **MMOD Whipple shields** on the ISS for hypervelocity impact resistance.
- [ ] **SiC/SiC (CMC)** — used in **next-generation space-plane engines** and hot structures.
- [ ] **Rule of Mixtures** — $E_{c,L} = E_f V_f + E_m(1 - V_f)$ — applies **only along the fiber direction** (isostrain).
- [ ] **Inverse Rule of Mixtures** — applies **transverse to fibers** (isostress).
- [ ] **CTE Matching** — Carbon fibers have **negative axial CTE**; matrix must be tuned for joint compatibility.
- [ ] **Outgassing Standard** — **ECSS-Q-ST-70-02C** is the European standard; NASA uses **ASTM E595** (TML < 1 %, CVCM < 0.1 %).
- [ ] **Sandwich Composites** — CFRP skins + honeycomb core = high bending stiffness at minimum mass.
- [ ] **COPV Tanks** — Carbon-fiber overwrapped pressure vessels save **30 %** mass over all-metal cryogenic tanks.
- [ ] **Launch Cost Link** — Saving 1 kg ≈ **\$2,000–\$25,000** to LEO; mass reduction is **directly monetary**.
- [ ] **Hot Structures** — C/C and SiC/SiC are mandatory above $1000^\circ\text{C}$ service temperatures.
- [ ] **Remember the Rocket Equation** — $\Delta v = I_{sp}\,g_0\,\ln(m_0/m_f)$ — explains *why* every gram matters.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Board Tip:** When the question contains the phrase *"justify"*, *quantify* your answer. A single numerical comparison ($\sigma/\rho$ table) is worth 3 marks by itself. Always end with a one-line *real-world example* (e.g., *"This is why SpaceX's Falcon 9 interstage uses CFRP honeycomb sandwich"*).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transportation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT744 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Transportation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the domain of Composite Materials, the term **"Transportation"** refers to the engineering discipline that studies the application, design, manufacturing, performance evaluation, and failure analysis of fibre-reinforced and particulate-reinforced composite systems deployed in land vehicles (automotive, railway), aerospace structures (airframes, helicopter rotor blades, spacecraft), and marine vessels (hulls, decks, masts).

A **transportation composite** is a heterogeneous, multi-phase engineered material consisting of a high-strength **reinforcement phase** (carbon fibre, glass fibre, aramid fibre, natural fibre) embedded within a continuous **matrix phase** (polymer, metal, or ceramic), specifically designed to meet the unique performance triad of transportation engineering: **lightweighting**, **fuel efficiency**, and **structural integrity under dynamic loading**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Transportation is the single largest commercial application sector for advanced composites. As per Module 1 outcomes, students must be able to *classify composites by matrix/reinforcement*, *identify transportation applications*, and *justify material selection* using quantitative performance indices.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are building a **mud house** in Kerala during monsoon season. Pure mud (matrix alone) cracks and dissolves. If you mix in **coconut husk fibres** (reinforcement) into the mud, the walls become dramatically stronger, lighter to handle, and resistant to cracking. The mud binds the fibres together (matrix role) while the fibres carry the load (reinforcement role). A **transportation composite works identically**: the matrix (epoxy resin) is the "mud" that distributes load, and the fibre (carbon/glass) is the "husk" that carries the mechanical stress. Together, they outperform either material alone — just as the reinforced mud wall outperforms either pure mud or loose husk.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following **specific performance indices** govern composite selection in transportation engineering:

- **Specific Strength** $\left(\sigma/\rho\right)$ : ratio of ultimate tensile strength to density. Unit: **MPa·cm³/g** or **kN·m/kg**
- **Specific Modulus** $\left(E/\rho\right)$ : ratio of Young's modulus to density. Unit: **GPa·cm³/g** or **MN·m/kg**
- **Density** $\left(\rho\right)$ : typically **1.5–2.0 g/cm³** for CFRP versus **2.7 g/cm³** for aluminium and **7.8 g/cm³** for steel
- **Fatigue Strength Ratio (FSR)** : endurance limit at $10^7$ cycles divided by ultimate tensile strength

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Box — Specific Strength:** The strength of a material per unit weight. It answers the question: *"For every kilogram I put into a structure, how much load can it carry?"* — the single most important metric in aerospace and automotive design.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Composites Used in Transportation

Composites deployed in transportation are classified along **three orthogonal axes** as per the KTU 2024 syllabus:

**A. By Matrix Phase**
- **Polymer Matrix Composites (PMC)** — dominant class; epoxy/carbon, polyester/glass
- **Metal Matrix Composites (MMC)** — Al/SiC pistons, Mg/Al₂O₃ engine brackets
- **Ceramic Matrix Composites (CMC)** — SiC/SiC turbine exhaust, re-entry tiles

**B. By Reinforcement Geometry**
- **Fibre-reinforced** (continuous, woven, chopped) — primary load-bearing class
- **Particulate-reinforced** — wear-resistant, isotropic
- **Laminate/sandwich** — honeycomb-cored panels for floorboards and wing skins

**C. By Application Sector**
- Aerospace → highest performance CFRP
- Automotive → mid-range GFRP, natural-fibre composites
- Marine → glass/polyester, sandwich hulls
- Rail → phenolic-based fire-retardant composites

### 2.2 Operational Logic: Why Composites are Indispensable in Transportation

The engineering rationale is captured in this **six-step logic chain**:

1. **Emission Mandate** → Global regulations (CAFE, Euro 7, ICAO CORSIA) demand reduced fuel burn and CO₂ output.
2. **Weight as the Lever** → Vehicle mass is the dominant controllable variable; every **1% reduction in mass** yields ~**0.7% fuel saving** in aircraft and ~**1%** in automobiles.
3. **Conventional Material Ceiling** → Steel (ρ = 7.8 g/cm³) and aluminium (ρ = 2.7 g/cm³) cannot meet ambitious weight targets below safe stiffness limits.
4. **Composite Solution** → CFRP offers ρ ≈ 1.6 g/cm³ with E ≈ 140–700 GPa — outperforming aluminium stiffness at half the weight.
5. **Manufacturing Feasibility** → Resin transfer moulding (RTM), autoclave curing, and pultrusion enable mass production of complex aerodynamic shapes.
6. **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** → Although raw composite cost is higher, life-cycle savings in fuel, maintenance (corrosion-free), and payload revenue offset the initial premium.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$ | **Rule of Mixtures — Strength** (iso-strain) | MPa |
| 2 | $E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ | **Rule of Mixtures — Modulus** (iso-strain, parallel loading) | GPa |
| 3 | $\dfrac{1}{E_c} = \dfrac{V_f}{E_f} + \dfrac{V_m}{E_m}$ | **Inverse Rule of Mixtures** (iso-stress, series loading) | 1/GPa |
| 4 | $V_f + V_m = 1$ | **Volume Conservation** | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$ | **Rule of Mixtures — Density** | g/cm³ |
| 6 | $\left(\sigma/\rho\right)_c = \dfrac{\sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m}{\rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m}$ | **Specific Strength** of composite | kN·m/kg |
| 7 | $\left(E/\rho\right)_c = \dfrac{E_f V_f + E_m V_m}{\rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m}$ | **Specific Modulus** of composite | MN·m/kg |
| 8 | $\Delta W\,(\%) = \left(1 - \dfrac{\rho_{composite}}{\rho_{baseline}}\right) \times 100$ | **Weight Saving Percentage** vs. metal baseline | % |
| 9 | $\eta = \dfrac{F_{saved}}{F_{extra}} = \dfrac{\Delta m \cdot g}{\Delta m_{fuel}/\text{year}}$ | **Fuel-Burn Efficiency Gain** | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **LaTeX Safety Notice:** Vertical bars $\vert$ have been deliberately rendered using `\vert` / `\mid` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. Always follow this convention in your KTU answer sheets when typesetting in markdown.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The above formulas are used by transportation engineers at **four decisive design gates**:

1. **Conceptual Design Gate** → Specific strength/modulus tables rank candidate materials.
2. **Preliminary Sizing Gate** → Rule-of-mixtures predicts laminate stiffness from layup.
3. **Certification Gate** → Weight-saved % must satisfy airworthiness (FAR 25) or automotive (ECE R100) regulations.
4. **Cost Gate** → TCO model determines whether the composite variant is procured.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Rule of Mixtures for Composite Density

We derive the density of a unidirectional fibre-reinforced composite from **first principles** (mass conservation).

**Step 1 — Setup.** Consider a composite element of total volume $V_c$ containing fibre volume $V_f$ and matrix volume $V_m$. From volume conservation:

$$
V_c = V_f + V_m
$$

Dividing through by $V_c$, we obtain the **volume fractions**:

$$
V_{f,\text{vol}} + V_{m,\text{vol}} = 1
$$

**Step 2 — Mass Definition.** The total mass $m_c$ of the composite is the sum of fibre mass $m_f$ and matrix mass $m_m$:

$$
m_c = m_f + m_m
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Mass-Volume.** Since mass = density × volume:

$$
\rho_c V_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m
$$

**Step 4 — Divide by Total Volume.** Dividing both sides by $V_c$:

$$
\rho_c = \rho_f \dfrac{V_f}{V_c} + \rho_m \dfrac{V_m}{V_c}
$$

**Step 5 — Introduce Volume Fractions.** Using $v_f = V_f / V_c$ and $v_m = V_m / V_c$:

$$
\boxed{\rho_c = \rho_f \, v_f + \rho_m \, v_m}
$$

where $v_f + v_m = 1$. This is the **density rule of mixtures** — the foundation for all specific-property calculations in transportation composite selection.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Specific Strength of a Unidirectional CFRP

**Given data (typical aerospace-grade T700 carbon / epoxy system):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_f &= 4900 \text{ MPa}, \quad \rho_f = 1.80 \text{ g/cm}^3 \\
\sigma_m &=  85 \text{ MPa}, \quad \rho_m = 1.20 \text{ g/cm}^3 \\
v_f &= 0.60, \quad v_m = 0.40
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Compute Composite Strength (Iso-Strain ROM):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_c &= \sigma_f v_f + \sigma_m v_m \\
         &= (4900)(0.60) + (85)(0.40) \\
         &= 2940 + 34 \\
         &= 2974 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Composite Density:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_c &= \rho_f v_f + \rho_m v_m \\
       &= (1.80)(0.60) + (1.20)(0.40) \\
       &= 1.080 + 0.480 \\
       &= 1.560 \text{ g/cm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Specific Strength:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left(\sigma/\rho\right)_c &= \dfrac{2974 \text{ MPa}}{1.560 \text{ g/cm}^3} \\
                           &= 1906.4 \text{ MPa·cm}^3/\text{g} \\
                           &= 1906.4 \text{ kN·m/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare with Aluminium 2024-T3 baseline:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{Al} &= 470 \text{ MPa}, \quad \rho_{Al} = 2.70 \text{ g/cm}^3 \\
\left(\sigma/\rho\right)_{Al} &= \dfrac{470}{2.70} = 174.1 \text{ kN·m/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Specific Strength Advantage:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Advantage} &= \dfrac{(\sigma/\rho)_c}{(\sigma/\rho)_{Al}} = \dfrac{1906.4}{174.1} \\
                &\approx 10.95
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** The CFRP delivers **roughly 11× the specific strength of aluminium 2024-T3**. This single number justifies the use of composites in every primary aerospace structure (Boeing 787, Airbus A350 wing, SpaceX Dragon fairing).

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Weight Saving Calculation for an Automotive Hood

A passenger car's **bonnet (hood panel)** is redesigned from aluminium (baseline) to a **glass-fibre-reinforced polymer (GFRP)** laminate.

**Given:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Baseline mass} &= 9.5 \text{ kg (Al 5182)} \\
\rho_{Al} &= 2.70 \text{ g/cm}^3 \\
\rho_{GFRP} &= 1.65 \text{ g/cm}^3 \quad (\text{typical SMC, } v_f = 0.30)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Apply Weight-Saving Formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta W\,(\%) &= \left(1 - \dfrac{\rho_{GFRP}}{\rho_{Al}}\right) \times 100 \\
               &= \left(1 - \dfrac{1.65}{2.70}\right) \times 100 \\
               &= (1 - 0.6111) \times 100 \\
               &= 38.89\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute New Hood Mass:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{GFRP} &= 9.5 \times \left(1 - 0.3889\right) \\
        &= 9.5 \times 0.6111 \\
        &= 5.81 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Translate into Annual Fuel Savings (assuming $1\%$ mass reduction $\rightarrow 0.7\%$ fuel saving, $15\,000$ km/yr, $6$ L/100 km):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Fuel saving/yr} &= 0.3889 \times 0.7 \times 900 \text{ L} \\
                     &= 245.0 \text{ L/yr} \\
\text{CO}_2 \text{ avoided} &= 245 \times 2.31 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{L} \\
                           &= 565.95 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{yr per vehicle}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering Verdict:** A single composite hood replacement cuts ~**566 kg of CO₂/year** per vehicle. Scaled across a million-vehicle fleet, this is ~**0.57 million tonnes of CO₂ abated annually**.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Application-to-Material Mapping

| Application | Composite System | Justification | KTU Concept Tested |
|---|---|---|---|
| Boeing 787 wing | CFRP (T800/3900-2 epoxy) | 50% weight saving vs Al | Specific modulus, fatigue |
| Airbus A320neo sharklet | CFRP thermoplastic | Aerodynamic efficiency | Manufacturing: RTM |
| Tesla Model Y floor | GFRP/SMC + carbon | Cost-effective mass saving | Rule of mixtures |
| Wind turbine blade (75 m) | E-glass/epoxy | Highest specific stiffness | Specific modulus |
| Indian Railways LHB coach | Phenolic GFRP | Fire-retardant, smoke class | Fire, smoke standards |
| Navy mine-hunter hull | Sandwich GFRP/cored | Non-magnetic signature | Magnetic neutrality |
| F1 monocoque | CFRP honeycomb | Crash energy absorption | Crashworthiness, FSR |
| LNG cargo tank insulation | Foam-cored composite | Cryogenic thermal barrier | Thermal design |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Material Selection for a Transportation Component

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Design Requirement: Reduce Weight] --> B{Load Type?}
    B -->|Tension/Dominated| C[Unidirectional CFRP]
    B -->|Biaxial/Bending| D[Woven Fabric Composite]
    B -->|Impact/Dynamic| E[Hybrid Fibre Laminate]
    B -->|Compression Buckling| F[Honeycomb Sandwich]
    C --> G{Volume Fraction Optimisation}
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Apply Rule of Mixtures]
    H --> I{Specific Strength >= Target?}
    I -->|Yes| J[Validate: Fatigue, Creep, Fire]
    I -->|No| K[Increase v_f or Switch Fibre]
    K --> G
    J --> L{Manufacturing Route Feasible?}
    L -->|Autoclave OK| M[Airframe Structure]
    L -->|RTM Required| N[Automotive Body Panel]
    L -->|Pultrusion OK| O[Rail/Marine Profile]
```

### 4.2 Schematic: Composite Share by Transportation Sector

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Aerospace
        A1[Fuselage 53% CFRP] --> A2[Wing 50% CFRP]
        A2 --> A3[Empennage CFRP]
    end
    subgraph Automotive
        B1[Body Panels SMC] --> B2[Chassis CFRP]
        B2 --> B3[Interior Natural Fibre]
    end
    subgraph Marine
        C1[Hull Sandwich GFRP] --> C2[Deck CFRP]
        C2 --> C3[Mast Aramid]
    end
    subgraph Rail
        D1[Coach Side Phenolic GFRP] --> D2[Bogies GFRP]
        D2 --> D3[Cabin CFRP]
    end
    Aerospace --> Shared[(Common Knowledge Base)]
    Automotive --> Shared
    Marine --> Shared
    Rail --> Shared
```

### 4.3 Schematic: Property Hierarchy for Transportation Composites

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Property Demands in Transportation] --> P1[Lightweighting]
    P --> P2[High Specific Strength]
    P --> P3[Fatigue Resistance]
    P --> P4[Corrosion Resistance]
    P --> P5[Fire / Smoke Compliance]
    P1 --> R[Achieved via Low Density Matrix]
    P2 --> R
    P3 --> R
    P4 --> R
    P5 --> R
    R --> S[Composite Solution]
    S --> T[CFRP / GFRP / Aramid / Sandwich / Hybrid]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define the term "specific strength" and explain why it is the most important performance metric in transportation composite selection.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Specific strength is defined as the ratio of a material's ultimate tensile strength to its density, $\sigma/\rho$, expressed in **kN·m/kg**. It quantifies the load-carrying capacity per unit weight, and is the decisive metric in transportation because every kilogram saved translates directly into payload gain, fuel reduction, and emissions cut. For example, CFRP has $\sigma/\rho \approx 1900$ kN·m/kg versus 174 kN·m/kg for aluminium — a **~11× advantage** — which justifies its use in aircraft wings, helicopter blades, and EV battery enclosures. (3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for unit, 1 for example)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*List any four major application areas of composites in the aerospace sector, naming the type of composite used in each.*

**Model Answer (4 × 0.75 = 3 marks):**

| Application | Composite Used |
|---|---|
| Aircraft fuselage skin | CFRP (woven) |
| Helicopter rotor blade | CFRP + honeycomb core |
| Rocket nozzle throat | Carbon-carbon (C/C) |
| Spacecraft re-entry tile | Ceramic matrix composite (SiC/SiC) |
| Satellite antenna reflector | CFRP sandwich with aluminium honeycomb |

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

**Q3. (a) [KTU University Exam — May 2024, 7 marks]**
*Derive the rule of mixtures for the density of a unidirectional fibre-reinforced composite, clearly stating the underlying assumptions.*

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

1. **Assumption — Volume additivity:** The total volume equals the sum of fibre and matrix volumes, i.e. $V_c = V_f + V_m$ — valid when there is no void content. **(1 mark)**
2. **Mass conservation:** $m_c = m_f + m_m$. **(1 mark)**
3. **Substitute** $m = \rho V$: $\rho_c V_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$. **(1 mark)**
4. **Divide** by $V_c$ to get $\rho_c = \rho_f (V_f/V_c) + \rho_m (V_m/V_c)$. **(1 mark)**
5. **Introduce volume fractions** $v_f = V_f/V_c$, $v_m = V_m/V_c$:

$$
\boxed{\rho_c = \rho_f \, v_f + \rho_m \, v_m, \quad \text{with } v_f + v_m = 1}
$$

**(2 marks)** for the boxed final expression.
6. **Discussion (1 mark):** The relation is exact only when voids are absent. In real composites, $v_f + v_m < 1$ introduces a void volume, slightly reducing $\rho_c$ below the predicted value.

---

**Q3. (b) [KTU University Exam — May 2024, 7 marks]**
*A unidirectional CFRP lamina is fabricated with $v_f = 0.55$. If the carbon fibre has $\sigma_f = 4900$ MPa, $\rho_f = 1.80$ g/cm³ and the epoxy matrix has $\sigma_m = 80$ MPa, $\rho_m = 1.20$ g/cm³, compute (i) composite strength, (ii) composite density, and (iii) specific strength. Comment on the suitability of this material for an aircraft wing skin.*

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**(i) Composite strength — Rule of Mixtures (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_c &= \sigma_f v_f + \sigma_m v_m \\
         &= (4900)(0.55) + (80)(0.45) \\
         &= 2695 + 36 \\
         &= 2731 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Composite density (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_c &= \rho_f v_f + \rho_m v_m \\
       &= (1.80)(0.55) + (1.20)(0.45) \\
       &= 0.990 + 0.540 \\
       &= 1.530 \text{ g/cm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Specific strength (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left(\sigma/\rho\right)_c &= \dfrac{2731}{1.530} = 1785.0 \text{ MPa·cm}^3/\text{g} \\
                           &= 1785 \text{ kN·m/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment (1 mark):** With specific strength $\approx 1785$ kN·m/kg — over **10× that of aluminium 2024-T3** — this CFRP is exceptionally well-suited for an aircraft wing skin where weight saving, fatigue resistance, and stiffness are critical.

---

## Part B — Alternative Choice

**Q4. (a) [7 marks]**
*Explain with examples the classification of composites used in the transportation industry based on (i) matrix phase and (ii) reinforcement geometry. Mention one specific application for each class.*

**Model Answer Outline (7 marks — 3.5 marks each section):**
- **Matrix classification** — PMC (e.g. CFRP/epoxy wing skin, Boeing 787), MMC (e.g. Al/SiC piston in diesel engine, Toyota), CMC (e.g. SiC/SiC jet-engine combustor, GE LEAP). (3.5 marks)
- **Reinforcement geometry** — Fibre-reinforced (CFRP drive shaft in BMW i8), particulate-reinforced (SiC particle-reinforced Al brake rotor), laminate/sandwich (honeycomb-cored CFRP floor panel in Airbus A350). (3.5 marks)

---

**Q4. (b) [7 marks]**
*The windshield frame of an electric car is redesigned from aluminium alloy ($\rho = 2.70$ g/cm³) to a CFRP composite ($\rho = 1.55$ g/cm³). If the original part mass is 7.2 kg, calculate the percentage weight saving and the new part mass. Discuss the implications for EV range.*

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Weight saving % (3 marks):**

$$
\Delta W\,(\%) = \left(1 - \dfrac{1.55}{2.70}\right)\times 100 = 42.59\,\%
$$

**Step 2 — New part mass (2 marks):**

$$
m_{CFRP} = 7.2 \times (1 - 0.4259) = 7.2 \times 0.5741 = 4.13 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 3 — Range implications (2 marks):** A 42.6% mass reduction on a single component (3.07 kg lighter) contributes to a vehicle-level mass drop of similar order when applied to multiple panels. Using the standard heuristic *1% mass reduction ⇒ ~1% range increase in EVs*, this alone delivers ~0.4% additional range per component, which scales to significant gains when applied across 30+ such components in a typical EV.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch in specific strength** — students write MPa/g·cm³ without conversion to kN·m/kg, losing 1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting volume conservation** $v_f + v_m = 1$ — losing 1 mark when $v_f$ is given but $v_m$ must be derived.
> 3. **Confusing iso-strain vs iso-stress ROM** — iso-strain is for parallel (fibre-aligned) loading; iso-stress is for transverse loading. Mixing these in a single calculation = 2-mark loss.
> 4. **Neglecting voids** — in transport composites, void content $v_v \approx 0.01$–$0.03$ is significant and must be mentioned in derivation for full marks.
> 5. **Generic "lightweight" answers** without quantitative justification = loss of 2 marks in 14-mark questions. Always quote **specific strength numbers** in kN·m/kg.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Transportation is the largest commercial driver of composite materials** — aerospace, automotive, marine, and rail all rely on the lightweighting–strength advantage.
- **Specific strength** ($\sigma/\rho$) and **specific modulus** ($E/\rho$) are the two **decisive performance indices** in any transportation material-selection problem.
- **Rule of Mixtures (RoM)** under **iso-strain** loading: $\sigma_c = \sigma_f v_f + \sigma_m v_m$ and $E_c = E_f v_f + E_m v_m$ — applicable when fibres are aligned with the load.
- **Inverse RoM** under **iso-stress** loading: $\dfrac{1}{E_c} = \dfrac{v_f}{E_f} + \dfrac{v_m}{E_m}$ — applicable for transverse loading of unidirectional lamina.
- **Volume conservation** $v_f + v_m + v_v = 1$ (with void fraction $v_v$) — always stated in derivations.
- **CFRP** offers ~**10–11× the specific strength of aluminium** and ~**20× that of mild steel** — the headline justification for transport composite adoption.
- **Weight saving formula:** $\Delta W\,(\%) = \left(1 - \rho_{composite}/\rho_{baseline}\right) \times 100$.
- **Application snapshot:** Boeing 787 — 50% CFRP; Airbus A350 — 53% CFRP; F1 cars — >75% CFRP; modern metro coaches — GFRP phenolic for fire compliance.
- **Industry targets:** *1% mass saving ⇒ 0.7% fuel saving (aircraft)*, *1% mass saving ⇒ 1% range gain (EV)* — memorise these two heuristic ratios.
- **Matrix classification in transportation:** PMC (dominant), MMC (engine internals), CMC (high-temperature exhaust/turbine).
- **Reinforcement classification:** continuous fibre (primary load path), woven fabric (balanced bi-axial), chopped fibre (low-cost SMC), particulate (wear resistance), sandwich (bending stiffness).
- **Always end a transportation composite answer** with a **quantitative** statement (e.g., "CFRP gives $\sigma/\rho \approx 1785$ kN·m/kg, enabling a 42.6% weight saving over aluminium") — never a vague qualitative claim.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy and Electronics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT744 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Energy and Electronics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):** *Energy and Electronics applications of composites* refer to the strategic deployment of **fibre-reinforced polymers (FRPs)**, **particulate composites**, **laminates**, and **nanocomposites** as functional and structural materials in energy generation, energy storage, energy conversion, and electronic/electrical device systems. These applications exploit the tailorable properties of composites — such as high specific strength, low density, tunable electrical conductivity, corrosion resistance, and thermal stability — to overcome the inherent limitations of monolithic metals, ceramics, and neat polymers.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** framework, this topic bridges the fundamental constituents (matrix + reinforcement) covered in earlier syllabus units with the downstream **engineering utility** that justifies composite design. Two major engineering domains are emphasised:

- **Energy Sector:** Wind turbine blades, photovoltaic (PV) panel substrates, fuel cell bipolar plates, hydrogen storage vessels, battery separators, and flywheel rotors.
- **Electronics Sector:** Printed circuit board (PCB) substrates, semiconductor encapsulation/packaging, Electromagnetic Interference (EMI) shielding enclosures, conductive inks, sensors, and flexible optoelectronic devices.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Composites for Energy & Electronics?**
> Traditional materials (steel, aluminium, neat polymers) cannot simultaneously satisfy the conflicting demands of modern systems — e.g., the need for *light weight + high stiffness + fatigue resistance* in a wind blade, or *low dielectric loss + thermal conductivity + dimensional stability* in a PCB substrate. Composites offer **property-by-design** flexibility, making them indispensable.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world Analogy — "The Swiss Army Knife of Materials"**
> Think of a composite material as a **Swiss Army knife**. A plain steel blade (monolithic metal) is strong, but heavy and rust-prone. A wooden handle (neat polymer) is light, but weak. A composite — carbon fibres embedded in epoxy — is like a knife where the *steel core is wrapped in a reinforced polymer grip*: it becomes lighter, stronger in specific directions, corrosion-resistant, and electrically tuneable. Now imagine scaling that knife up to the size of a **50-metre wind turbine blade** or down to a **microscopic capacitor dielectric** — that is the design freedom composites offer to the energy and electronics engineer.

**Key engineering metrics (must memorise):**

| Metric | Typical Composite Value | Conventional Material |
|---|---|---|
| **Specific strength** (strength/density) | 200–1500 kN·m/kg | 50–200 kN·m/kg (steel) |
| **Specific modulus** | 10–200 MN·m/kg | 25–27 MN·m/kg (steel) |
| **Electrical conductivity** | 10⁻¹⁰ – 10⁵ S/m (tunable) | 10⁻¹⁸ S/m (neat epoxy) |
| **Dielectric constant (1 MHz)** | 2.5–12 (tunable) | 4.5 (FR-4 epoxy) |
| **Thermal conductivity** | 0.2 – 1000 W/m·K | 0.2 W/m·K (neat polymer) |

## 1.3 Scope of the Topic (KTU Module Mapping)

The topic "Energy and Electronics" in Module 1 is positioned as the **terminal application module**, drawing from prior learning on:

- Matrix materials (thermosets, thermoplastics, metals, ceramics)
- Reinforcement types (glass, carbon, aramid, natural fibres, nano-fillers)
- Rule of mixtures, Halpin–Tsai, and other micromechanical models
- Manufacturing methods (hand lay-up, pultrusion, RTM, filament winding)

The student is expected to **apply** this foundation to discuss engineering case studies.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Composites in the **Energy Sector**

### 2.1.1 Wind Energy — Turbine Blades

Wind turbine blades (currently up to **115 m** in length for offshore units) are the largest single composite structures in commercial production. The dominant material is **E-glass fibre / epoxy** for the spar caps and shells, with **carbon fibre/epoxy** introduced in the spar caps of blades beyond 60 m to reduce mass and tower-head loading.

**Design drivers (Why composites?):**
- High **fatigue resistance** under 10⁸–10⁹ cyclic load cycles (20–25-year service life).
- Anisotropic stiffness tailoring — the spar cap runs along the blade length (0° plies) for bending stiffness, while the shell uses ±45° plies for torsional rigidity.
- Low density (~**1800–2000 kg/m³** for glass/epoxy) minimises gravitational and centrifugal loading.
- Corrosion resistance in marine/offshore environments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The fatigue life of a composite blade is governed by the **S–N curve (Wöhler curve)** of the unidirectional laminate. Unlike metals, composites exhibit a *gradual stiffness loss* rather than a sharp yield, so failure is defined at a critical stiffness reduction (typically 10%) — **not** at fracture.

### 2.1.2 Solar Energy — Photovoltaic (PV) Systems

Composites serve in two principal PV roles:
1. **Structural support & backsheets** — fibreglass/polyester panels for mounting frames and module backsheets.
2. **Concentrated Solar Power (CSP)** — carbon-fibre-reinforced polymers (CFRP) for parabolic trough and heliostat mirrors/structures, where low thermal expansion (~0–1 × 10⁻⁶/K) is critical for tracking accuracy.

Emerging application: **Perovskite solar cells** employ polymer–nanoparticle composite electron-transport layers (e.g., **TiO₂/PEDOT:PSS**) to enhance charge extraction.

### 2.1.3 Hydrogen Storage & Fuel Cells

- **Type IV hydrogen storage tanks** (350–700 bar) use a **high-density polyethylene (HDPE) liner over-wrapped with carbon-fibre/epoxy** composite. The composite carries the structural load; the polymer liner acts as a gas barrier.
- **Proton-Exchange Membrane Fuel Cells (PEMFC)** employ **graphite–polymer composite bipolar plates** (a carbon-filler/polymer matrix) as a metal-replacement offering corrosion resistance, low density, and mouldability.

### 2.1.4 Battery & Supercapacitor Components

- **Separator membranes**: microporous polyolefin composites (PE/PP) with ceramic (Al₂O₃, SiO₂) coatings for thermal stability.
- **Current collectors**: copper–carbon and aluminium–carbon composites replacing pure metal foils to reduce weight.
- **Electrode binders**: PVDF (polyvinylidene fluoride) and CMC/SBR composite binders replacing toxic solvents.

### 2.1.5 Flywheel Energy Storage

Energy stored in a spinning mass: $E = \tfrac{1}{2} I \omega^{2}$. To maximise specific energy, the rim is built from **carbon-fibre/epoxy** wound at high hoop-stress orientation, since the specific strength of CFRP (~**500 kN·m/kg**) is 5–10× that of maraging steel.

## 2.2 Composites in the **Electronics Sector**

### 2.2.1 Printed Circuit Board (PCB) Substrates

The industry-standard **FR-4** is itself a composite: **woven E-glass fabric** (reinforcement) impregnated with **brominated epoxy resin** (matrix). Key performance parameters:

- **Dielectric constant** $\varepsilon_{r}$: 4.2–4.5 at 1 MHz
- **Loss tangent** $\tan \delta$: 0.020–0.025
- **Glass transition temperature** $T_{g}$: 130–180 °C
- **Coefficient of thermal expansion (CTE)**: matched to copper (17 × 10⁻⁶/K) to prevent via-barrel cracking

High-frequency (>5G, mm-wave) applications shift to **PTFE/ceramic composites** with $\varepsilon_{r}$ down to 2.1 and $\tan \delta$ < 0.001.

### 2.2.2 Electronic Packaging & Encapsulation

- **Epoxy moulding compounds (EMCs)** filled with fused silica (SiO₂) at 85–92 wt% protect IC chips from moisture and mechanical shock.
- **Underfills** in flip-chip packaging use silica-filled epoxy composites to reduce CTE mismatch between silicon chip (2.6 ppm/K) and PCB (15–17 ppm/K).

### 2.2.3 Electromagnetic Interference (EMI) Shielding

Modern electronics emit EM radiation that must be contained. **Conductive polymer composites (CPCs)** — polymers loaded with carbon black, carbon nanotubes (CNT), graphene, or metal fibres — provide shielding effectiveness:

$$
SE_{total} = SE_{absorption} + SE_{reflection} + SE_{multi\text{-}reflection}
$$

$$
SE_{absorption} = 8.7 \, t \sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}
$$

where $t$ = thickness, $f$ = frequency, $\mu$ = permeability, $\sigma$ = electrical conductivity.

A typical target is **SE ≥ 60 dB** for military enclosures, achievable with 15–25 wt% CNT loading.

### 2.2.4 Sensors, Actuators & Flexible Electronics

- **Piezoresistive sensors**: carbon-black/silicone or CNT/elastomer composites change resistance under strain.
- **Dielectric elastomer actuators (DEA)**: acrylic or silicone elastomer composites coated with compliant electrodes convert voltage to mechanical strain.
- **Flexible displays**: indium-tin-oxide (ITO) is brittle; replacement candidates are **silver-nanowire/PEDOT:PSS composites** offering >90% transparency at <50 Ω/sq sheet resistance.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are KTU-board-exam-validated. Memorise the symbol, the equation, and the engineering context.**

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Application Context |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma_{c} = \sigma_{f} V_{f} + \sigma_{m} V_{m}$ | Rule of mixtures for strength | Unidirectional lamina, iso-strain |
| 2 | $E_{c} = E_{f} V_{f} + E_{m} V_{m}$ | Rule of mixtures for modulus | Unidirectional lamina, iso-strain |
| 3 | $E_{c} = \dfrac{E_{m} E_{f}}{E_{f} V_{m} + E_{m} V_{f}}$ | Inverse rule of mixtures | Transverse modulus, iso-stress |
| 4 | $E_{c} = E_{m} \dfrac{1 + \xi \eta V_{f}}{1 - \eta V_{f}}$, $\eta = \dfrac{E_{f}/E_{m} - 1}{E_{f}/E_{m} + \xi}$ | Halpin–Tsai equation | Short-fibre/modulus prediction |
| 5 | $E = \dfrac{\sigma}{\varepsilon}$ | Hooke's law | General stiffness |
| 6 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_{0}}$ | Engineering strain | Deflection/strain sensors |
| 7 | $R = R_{0}(1 + k\varepsilon)$ | Piezoresistive relation | Strain-gauge composite sensors |
| 8 | $SE = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{E_{0}}{E_{t}}\right) \text{ dB}$ | Shielding effectiveness | EMI shielding |
| 9 | $\sigma_{ac} = \dfrac{\sigma_{m}}{(1 - V_{f})/3}$ | Percolation scaling near threshold | Conductive composites |
| 10 | $\varepsilon_{r,eff} = \varepsilon_{m}(1 - V_{f}) + \varepsilon_{f} V_{f}$ | Dielectric rule of mixtures | PCB / capacitor design |
| 11 | $CTE_{c} = \alpha_{f} V_{f} + \alpha_{m} V_{m}$ | Thermal expansion (linear) | CTE-matched packaging |
| 12 | $\lambda_{c} = \lambda_{m}(1 - V_{f}) + \lambda_{f} V_{f}$ | Thermal conductivity (parallel) | Heat-spreader composites |
| 13 | $\rho_{c} = \rho_{f} V_{f} + \rho_{m} V_{m}$ | Density rule of mixtures | Specific-property calculations |
| 14 | $V_{f,crit} = \dfrac{1}{1 + (d_{f}/d_{c})^{2}}$ | Critical fibre volume (length factor) | Strength-design verification |
| 15 | $\dfrac{1}{E_{L}} = \dfrac{V_{f}}{E_{f}} + \dfrac{V_{m}}{E_{m}}$ | Inverse rule, longitudinal check | Cross-verification |

> [!WARNING]
> **Equation Pitfall:** When using Rule of Mixtures (ROM), always state the **iso-strain** (Voigt) or **iso-stress** (Reuss) assumption explicitly. The KTU examiner will deduct **1 mark** for a missing assumption statement. Also, when transverse loading is implied, use ROM only if asked; **Halpin–Tsai** is the preferred KTU-accepted model for transverse modulus.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Sector | Composite System | Performance Gain vs. Conventional |
|---|---|---|
| **Wind blades** | Glass/epoxy, hybrid glass-carbon/epoxy | 40% mass reduction, 3× fatigue life vs. steel |
| **Hydrogen tanks** | CFRP/HDPE Type IV | 4× gravimetric density (6 wt% H₂) vs. steel Type I |
| **Aircraft** | CFRP/epoxy (e.g., Boeing 787, Airbus A350) | 20% fuel saving; 50% composite by mass |
| **5G PCBs** | PTFE/ceramic composite | $\tan\delta$ < 0.001, enabling mm-wave signal integrity |
| **EMI enclosures** | CNT/PC, graphene/PP | 60–80 dB SE at 1–10 GHz, 30% lighter than Al |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section provides KTU-style worked derivations. Every algebraic transition is expanded explicitly. These derivations are **directly aligned with previous KTU University Exam questions** for 14-mark problems.

---

## 3.1 Worked Derivation — Minimum Fibre Volume for a Turbine Blade Spar Cap

**Problem Statement (Typical 7-mark sub-part):**
A unidirectional glass-fibre/epoxy composite spar cap is designed for a wind turbine blade. The fibre has $E_{f} = 72$ GPa, $\sigma_{f}^{u} = 2400$ MPa; the matrix has $E_{m} = 3.5$ GPa, $\sigma_{m}^{u} = 80$ MPa. Determine the **minimum fibre volume fraction** $V_{f,min}$ so that the composite strength $\sigma_{cu} \geq 1500$ MPa, assuming both fibre and matrix fail simultaneously at their ultimate strains and isostrain conditions apply.

### Solution Steps

**Step 1 — Identify the governing equation.**
For an isostrain (iso-stress-free, parallel-loaded) unidirectional composite, the strength rule of mixtures is:

$$
\sigma_{cu} = \sigma_{fu} V_{f} + \sigma_{mu}(1 - V_{f})
$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange for $V_{f}$.**
Bring the $\sigma_{mu}$ term to the LHS:

$$
\sigma_{cu} = \sigma_{fu} V_{f} + \sigma_{mu} - \sigma_{mu} V_{f}
$$

$$
\sigma_{cu} - \sigma_{mu} = V_{f}(\sigma_{fu} - \sigma_{mu})
$$

$$
V_{f} = \dfrac{\sigma_{cu} - \sigma_{mu}}{\sigma_{fu} - \sigma_{mu}}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$
V_{f,min} = \dfrac{1500 - 80}{2400 - 80} = \dfrac{1420}{2320}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the ratio.**

$$
V_{f,min} = 0.6121 \approx 61.2\%
$$

**Step 5 — Engineering interpretation.**
Since this value is **above the practical upper limit of $V_{f} \approx 60\%$** for unidirectional lay-ups, a **hybrid spar cap** (mixing carbon fibres in critical regions) is recommended. This is a standard KTU "design-judgment" follow-up worth **1 additional mark**.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory trick:** $V_{f,min}$ — *subtract matrix from composite, divide by (fibre minus matrix)*. Practice writing this derivation in **3 lines** in the exam to save time.

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation — Percolation Threshold for a Conductive Composite

**Problem Statement (Typical 7-mark sub-part):**
A polymer (matrix resistivity $10^{16}\ \Omega\cdot\text{cm}$) is filled with carbon-black particles (resistivity $10^{-2}\ \Omega\cdot\text{cm}$) of diameter $1\ \mu\text{m}$. Estimate the **critical volume fraction (percolation threshold)** $V_{f,c}$ using the excluded-volume model, and compute the composite conductivity at $V_{f} = 0.20$ and $V_{f} = 0.30$ using the scaling law $\sigma \propto (V_{f} - V_{f,c})^{t}$ with $t = 2.0$. Take the proportionality constant $A = 10^{4}\ \text{S/m}$ (carbon-black network limit).

### Solution Steps

**Step 1 — Percolation threshold for spherical particles.**

For 3D random packing of hard spheres, the classical excluded-volume result gives:

$$
V_{f,c} \approx 0.16 \quad \text{(3D site percolation, random close packing)}
$$

For 1 µm particles in a 3D network, this is a reasonable engineering estimate. (KTU accepts values in the range **0.15 – 0.18** for spherical carbon black.)

**Step 2 — Scaling law.**

$$
\sigma = A (V_{f} - V_{f,c})^{t}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute for $V_{f} = 0.20$.**

$$
\sigma_{0.20} = 10^{4} \times (0.20 - 0.16)^{2.0} = 10^{4} \times (0.04)^{2}
$$

$$
(0.04)^{2} = 0.0016
$$

$$
\sigma_{0.20} = 10^{4} \times 0.0016 = 16\ \text{S/m}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute for $V_{f} = 0.30$.**

$$
\sigma_{0.30} = 10^{4} \times (0.30 - 0.16)^{2.0} = 10^{4} \times (0.14)^{2}
$$

$$
(0.14)^{2} = 0.0196
$$

$$
\sigma_{0.30} = 10^{4} \times 0.0196 = 196\ \text{S/m}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.**
Increasing $V_{f}$ from 0.20 to 0.50 in the *post-percolation* regime yields a $\sim$12× conductivity jump, which is why conductive composites are designed slightly *above* $V_{f,c}$. The exponent $t$ is empirical (typically 1.6–2.0 in 3D).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common error:** Using $V_{f} < V_{f,c}$ in the scaling law gives a **negative argument**, producing mathematically invalid/imaginary results. Always state that $V_{f} > V_{f,c}$ before applying the scaling law.

---

## 3.3 Worked Derivation — Halpin–Tsai Modulus of a Short-Fibre Composite

**Problem Statement (7-mark):**
A polypropylene (PP) matrix is reinforced with 30 wt% short glass fibres (length = 3 mm, diameter = 15 µm). $E_{f} = 72$ GPa, $E_{m} = 1.7$ GPa. Compute the longitudinal modulus of the composite using the Halpin–Tsai equation. Assume fibre density = 2.55 g/cm³, matrix density = 0.91 g/cm³.

### Solution Steps

**Step 1 — Convert weight fraction to volume fraction.**

$$
V_{f} = \dfrac{w_{f}/\rho_{f}}{w_{f}/\rho_{f} + w_{m}/\rho_{m}}
$$

$$
V_{f} = \dfrac{0.30/2.55}{0.30/2.55 + 0.70/0.91} = \dfrac{0.1176}{0.1176 + 0.7692} = \dfrac{0.1176}{0.8868}
$$

$$
V_{f} = 0.1327 \quad (\text{or } 13.27\%)
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the aspect ratio and the Halpin parameter $\xi$.**

$$
\text{Aspect ratio } a = \dfrac{L}{d} = \dfrac{3000}{15} = 200
$$

$$
\xi = 2 \times \dfrac{L}{d} = 2a = 400
$$

(For longitudinal modulus in Halpin–Tsai, $\xi = 2(L/d)$.)

**Step 3 — Apply the Halpin–Tsai equation.**

$$
E_{L} = E_{m} \dfrac{1 + \xi \eta V_{f}}{1 - \eta V_{f}}
$$

Compute $\eta$:

$$
\eta = \dfrac{E_{f}/E_{m} - 1}{E_{f}/E_{m} + \xi} = \dfrac{72/1.7 - 1}{72/1.7 + 400} = \dfrac{42.35 - 1}{42.35 + 400} = \dfrac{41.35}{442.35}
$$

$$
\eta = 0.0935
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $V_{f}$ and $\eta$.**

$$
E_{L} = 1.7 \times \dfrac{1 + 400 \times 0.0935 \times 0.1327}{1 - 0.0935 \times 0.1327}
$$

Compute numerator term:

$$
400 \times 0.0935 \times 0.1327 = 4.965
$$

$$
1 + 4.965 = 5.965
$$

Compute denominator term:

$$
0.0935 \times 0.1327 = 0.01241
$$

$$
1 - 0.01241 = 0.98759
$$

**Step 5 — Final value.**

$$
E_{L} = 1.7 \times \dfrac{5.965}{0.98759} = 1.7 \times 6.040 = 10.27\ \text{GPa}
$$

**Step 6 — Engineering interpretation.**
Pure PP modulus ≈ 1.7 GPa. The composite modulus **6× higher** at only 13 vol% fibre — demonstrating the **anisotropic stiffness efficiency** of aligned short-fibre composites. For random orientation, this value would be ≈ $E_{L}/3$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Conventional vs. Composite Systems

> [!NOTE]
> **The following matrix is a KTU-expected "discussion" frame for 7-mark questions.** Use this format in your answers.

| Property | Steel (Conventional) | Aluminium | CFRP Composite | GFRP Composite |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Density (kg/m³) | 7850 | 2700 | 1600 | 1900 |
| Tensile strength (MPa) | 400 | 270 | 1500–3000 | 500–1500 |
| Young's modulus (GPa) | 200 | 70 | 130–250 | 35–55 |
| Specific strength (kN·m/kg) | 51 | 100 | 940–1875 | 263–789 |
| Specific modulus (MN·m/kg) | 25.5 | 25.9 | 81–156 | 18–29 |
| Corrosion resistance | Poor | Moderate | Excellent | Excellent |
| Fatigue limit (10⁷ cycles) | 0.5 × UTS | 0.4 × UTS | 0.6 × UTS | 0.3 × UTS |
| Cost (INR/kg, 2024) | 60–80 | 200–250 | 3000–8000 | 400–900 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagrams summarise the functional architecture of composite applications in Energy and Electronics. These diagrams map **material flow / function flow** rather than physical appearance, in line with the system constraints for non-graphical Mermaid rendering.

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Composite Application Architecture in Energy Systems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Energy Source Wind Solar Hydrogen Nuclear] --> B[Composite Subsystem Selection]
    B --> C1[Blade Shell Eglass Epoxy]
    B --> C2[Spar Cap Carbon Epoxy]
    B --> C3[Tank Liner HDPE]
    B --> C4[Tank Wrap Carbon Epoxy]
    B --> C5[PV Backsheet Polyester Glass]
    B --> C6[Fuel Cell Plate Graphite Polymer]
    C1 --> D[Energy Output Electricity Heat Motion]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    C5 --> D
    C6 --> D
    D --> E[Grid Storage End User]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The energy source feeds into a composite-subsystem selection block, which distributes the load among six functionally distinct composite components. Each component's engineered property targets a specific operating envelope, ultimately converging on a power/output stream.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Composite Function Map in Electronics

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Composite Material System] --> B[Functional Property]
    B --> C1[Mechanical Support]
    B --> C2[Electrical Conduction]
    B --> C3[Dielectric Insulation]
    B --> C4[Thermal Management]
    B --> C5[EMI Shielding]
    C1 --> D1[PCB Substrate FR4]
    C1 --> D2[Package Encapsulant Epoxy SiO2]
    C2 --> D3[Conductive Tracks Cu Clad]
    C2 --> D4[CPC CNT Polymer]
    C3 --> D5[High Frequency PTFE Ceramic]
    C4 --> D6[Heat Spreader Graphite Al]
    C5 --> D7[Shielding Enclosure Metal Fibre Composite]
```

**Reading the diagram:** A single composite material is engineered along five functional vectors, each producing a specific electronic component. This **multi-functionality** is the key advantage over monolithic materials.

## 4.3 Process Flow — Wind Blade Composite Manufacturing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Fibre Tow Glass or Carbon] --> M1[Resin Bath Epoxy]
    M1 --> P1[Prepreg Winding or Pultrusion]
    P1 --> P2[Layup on Blade Mold]
    P2 --> P3[Vacuum Bagging RTM or VARTM]
    P3 --> P4[Autoclave Curing 80 to 180 C]
    P4 --> P5[DEMOLDING]
    P5 --> P6[TRIMMING and BALANCING]
    P6 --> P7[INSTALLATION on Nacelle]
```

## 4.4 Hierarchical Decision Tree — Selecting a Composite for an Energy/Electronic Application

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Design Requirement] --> S1{Load Bearing}
    S1 --> S2[Yes] --> S3{Operating Temperature}
    S3 --> S4[Less than 200 C] --> S5[Glass or Carbon Epoxy]
    S3 --> S6[Greater than 200 C] --> S7[BMI or Polyimide Composite]
    S1 --> S8[No Functional Need] --> S9{Electrical Function}
    S9 --> S10[Conductive] --> S11[CNT or Metal Filled Polymer]
    S9 --> S12[Insulating] --> S13[Neat Polymer or Glass Epoxy]
    S9 --> S14[EMI Shielding] --> S15[Carbon Filled Polymer 15 to 25 wt pct]
    S8 --> S16{Thermal Function}
    S16 --> S17[Conductive] --> S18[Graphite or Al Filled Composite]
    S16 --> S19[Insulating] --> S20[Aerogel or Hollow Sphere Composite]
```
