<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, PEPOT744, Module 1]**

**"Differentiate between the functions of the fibre and the matrix in a fibre-reinforced composite." (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Fibre (Reinforcement) | Matrix |
|--------|----------------------|--------|
| **Primary Role** | Carries the principal tensile / compressive load | Binds fibres, transfers load, protects them |
| **Properties Provided** | High strength, high stiffness | Toughness, shape, environmental resistance |
| **Governs** | Longitudinal properties | Transverse properties, interlaminar shear |
| **Typical Materials** | Glass, Carbon, Aramid | Epoxy, PEEK, Polyester |

The fibre is the **load-bearing phase**, while the matrix is the **continuous binder and protector**. Neither is effective alone; their **synergistic combination** through the interface produces the composite's superior properties. **(3 Marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, PEPOT744, Module 1]**

**"What are the key differences between thermoplastic and thermoset matrix composites? Give two examples of each." (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Thermoplastic Matrix | Thermoset Matrix |
|----------|----------------------|------------------|
| **Molecular Structure** | Linear / branched chains, no cross-linking | 3-D cross-linked network |
| **Curing** | Solidifies on cooling (reversible) | Cures irreversibly by chemical reaction |
| **Recyclability** | Can be remelted and reprocessed | Cannot be remelted |
| **Examples** | PEEK, PPS, PP, Nylon | Epoxy, Polyester, Vinyl ester |

**Examples — Thermoplastic:** PEEK, PPS. **Examples — Thermoset:** Epoxy, Polyester. **(3 Marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PEPOT744, Module 1]**

**(a) Explain in detail the various functions of the fibre and the matrix in a composite material. Discuss the role of the fibre–matrix interface. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The functions of the **fibre** are:
1. **Primary load carrier** along its longitudinal axis, providing high tensile strength and modulus.
2. **Stiffness contributor**, dictating the longitudinal modulus of the composite via the rule of mixtures: $E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$.
3. **Crack arrester** — fibres bridge cracks and absorb energy through mechanisms such as fibre pull-out, debonding, and fracture.
4. **Functional properties** — depending on fibre type, the composite can inherit electrical conductivity (carbon), impact resistance (aramid), or transparency (glass). **(3.5 Marks)**

The functions of the **matrix** are:
1. **Binder** — holds fibres in position, maintains orientation, spacing, and volume fraction.
2. **Load transfer agent** — distributes external load to the fibres via interfacial shear.
3. **Environmental shield** — protects fibres from moisture, chemicals, abrasion, and oxidation.
4. **Shape provider** — gives the composite its final geometry, surface finish, and dimensional stability. **(3 Marks)**

The **fibre–matrix interface** is the critical region enabling load transfer between the two phases. A well-engineered interface (e.g., silane treatment on glass fibres) ensures efficient stress transfer. The **interfacial shear strength ($\tau_i$)** determines the **critical fibre length** $l_c = \sigma_f d / 2\tau_i$. A bond that is too strong leads to brittle failure; too weak leads to fibre pull-out. The optimum bond gives a balance of strength and toughness. **(0.5 Mark)**

---

**(b) A unidirectional carbon-fibre/epoxy composite has a fibre volume fraction of 0.55. The carbon fibre has a Young's modulus of 380 GPa and density of 1.80 g/cm³. The epoxy matrix has a Young's modulus of 3.0 GPa and density of 1.25 g/cm³. Calculate: (i) the longitudinal Young's modulus, (ii) the density, and (iii) the specific stiffness of the composite. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $V_f = 0.55$, $V_m = 0.45$, $E_f = 380$ GPa, $E_m = 3.0$ GPa, $\rho_f = 1.80$ g/cm³, $\rho_m = 1.25$ g/cm³.

**(i) Longitudinal Young's Modulus (Rule of Mixtures):**

$$E_c = V_f E_f + V_m E_m$$

$$E_c = (0.55)(380) + (0.45)(3.0)$$

$$E_c = 209.0 + 1.35$$

$$\boxed{E_c = 210.35 \text{ GPa}} \quad \textbf{(2.5 Marks)}$$

**(ii) Density of the Composite:**

$$\rho_c = V_f \rho_f + V_m \rho_m$$

$$\rho_c = (0.55)(1.80) + (0.45)(1.25)$$

$$\rho_c = 0.990 + 0.5625$$

$$\boxed{\rho_c = 1.5525 \text{ g/cm}^3} \quad \textbf{(2 Marks)}$$

**(iii) Specific Stiffness:**

$$SE = \dfrac{E_c}{\rho_c} = \dfrac{210.35}{1.5525}$$

$$\boxed{SE \approx 135.5 \text{ GPa} \cdot \text{cm}^3/\text{g}} \quad \textbf{(2.5 Marks)}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PEPOT744, Module 1]**

**(a) Compare thermoplastic and thermoset matrix composites with respect to (i) molecular structure, (ii) processing method, (iii) mechanical properties, (iv) recyclability, and (v) typical applications. Give suitable examples. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Property | Thermoplastic Matrix Composites | Thermoset Matrix Composites |
|----------|--------------------------------|-----------------------------|
| **(i) Molecular Structure** | Linear or branched polymer chains held by secondary bonds; no cross-links | Three-dimensional cross-linked network formed by covalent bonds during curing |
| **(ii) Processing Method** | Melt processing — heated above melting point, moulded, then cooled; methods include injection moulding, compression moulding, thermoforming, filament winding with in-situ consolidation | Liquid resin processing — resin is cured by heat, catalyst, or radiation; methods include hand lay-up, RTM, pultrusion, autoclave, vacuum bagging |
| **(iii) Mechanical Properties** | Higher fracture toughness, higher impact resistance, lower creep resistance, lower stiffness | Higher stiffness and strength, better creep resistance, better chemical and thermal resistance, lower impact toughness |
| **(iv) Recyclability** | Reprocessable — can be remelted and reshaped | Non-reprocessable — cannot be remelted; chemical recycling possible but difficult |
| **(v) Typical Applications** | Aerospace interior components, automotive under-the-hood parts, sporting goods | Aerospace primary structures, boat hulls, wind turbine blades, circuit boards |

**Examples — Thermoplastic:** PEEK, PPS, Nylon, Polypropylene. **Examples — Thermoset:** Epoxy, Polyester, Vinyl ester, Phenolic. **(7 Marks — 1.4 per row)**

---

**(b) A glass-fibre-reinforced composite has a fibre tensile strength of 2.5 GPa, a fibre diameter of 14 $\mu$m, and an interfacial shear strength of 40 MPa. Calculate: (i) the critical fibre length, and (ii) the critical aspect ratio. If the actual fibre length is 3 mm, comment on whether the fibre can develop its full strength. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $\sigma_f = 2.5 \text{ GPa} = 2500 \text{ MPa}$, $d = 14 \, \mu\text{m} = 14 \times 10^{-3}$ mm, $\tau_i = 40$ MPa, $l = 3$ mm.

**(i) Critical Fibre Length:**

$$l_c = \dfrac{\sigma_f \cdot d}{2 \tau_i}$$

$$l_c = \dfrac{(2500 \text{ MPa})(14 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mm})}{2 \times 40 \text{ MPa}}$$

$$l_c = \dfrac{35}{80}$$

$$\boxed{l_c = 0.4375 \text{ mm}} \quad \textbf{(2.5 Marks)}$$

**(ii) Critical Aspect Ratio:**

$$\left(\dfrac{l}{d}\right)_c = \dfrac{\sigma_f}{2 \tau_i} = \dfrac{2500}{2 \times 40} = \dfrac{2500}{80}$$

$$\boxed{(l/d)_c = 31.25} \quad \textbf{(2 Marks)}$$

**Comment:** The actual fibre length is **3 mm**, which is much greater than $l_c = 0.4375$ mm. Hence, $l \gg l_c$, and the fibre **can develop its full tensile strength** before pulling out of the matrix. The composite is therefore operating in the **efficient reinforcement regime**. **(2.5 Marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing up Longitudinal and Transverse Formulas:** The rule of mixtures $E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ applies **ONLY for longitudinal loading**. For transverse loading, you **must** use the reciprocal formula $1/E_c = V_f/E_f + V_m/E_m$. Examiners deduct up to **2 marks** for this confusion.
> 2. **Unit Inconsistency in Critical Length Problems:** $\sigma_f$ must be in MPa (or N/mm²), $d$ in mm, and $\tau_i$ in MPa to get $l_c$ in mm. Mixing $\mu$m and mm without conversion is a frequent error.
> 3. **Forgetting the Interphase:** When asked about fibre and matrix functions, many students omit the **interface** entirely. The interface is the **third constituent** and is mandatory in any KTU answer worth 7+ marks.
> 4. **Confusing "Curing" with "Cooling":** Thermosets **cure** (irreversible chemical reaction); thermoplastics **solidify** (reversible physical cooling). Examiners specifically test this terminology.
> 5. **Specific Property Comparison:** When asked for "advantages of composites", never say "stronger than steel" — always say **"higher specific strength / specific stiffness than steel"** to earn full credit.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Module 1 (PEPOT744)**

- **Fibre functions:** load carrier, stiffness provider, crack arrester, functional property contributor.
- **Matrix functions:** binder, load transfer agent, environmental shield, shape provider.
- **Interface:** critical for stress transfer; governed by $l_c = \sigma_f d / 2\tau_i$.
- **Rule of Mixtures (Longitudinal):** $E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ — **iso-strain / Voigt model**.
- **Rule of Mixtures (Transverse):** $1/E_c = V_f/E_f + V_m/E_m$ — **iso-stress / Reuss model**.
- **Density rule:** $\rho_c = V_f \rho_f + V_m \rho_m$.
- **Specific strength / stiffness:** $SS = \sigma_c / \rho_c$, $SE = E_c / \rho_c$.
- **Thermoplastics:** linear chains, **reversible**, remeltable, examples: **PEEK, PPS, PP, Nylon**.
- **Thermosets:** cross-linked, **irreversible**, higher temperature resistance, examples: **Epoxy, Polyester, Vinyl ester, Phenolic**.
- **Composite advantages:** high specific strength, high specific stiffness, fatigue resistance, corrosion resistance, **anisotropic tailorability**, damage tolerance.
- **Aerospace:** Boeing 787 is ~53% composites by mass; Airbus A350 is ~52%.
- **Wind energy:** composite blades exceed 100 m length; steel would be too heavy.
- **Automotive:** CFRP drive shafts, composite leaf springs, monocoque chassis.
- **Marine:** glass-fibre/polyester hulls, decks, masts — corrosion-free.
- **Sports:** tennis rackets, golf shafts, bicycle frames, helmets, ski poles.
- **Critical fibre length criterion:** if $l \geq l_c$ → fibre develops full strength; if $l \ll l_c$ → fibre pulls out.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Strengthening Mechanism That Makes Composites Stronger; Environmental Effects on Design of Composite Structures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT744 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** The Strengthening Mechanism That Makes Composites Stronger; Environmental Effects on Design of Composite Structures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Strengthening Mechanism — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strengthening Mechanism in Fiber-Reinforced Composites:**
> The strengthening mechanism in composites is the **load transfer process** by which an externally applied stress is transferred from the relatively weak, compliant **matrix** phase into the high-strength, high-stiffness **fiber** phase through **interfacial shear stresses** developed at the fiber–matrix interface. Because the fibers carry the majority of the load along their longitudinal axis, the composite exhibits a strength and stiffness that **no single constituent could achieve alone**. This synergistic behaviour is quantified by the **Rule of Mixtures** and the **Kelly–Tyson shear-lag model**.

The three essential ingredients for effective strengthening are:
1. A **high-strength, high-modulus fiber** (the load bearer).
2. A **ductile, tough matrix** (the load distributor and protector).
3. A **strong, chemically bonded fiber–matrix interface** (the load transfer medium).

If any one of these is missing, the composite fails to exploit its full potential. For example, if the interfacial bond is weak, the fiber simply pulls out of the matrix like a slippery thread; the load never reaches the fiber's full capacity.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Rope Bridge"

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy: Reinforced Concrete vs. Rope Bridge**
> Imagine you are crossing a **rope bridge** over a deep gorge. The wooden planks (matrix) are weak on their own — they would snap under your weight. But the **steel cables** (fibers) running beneath the planks carry almost all the load. The planks are glued/bonded to the cables, so as you step on the planks, they sag slightly, the **shear at the plank–cable interface** pulls the cables taut, and the cables stretch in tension. You walk safely because the cables do the work. Now imagine the planks are *not* bonded to the cables: the cables would just hang loose and you would fall. **The bond is everything.**
> In composites, the fiber is the steel cable, the matrix is the plank, and the interface is the glue. **No glue → no strengthening.**

### 1.3 Environmental Effects — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Environmental Effects on Composite Design:**
> Environmental effects refer to the **degradation of mechanical, physical, and chemical properties** of a composite when it is exposed in service to **moisture (water/humidity), elevated or cryogenic temperatures, ultra-violet (UV) radiation, aggressive chemicals (acids, alkalis, fuels, solvents), and thermal cycling**. These effects alter the **fiber–matrix interface**, reduce the **glass transition temperature (Tg)** of the polymer matrix, cause **swelling and internal stresses**, and ultimately force the designer to apply **knock-down (degradation) factors** to the laboratory-measured design strengths.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — "The Sponge in the Sun"

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy: The Wet Wooden Plank**
> A freshly cut wooden plank (virgin composite) is strong. Leave it outdoors for a year: it absorbs rainwater (moisture uptake), the sun bakes it (UV + temperature), and freeze–thaw cycles stress it. The plank **swells**, **warps**, **cracks at the grain boundaries** (analogous to fiber–matrix interface), and its load-bearing capacity drops. A wise carpenter does not design the deck using the strength of the *fresh* plank — he applies a **safety/reduction factor** because he *knows* the environment will weaken it. The composite designer does exactly the same thing using **environmental knock-down factors** and the **hygrothermal design curve**.

### 1.5 Standard Metrics Used in This Module

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Material Metrics (Board-Exam Favourites):**
> - **Young's Modulus (E):** measured in **GPa** (Gigapascals).
> - **Ultimate Tensile Strength (σ):** measured in **MPa** (Megapascals).
> - **Fiber Volume Fraction (Vf):** dimensionless, $0 \leq V_f \leq 1$.
> - **Aspect Ratio (l/d):** dimensionless ratio of fiber length to diameter.
> - **Critical Fiber Length (lc):** measured in **mm** or **µm**.
> - **Glass Transition Temperature (Tg):** measured in **°C** or **K**.
> - **Diffusion Coefficient (D):** measured in **mm²/s** or **m²/s**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Are Composites Stronger? — The Logic Chain

The strengthening of a fiber-reinforced composite is **not magic**; it follows a precise four-step load-transfer logic, which every KTU examiner expects the student to be able to write down in order:

- **Step 1 — External Loading:**
  When a tensile load $P$ is applied to the composite, the stress $\sigma_c = P/A_c$ is initially shared by both fiber and matrix according to their **iso-strain** (Voigt) or **iso-stress** (Reuss) condition, depending on geometry.

- **Step 2 — Modulus Mismatch Creates Shear:**
  Fibers are much stiffer than the matrix ($E_f \gg E_m$). Under the same strain, the fiber would "want" to carry far more stress than the matrix. Because they are bonded at the interface, the matrix *drags* along with the fiber, and this mismatch produces **interfacial shear stress $\tau_i$** along the cylindrical surface of the fiber.

- **Step 3 — Shear-Lag Load Build-Up:**
  The fiber does not experience the full applied stress at its ends. Instead, the stress builds up from **zero at the fiber ends** to a **maximum plateau** in the middle, over a characteristic length called the **load-transfer length** or **ineffective length** $\delta$. The relationship is parabolic near the ends and flat in the centre (Kelly–Tyson solution).

- **Step 4 — Fiber Failure or Pull-Out:**
  - If the fiber length $l \geq l_c$ (the **critical fiber length**), the fiber reaches its ultimate strength $\sigma_{fu}$ in the middle and **fractures first**, leaving the matrix to bridge the crack.
  - If $l < l_c$, the fiber **pulls out** of the matrix — a comparatively weaker and more ductile failure mode. Hence designers always prefer $l \gg l_c$.

### 2.2 The Three Fiber-Length Regimes

| Regime | Condition | Failure Mode | Efficiency |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sub-critical (short) | $l < l_c$ | Fiber pull-out | Low |
| Critical | $l = l_c$ | Simultaneous fracture + pull-out | Moderate |
| Super-critical (long/continuous) | $l \gg l_c$ | Fiber fracture at random weak points | High |

### 2.3 Rule of Mixtures — The Backbone Equation

For **continuous, unidirectional, perfectly bonded** fibers aligned with the load:

- **Longitudinal Modulus (iso-strain):**

$$E_{cL} = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$$

- **Longitudinal Strength:**

$$\sigma_{cL} = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma'_m V_m$$

- **Transverse Modulus (iso-stress, Reuss):**

$$\frac{1}{E_{cT}} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m}$$

- **Transverse Strength (often approximated by the matrix-dominated form):**

$$\sigma_{cT} \approx \sigma'_m \left[1 - 2\sqrt{\frac{V_f}{\pi}}\right] + \sigma_f V_f$$

For **short, randomly oriented** fibers, an **orientation efficiency factor $\eta_o \approx 0.2$** and a **length efficiency factor $\eta_l$** are introduced:

$$\sigma_c = \eta_o \eta_l \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these equations — they appear every semester.**

| # | Equation | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $l_c = \dfrac{\sigma_f d}{2 \tau_i}$ | Critical fiber length | mm |
| 2 | $\dfrac{l_c}{d} = \dfrac{\sigma_f}{2 \tau_i}$ | Critical aspect ratio | — |
| 3 | $E_{cL} = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ | Longitudinal modulus (Voigt) | GPa |
| 4 | $E_{cT} = \dfrac{E_f E_m}{E_m V_f + E_f V_m}$ | Transverse modulus (Reuss) | GPa |
| 5 | $\sigma_{cL} = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma'_m V_m$ | Longitudinal strength | MPa |
| 6 | $\sigma_c = \eta_l \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$ | Strength with length efficiency | MPa |
| 7 | $\eta_l = 1 - \dfrac{l_c}{2l}$ | Length efficiency factor (short fiber) | — |
| 8 | $M(t) = M_m\!\left[1 - \dfrac{8}{\pi^2}\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\dfrac{1}{(2n+1)^2}e^{-(2n+1)^2\pi^2 Dt/h^2}\right]$ | Fickian moisture uptake | % mass |
| 9 | $M(t) \approx M_m\!\left[1 - e^{-7.3(Dt/h^2)^{0.75}}\right]$ | Approximate Fickian absorption | % mass |
| 10 | $\sigma_{design} = \dfrac{\sigma_{lab}}{K}$ | Knock-down design equation | MPa |
| 11 | $K = K_1 \cdot K_2 \cdot K_3 \cdots$ | Cumulative knock-down (multiplicative) | — |
| 12 | $D = D_0 e^{-E_a/RT}$ | Arrhenius diffusion vs. temperature | mm²/s |

### 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why This Matters in Industry:**
> - **Aerospace (Boeing 787, Airbus A350):** ~50% of the airframe is CFRP composite. Designers must use the **A-basis / B-basis** allowables and apply moisture + temperature knock-downs, because at 35,000 ft + cruise Mach 0.85, the skin can reach 100 °C and −50 °C on the same flight.
> - **Wind-Turbine Blades (TPI, LM Wind):** Glass-fiber/epoxy blades running 25 years offshore absorb ~1% seawater by mass; their **flapwise fatigue strength** is designed with hygrothermal knock-downs.
> - **Marine Hulls (US Navy DDG-1000):** Vinyl-ester/glass composites operate fully submerged; **blistering** and **osmotic degradation** are designed against using barrier coats and conservative Tg.
> - **Civil Bridges (FRP-rebar):** Carbon/aramid rebars must be designed for **alkaline pore-water** in concrete — a key environmental effect.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Critical Fiber Length from First Principles

We consider a **single short fiber** of diameter $d$ and length $l$ embedded in a matrix, loaded in tension. The fiber is gripped by interfacial shear stress $\tau_i$ acting on its curved lateral surface.

**Step 1 — Force balance on the fiber end:**

A fiber can be pulled out unless the surrounding matrix can develop enough frictional/bond shear to build up a tensile stress equal to the fiber's ultimate tensile stress $\sigma_f$ at the centre.

The **shear force** transferred over half the fiber length (from one end to the midpoint) is:

$$F_{shear} = \tau_i \cdot (\text{cylindrical area}) = \tau_i \cdot \pi d \cdot \frac{l}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Tensile force in the fiber at the midpoint:**

The cross-sectional area of the fiber is:

$$A_f = \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$$

The tensile force in the fiber at its centre is therefore:

$$F_{tension} = \sigma_f \cdot A_f = \sigma_f \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$$

**Step 3 — Equate shear and tension at the critical condition:**

At the **critical fiber length** $l_c$, the fiber just reaches $\sigma_f$ at its centre. Equating the two forces:

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{shear} &= F_{tension} \\[4pt]
\tau_i \cdot \pi d \cdot \frac{l_c}{2} &= \sigma_f \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $l_c$:**

Cancel $\pi d$ from both sides:

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_i \cdot \frac{l_c}{2} &= \sigma_f \cdot \frac{d}{4} \\[4pt]
l_c &= \frac{\sigma_f \cdot d}{2 \tau_i}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Final compact form and aspect ratio:**

$$\boxed{\;l_c = \frac{\sigma_f \, d}{2 \tau_i} \quad\text{and}\quad \frac{l_c}{d} = \frac{\sigma_f}{2 \tau_i}\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** Doubling the interfacial shear strength $\tau_i$ **halves** the critical length. This is why surface treatments (silanes on glass fibers, sizing on carbon fibers) are so commercially important.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Length Efficiency Factor $\eta_l$

For a fiber shorter than the critical length ($l < l_c$), the **average stress in the fiber** is not $\sigma_f$ but a triangular distribution going from zero at each end to $\sigma_f$ at the centre.

**Step 1 — Average stress in a sub-critical fiber:**

The stress profile is triangular; therefore the average is half the peak:

$$\bar{\sigma}_f = \frac{\sigma_f}{2} \quad \text{when } l = l_c$$

**Step 2 — Generalise for any short fiber length $l$:**

When the fiber is *less* than the critical length, the peak stress is *less* than $\sigma_f$ (the matrix would debond first). The peak is:

$$\sigma_{f,\text{peak}} = \sigma_f \cdot \frac{l}{l_c}$$

**Step 3 — Average stress:**

$$\bar{\sigma}_f = \frac{1}{2} \sigma_f \cdot \frac{l}{l_c} = \frac{\sigma_f \, l}{2 \, l_c}$$

**Step 4 — Define the length efficiency factor:**

$$\eta_l = \frac{\bar{\sigma}_f}{\sigma_f} = \frac{l}{2 l_c} \quad \text{for } l < l_c$$

**Step 5 — Combine with the rule of mixtures:**

For $l \geq l_c$, $\eta_l = 1 - \dfrac{l_c}{2l}$, and the composite strength becomes:

$$\sigma_c = \eta_l \, \sigma_f V_f + \sigma'_m V_m$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always state whether your equation is for $l < l_c$ or $l \geq l_c$. A common mistake is to apply $\eta_l = l / 2 l_c$ to continuous fibers and get the answer **half** the correct value.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Fick's Second Law of Diffusion (1-D)

Moisture absorption into a thick polymer composite is governed by **Fick's second law**. For a 1-D slab of half-thickness $h$:

**Step 1 — Governing PDE:**

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$$

where $C(x,t)$ is the local moisture concentration, $D$ is the diffusion coefficient, and $x$ is measured from the centre of the slab.

**Step 2 — Boundary conditions (symmetric slab):**

- At $x = 0$ (centre), $\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial x} = 0$ (no flux by symmetry).
- At $x = h$ (surface), $C = C_s$ (surface concentration, assumed constant).

**Step 3 — Initial condition:**

- At $t = 0$, $C = C_i$ throughout.

**Step 4 — Separation of variables / series solution:**

The classical solution (Crank, *The Mathematics of Diffusion*, Oxford) is:

$$\frac{C(t) - C_i}{C_s - C_i} = 1 - \frac{4}{\pi}\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\frac{(-1)^n}{2n+1}\exp\!\left[-\frac{(2n+1)^2 \pi^2 D t}{4 h^2}\right]\cos\!\left[\frac{(2n+1)\pi x}{2 h}\right]$$

**Step 5 — Integrate over the thickness to get bulk moisture content $M(t)$:**

$$M(t) = M_m\!\left[1 - \frac{8}{\pi^2}\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\frac{1}{(2n+1)^2}\exp\!\left[-(2n+1)^2\pi^2 Dt/h^2\right]\right]$$

**Step 6 — Short-time approximation ($M(t)/M_m < 0.5$):**

$$M(t) \approx \frac{4}{h}\sqrt{\frac{Dt}{\pi}}$$

(linear in $\sqrt{t}$ — the Fickian "signature").

**Step 7 — Long-time approximation:**

$$M(t) \approx M_m\!\left[1 - \frac{8}{\pi^2}e^{-\pi^2 Dt/h^2}\right]$$

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A glass/epoxy composite has fibers of diameter $d = 10\ \mu m$, ultimate tensile strength $\sigma_f = 2.5\ \text{GPa}$, and an interfacial shear strength $\tau_i = 35\ \text{MPa}$. Determine (a) the critical fiber length, (b) the critical aspect ratio, and (c) the length efficiency factor for a fiber of length $l = 0.4\ \text{mm}$.

**Solution:**

**(a) Critical fiber length:**

$$\begin{aligned}
l_c &= \frac{\sigma_f d}{2 \tau_i} = \frac{(2.5 \times 10^3\ \text{MPa})(10 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{mm})}{2 \times 35\ \text{MPa}} \\[4pt]
    &= \frac{25}{70}\ \text{mm} = 0.357\ \text{mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**(b) Critical aspect ratio:**

$$\frac{l_c}{d} = \frac{0.357\ \text{mm}}{10 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{mm}} = 35.7$$

**(c) Length efficiency factor (since $l = 0.4\ \text{mm} > l_c = 0.357\ \text{mm}$, the fiber is super-critical):**

$$\eta_l = 1 - \frac{l_c}{2l} = 1 - \frac{0.357}{2 \times 0.4} = 1 - 0.446 = 0.554$$

**Interpretation:** Even though the fiber is slightly longer than the critical length, it is still only **55% efficient** in carrying load. A 2 mm fiber would give $\eta_l = 1 - 0.357/4 = 0.911$ (91% efficient) — this is why chopped-fiber composites are weaker than continuous-fiber ones.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Load Transfer Mechanism in a Short Fiber

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["External Load P Applied to Composite"] --> B["Matrix Carries Initial Stress sigma_m"]
    B --> C{"Modulus Mismatch E_f >> E_m"}
    C -- "Yes" --> D["Interfacial Shear Stress tau_i Develops at Fiber-Matrix Boundary"]
    D --> E["Shear-Lag Load Build-Up Along Fiber"]
    E --> F{"Is Fiber Length l >= Critical Length l_c?"}
    F -- "No: Sub-critical" --> G["Fiber Pull-Out Failure - Low Strength"]
    F -- "Yes: Super-critical" --> H["Fiber Fracture in Middle - High Strength"]
    H --> I["Matrix Bridges Crack - Composite Fails Ductilely"]
    G --> J["Composite Strength = eta_l * sigma_f * V_f + sigma_m * V_m"]
    I --> J
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Stress Distribution Along a Short Fiber (Kelly–Tyson Model)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FiberAxialStress["Kelly-Tyson Stress Profile Along Fiber"]
        L["Fiber End x = 0: sigma_f = 0"] --> M["Quadratic Rise: sigma_f = 2 tau_i x / d"]
        M --> N["Midpoint x = l/2: sigma_f PEAK = sigma_fu"]
        N --> O["Symmetric Descent: sigma_f = 2 tau_i l-x / d"]
        O --> P["Fiber End x = l: sigma_f = 0"]
    end
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Environmental Degradation Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Env["Service Environment Exposure"] --> Mo["Moisture / Humidity Uptake"]
    Env --> Th["Thermal Cycling"]
    Env --> UV["UV Radiation"]
    Env --> Ch["Chemical Attack Fuels Acids Solvents"]
    Mo --> IF["Interface De-bonding and Matrix Plasticization"]
    Th --> Tg["Glass Transition Temperature Tg Drops"]
    UV --> Ox["Surface Oxidation and Micro-cracking"]
    Ch --> ESCR["Environmental Stress Cracking"]
    IF --> KD["Knock-down Factor K applied"]
    Tg --> KD
    Ox --> KD
    KD --> SD["sigma_design = sigma_lab / K"]
    SD --> Safe["Safe Composite Design with Adequate Margin"]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Hygrothermal Design Allowable Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Lab Test at RT Dry: sigma_lab"] --> B["Apply Moisture Knock-down K_m = 0.85"]
    B --> C["Apply Temperature Knock-down K_T = 0.90"]
    C --> D["Apply UV Knock-down K_uv = 0.95"]
    D --> E["Apply Scatter Knock-down K_s = 0.80"]
    E --> F["Apply Safety Factor SF = 1.5"]
    F --> G["Allowable Design Stress: sigma_allow = sigma_lab / K_m * K_T * K_uv * K_s * SF"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define critical fiber length. Why is it important in the design of fiber-reinforced composites?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Fiber Length $l_c$:** The minimum length of a fiber that allows the tensile stress in the fiber to build up from zero at its ends to the fiber's ultimate tensile strength $\sigma_{fu}$ at its centre, via interfacial shear transfer. It is given by $l_c = \dfrac{\sigma_f d}{2 \tau_i}$.
> **Importance:** (i) It separates the **pull-out regime** (short fibers, weak composite) from the **fracture regime** (long fibers, strong composite). (ii) Designers select $l \gg l_c$ to ensure the fiber is used near its full strength. (iii) It is the basis of the **length efficiency factor** used in the rule of mixtures. (iv) It guides fiber surface treatment — better bonding (higher $\tau_i$) lowers $l_c$, allowing shorter, cheaper fibers.

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]`
**List any four environmental factors that affect the long-term performance of polymer-matrix composites.**

**Model Answer:**

The four major environmental factors are:

1. **Moisture / Humidity Absorption** — causes matrix plasticisation, lowering Tg and strength.
2. **Elevated / Sub-zero Temperature** — reduces matrix-dominated properties near Tg; induces thermal mismatch stresses at the interface.
3. **Ultra-violet (UV) Radiation** — degrades the polymer surface (chain scission, oxidation), creating micro-cracks.
4. **Chemical Exposure (fuels, acids, alkalis, solvents)** — induces environmental stress cracking, swelling, and fibre-matrix de-bonding.

*(A fifth acceptable answer: thermal cycling / freeze–thaw.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Question (14 Marks) — Module 1 Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **critical fiber length** in a fiber-reinforced composite, starting from a force-balance on a single short fiber. Explain the physical significance of each term. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A unidirectional glass-fiber/epoxy composite contains 40% by volume of continuous fibers. Given $E_f = 72\ \text{GPa}$, $E_m = 3.5\ \text{GPa}$, $\sigma_{fu} = 2.0\ \text{GPa}$ and $\sigma_{mu} = 80\ \text{MPa}$, compute (i) the **longitudinal Young's modulus**, (ii) the **longitudinal tensile strength** using the rule of mixtures, and (iii) the **transverse modulus** using the inverse rule of mixtures. Comment on the anisotropy. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation of critical fiber length:**

- Consider a short fiber of diameter $d$ and length $l$ embedded in a matrix, loaded in tension along the fiber axis.
- The fiber is bonded to the matrix; load is transferred by **interfacial shear stress $\tau_i$** acting on the cylindrical surface.
- For a fiber of length $l$, the **shear force** transferred over its lateral surface is $F_{shear} = \tau_i \cdot \pi d l$ (full length, but in equilibrium we use half because stress builds from both ends to the centre):

$$F_{shear} = \tau_i \cdot \pi d \cdot \frac{l}{2}$$

- The **tensile force** that this shear must develop in the fiber at its midpoint is:

$$F_{tension} = \sigma_f \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$$

- Setting $F_{shear} = F_{tension}$ at the critical condition and solving:

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_i \cdot \pi d \cdot \frac{l_c}{2} &= \sigma_f \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \\[4pt]
l_c &= \frac{\sigma_f d}{2 \tau_i}
\end{aligned}$$

- **Physical significance of each term:** $\sigma_f$ = fiber ultimate stress; $d$ = fiber diameter (smaller $d$ gives smaller $l_c$); $\tau_i$ = interfacial shear strength (stronger interface gives smaller $l_c$, allowing shorter fibers to be effective).

**(b) Numerical calculation:**

Given: $V_f = 0.40$, $V_m = 0.60$, $E_f = 72\ \text{GPa}$, $E_m = 3.5\ \text{GPa}$, $\sigma_{fu} = 2.0\ \text{GPa} = 2000\ \text{MPa}$, $\sigma_{mu} = 80\ \text{MPa}$.

**(i) Longitudinal Young's modulus (Rule of Mixtures, Voigt model):**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{cL} &= E_f V_f + E_m V_m \\[4pt]
       &= (72)(0.40) + (3.5)(0.60) \\[4pt]
       &= 28.8 + 2.1 = 30.9\ \text{GPa}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Longitudinal tensile strength:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{cL} &= \sigma_{fu} V_f + \sigma_{mu} V_m \\[4pt]
            &= (2000)(0.40) + (80)(0.60) \\[4pt]
            &= 800 + 48 = 848\ \text{MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) Transverse modulus (Inverse Rule of Mixtures, Reuss model):**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{cT} &= \frac{E_f E_m}{E_m V_f + E_f V_m} \\[4pt]
       &= \frac{(72)(3.5)}{(3.5)(0.40) + (72)(0.60)} \\[4pt]
       &= \frac{252}{1.4 + 43.2} = \frac{252}{44.6} = 5.65\ \text{GPa}
\end{aligned}$$

**Comment on anisotropy:** $E_{cL} = 30.9\ \text{GPa}$ along the fibers, $E_{cT} = 5.65\ \text{GPa}$ across the fibers. The ratio $E_{cL}/E_{cT} \approx 5.5$, showing that unidirectional composites are **strongly anisotropic**. They are very stiff and strong along the fiber direction but behave more like the matrix in the transverse direction.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2022]`

**(a)** Explain the **Kelly–Tyson shear-lag model** of load transfer in a short-fiber composite. Sketch the axial stress distribution along a fiber of length greater than the critical length. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss, with examples, the **effect of moisture absorption and elevated temperature on the design of composite structures**. How are these effects accounted for in design allowables? **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**(a) Kelly–Tyson shear-lag model:**

- Kelly and Tyson (1965) proposed that in a fiber-reinforced composite under tension, the load is transferred from the matrix to the fiber by **interfacial shear stresses** that develop because of the **modulus mismatch** between the two phases.
- The **axial tensile stress in the fiber is zero at the fiber ends** (no load transfer at a free surface) and **builds up parabolically** toward the centre, reaching a maximum plateau once the fiber length exceeds the load-transfer length $\delta$.
- The shear stress at the interface is constant $\tau_i$ (idealised), so the fiber stress profile is:

$$\sigma_f(x) = \frac{2 \tau_i x}{d}\quad\text{for } 0 \leq x \leq \delta$$

$$\sigma_f(x) = \sigma_{f,\text{peak}}\quad\text{for } \delta \leq x \leq l - \delta$$

$$\sigma_f(x) = \frac{2 \tau_i (l - x)}{d}\quad\text{for } l - \delta \leq x \leq l$$

- When $l \geq l_c = \sigma_{fu} d / (2 \tau_i)$, the central plateau reaches $\sigma_{fu}$ and the fiber fractures there.
- When $l < l_c$, the central plateau never reaches $\sigma_{fu}$, and the fiber pulls out of the matrix.

*(A neat sketch with a triangular/parabolic profile rising from both ends to a central plateau earns full credit.)*

**(b) Moisture and temperature effects on design:**

- **Moisture absorption:** Polymer matrices absorb water (typically 0.1 – 3% by mass for epoxy/glass), which **plasticises the matrix** (lowers Tg, reduces modulus and strength), causes **swelling and internal stresses**, and **degrades the fiber–matrix interface** by hydrolysing the silane or epoxy bonds. *Example:* Marine GRP hulls blister and lose stiffness after 5–10 years of immersion.
- **Elevated temperature:** As temperature approaches the matrix's **glass transition temperature Tg**, the matrix softens dramatically, the composite loses its stiffness and strength, and the interface may fail by **differential thermal expansion** (fibers typically have near-zero CTE, matrix CTE is 50–100 × 10⁻⁶/°C). *Example:* Aircraft CFRP skins can reach 100 °C on a hot tarmac; designers must use the **hot-wet** design curve.
- **Design treatment:** A **knock-down (degradation) factor** $K$ is applied multiplicatively to the laboratory-measured "dry, room-temperature" strength:

$$\sigma_{design} = \frac{\sigma_{lab,\text{dry}}}{K_1 \cdot K_2 \cdot K_3 \cdots \cdot SF}$$

where typical factors are $K_{\text{moisture}} \approx 0.80$–$0.90$, $K_{\text{temperature}} \approx 0.85$–$0.95$, $K_{\text{UV}} \approx 0.95$, $K_{\text{scatter}} \approx 0.80$ (B-basis), and $SF$ (safety factor) $\approx 1.5$–$2.0$. Conservative design uses the **"hot–wet"** allowable (typically the worst combination of temperature and moisture that the structure will see in 25+ years of service).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Losing Mistakes (Read Before Writing the Exam):**
> 1. **Mixing up the shear-area:** Students often write $F_{shear} = \tau_i \cdot \pi d l$ (using the *full* length instead of $l/2$). This gives $l_c = \sigma_f d / \tau_i$ — **double the correct answer** and immediate loss of 2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the $\eta_l$ condition:** Applying $\eta_l = l / 2 l_c$ to a continuous fiber ($l \to \infty$) gives $\eta_l = \infty$, which is absurd. Use $\eta_l = 1 - l_c / (2l)$ for $l \geq l_c$.
> 3. **Confusing $V_f$ with $V_m$ in transverse modulus:** The Reuss formula uses the *complement*, so a swapped term gives the wrong answer.
> 4. **Stating Fick's law without the minus sign:** Always write $J = -D \dfrac{\partial C}{\partial x}$; examiners specifically look for the sign.
> 5. **Not mentioning the "hot-wet" condition:** A 14-mark answer on environmental effects that omits the **combined hot-wet allowable** is considered incomplete.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Read 30 Minutes Before the Exam):**
> - **Strengthening mechanism = load transfer via interfacial shear** from matrix to fiber (Kelly–Tyson shear-lag).
> - **Critical fiber length:** $l_c = \sigma_f d / (2 \tau_i)$ — derived by equating shear and tensile forces on a fiber.
> - **Three regimes:** $l < l_c$ (pull-out, weak), $l = l_c$ (transition), $l \gg l_c$ (fracture, strong).
> - **Length efficiency factor:** $\eta_l = l / (2 l_c)$ for $l < l_c$ and $\eta_l = 1 - l_c / (2l)$ for $l \geq l_c$.
> - **Rule of mixtures (longitudinal):** $E_{cL} = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ and $\sigma_{cL} = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma'_m V_m$.
> - **Inverse rule of mixtures (transverse):** $1/E_{cT} = V_f/E_f + V_m/E_m$.
> - **Environmental effects:** moisture (lowers Tg, plasticises matrix), temperature (Tg drop, CTE mismatch), UV (surface chain scission), chemicals (ESCR, blistering).
> - **Fickian diffusion:** $M(t) = M_m[1 - (8/\pi^2)\sum \exp(-(2n+1)^2 \pi^2 Dt/h^2)]$; short-time $M \propto \sqrt{t}$.
> - **Design treatment:** Apply multiplicative **knock-down factors** $K_m, K_T, K_{uv}, K_{scatter}$ and a **safety factor $SF$** to the dry-RT lab strength; report the **hot–wet allowable** for aerospace/marine structures.
> - **Anisotropy:** Unidirectional composites are very strong along the fiber, weak transverse to it — design must respect the principal material directions.
> - **Why composites are stronger than either phase alone:** synergistic load sharing, crack-bridging, fibre fracture energy, and stress redistribution.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Traditional and Novel Approaches; Process Fundamentals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT744 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Traditional and Novel Approaches; Process Fundamentals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Traditional Approaches to Composite Processing — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> **Traditional composite processing approaches** are the *time-tested, well-established manufacturing routes* that have been used industrially since the 1960s to consolidate reinforcements (fibers, fabrics, particulates) with a polymeric, metallic, or ceramic matrix. They rely on **bulk forming principles** such as *contact molding, filament-based wet processes, and pressurized autoclave consolidation*, and have formed the backbone of the aerospace, marine, automotive, and wind-energy composite industries for over six decades.

The traditional family comprises:

- **Open-mold (contact) processes** — Hand lay-up, Spray lay-up
- **Closed-mold (pressure-driven) processes** — Compression molding, Injection molding, RTM
- **Continuous processes** — Filament winding, Pultrusion
- **Vacuum/Pressure-bag processes** — Vacuum bag molding (VBM), autoclave curing
- **Prepreg-based processes** — Lay-up and autoclave consolidation

## 1.2 Novel Approaches to Composite Processing — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> **Novel (or advanced) composite processing approaches** are *emergent, digitally-controlled, out-of-autoclave, additive, or hybrid manufacturing routes* that address the limitations of traditional methods. They emphasize **automation, near-net-shape forming, microstructural control, sustainability, and integration of nano-/multi-scale reinforcements** to achieve superior performance, repeatability, and lower life-cycle cost.

The novel family comprises:

- **Additive Manufacturing (AM) / 3D Printing** of polymer, metal, and ceramic matrix composites
- **Automated Fiber Placement (AFP)** and **Automated Tape Laying (ATL)**
- **Out-of-Autoclave (OoA)** processes — VARTM, VBO (Vacuum Bag Only), Resin Film Infusion
- **Liquid Composite Molding (LCM)** variants
- **In-situ polymerization / Reactive processing**
- **Friction Stir Processing (FSP)** for metal matrix composites
- **Nanocomposite processing routes** — in-situ polymerization, sol-gel, melt intercalation

## 1.3 Process Fundamentals — The Universal Backbone

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Concept — Process Fundamentals:**
> Regardless of whether a route is "traditional" or "novel", every composite manufacturing process is governed by the **same five universal fundamentals**: (i) reinforcement placement, (ii) matrix introduction/wetting, (iii) consolidation under pressure, (iv) cure/solidification kinetics, and (v) defect (void, porosity, residual stress) management.

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — Intuition for a First-Time Learner

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — Making a Sandwich Cake (Layer Cake):**
> Imagine you are baking a layered sandwich cake where alternating layers of **bread (fiber reinforcement)** are held together by **mayonnaise/cheese (matrix resin)**.
> - **Traditional methods** (hand lay-up, autoclave) are like manually spreading mayo and stacking the layers — they work, but are slow, labor-intensive, and operator-dependent.
> - **Novel methods** (AFP, 3D printing) are like using a robotic dispenser that precisely places each layer and squeezes the cheese to the exact thickness — fast, repeatable, and far less wasteful.
> - The **process fundamentals** are the underlying physics — how the mayo spreads (resin flow), how the bread absorbs moisture (fiber wetting), how the cake firms up under refrigeration (cure kinetics), and how to avoid air bubbles (void control).
>
> *Just as a chef must master heat, moisture, and timing, a composite engineer must master pressure, temperature, viscosity, and cure.*

## 1.5 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024 Examinations

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Note:**
> Module 2 of PEPOT744 forms the **manufacturing heart** of the syllabus. Questions on this module are frequently asked in the **ESE (End Semester Examination)** carrying **14 marks** and frequently appear in **CAT-2 (Continuous Assessment Test)** as 7-mark analytical problems. A clear mental model of *traditional vs novel* plus the **underlying physics of each process** is mandatory for full marks.

---

## 1.6 Quick-Reference Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Traditional Approaches | Novel Approaches |
|---|---|---|
| Capital Cost | Low to Medium | Medium to High |
| Labor Intensity | High | Low (highly automated) |
| Production Rate | Low to Medium | Medium to High |
| Repeatability | Operator-dependent | Digitally controlled |
| Defect Tolerance | Higher (e.g., hand lay-up voids ~3–5%) | Very low (e.g., AFP voids $<$1%) |
| Typical Industry | Marine, civil infra | Aerospace, biomedical, defense |
| Process Control | Manual / analog | Sensor-driven / closed-loop |
| Sustainability | High waste, high VOC | Lower waste, recyclable potential |
| Part Complexity | Simple-to-moderate geometry | Complex, tailored mesostructure |

---

## 1.7 Key Standard Metrics Used in This Module (Bolded for Recall)

- **Fiber Volume Fraction ($V_f$):** typically **0.4 to 0.65** for structural composites
- **Void Content ($V_v$):** acceptable limit **$<$ 1%** (aerospace), **$<$ 3%** (industrial)
- **Cure Temperature ($T_{cure}$):** typically **120 °C to 180 °C** for epoxy systems
- **Consolidation Pressure ($P_c$):** typically **0.1 MPa to 0.7 MPa** (autoclave uses 0.1–1.0 MPa)
- **Resin Viscosity ($\eta$):** targeted to **0.05 to 0.5 Pa·s** at injection for LCM

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Theoretical Decomposition — The Five Universal Process Fundamentals

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every composite manufacturing route, traditional or novel, can be reduced to **five sequential physical phenomena**. Mastering these five steps means mastering the entire module.

### Fundamental 1 — Reinforcement Placement
- **What:** Fibers, fabrics, or particles are deposited in a controlled architecture.
- **Why it matters:** Determines the **anisotropy**, **stiffness tensor**, and **load path**.
- **Traditional:** Manual draping of woven roving (hand lay-up).
- **Novel:** Robotic placement of prepreg tow (AFP) with **laser-aligned steering heads**.

### Fundamental 2 — Matrix Introduction & Fiber Wetting
- **What:** Liquid resin (thermoset) or molten polymer (thermoplastic) is brought into contact with the reinforcement.
- **Why it matters:** Incomplete wetting creates **dry spots**, **voids**, and **interfacial debonds**.
- **Governed by:** **Young–Laplace capillary pressure**, contact angle, surface energy.
- **Traditional:** Brushed/rolled resin (hand lay-up).
- **Novel:** Injected under pressure in RTM/VARTM.

### Fundamental 3 — Consolidation Under Pressure
- **What:** External pressure squeezes out entrapped air and compacts the laminate to target $V_f$.
- **Why it matters:** Directly governs **void content** and **fiber wet-out**.
- **Traditional:** Roller pressure (manual), autoclave isostatic pressure.
- **Novel:** Caul plates, smart bladder systems, in-situ compaction rollers.

### Fundamental 4 — Cure / Solidification Kinetics
- **What:** Thermoset resin undergoes crosslinking; thermoplastic crystallizes or vitrifies.
- **Why it matters:** Controls **residual stress**, **$T_g$ (glass transition temperature)**, and final mechanical performance.
- **Traditional:** Ramp-and-hold cure cycle in oven/autoclave.
- **Novel:** Dielectric / ultrasonic cure monitoring with closed-loop control.

### Fundamental 5 — Defect Management (Voids, Porosity, Residual Stress)
- **What:** Post-process and in-process control of microstructural imperfections.
- **Why it matters:** A **2% increase in void content** can reduce **interlaminar shear strength (ILSS) by up to 10%**.
- **Traditional:** Visual inspection, ultrasonic NDT.
- **Novel:** In-situ optical coherence tomography, acoustic emission.

---

## 2.2 Traditional Approaches — Deeper Mechanics

### 2.2.1 Hand Lay-Up & Spray Lay-Up
- The **oldest** open-mold process. Resin is brushed/rolled/sprayed onto a mold, and fibers are manually placed.
- **Resin:** Unsaturated polyester, vinyl ester, epoxy.
- **Reinforcement:** Chopped strand mat (CSM), woven roving.
- **Cure:** Room temperature (with MEKP catalyst) or post-cured.
- **Merits:** Low tooling cost; **Demerits:** High VOC emission, low $V_f$, high void content.

### 2.2.2 Filament Winding
- Continuous rovings are pulled through a **resin bath** and wound onto a rotating mandrel at a controlled **winding angle $\alpha$**.
- **Winding Law:** $\tan(\alpha) = \dfrac{\pi D}{L}$ (geodesic path approximation).
- **Best for:** Pressure vessels, pipes, rocket motor casings.

### 2.2.3 Pultrusion
- Continuous process: fibers are pulled through a resin bath → forming die → heated die → cured profile.
- **Output:** Constant cross-section profiles (I-beams, rods, channels).
- **Continuous production rate** is the key advantage.

### 2.2.4 Autoclave Processing
- The **gold standard** for aerospace prepreg laminates.
- Laminate is vacuum-bagged, then subjected to **combined heat $\sim$ 180 °C and pressure $\sim$ 0.7 MPa**.
- Produces the **lowest void content ($<$ 0.5%)** but is **capital and energy intensive**.

### 2.2.5 Resin Transfer Molding (RTM)
- Closed-mold process: dry reinforcement is placed in a mold, mold is closed, and resin is injected.
- Resin flow is governed by **Darcy's Law** (see Formula Sheet).

---

## 2.3 Novel Approaches — Deeper Mechanics

### 2.3.1 Automated Fiber Placement (AFP) / Automated Tape Laying (ATL)
- A **robotic head** places 3–32 prepreg tows simultaneously at rates up to **50 kg/h**.
- **Steering angle limit:** typically **± 20° to ± 45°** due to tow buckling.
- **Real-time monitoring:** laser profilometry, IR thermography.

### 2.3.2 Out-of-Autoclave (OoA) Processing — VARTM
- **Vacuum-Assisted RTM** uses only atmospheric pressure ($\sim$ **0.1 MPa**) on one side and vacuum on the other.
- Used to manufacture **large wind-turbine blades (up to 80 m)** cost-effectively.

### 2.3.3 Additive Manufacturing (AM) of Composites
- Techniques include **Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM)** with chopped/continuous fiber, **Stereolithography (SLA)** with nano-reinforced resins, and **Selective Laser Sintering (SLS)**.
- Enables **topology-optimized, lattice, and functionally graded** composites.

### 2.3.4 Liquid Composite Molding (LCM) Variants
- Includes **RTM, VARTM, SCRIMP (Seemann Composites Resin Infusion Molding Process), RFI (Resin Film Infusion)**.
- Common feature: **dry preform is infiltrated by low-viscosity resin**.

### 2.3.5 In-situ Polymerization & Reactive Processing
- Monomers are impregnated into fibers and polymerize *in situ*.
- Critical for **thermoplastic matrix composites (e.g., in-situ PA6 from $\epsilon$-caprolactam)**.

---

## 2.4 The Physics of Cure Kinetics — Arrhenius Framework

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Idea:** The cure rate of a thermoset increases **exponentially** with temperature. This is modeled using the **Arrhenius equation**, the single most important equation in composite cure modeling.

**Single-Arrhenius Model:**

$$\frac{d\alpha}{dt} = A \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{R T}\right) f(\alpha)$$

where:
- $\alpha$ = degree of cure (0 to 1)
- $A$ = pre-exponential factor (s$^{-1}$)
- $E_a$ = activation energy (J/mol)
- $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)
- $f(\alpha)$ = a kinetic model function, e.g., $f(\alpha) = (1-\alpha)^n$ for $n$-th order cure.

For epoxy/amine systems, a typical **autocatalytic (Kamal) model** is more accurate:

$$\frac{d\alpha}{dt} = \left(k_1 + k_2 \alpha^m\right)(1-\alpha)^n$$

where $k_1 = A_1 \exp(-E_1/RT)$ and $k_2 = A_2 \exp(-E_2/RT)$.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — Process Fundamentals

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning / Use | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $v = -\dfrac{K}{\mu} \, \nabla P$ | **Darcy's Law** for resin flow in a porous preform | m/s |
| 2 | $K = \dfrac{d_f^2 \, \varepsilon^3}{150 \, (1-\varepsilon)^2}$ | **Kozeny–Carman** permeability model (where $d_f$ = fiber diameter) | m$^2$ |
| 3 | $t_{fill} = \dfrac{\mu \, L^2}{2 \, K \, \Delta P}$ | RTM/VARTM fill time for 1D flow | s |
| 4 | $V_f = \dfrac{N \cdot \rho_{\text{areal}}}{\rho_f \cdot t_{\text{ply}}}$ | Fiber volume fraction from $N$ plies of areal density $\rho_{\text{areal}}$ and ply thickness $t_{\text{ply}}$ | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\Delta P_{\text{cap}} = \dfrac{2 \, \gamma_{LV} \, \cos\theta}{r}$ | **Young–Laplace** capillary pressure for fiber wetting | Pa |
| 6 | $\dfrac{d\alpha}{dt} = A \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)(1-\alpha)^n$ | **Arrhenius cure kinetics** (n-th order) | s$^{-1}$ |
| 7 | $\eta(T) = \eta_\infty \exp\!\left(\dfrac{E_\eta}{RT}\right)$ | Resin viscosity temperature dependence (Williams–Landel–Ferry / Arrhenius) | Pa·s |
| 8 | $V_v \approx \dfrac{P_{\text{void}}}{P_{\text{applied}}} \cdot f(T, \dot{\epsilon})$ | Empirical void content (depends on pressure, temperature, strain rate) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $T_{gd} = T_{gw} - 0.5 \cdot \text{wet} \cdot \Delta T_h$ | Glass transition depression by absorbed moisture (wet $T_g$) | K or °C |
| 10 | $\sigma_r = \dfrac{E_f V_f \Delta\alpha \, \Delta T}{1 - \nu_f V_f}$ | Residual thermal stress in fiber direction | Pa |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always write **units** in your final numerical answer. In KTU scripts, a correct number **without units** often loses **0.5 to 1 mark** in 7-mark derivations.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** AFP + autoclave for Boeing 787 fuselage (CFRP); voids must be **$<$ 0.5%** by volume.
- **Wind Energy:** VARTM for blades where autoclave size is impractical.
- **Automotive:** High-pressure RTM (HP-RTM) for BMW 7-series carbon-fiber body parts; **cycle time $\sim$ 5 min**.
- **Biomedical:** 3D-printed PEEK/HA composite patient-specific implants.
- **Marine:** Hand lay-up still dominates boat hulls due to size and cost.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exhaustive Derivation Mandate:** Every algebraic step is shown in full. No shortcuts, no skipped lines.

---

## 3.1 Derivation A — Darcy's Law Application to 1D RTM Fill Time

**Statement:** Derive the fill-time equation for a 1D RTM process in which resin flows through a rectangular preform of length $L$, permeability $K$, viscosity $\mu$, under a pressure differential $\Delta P$.

### Step 1 — Start with Darcy's Law

The macroscopic flow of a Newtonian fluid through a porous medium is given by:

$$v = -\frac{K}{\mu} \frac{dP}{dx}$$

For 1D flow along the $x$-axis with the inlet at $x=0$ and the flow front at $x = x_f(t)$:

$$v = \frac{dx_f}{dt} = -\frac{K}{\mu} \frac{dP}{dx}$$

### Step 2 — Apply Pressure Boundary Conditions

Assume the inlet pressure is $P_0$ and the flow-front pressure is $P_f$ (often $P_f = 0$ for vacuum-assisted). For **fully developed flow in a 1D porous medium**, the pressure gradient is linear:

$$\frac{dP}{dx} = -\frac{P_0 - P_f}{x_f} = -\frac{\Delta P}{x_f}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute into Darcy's Law

$$\frac{dx_f}{dt} = -\frac{K}{\mu} \cdot \left(-\frac{\Delta P}{x_f}\right) = \frac{K \, \Delta P}{\mu \, x_f}$$

### Step 4 — Separate Variables

$$x_f \, dx_f = \frac{K \, \Delta P}{\mu} \, dt$$

### Step 5 — Integrate Both Sides

Integrate from $x_f = 0$ at $t = 0$ to $x_f = L$ at $t = t_{fill}$:

$$\int_0^L x_f \, dx_f = \int_0^{t_{fill}} \frac{K \, \Delta P}{\mu} \, dt$$

$$\frac{x_f^2}{2} \bigg|_0^L = \frac{K \, \Delta P}{\mu} \, t \, \bigg|_0^{t_{fill}}$$

$$\frac{L^2}{2} = \frac{K \, \Delta P}{\mu} \, t_{fill}$$

### Step 6 — Solve for Fill Time

$$\boxed{\,t_{fill} = \frac{\mu \, L^2}{2 \, K \, \Delta P}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** The factor of 1/2 arises from the *quadratic growth* of the flow front position with time. This is a **direct result of mass conservation**: as the flow front advances, the same volume of resin must traverse an ever-larger preform region, slowing the front.

### Step 7 — Numerical Example

Given: $L = 0.5$ m, $\mu = 0.2$ Pa·s, $K = 1 \times 10^{-10}$ m$^2$, $\Delta P = 0.1$ MPa $= 1 \times 10^5$ Pa.

$$t_{fill} = \frac{0.2 \times (0.5)^2}{2 \times 10^{-10} \times 10^5}$$

$$t_{fill} = \frac{0.2 \times 0.25}{2 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{0.05}{2 \times 10^{-5}} = 2500 \text{ s} \approx 41.7 \text{ min}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation B — Kozeny–Carman Permeability Model

**Statement:** Show that the permeability of an unidirectional fibrous preform is given by the Kozeny–Carman equation.

### Step 1 — Define Porosity

The porosity $\varepsilon$ is the void volume fraction in the preform:

$$\varepsilon = 1 - V_f$$

### Step 2 — Start with Hydraulic Diameter Concept

For flow parallel to a bundle of fibers, the **hydraulic diameter** $D_h$ is defined as:

$$D_h = \frac{4 \times \text{flow cross-section}}{\text{wetted perimeter per unit length}}$$

For a fiber of diameter $d_f$ in a unit cell:

$$D_h = \frac{d_f \, \varepsilon}{1 - \varepsilon}$$

### Step 3 — Combine with Poiseuille Flow

For flow in capillary channels of hydraulic diameter $D_h$, the volumetric flow rate scales as:

$$Q \propto \frac{D_h^2 \, \Delta P}{\mu \, L}$$

### Step 4 — Assemble Permeability

The permeability $K$ is defined such that $v = (K/\mu)(dP/dx)$. Substituting $D_h$ and after geometric simplification:

$$\boxed{\,K = \frac{d_f^2 \, \varepsilon^3}{150 \, (1-\varepsilon)^2}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** The factor 150 emerges from the *tortuosity correction* (paths are not straight) and the *specific surface area* of cylindrical fibers. Different authors use 180 instead of 150 depending on the assumed geometry.

---

## 3.3 Derivation C — Arrhenius Cure Time at a Given Temperature

**Statement:** Derive the time required to reach a target degree of cure $\alpha_t$ at a constant temperature $T$.

### Step 1 — Write the n-th Order Kinetic Model

$$\frac{d\alpha}{dt} = A \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right)(1-\alpha)^n$$

### Step 2 — Separate Variables

$$\frac{d\alpha}{(1-\alpha)^n} = A \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right) dt$$

### Step 3 — Integrate

For $n \neq 1$:

$$\int_0^{\alpha_t} \frac{d\alpha}{(1-\alpha)^n} = A \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right) t_{cure}$$

$$\frac{(1-\alpha_t)^{1-n} - 1}{n-1} = A \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right) t_{cure}$$

### Step 4 — Solve for Cure Time

$$\boxed{\,t_{cure} = \frac{(1-\alpha_t)^{1-n} - 1}{(n-1) \, A \, \exp(-E_a/RT)}\,}$$

For $n = 1$ (first-order):

$$\boxed{\,t_{cure} = -\frac{\ln(1-\alpha_t)}{A \, \exp(-E_a/RT)}\,}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation D — Fiber Volume Fraction from Lay-Up Parameters

**Statement:** Derive $V_f$ from $N$ plies of prepreg.

### Step 1 — Define Areal Density

The areal density $\rho_{\text{areal}}$ (kg/m$^2$) of a single ply times the number of plies $N$ gives the total fiber mass per unit area. The total laminate thickness is:

$$t_{\text{lam}} = N \cdot t_{\text{ply}}$$

### Step 2 — Mass and Volume Relationship

Mass of fiber per unit area = $N \cdot \rho_{\text{areal}}$.

Volume of fiber per unit area = $\dfrac{N \cdot \rho_{\text{areal}}}{\rho_f}$.

### Step 3 — Form Fiber Volume Fraction

$$V_f = \frac{\text{Fiber volume per unit area}}{\text{Total laminate thickness}} = \frac{N \, \rho_{\text{areal}}}{\rho_f \, t_{\text{ply}}}$$

$$\boxed{\,V_f = \frac{N \cdot \rho_{\text{areal}}}{\rho_f \cdot t_{\text{ply}}}\,}$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Equation

| Engineering Question | Governing Equation | Key Variables |
|---|---|---|
| How long will my mold fill? | Darcy 1D fill-time | $\mu, L, K, \Delta P$ |
| What is the permeability of my preform? | Kozeny–Carman | $d_f, \varepsilon$ |
| What cure cycle do I need? | Arrhenius cure | $T, E_a, A, \alpha_t$ |
| What is the laminate stiffness? | Rule of mixtures | $V_f, E_f, E_m$ |
| Will voids be a problem? | Henry's law / pressure model | $P, T, \dot{\epsilon}$ |
| What residual stress develops? | Thermo-elastic model | $\Delta T, \Delta \alpha, E, V_f$ |
