## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q:** List any **three** traditional composite manufacturing processes and state **two merits and two demerits** of hand lay-up.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Three traditional processes:**
> 1. Hand Lay-up
> 2. Filament Winding
> 3. Pultrusion
>
> *(1 mark for correctly listing any three with brief mention)*
>
> **Hand Lay-up — Merits:**
> - Low tooling cost; suitable for very large parts (boat hulls, wind turbine nacelle covers).
> - Simple process; minimal skill requirement to begin production.
>
> **Hand Lay-up — Demerits:**
> - High void content (typically 3–5%) due to manual consolidation.
> - Labor-intensive and operator-dependent; poor reproducibility.
>
> *(2 marks for merits and demerits — one mark each pair)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Q:** What are **novel approaches** in composite manufacturing? Give **two examples** and explain how they overcome the limitations of traditional methods.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Novel approaches** are advanced, automation-driven, digitally-controlled composite manufacturing routes that improve repeatability, reduce waste, and enable complex geometries. They address the **void content, cycle time, and labor** limitations of traditional contact molding.
>
> *(1 mark for definition)*
>
> **Two Examples:**
> 1. **Automated Fiber Placement (AFP):** Uses a robotic head to place multiple prepreg tows simultaneously with closed-loop laser inspection. Achieves void content $<$ 1% and reduces layup time by up to 70% compared to hand lay-up.
> 2. **VARTM (Vacuum-Assisted RTM):** Uses only atmospheric pressure to infuse large preforms, replacing the autoclave. Reduces capital cost by ~60% and enables wind-turbine blades up to 80 m long.
>
> *(2 marks: 1 mark per example with explanation of overcoming limitations)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:**
> - When asked to **compare** traditional and novel processes, students frequently forget to mention the **specific quantitative metric** (e.g., void content, cycle time) and lose **2 marks** out of 7.
> - In derivations, **forgetting the units** in the final answer is the most common single-mark deduction.
> - Avoid generic sentences like "novel methods are better" — KTU examiners expect **engineering trade-off analysis**.

---

### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **traditional hand lay-up and autoclave processing routes** for polymer matrix composites. Compare their **process steps, capital cost, achievable fiber volume fraction, void content, and typical applications**.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Hand Lay-up | Autoclave |
|---|---|---|
| Process steps | Mold prep → gelcoat → resin + fiber manual layup → roller consolidation → room-temp cure → demold | Prepreg cut → layup on tool → vacuum bag → autoclave (heat + pressure) → cure → debag → NDT |
| Capital Cost | Very low (~$5K–$50K) | Very high (~$1M–$10M) |
| Achievable $V_f$ | 0.25–0.40 | 0.55–0.65 |
| Void Content | 3–5% | $<$ 0.5% |
| Typical Application | Marine hulls, bathtubs, low-cost panels | Aircraft primary structures (fuselage, wings) |

*(4 marks: tabular comparison with correct qualitative and quantitative statements)*

**Additional Detailed Explanation:**

- **Hand Lay-up:** Open-mold process where successive layers of reinforcement (chopped strand mat or woven roving) are manually impregnated with liquid resin using brushes or rollers. Curing can occur at room temperature (for polyesters with MEKP catalyst) or in an oven. The process is limited by **operator skill** and **environmental conditions** (humidity, temperature).
- **Autoclave Processing:** A **pressure vessel** that combines **vacuum (0.09 MPa)** from inside the bag with **external isostatic pressure (0.7 MPa)** and **controlled heating (typically 1–3 °C/min ramp to 180 °C)**. The pressure removes volatiles and compacts the laminate, while heat drives the cure. Used for **aerospace-grade prepreg laminates** (e.g., Boeing 787 fuselage).

*(3 marks: detailed explanation with at least two process steps each, mentioning specific numerical values)*

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the **fill-time equation for 1D Darcy flow** in RTM. A rectangular preform of length 0.4 m, permeability $1 \times 10^{-10}$ m$^2$, is infiltrated with resin of viscosity 0.15 Pa·s under a pressure differential of $1.5 \times 10^5$ Pa. Calculate the fill time.

**Model Solution:**

From the derivation in **Section 3.1**:

$$t_{fill} = \frac{\mu L^2}{2 K \Delta P}$$

*(3 marks for the full derivation: starting from Darcy's law, integrating with proper boundary conditions, separating variables, and arriving at the boxed equation.)*

**Numerical Substitution:**

$$t_{fill} = \frac{0.15 \times (0.4)^2}{2 \times (1 \times 10^{-10}) \times (1.5 \times 10^5)}$$

$$t_{fill} = \frac{0.15 \times 0.16}{3 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{0.024}{3 \times 10^{-5}} = 800 \text{ s}$$

*(3 marks: 1 mark for substitution, 1 mark for arithmetic, 1 mark for correct units)*

**Answer:** **$t_{fill} = 800$ s $\approx$ 13.3 minutes.**

*(1 mark for final numerical statement with correct unit and approximate time conversion)*

---

### Question B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **novel approaches** in composite processing: **Automated Fiber Placement (AFP), VARTM, and Additive Manufacturing (3D printing)**. Highlight the **process principle, advantages, and limitations** of each.

**Model Answer:**

**1. Automated Fiber Placement (AFP):**
- **Principle:** A multi-axis robotic gantry or articulated arm head lays down **3 to 32 narrow prepreg tows** simultaneously at controlled speeds (up to 50 m/min). Real-time laser inspection detects gaps, overlaps, and wrinkles.
- **Advantages:** High deposition rate; consistent tow tension; ability to steer tow paths for **load-aligned** placement; reduced scrap.
- **Limitations:** High capital cost; limited steering angle (typically $\pm 20°$ to $\pm 45°$); difficulty with thick laminates due to heat transfer.

**2. VARTM (Vacuum-Assisted RTM):**
- **Principle:** A dry preform is sealed under a vacuum bag on a rigid mold. Resin is drawn into the preform under **only atmospheric pressure (~0.1 MPa)**, often through a flow-distribution medium.
- **Advantages:** Low capital cost (no autoclave); scalable to very large parts (wind blades); low VOC emissions.
- **Limitations:** Lower $V_f$ than autoclave; longer cycle time; flow-front monitoring needed to avoid dry spots.

**3. Additive Manufacturing (3D Printing):**
- **Principle:** Layer-by-layer deposition of **fiber-reinforced filaments** (FDM) or **photo-cured nano-reinforced resins** (SLA). Recent systems print **continuous carbon fibers** within a thermoplastic matrix.
- **Advantages:** Enables **complex topology-optimized** geometries; minimal material waste; on-demand customization.
- **Limitations:** Limited mechanical performance vs. autoclave laminates; anisotropic properties depend on print orientation; slower for high $V_f$ parts.

*(7 marks: roughly 2 marks per process for principle, advantages, and limitations combined; awards full marks if specific numerical examples are included, e.g., AFP speed, VARTM scale, AM waste reduction)*

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Arrhenius cure kinetics model** for a thermoset matrix composite. The activation energy of an epoxy system is 70 kJ/mol and the pre-exponential factor is $1.2 \times 10^8$ s$^{-1}$. Calculate the cure time at 160 °C to reach a degree of cure of 0.8 for a first-order reaction.

**Model Solution:**

For first-order ($n = 1$) kinetics:

$$\frac{d\alpha}{dt} = A \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right)(1-\alpha)$$

Separating and integrating:

$$-\ln(1-\alpha) = A \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right) t$$

Solving for $t$:

$$t_{cure} = -\frac{\ln(1-\alpha_t)}{A \exp(-E_a/RT)}$$

*(3 marks for derivation: kinetic equation → integration → final expression)*

**Numerical Calculation:**

- $E_a = 70 \times 10^3$ J/mol
- $A = 1.2 \times 10^8$ s$^{-1}$
- $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)
- $T = 160 + 273 = 433$ K
- $\alpha_t = 0.8$

**Step 1 — Exponent:**

$$-\frac{E_a}{RT} = -\frac{70{,}000}{8.314 \times 433} = -\frac{70{,}000}{3599.96} \approx -19.446$$

**Step 2 — Exponential factor:**

$$\exp(-19.446) \approx 3.59 \times 10^{-9}$$

**Step 3 — Numerator:**

$$-\ln(1-0.8) = -\ln(0.2) = 1.609$$

**Step 4 — Denominator:**

$$A \times \exp(-E_a/RT) = 1.2 \times 10^8 \times 3.59 \times 10^{-9} = 0.431 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

**Step 5 — Final Cure Time:**

$$t_{cure} = \frac{1.609}{0.431} \approx 3.73 \text{ s}$$

*(3 marks: 1 mark for exponent, 1 mark for numerator/denominator, 1 mark for final value)*

**Answer:** **$t_{cure} \approx 3.73$ seconds.**

*(1 mark for stating final answer with units and physical interpretation: very fast — this is the instantaneous rate; in practice, the *integrated* cycle is much longer due to ramp-up and diffusion.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often forget to convert **°C to K** before substituting into the Arrhenius equation. This error alone costs **1 mark** in the numerical part.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print This Before Exam:**

- **Two families:** Traditional (hand layup, filament winding, pultrusion, autoclave, RTM) vs Novel (AFP, VARTM, AM, in-situ).
- **Five fundamentals:** Placement → Wetting → Consolidation → Cure → Defect control.
- **Autoclave = gold standard**, lowest void content ($<$ 0.5%), highest capital cost.
- **Hand layup = lowest cost**, highest void content (3–5%), highest labor.
- **VARTM uses only ~0.1 MPa**, ideal for large wind blades.
- **AFP deposition rate up to ~50 m/min**, steering limit $\pm 20°$–$45°$.
- **Darcy's Law** governs LCM fill: $v = -\dfrac{K}{\mu}\nabla P$.
- **RTM fill time (1D):** $t_{fill} = \dfrac{\mu L^2}{2 K \Delta P}$.
- **Kozeny–Carman:** $K = \dfrac{d_f^2 \varepsilon^3}{150 (1-\varepsilon)^2}$.
- **Arrhenius cure:** $\dfrac{d\alpha}{dt} = A \exp(-E_a/RT)(1-\alpha)^n$.
- **First-order cure time:** $t = -\ln(1-\alpha_t) / [A \exp(-E_a/RT)]$.
- **Always convert T to Kelvin** before Arrhenius substitution.
- **$V_f$ target:** 0.55–0.65 for structural composites; 0.40 for hand layup.
- **Void penalty:** ~10% ILSS loss per 2% void content increase.
- **WLF/Arrhenius** describe resin viscosity drop with temperature.
- **Capillary pressure** $\Delta P_{\text{cap}} = \dfrac{2 \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta}{r}$ governs fiber wet-out.
- **KTU marks lost most often:** wrong units, missing pressure conversion (MPa ↔ Pa), forgetting to convert °C → K, generic qualitative answers without numbers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fundamental Physics in Composite Manufacturing; Manufacturing Processes for Polymeric Composites
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT744 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fundamental Physics in Composite Manufacturing; Manufacturing Processes for Polymeric Composites

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Fundamental Physics in Composite Manufacturing

**Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
The *fundamental physics* governing composite manufacturing encompasses the interrelated phenomena of **surface tension, wetting, capillary action, resin viscosity, cure kinetics, and coupled heat-mass transfer** that dictate how a liquid matrix infiltrates, wets, consolidates, and solidifies around a fibrous reinforcement to form a defect-free composite.

In the KTU 2024 scheme, this is treated as the *science base* without which process selection becomes empirical guesswork.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (CO2):** Every manufacturing route (hand lay-up, RTM, pultrusion, filament winding, autoclave) is a deliberate engineering response to one or more of these physics — *wetting, flow, gelation, and cure.*

### The Four Pillars of Manufacturing Physics

| # | Pillar | Core Question It Answers |
|---|--------|--------------------------|
| 1 | **Wetting & Surface Tension** | Will the resin spread on the fibre, or bead up? |
| 2 | **Capillary Action & Darcy Flow** | How fast will the resin impregnate the fibre tow? |
| 3 | **Viscosity Evolution** | Is the resin still processable, or has it gelled? |
| 4 | **Cure Kinetics & Exotherm** | When, where, and how fast does the matrix harden? |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Kitchen Sponge Model"**
> Imagine dipping a dry kitchen sponge into a bowl of honey. The honey does NOT instantly fill every pore — it first *wets* the surface (governed by surface tension), then creeps *upward* through the narrow channels (capillary action), but only if the honey is thin enough (viscosity). If the honey starts to harden while still inside the sponge (cure), pockets of dry sponge remain. A composite laminate behaves **identically** — fibre tows act as the "channels" and resin acts as the "honey."

---

## 1.2 Manufacturing Processes for Polymeric Composites

**Definition:**
Polymeric composite manufacturing processes are classified *systematically* based on (a) mould openness, (b) reinforcement form, and (c) resin delivery mechanism. The KTU syllabus groups them into **Open-Mould, Closed-Mould, and Continuous** families.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Cooking Methods Metaphor"**
> - **Hand Lay-up** = Grilling (open, manual, low capital)
> - **RTM / VARTM** = Pressure Cooking (closed, vacuum/pressure assisted, repeatable)
> - **Pultrusion** = Extrusion of pasta (continuous, constant cross-section)
> - **Filament Winding** = Wrapping wool around a balloon (rotational, axisymmetric)
> - **Autoclave** = Baking in a pressure oven (highest quality, highest cost)

### 1.2.1 Master Classification Table

| Family | Process | Mould | Reinforcement Form | Resin Form |
|--------|---------|-------|--------------------|------------|
| **Open Mould** | Hand Lay-up | Open | Woven fabric / mat | Liquid |
| | Spray-up | Open | Chopped fibres | Liquid + spray |
| **Closed Mould** | RTM | Closed | Woven / mat | Injected liquid |
| | VARTM / SCRIMP | Closed (vacuum) | Woven / mat | Infused liquid |
| | Compression Moulding (SMC/BMC) | Closed (heated) | Pre-impregnated charge | Pre-mixed |
| | Autoclave | Closed (pressurised) | Prepreg | Pre-impregnated |
| **Continuous** | Pultrusion | Heated die | Rovings / mats | Liquid bath |
| | Filament Winding | Rotating mandrel | Rovings | Liquid bath |
| | Resin Film Infusion (RFI) | Closed | Preform + resin film | Film |

### 1.2.2 Why Thermosets vs. Thermoplastics Demand Different Physics

- **Thermosets** (epoxy, polyester, vinylester) are processed in a *low-viscosity liquid state* → manufacturing physics is dominated by **wetting, flow, gelation, and exothermic cure**.
- **Thermoplastics** (PEEK, PPS, PEKK) are processed *molten* → manufacturing physics is dominated by **melt viscosity, crystallisation kinetics, and heat removal**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Take-away:** When answering theory questions, always identify whether the process is **thermoset-based (liquid → solid via cure)** or **thermoplastic-based (solid → melt → solid via cooling)** — this single distinction decides the dominant physics in play.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pillar 1 — Surface Tension & Wetting

### 2.1.1 Surface Tension (γ)
Surface tension is the excess free energy per unit area at a liquid surface, arising from unbalanced cohesive forces among molecules at the interface.

- Units: **N/m** or **J/m²** (numerically equivalent).
- For typical thermoset resins: **γ ≈ 0.03 – 0.045 N/m** (lower than water's 0.072 N/m).

### 2.1.2 Young's Equation — Contact Angle (θ)

At the three-phase contact line (solid–liquid–vapour):

$$\theta = \cos^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{sl}}{\gamma_{lv}}\right)$$

Where:
- $\gamma_{sv}$ = solid–vapour interfacial energy
- $\gamma_{sl}$ = solid–liquid interfacial energy
- $\gamma_{lv}$ = liquid–vapour (surface) tension

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation Rule:** $\theta < 90°$ → wetting; $\theta > 90°$ → non-wetting. For carbon-fibre/epoxy, $\theta$ is typically **< 30°** → excellent wetting.

### 2.1.3 Work of Adhesion (Wₐ)
Energy released when unit area of solid–liquid interface forms from separate solid and liquid:

$$W_a = \gamma_{sv} + \gamma_{lv} - \gamma_{sl} = \gamma_{lv}\,(1 + \cos\theta)$$

Higher $W_a$ → stronger fibre–matrix interface → better composite mechanical performance.

### 2.1.4 Why this matters in manufacturing
- Poor wetting → **dry spots, voids, delamination** at the fibre–matrix interface.
- Surface treatments (e.g., **sizing on carbon fibres**, plasma treatment) lower $\theta$ and raise $W_a$.

---

## 2.2 Pillar 2 — Capillary Action & Darcy's Law for Resin Flow

### 2.2.1 Capillary Pressure (Young–Laplace)
For a cylindrical pore of radius $r$ wetted by a liquid of contact angle $\theta$:

$$\Delta P_{cap} = \frac{2\,\gamma_{lv}\,\cos\theta}{r}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention:** If $\theta < 90°$, $\cos\theta > 0$ → capillary pressure is **positive** → liquid is *pulled* into the pore (e.g., VARTM infusion). If $\theta > 90°$, pressure is negative → liquid is *pushed out* (e.g., mercury in glass).

### 2.2.2 Darcy's Law (Resin Flow Through Porous Preform)

$$\vec{v} = -\frac{K}{\mu}\,\nabla P$$

Where:
- $\vec{v}$ = superficial (Darcy) velocity [m/s]
- $K$ = permeability of the preform [m²]
- $\mu$ = resin dynamic viscosity [Pa·s]
- $\nabla P$ = pressure gradient [Pa/m]

For **1-D flow in a rectangular cavity** of length $L$, from inlet pressure $P_{in}$ to outlet $P_{out}$:

$$v = \frac{K\,(P_{in} - P_{out})}{\mu\,L}$$

**Fill time (t):**

$$t = \frac{\mu\,L^{2}}{2\,K\,(P_{in} - P_{out})}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Doubling the cavity length *quadruples* fill time — this is why designers use **flow media** and **race-tracking channels** in VARTM.

### 2.2.3 Kozeny–Carman Equation (Permeability Estimate)

$$K = \frac{\phi^{3}}{c\,S^{2}\,(1-\phi)^{2}}$$

Where $\phi$ = porosity, $S$ = specific surface area, $c$ = Kozeny constant.

---

## 2.3 Pillar 3 — Viscosity Evolution & Gelation

### 2.3.1 Newtonian vs. Non-Newtonian Behaviour
Most uncured thermosets are *near-Newtonian* at low shear rates. Once cure begins, viscosity rises sharply.

### 2.3.2 Arrhenius Viscosity Model (Most Common in KTU Problems)

$$\mu(T) = \mu_{\infty}\,\exp\!\left(\frac{E_{\mu}}{R\,T}\right)$$

Where $E_{\mu}$ = activation energy of flow, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), $T$ = absolute temperature [K].

### 2.3.3 Gel Point
At the **gel point**, an infinite molecular network first appears; viscosity theoretically diverges. For epoxy, gel typically occurs at **20–40% conversion** depending on hardener.

---

## 2.4 Pillar 4 — Cure Kinetics & Heat Transfer

### 2.4.1 Degree of Cure (α)
$$0 \le \alpha = \frac{\text{heat released up to time }t}{\text{total heat of reaction}} \le 1$$

### 2.4.2 Kamal–Ryan Cure Model (Phenomenological)

$$\frac{d\alpha}{dt} = (k_1 + k_2\,\alpha^{m})\,(1-\alpha)^{n}$$

Where $k_1,\,k_2$ follow Arrhenius form:

$$k_i = A_i\,\exp\!\left(-\frac{E_i}{R\,T}\right)$$

### 2.4.3 Total Heat Released (Exotherm)
$$Q_{total} = \int_{0}^{t_{final}} \rho\,H_{r}\,\frac{d\alpha}{dt}\,dt$$

Where $H_r$ = specific heat of reaction [J/kg]. For typical epoxy: $H_r \approx 300\text{–}500$ kJ/kg.

> [!WARNING]
> **Thick Laminate Trap:** In thick parts, low thermal conductivity of polymer (~0.2 W/m·K) traps exothermic heat → temperature overshoot → thermal degradation or matrix cracking. **This is a high-weightage KTU concept.**

### 2.4.4 1-D Transient Heat Equation in a Curing Laminate

$$\rho\,c_p\,\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = k_{th}\,\frac{\partial^{2} T}{\partial x^{2}} + \rho\,H_{r}\,\frac{d\alpha}{dt}$$

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Memorise the *form*, not the *literal constants* — KTU 2024 supplies all numerical values in the question paper for problem-solving.

| # | Phenomenon | Governing Equation | Key Variables | Typical Units | Engineering Use |
|---|------------|--------------------|---------------|---------------|----------------|
| 1 | Contact angle | $\theta = \cos^{-1}\!\big((\gamma_{sv}-\gamma_{sl})/\gamma_{lv}\big)$ | $\gamma$ | N/m | Predict wettability |
| 2 | Work of adhesion | $W_a = \gamma_{lv}(1+\cos\theta)$ | $\gamma, \theta$ | J/m² | Fibre–matrix bond quality |
| 3 | Capillary pressure | $\Delta P = 2\gamma_{lv}\cos\theta / r$ | $\gamma, \theta, r$ | Pa | Drive VARTM/RTM infusion |
| 4 | Darcy velocity | $v = -(K/\mu)\,\nabla P$ | $K, \mu, P$ | m/s | Resin front speed |
| 5 | Fill time (1-D) | $t = \mu L^{2}/(2K\,\Delta P)$ | $L, \mu, K, \Delta P$ | s | RTM cycle design |
| 6 | Arrhenius viscosity | $\mu = \mu_{\infty}\exp(E_{\mu}/RT)$ | $E_{\mu}, T$ | Pa·s | Hot-melt, prepreg |
| 7 | Kamal–Ryan cure | $d\alpha/dt = (k_1+k_2\alpha^{m})(1-\alpha)^{n}$ | $k, m, n$ | s⁻¹ | Cure cycle design |
| 8 | Heat of reaction | $Q = \rho H_{r}\,\alpha$ | $\rho, H_r$ | J/m³ | Exotherm estimation |
| 9 | Heat conduction (1-D) | $\partial T/\partial t = \alpha_{th}\,\partial^{2}T/\partial x^{2} + \dot{q}/(\rho c_p)$ | $\alpha_{th}, \dot q$ | K/s | Cure thermal management |
| 10 | Kozeny–Carman | $K = \phi^{3}/[cS^{2}(1-\phi)^{2}]$ | $\phi, S$ | m² | Permeability prediction |
| 11 | Degree of cure | $\alpha = Q(t)/Q_{total}$ | $Q$ | — | Cure state tracking |
| 12 | Gelation criterion | $G' = G''$ (crossover) | $G', G''$ | Pa | Pot-life, processing window |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Table Notation:** All $\vert \cdot \vert$ or $\mid \cdot \mid$ symbols in equations have been rendered with regular mathematical notation to **preserve markdown table integrity**. Do not insert raw pipe characters inside table cells.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Utility Snapshot

| Industry | Dominant Process | Dominant Physics Concern |
|----------|------------------|--------------------------|
| Aerospace primary structure (Boeing 787, A350) | Autoclave (prepreg) | Cure exotherm in thick laminates, void minimisation |
| Wind turbine blades (60+ m) | VARTM | Resin flow through thick multi-layer preforms |
| Automotive body panels | RTM / Compression (SMC) | Cycle time, permeability of random mats |
| Civil rebar (FRP) | Pultrusion | Capillary-driven die injection, die heating |
| Pressure vessels, rocket motor cases | Filament winding | Fibre tension control, resin bath wet-out |
| Marine hulls | Hand lay-up / Infusion | Wetting of woven rovings, void content |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Capillary Rise in a Single Fibre Channel (Classical Young–Laplace Application)

### Setup
Consider a horizontal cylindrical pore of radius $r$ inside a fibre tow, with the liquid resin making a contact angle $\theta$ with the fibre surface. We wish to derive the **capillary pressure** that drives the resin front.

### Step 1 — Surface Force Balance on the Meniscus
The liquid surface is curved, with principal radii of curvature $R_1$ and $R_2$. The Young–Laplace equation gives the pressure jump across any curved interface:

$$\Delta P = \gamma_{lv}\!\left(\frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2}\right)$$

### Step 2 — Apply Cylindrical Geometry
For a cylindrical pore, the meniscus is a section of a sphere. By geometry, the two principal radii are:

$$R_1 = \frac{r}{\cos\theta}, \qquad R_2 = \frac{r}{\cos\theta}$$

(For a thin cylindrical capillary, both are equal.)

### Step 3 — Substitute into Young–Laplace

$$\Delta P = \gamma_{lv}\!\left(\frac{\cos\theta}{r} + \frac{\cos\theta}{r}\right) = \frac{2\,\gamma_{lv}\,\cos\theta}{r}$$

### Step 4 — Physical Interpretation
- If $\theta < 90°$ (wetting case, e.g., epoxy on sized carbon fibre), $\cos\theta > 0$, so $\Delta P > 0$ → liquid is **drawn inwards**.
- If $\theta > 90°$ (non-wetting), $\cos\theta < 0$, so $\Delta P < 0$ → liquid is **pushed outwards**.

### Step 5 — Numerical Illustration (KTU Standard Type)
Given: $\gamma_{lv} = 0.035$ N/m, $\theta = 25°$, $r = 5\,\mu m = 5\times 10^{-6}$ m.

$$\Delta P = \frac{2 \times 0.035 \times \cos 25°}{5\times 10^{-6}} = \frac{0.0700 \times 0.9063}{5\times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\Delta P = \frac{0.06344}{5\times 10^{-6}} \approx 1.269\times 10^{4}\ \text{Pa} \approx 12.7\ \text{kPa}$$

This is exactly the magnitude of vacuum typically applied in **VARTM** to assist impregnation — a direct validation of the formula.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: 1-D Darcy Fill Time in a Rectangular Cavity

### Setup
A rectangular mould cavity of length $L$, thickness $h$, and width $w$ is being filled from one end under a constant pressure differential $\Delta P = P_{in} - P_{out}$. The preform permeability is $K$ and resin viscosity is $\mu$ (assumed constant during fill).

### Step 1 — Darcy's Law in 1-D

$$v(t) = \frac{K}{\mu}\cdot\frac{\Delta P}{x(t)}$$

where $x(t)$ is the instantaneous position of the resin front.

### Step 2 — Mass (Volume) Conservation
The cumulative volume infused at time $t$ is $V = A\,x(t)$ where $A = h\,w$. Equivalently:

$$\frac{dV}{dt} = A\,v(t)$$

### Step 3 — Combine and Separate Variables

$$A\,\frac{dx}{dt} = A\,\frac{K}{\mu}\cdot\frac{\Delta P}{x}$$

$$\frac{dx}{dt} = \frac{K\,\Delta P}{\mu\,x}$$

$$x\,dx = \frac{K\,\Delta P}{\mu}\,dt$$

### Step 4 — Integrate from $x=0$ at $t=0$ to $x=L$ at $t=t_{fill}$:

$$\int_{0}^{L} x\,dx = \frac{K\,\Delta P}{\mu}\int_{0}^{t_{fill}} dt$$

$$\frac{L^{2}}{2} = \frac{K\,\Delta P}{\mu}\,t_{fill}$$

### Step 5 — Solve for Fill Time

$$t_{fill} = \frac{\mu\,L^{2}}{2\,K\,\Delta P}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering implication:** $t_{fill}\propto L^{2}$. Hence, for an 8 m wind-turbine blade (RTM infusion), fill time becomes a critical bottleneck — solved by **flow media** (high-permeability net) and **multiple inlet ports**.

### Step 6 — Numerical Illustration
Given: $\mu = 0.3$ Pa·s, $L = 1.0$ m, $K = 1\times 10^{-9}$ m², $\Delta P = 100$ kPa = $1\times 10^{5}$ Pa.

$$t_{fill} = \frac{0.3 \times 1.0^{2}}{2 \times 10^{-9} \times 10^{5}} = \frac{0.3}{2\times 10^{-4}} = 1500\ \text{s} = 25\ \text{min}$$

A realistic RTM cycle time for a 1 m part.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Adiabatic Exotherm Temperature Rise in a Curing Laminate

### Step 1 — Energy Released by Cure
Total heat released per unit volume on full cure ($\alpha = 1$):

$$Q_{total} = \rho\,H_{r}$$

### Step 2 — Energy Balance (Adiabatic, No Heat Loss)
All reaction heat raises the temperature of the laminate:

$$\rho\,H_{r} = \rho\,c_{p}\,\Delta T_{adiabatic}$$

### Step 3 — Solve for Temperature Rise

$$\Delta T_{adiabatic} = \frac{H_{r}}{c_{p}}$$

### Step 4 — Numerical Illustration
For epoxy: $H_r = 4\times 10^{5}$ J/kg, $c_p = 1200$ J/(kg·K):

$$\Delta T_{adiabatic} = \frac{4\times 10^{5}}{1200} \approx 333\ \text{K}$$

This means an unconstrained thick laminate could rise by **~333 °C** above its starting temperature — clearly catastrophic in practice, which is why real cures are **non-adiabatic** and designed with controlled heating ramps.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Process vs. Dominant Physics

| Process | Surface Tension / Wetting | Capillary / Darcy | Viscosity Window | Cure / Thermal | Compaction / Pressure |
|---------|--------------------------|-------------------|------------------|----------------|------------------------|
| Hand Lay-up | ★★★★ (poor control) | ★ | ★★★ (manual roll) | ★★ (room-temp cure) | ★ |
| Spray-up | ★★ | ★ | ★★ | ★ | ★ |
| RTM | ★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★ (mould clamp) |
| VARTM | ★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★ | ★★ (vacuum only) |
| Compression (SMC) | ★ | ★ | ★★★★ (high-T low-η) | ★★★★ | ★★★★★ |
| Autoclave (prepreg) | ★★ (pre-impregnated) | ★ | ★★ (B-staged) | ★★★★★ | ★★★★★ (7 bar) |
| Pultrusion | ★★★ (resin bath) | ★★★ (die injection) | ★★★ | ★★★★ (die heated) | ★★★ |
| Filament Winding | ★★★ | ★★ | ★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★ (tension) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagrams map the **functional architecture** of the major manufacturing processes. They are validated to use only alphanumeric node IDs and clean uppercase labels, with no markdown formatting inside node text.

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Hand Lay-up (Open Mould) Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Step A: Mould Preparation] --> A2[Step B: Gelcoat Application if Required]
    A2 --> A3[Step C: Lay First Reinforcement Layer]
    A3 --> A4[Step D: Resin Pouring and Brushing]
    A4 --> A5[Step E: Hand Roller Compaction]
    A5 --> A6{More Layers?}
    A6 -- Yes --> A3
    A6 -- No --> A7[Step F: Final Wet Layup and Cover Layer]
    A7 --> A8[Step G: Room Temperature or Oven Cure]
    A8 --> A9[Step H: Demould and Trim]
    A9 --> A10[Finished Composite Part]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — VARTM / SCRIMP Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B1[Step A: Tool Surface Prep and Release Agent] --> B2[Step B: Layup Dry Preform]
    B2 --> B3[Step C: Place Peel Ply and Flow Media]
    B3 --> B4[Step D: Seal with Vacuum Bag and Tubing]
    B4 --> B5[Step E: Connect Inlet to Resin Trap and Outlet to Vacuum Pump]
    B5 --> B6[Step F: Apply Full Vacuum to Check for Leaks]
    B6 --> B7[Step G: Open Resin Inlet - Capillary and Pressure Drive Impregnation]
    B7 --> B8{Resin Front Reaches Outlet?}
    B8 -- No --> B7
    B8 -- Yes --> B9[Step H: Close Inlet, Maintain Vacuum, Begin Cure]
    B9 --> B10[Step I: Demould, Inspect for Voids]
    B10 --> B11[Finished Composite Part]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Pultrusion Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C1[Creel: Fibre Rovings] --> C2[Resin Bath Wet-out Tank]
    C2 --> C3[Pre-forming Die Guides]
    C3 --> C4[Heated Curing Die 120 to 180 C]
    C4 --> C5[Pulling System Caterpillar or Reciprocating]
    C5 --> C6[Cutting Saw Stationary or In-line]
    C6 --> C7[Finished Pultruded Profile]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Filament Winding Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D1[Step 1: Creel Roving Spools] --> D2[Step 2: Resin Bath Impregnation]
    D2 --> D3[Step 3: Fibre Tensioner and Guides]
    D3 --> D4[Step 4: Rotating Mandrel with Programmed Winding Pattern]
    D4 --> D5{Required Thickness Achieved?}
    D5 -- No --> D4
    D5 -- Yes --> D6[Step 5: Cure on Mandrel Oven or Ambient]
    D6 --> D7[Step 6: Extract Mandrel and Machine if Needed]
    D7 --> D8[Finished Axisymmetric Composite]
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram 5 — Autoclave Prepreg Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    E1[Step 1: Cut Prepreg from Freezer Cold Storage] --> E2[Step 2: Layup Plies on Tool with Definite Stacking Sequence]
    E2 --> E3[Step 3: Apply Breather Bleeder and Vacuum Bag]
    E3 --> E4[Step 4: Load into Autoclave]
    E4 --> E5[Step 5: Apply Vacuum Then Pressure 7 bar Typical]
    E5 --> E6[Step 6: Apply Controlled Heating Ramp 1 to 3 C per min]
    E6 --> E7[Step 7: Hold at Cure Temperature 120 to 180 C]
    E7 --> E8[Step 8: Cool Down Under Pressure to Avoid Thermal Stresses]
    E8 --> E9[Step 9: Remove from Autoclave and Debag]
    E9 --> E10[Finished High Performance Composite]
```

## 4.6 Mermaid Diagram 6 — Process Selection Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F1{Is Production Volume High?} -->|No| F2{Is Part Geometry Complex 3D?}
    F1 -->|Yes| F3{Consistent Cross Section?}
    F2 -->|Yes Open Mould OK| F4[Use Hand Layup or Spray up]
    F2 -->|No Need Quality| F5[Use VARTM or RTM]
    F3 -->|Yes| F6[Use Pultrusion]
    F3 -->|No Axisymmetric| F7[Use Filament Winding]
    F5 --> F8{Need Aerospace Grade Low Void?}
    F8 -->|Yes| F9[Use Autoclave Prepreg]
    F8 -->|No| F10[Use VARTM or RTM Final]
```

# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below are calibrated to **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern**: Part A (3 marks each), Part B (14 marks, internal choice, sub-parts a and b of 7 marks each). Model solutions are provided.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define the contact angle and explain its role in determining wettability of fibres by a resin.**  `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The contact angle $\theta$ is the angle formed at the three-phase contact line between the tangent to the liquid–vapour interface and the solid surface, defined by **Young's equation**:

$$\cos\theta = \frac{\gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{sl}}{\gamma_{lv}}$$

- If $\theta < 90°$ → the liquid **wets** the solid (resin spreads on fibre).
- If $\theta > 90°$ → the liquid does **not wet** the solid (resin beads up).

In composite manufacturing, $\theta$ determines whether the resin will spontaneously impregnate the fibre tow via capillary action. A low $\theta$ (typical for sized carbon fibres, $\theta \approx 20°\text{–}30°$) ensures good fibre–matrix interface and low void content. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Q2. List any four polymeric composite manufacturing processes and identify whether each is open-mould, closed-mould, or continuous.**  `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Process | Category |
|---------|----------|
| Hand Lay-up | Open Mould |
| Spray-up | Open Mould |
| RTM | Closed Mould |
| Pultrusion | Continuous |
| Filament Winding | Continuous |
| Autoclave (prepreg) | Closed Mould |

(Any four with correct categorisation fetch full marks.) **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Pattern with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU ESE Part B carries 14 marks. Each sub-part is typically 7 marks. Solve **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**  `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]`

#### **Part (a)** — Derive the expression for capillary pressure driving resin flow into a cylindrical fibre tow and compute its value for a given system. **(7 marks)**

**Given:** Surface tension $\gamma_{lv} = 0.040$ N/m, contact angle $\theta = 30°$, effective pore radius $r = 5\,\mu\text{m}$.

**Step 1 — Start from Young–Laplace equation for a curved interface:**

$$\Delta P = \gamma_{lv}\left(\frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Apply cylindrical geometry of the fibre tow channel:**

$$R_1 = R_2 = \frac{r}{\cos\theta}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute:**

$$\Delta P = \gamma_{lv}\left(\frac{\cos\theta}{r} + \frac{\cos\theta}{r}\right) = \frac{2\,\gamma_{lv}\cos\theta}{r}$$

**Step 4 — Insert numerical values:**

$$\Delta P = \frac{2 \times 0.040 \times \cos 30°}{5 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$= \frac{0.080 \times 0.8660}{5 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{0.0693}{5\times 10^{-6}}$$

$$= 1.386 \times 10^{4}\ \text{Pa} \approx 13.86\ \text{kPa}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:** A capillary suction of ~13.9 kPa is generated, comparable to typical VARTM vacuum levels (≥ 90 kPa), confirming that capillary action *assists* but is *not solely sufficient* for large-part infusion. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Part (b)** — Explain the Resin Transfer Moulding (RTM) process with neat schematic description. Discuss the role of permeability, viscosity, and pressure gradient in determining fill time. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer Outline:**

**Step 1 — RTM Process Description (3 marks):**
- A dry fibre preform is placed in a closed, matched-metal mould.
- A low-viscosity thermoset resin is injected under pressure (typically 1–10 bar) via inlet ports.
- The mould is heated to accelerate cure; the part is demoulded after gelation.

**Step 2 — Darcy's Law Application (2 marks):**
$$v = -\frac{K}{\mu}\,\nabla P$$

Higher permeability $K$ (looser preform, flow media) → faster flow. Higher viscosity $\mu$ → slower flow. Higher pressure gradient → faster flow.

**Step 3 — Fill Time Equation (2 marks):**
$$t_{fill} = \frac{\mu L^{2}}{2K\,\Delta P}$$

Quadratic dependence on $L$ → long parts are bottlenecks; multiple inlets and high-permeability flow media are used in practice.

**(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**  `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]`

#### **Part (a)** — Explain the pultrusion process with a functional flow diagram. List any four key process variables and their effect on part quality. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer Outline:**

**Step 1 — Pultrusion Process (3 marks):**
Pultrusion is a continuous process for manufacturing constant-cross-section composite profiles. The sequence is:

1. **Fibre creels** feed rovings (and/or mats) into the line.
2. **Resin bath** wets the fibres.
3. **Pre-forming guides** consolidate and shape the wet bundle.
4. **Heated die** (120–180 °C) simultaneously cures and shapes the profile.
5. **Pulling system** (caterpillar or reciprocating) draws the cured profile at a controlled speed.
6. **Cut-off saw** cuts profiles to length.

**Step 2 — Four Key Process Variables (4 marks):**

| Variable | Effect on Quality |
|----------|-------------------|
| Die temperature | Too low → incomplete cure; Too high → thermal degradation |
| Pull speed | Too high → under-cured exit; Too low → productivity loss, possible over-cure |
| Resin viscosity | Controls wet-out quality and die pressure |
| Fibre tension | Affects fibre alignment, void content, and straightness |

**(7 marks)**

---

#### **Part (b)** — Discuss the cure kinetics of a thermoset matrix using the Kamal–Ryan model. Why is exothermic heat management critical in thick laminates? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer Outline:**

**Step 1 — Kamal–Ryan Model (3 marks):**
The rate of cure is:

$$\frac{d\alpha}{dt} = (k_1 + k_2\,\alpha^{m})\,(1-\alpha)^{n}$$

where $\alpha$ is the degree of cure, and the rate constants follow Arrhenius dependence on temperature:

$$k_i = A_i\,\exp\!\left(-\frac{E_i}{R\,T}\right)$$

This is an **autocatalytic** model (the $k_2\,\alpha^m$ term) that captures the fact that reaction products catalyse further reaction — a hallmark of amine-cured epoxies.

**Step 2 — Why Exotherm Management Matters (4 marks):**
- Polymer matrix thermal conductivity is low (~0.2 W/m·K).
- In thick laminates, the interior cannot reject the heat of reaction ($H_r \approx 300\text{–}500$ kJ/kg for epoxy).
- Adiabatic temperature rise can reach **several hundred °C**.
- Consequences:
  * **Thermal degradation** of matrix (char, reduced $T_g$).
  * **Residual stresses** on cool-down → microcracking.
  * **Non-uniform** degree of cure through thickness.
- Mitigation: staged cure ramps, low-temperature holds, use of low-exotherm resin systems, **and** simulation-based cure cycle design.

**(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing $W_a$ (work of adhesion) with $W_c$ (work of cohesion).** Only $W_a$ involves the solid interface.
> 2. **Wrong units in Darcy's Law** — $K$ is in **m²**, NOT m/s. Mixing up leads to dimensional chaos.
> 3. **Forgetting the $(1-\alpha)^n$ term in Kamal–Ryan** — students often write only the autocatalytic part, losing 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Mixing up pultrusion (continuous, pulling) with extrusion (molten thermoplastic, pushing).** Pultrusion is fibre-dominated; extrusion is matrix-dominated.
> 5. **Not stating assumptions** in derivations (e.g., "1-D flow", "constant viscosity", "isothermal cure") — KTU examiners deduct 1 mark per missing assumption.
> 6. **Forgetting to convert μm to m** in capillary pressure calculations — silent factor-of-10⁶ error.

---

## 5.3 🎯 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-minute KTU revision — read this section 5 minutes before the exam.**

- [ ] **Contact angle $\theta < 90°$ ⇒ wetting; $\theta > 90°$ ⇒ non-wetting.** Sizing on fibres lowers $\theta$.
- [ ] **Young–Laplace: $\Delta P = 2\gamma\cos\theta/r$** — drives capillary infusion in VARTM.
- [ ] **Darcy: $v = -(K/\mu)\nabla P$** — fundamental for RTM and VARTM fill analysis.
- [ ] **Fill time: $t = \mu L^2/(2K\Delta P)$** — quadratic in $L$, hence the need for flow media.
- [ ] **Arrhenius viscosity: $\mu = \mu_\infty \exp(E_\mu/RT)$** — high $T$ lowers $\mu$, aids processing.
- [ ] **Kamal–Ryan cure: $d\alpha/dt = (k_1 + k_2\alpha^m)(1-\alpha)^n$** — autocatalytic thermoset.
- [ ] **Adiabatic $\Delta T = H_r/c_p$** — for epoxy ≈ 300 °C rise, so cure is *never* truly adiabatic in practice.
- [ ] **Process families:** Open (hand lay-up, spray-up), Closed (RTM, VARTM, autoclave, compression), Continuous (pultrusion, filament winding).
- [ ] **Thermoset vs. Thermoplastic:** Liquid→solid (cure) vs. Solid→melt→solid (cooling) — different dominant physics.
- [ ] **Hand lay-up:** cheapest, lowest quality, highest void content; for prototypes and marine.
- [ ] **VARTM:** uses vacuum + capillary; mid-to-high quality; for wind blades, marine.
- [ ] **Autoclave:** highest quality, very low voids (<1 %); for aerospace primary structure.
- [ ] **Pultrusion:** constant-cross-section profiles; for rebars, ladders, structural sections.
- [ ] **Filament winding:** axisymmetric parts; for pressure vessels, rocket motor casings.
- [ ] **Compression moulding (SMC/BMC):** high volume automotive and electrical parts.
- [ ] **Units to remember:** $\gamma$ in N/m, $K$ in m², $\mu$ in Pa·s, $H_r$ in J/kg, $c_p$ in J/(kg·K).
- [ ] **Constants:** $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Typical Defects Introduced in Manufacturing and the Methods Utilized to Minimize These Defects
Common Terminology in Composites Manufacturing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT744 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Common Terminology in Composites Manufacturing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Common Terminology in Composites Manufacturing"?

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEPOT744 — Composite Materials, Module 2)**, *Common Terminology in Composites Manufacturing* refers to the **standardized vocabulary, process descriptors, and quantitative parameters** used universally by engineers, shop-floor technicians, and quality inspectors when fabricating fibre-reinforced polymer (FRP) composite structures. These terms are the *lingua franca* of the composites industry — without them, design data sheets, layup records, autoclave run-cards, and non-destructive evaluation (NDE) reports cannot be unambiguously interpreted.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** The syllabus explicitly requires the student to *define, distinguish, and apply* the standard manufacturing vocabulary (prepreg, B-stage, tack, shelf-life, out-life, Tg, void content, fibre volume fraction, etc.) before progressing to specific processes like hand lay-up, RTM, filament winding, and pultrusion. Mastery of this terminology is a **CO1 / CO2 (Apply level)** expectation.

The terminology can be grouped into **four functional families**:

| Family | Purpose | Example Terms |
|---|---|---|
| **A. Material State Terms** | Describe *what* the raw material is | Prepreg, B-stage, neat resin, fibre tow, fabric |
| **B. Process Action Terms** | Describe *what is being done* | Layup, bagging, debulking, cure, consolidation |
| **C. Process Control Terms** | Describe *how the process is monitored* | Cure cycle, ramp rate, dwell, exotherm, pressure |
| **D. Quality / Property Terms** | Describe *how good the part is* | Void content, fibre volume fraction ($V_f$), $T_g$, porosity |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Layered Cake Factory"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for first-time learners:** Imagine a professional bakery that produces **layered celebration cakes**.

- The **sponge layers** = **reinforcement** (fibres / fabric).
- The **buttercream between layers** = **matrix** (resin).
- The **frosting applied before baking** = **prepreg** (resin *pre-impregnated* into fibres, tacky and ready to lay).
- The **cold-storage room** where frosting is kept before use = **freezer / shelf-life** of prepreg.
- The **decorator spreading cream gently to remove air pockets** = **debulking**.
- The **sealed plastic wrap pulled over the cake before the oven** = **vacuum bagging**.
- The **temperature-time-pressure recipe in the oven** = **cure cycle** (run inside an **autoclave** = the pressure oven).
- The **final slice** must have a fixed ratio of sponge-to-cream — that ratio is the **fibre volume fraction ($V_f$)**.
- Tiny air bubbles trapped in the slice = **voids** (measured as **void content, $V_v$**).

Just as a pastry chef must know what *“crumb,” “ganache,” “bench rest,”* and *“crumb coat”* mean, a composites engineer must know *prepreg, B-stage, debulk, and cure cycle*. The **vocabulary IS the craft**.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics in This Topic

- Standard laminate density of carbon/epoxy: **$\rho_c \approx 1.55$–$1.60 \text{ g/cm}^3$**
- Typical aerospace $V_f$ target: **$0.55 \leq V_f \leq 0.62$**
- Acceptable void content for primary structure: **$V_v < 1.0\%$** (aerospace); **$V_v < 3\%$** (industrial)
- Standard cure temperature for **3501-6 epoxy**: **$177 \pm 5 \,^\circ\text{C}$**
- Glass transition temperature $T_g$ of a fully cured 3501-6 system: **$\approx 190 \,^\circ\text{C}$**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every KTU Board paper for this module will expect at least one **numerical problem on $V_f$, $V_v$, $V_m$, or rule of mixtures** (mapped to **CO3 — Apply**). Memorize the formulae in Section 2.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 25 Most-Asked Terminology Clusters (Grouped)

### Cluster A — Material State Terminology

1. **Reinforcement** — The high-strength, high-stiffness phase that carries the load (carbon, glass, aramid, boron fibres).
2. **Matrix** — The continuous polymer phase (epoxy, polyester, vinylester, BMI, PEEK) that binds fibres, transfers load, and protects them.
3. **Fibre Tow / Roving** — An untwisted bundle of continuous filaments (e.g., 3K, 6K, 12K tow denotes 3,000/6,000/12,000 filaments).
4. **Fabric / Cloth** — Woven reinforcement (plain, twill, satin weave).
5. **Neat Resin** — Pure, unfilled, unreinforced polymer (used for viscosity / $T_g$ baselines).
6. **Prepreg** — *Pre-impregnated* reinforcement: fibres pre-coated with a **B-stage** (partially cured) resin. Stored at **$-18 \,^\circ\text{C}$**.
7. **B-Stage** — An intermediate cure state where the resin is *tack-free to the touch but still flowable under heat/pressure*. Prepregs are sold in the B-stage.
8. **C-Stage** — Final, fully cross-linked, insoluble, infusible state — the *cured* composite.
9. **Molding Compound** — Bulk ready-to-mold material (e.g., SMC — Sheet Molding Compound, BMC — Bulk Molding Compound).

### Cluster B — Process Action Terminology

10. **Layup** — The act of placing and stacking plies onto a tool in a defined **stacking sequence** and **fibre orientation**.
11. **Stacking Sequence** — The ordered list of ply angles, e.g., $[45/0/-45/90]_s$.
12. **Ply Orientation** — The angle (in degrees) of the fibre direction measured from a reference axis (usually the **x-axis** of the tool or the **rolling direction** of a unidirectional tape).
13. **Debulking** — Periodic application of vacuum (and sometimes heat) between ply groups to compact the layup, remove trapped air, and improve consolidation.
14. **Bagging** — Enclosing the laid-up stack with a flexible film (vacuum bag) sealed to the tool with sealant tape, for evacuation and pressure application.
15. **Bleeder / Bleeder Ply** — A porous fabric (e.g., glass breather) placed outside the release film to absorb excess resin during cure.
16. **Release Film** — A perforated or non-perforated film (e.g., PTFE, nylon) that lets volatiles and excess resin escape while preventing the laminate from sticking to the bleeder.
17. **Breather Fabric** — A felt-like fabric providing a uniform vacuum path and absorbing resin.
18. **Consolidation** — The process by which plies are compacted and voids are eliminated under heat and pressure.

### Cluster C — Process Control Terminology

19. **Cure Cycle** — The prescribed **temperature–time–pressure** profile executed in an oven or autoclave. Includes **ramp** (heating rate), **dwell** (hold at temperature), and **cool-down** stages.
20. **Ramp Rate** — Heating rate in **$^\circ\text{C/min}$** (typical: 1.5–3 $^\circ$C/min for thick laminates).
21. **Dwell** — An isothermal hold at a specified temperature (e.g., $80\,^\circ\text{C}$ hold for 30 min to allow resin flow before the $177\,^\circ\text{C}$ hold).
22. **Exotherm** — The self-heating of a curing resin due to the exothermic cross-linking reaction. In thick laminates, exotherm can **overshoot** the set-point and degrade the matrix.
23. **Gel Point / Gelation** — The instant the resin transitions from liquid to a rubbery network; viscosity rises dramatically. **Gel time** is the time-to-gelation at a given temperature.
24. **Tg (Glass Transition Temperature)** — The temperature at which the cured polymer transitions from a rigid glassy state to a rubbery state. A primary metric of **cure quality**.
25. **Autoclave** — A pressurized oven capable of applying **vacuum + external gas pressure (typically 7–15 bar)** simultaneously during cure.

### Cluster D — Quality & Property Terminology

26. **Fibre Volume Fraction ($V_f$)** — Volume of fibres ÷ total laminate volume.
27. **Matrix Volume Fraction ($V_m$)** — Volume of matrix ÷ total laminate volume.
28. **Void Volume Fraction ($V_v$)** — Volume of voids (air, volatiles) ÷ total volume.
29. **Void Content** — Often expressed in **%**, equals $V_v \times 100$.
30. **Shelf Life** — Total time a prepreg may be stored at $-18\,^\circ\text{C}$ while remaining within specification.
31. **Out-Life (or Tack Life)** — Maximum cumulative time a prepreg may spend *out of the freezer* (at room temperature) before its tack/drape degrades unacceptably.
32. **Tack** — The stickiness of a prepreg, governed by resin viscosity at room temperature.
33. **Drape** — The ability of a prepreg to conform to a contoured tool surface without wrinkling or fibre distortion.
34. **Tooling** — The mold (male/female) on which the laminate is built. Common materials: aluminum, Invar, carbon/epoxy, steel.
35. **Mold Release Agent** — A wax/silicone/PTFE coating applied to the tool to allow part removal.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The five equations below are the **highest-yield** items for KTU 2024 ESE questions on this module. Practice numerical substitution using the worked derivations in Section 3.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_f + V_m + V_v = 1$ | Volume balance of a cured composite | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $V_f = \dfrac{\rho_c \, W_f}{\rho_f}$ | $V_f$ from measured fibre weight fraction $W_f$ and densities | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $V_v = \dfrac{\rho_{th} - \rho_c}{\rho_{th}}$ | Void content from theoretical vs. measured density | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $\rho_{th} = \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{W_f}{\rho_f} + \dfrac{W_m}{\rho_m}}$ | Theoretical (void-free) density by **rule of mixtures** | g/cm³ |
| 5 | $V_f = \dfrac{\rho_c \, W_f}{\rho_f} = 1 - \dfrac{\rho_c \, W_m}{\rho_m} - V_v$ | Expanded form combining 1, 2, 3, 4 | Dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notation used throughout KTU Board papers:**
> $\rho_c$ = composite (measured) density, $\rho_f$ = fibre density, $\rho_m$ = matrix density, $\rho_{th}$ = theoretical (void-free) composite density, $W_f$ = fibre weight fraction, $W_m$ = matrix weight fraction, $V_v$ = void volume fraction.

---

## 2.3 Why This Terminology Matters in Real Engineering

- **Process engineers** read **traveler cards** and **run-cards** that use these exact words — a misread term can scrap a 30-ply aerospace layup.
- **Quality inspectors** reject parts if $V_v > 1\%$ per **ASTM D2734** (standard test for void content).
- **Design engineers** specify $V_f$ targets in **CMH-17 (Composite Materials Handbook)** to guarantee mechanical properties.
- **NDT technicians** correlate **ultrasonic attenuation** with $V_v$ — a term they must know to interpret C-scans.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** For the derivations below, every algebraic step is shown explicitly. KTU Board valuators award **method marks** for showing intermediate substitutions. Do NOT skip lines.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Volume Balance $V_f + V_m + V_v = 1$

A cured composite laminate consists of **three** mutually exclusive phases occupying the entire volume $V_c$:

$$
V_c = V_f + V_m + V_v
$$

Dividing every term by $V_c$:

$$
\frac{V_c}{V_c} = \frac{V_f}{V_c} + \frac{V_m}{V_c} + \frac{V_v}{V_c}
$$

This yields the **KTU staple equation**:

$$
1 = V_f + V_m + V_v
$$

**Engineering meaning:** If you know any two of the three fractions, the third is determined. This is the single most-tested identity in KTU Module 2 numericals.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Fibre Volume Fraction from Weight Fraction

**Starting point:** By definition, the fibre weight fraction is:

$$
W_f = \frac{m_f}{m_c}
$$

The mass of each phase is its volume times its density, so $m_f = \rho_f V_f$ and $m_c = \rho_c V_c$ (because the voids contribute negligible mass). Substituting:

$$
W_f = \frac{\rho_f V_f}{\rho_c V_c} = \frac{\rho_f V_f}{\rho_c \cdot 1} = \frac{\rho_f V_f}{\rho_c}
$$

Solving for $V_f$:

$$
\boxed{\,V_f = \frac{\rho_c \, W_f}{\rho_f}\,}
$$

This is the form tested in KTU Board problems (e.g., "A carbon-fibre/epoxy laminate has $W_f = 0.65$, $\rho_f = 1.80$ g/cm³, $\rho_c = 1.58$ g/cm³. Find $V_f$.").

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Theoretical Density by Rule of Mixtures

The **theoretical (void-free) density** of a composite is obtained when we assume the laminate is a perfect, void-free blend of fibre and matrix only. The specific volume of the composite is the sum of the specific volumes of its constituents:

$$
\frac{1}{\rho_{th}} = \frac{V_f}{\rho_f} + \frac{V_m}{\rho_m}
$$

Expressing the right-hand side in terms of weight fractions (since $V_f = \dfrac{m_f/\rho_f}{m_c/\rho_{th}}$ and $V_m = \dfrac{m_m/\rho_m}{m_c/\rho_{th}}$):

$$
\frac{1}{\rho_{th}} = \frac{m_f}{m_c \, \rho_f} + \frac{m_m}{m_c \, \rho_m} = \frac{W_f}{\rho_f} + \frac{W_m}{\rho_m}
$$

Taking the reciprocal:

$$
\boxed{\,\rho_{th} = \frac{1}{\dfrac{W_f}{\rho_f} + \dfrac{W_m}{\rho_m}}\,}
$$

This is the **density a void-free composite of the same composition would have**. Compare with the *measured* $\rho_c$ to extract $V_v$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Void Content from Density Comparison

The **measured** density is the *actual* mass of the laminate (fibres + matrix + air) divided by its total volume:

$$
\rho_c = \frac{m_f + m_m}{V_f V_c + V_m V_c + V_v V_c} = \frac{m_f + m_m}{V_c (1)} = \frac{m_c}{V_c}
$$

The **theoretical** density assumes the *same mass* is packed into a smaller volume (no voids):

$$
\rho_{th} = \frac{m_c}{V_c - V_v V_c} = \frac{m_c}{V_c (1 - V_v)} = \frac{\rho_c}{1 - V_v}
$$

Cross-multiplying:

$$
\rho_{th} (1 - V_v) = \rho_c
$$

Solving for $V_v$:

$$
1 - V_v = \frac{\rho_c}{\rho_{th}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,V_v = \frac{\rho_{th} - \rho_c}{\rho_{th}} = 1 - \frac{\rho_c}{\rho_{th}}\,}
$$

In percentage form, **void content (%)** $= V_v \times 100$. Aerospace acceptance criterion: $V_v < 0.01$ (i.e., $< 1\%$).

---

## 3.5 Worked Example (KTU-Style Numerical)

> **Problem:** A glass-fibre/epoxy laminate is tested per ASTM D2734. Given: $W_f = 0.70$, $\rho_f = 2.55$ g/cm³, $\rho_m = 1.20$ g/cm³, $\rho_c$ (measured) $= 1.85$ g/cm³. Compute (a) $V_f$, (b) $\rho_{th}$, (c) $V_v$, (d) $V_m$.

**Step (a) — Compute $V_f$:**

$$
V_f = \frac{\rho_c \, W_f}{\rho_f} = \frac{1.85 \times 0.70}{2.55} = \frac{1.295}{2.55} = 0.5078 \approx 0.508
$$

**Step (b) — Compute $\rho_{th}$:**

$$
W_m = 1 - W_f = 1 - 0.70 = 0.30
$$

$$
\rho_{th} = \frac{1}{\dfrac{0.70}{2.55} + \dfrac{0.30}{1.20}} = \frac{1}{0.2745 + 0.2500} = \frac{1}{0.5245} = 1.907 \text{ g/cm}^3
$$

**Step (c) — Compute $V_v$:**

$$
V_v = \frac{\rho_{th} - \rho_c}{\rho_{th}} = \frac{1.907 - 1.85}{1.907} = \frac{0.057}{1.907} = 0.0299 \approx 0.030
$$

So **void content $\approx 3.0\%$** — acceptable for industrial, *not* for primary aerospace structure.

**Step (d) — Compute $V_m$:**

$$
V_m = 1 - V_f - V_v = 1 - 0.508 - 0.030 = 0.462
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** $V_f + V_m + V_v = 0.508 + 0.462 + 0.030 = 1.000$ ✓

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Term-to-Symbol Mapping (Rapid Revision)

| Term | Symbol | Default Unit | Common Range in KTU Papers |
|---|---|---|---|
| Fibre volume fraction | $V_f$ | — | 0.45 – 0.65 |
| Matrix volume fraction | $V_m$ | — | 0.30 – 0.50 |
| Void volume fraction | $V_v$ | — | 0.00 – 0.05 |
| Fibre weight fraction | $W_f$ | — | 0.55 – 0.75 |
| Composite density | $\rho_c$ | g/cm³ | 1.55 – 1.95 |
| Fibre density (carbon) | $\rho_f$ | g/cm³ | 1.75 – 1.80 |
| Glass-fibre density | $\rho_f$ | g/cm³ | 2.50 – 2.62 |
| Epoxy density | $\rho_m$ | g/cm³ | 1.15 – 1.25 |
| Glass transition temp. | $T_g$ | $^\circ$C | 120 – 250 |
| Cure temperature | $T_{cure}$ | $^\circ$C | 80 – 200 |
| Autoclave pressure | $P$ | bar | 7 – 15 |
| Shelf life (prepreg) | — | months @ $-18\,^\circ$C | 6 – 18 |
| Out-life (prepreg) | — | days @ 22 $^\circ$C | 10 – 60 |
| Gel time | — | minutes | 5 – 180 |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Prepreg-Based Composite Manufacturing Workflow

> The diagram below maps the *terminology* to a *position in the manufacturing timeline*. Examiners often ask: *"At which step is X applied?"* — this diagram is the answer.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Fibre Tow / Fabric] --> B[Resin Impregnation]
    B --> C[B-Stage Prepreg]
    C --> D[Freezer Storage at -18 C]
    D --> E[Thaw to Room Temperature]
    E --> F[Cut Plies to Shape]
    F --> G[Layup on Tool with Stacking Sequence]
    G --> H[Debulking - 5 to 10 min Vacuum]
    H --> I[Repeat G and H for all Plies]
    I --> J[Vacuum Bagging with Breather and Bleeder]
    J --> K[Autoclave Cure Cycle]
    K --> L[Demonstrate Tg and Vv]
    L --> M[Accept Part or Reject]
    M --> N[Part to NDT and Trim]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Each node corresponds to a *terminology* you must define. For example:
- Node C → **B-Stage** (term definition)
- Node D → **Shelf Life** (concept)
- Node E → **Out-Life starts**
- Node H → **Debulking** (process action)
- Node J → **Bagging / Bleeder / Breather** (material terms)
- Node K → **Cure Cycle / Autoclave / Tg** (process control terms)
- Node L → **$V_f, V_v, T_g$** (quality terms)

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Volume-Fraction Identity (The "Composite Pie")

```mermaid
pie title Volume Fractions of a Cured Composite
    "V_f (Fibre)" : 50.8
    "V_m (Matrix)" : 46.2
    "V_v (Voids)" : 3.0
```

This pie represents the worked example in Section 3.5 — useful for visualizing why $V_f + V_m + V_v = 1$.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Process-Control Parameter Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    P[Cure Cycle]
    P --> Q[Temperature Profile]
    P --> R[Pressure Profile]
    P --> S[Time Profile]
    Q --> Q1[Ramp Rate in C per min]
    Q --> Q2[Dwell Temperature in C]
    R --> R1[Vacuum in bar]
    R --> R2[Autoclave Pressure in bar]
    S --> S1[Dwell Time in min]
    S --> S2[Cool-Down Rate]
    Q1 --> T[Outcome: Tg and Vf and Vv]
    Q2 --> T
    R1 --> T
    R2 --> T
    S1 --> T
    S2 --> T
```

This block-level architecture shows the *three independent control variables* of a cure cycle and their *outputs*, which is a frequently-asked KTU conceptual question (worth 3–7 marks).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Q1(a)]**

**Q1.** *Define the term **"prepreg"** and state the storage conditions and one consequence of improper storage.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **prepreg** is a *pre-impregnated* reinforcement in which fibres (carbon, glass, or aramid) are pre-coated with a partially cured (**B-stage**) thermoset resin to a controlled resin content. Prepregs are stored in a **freezer at $-18\,^\circ\text{C}$** to arrest further cure and preserve tack, drape, and shelf life. **Consequence of improper storage:** premature advance of cure reduces tack and drape, shortens out-life, lowers final $T_g$, and can cause porosity and dry spots in the cured laminate. *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for storage, 1 mark for consequence.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Q1(b)]**

**Q2.** *Differentiate between **"shelf life"** and **"out-life"** of a prepreg.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Shelf life** is the *total* time a prepreg may be stored in its sealed, frozen state at $-18\,^\circ\text{C}$ while remaining within the manufacturer's specifications (typically 6–18 months). *(1 mark)*
- **Out-life** is the *cumulative* time the prepreg is allowed to remain at room temperature (around 22 $^\circ\text{C}$) after being removed from the freezer — the time available for layup before tack and handleability degrade unacceptably (typically 10–60 days). *(1 mark)*
- The two together govern the **total usable life**: a prepreg whose freezer time + out-time exceeds shelf life is rejected. *(1 mark for distinction.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Q2]**
> *Note: KTU Part B questions for 14 marks are presented with internal choice. Question A and Question B are independent alternatives.*

### ❖ Question A (14 Marks)

**Q.A(a)** Explain the **volume balance of a cured composite** and derive the relationships for **fibre volume fraction ($V_f$)** and **theoretical density ($\rho_{th}$)** in terms of weight fractions and constituent densities. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Volume balance (2 marks):**
A cured laminate has three mutually exclusive phases — fibres ($V_f$), matrix ($V_m$), and voids ($V_v$) — occupying the total volume $V_c$. Therefore:

$$
V_c = V_f + V_m + V_v
$$

Dividing by $V_c$:

$$
1 = V_f + V_m + V_v
$$

**Step 2 — Derivation of $V_f$ from weight fraction (2.5 marks):**
By definition, $W_f = \dfrac{m_f}{m_c}$. Since $m_f = \rho_f V_f$ and $m_c = \rho_c V_c$, with $V_c = 1$ in fraction form:

$$
W_f = \frac{\rho_f V_f}{\rho_c}
$$

$$
\Rightarrow V_f = \frac{\rho_c W_f}{\rho_f}
$$

**Step 3 — Theoretical density by rule of mixtures (2.5 marks):**
For a void-free laminate, the specific volume is the sum of constituent specific volumes:

$$
\frac{1}{\rho_{th}} = \frac{W_f}{\rho_f} + \frac{W_m}{\rho_m}
$$

$$
\Rightarrow \rho_{th} = \frac{1}{\dfrac{W_f}{\rho_f} + \dfrac{W_m}{\rho_m}}
$$

This completes the derivation. *(Allocated 1 mark for algebraic clarity and unit consistency.)*

---

**Q.A(b)** A carbon-fibre/epoxy laminate has the following properties: $W_f = 0.62$, $\rho_f = 1.80$ g/cm³, $\rho_m = 1.25$ g/cm³, $\rho_c$ (measured) $= 1.55$ g/cm³. Calculate (i) $V_f$, (ii) $\rho_{th}$, (iii) void volume fraction $V_v$, and (iv) $V_m$. State whether the part is acceptable for primary aerospace structure (criterion: $V_v < 1\%$). *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Fibre volume fraction (2 marks):**

$$
V_f = \frac{\rho_c W_f}{\rho_f} = \frac{1.55 \times 0.62}{1.80} = \frac{0.961}{1.80} = 0.534
$$

**(ii) Theoretical density (2 marks):**
$W_m = 1 - 0.62 = 0.38$.

$$
\rho_{th} = \frac{1}{\dfrac{0.62}{1.80} + \dfrac{0.38}{1.25}} = \frac{1}{0.3444 + 0.3040} = \frac{1}{0.6484} = 1.542 \text{ g/cm}^3
$$

**(iii) Void volume fraction (1.5 marks):**

$$
V_v = \frac{\rho_{th} - \rho_c}{\rho_{th}} = \frac{1.542 - 1.55}{1.542} = \frac{-0.008}{1.542} = -0.0052
$$

**Negative result** indicates the measured density is *higher* than the theoretical, an *experimental inconsistency* (likely from absorbed moisture, resin-rich surface, or measurement error). The part is *not acceptable by ASTM D2734* and the test must be repeated.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Negative $V_v$:** A negative void fraction is a *physically impossible* result. Examiners award 0 marks if you report it without comment. **Always** interpret a negative $V_v$ as an experimental or density-input error and recommend re-testing per ASTM D2734. *(Common loss: 1–2 marks.)*

**(iv) Matrix volume fraction (1.5 marks):**
Using $V_m = 1 - V_f - V_v$ and treating $V_v = 0$ for the corrected (ideal) case:

$$
V_m = 1 - 0.534 - 0 = 0.466
$$

**Aerospace acceptability:** If $V_v$ is recomputed to be within tolerance, $V_f \approx 0.53$ is acceptable. If negative, **reject**.

---

### ❖ Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative to Question A*

**Q.B(a)** Define the following terms with one-line engineering relevance: **B-stage, debulking, breather, bleeder, gel time, exotherm, $T_g$**. *(7 marks; 1 mark each)*

**Model Answer (1 mark per term):**

| # | Term | Definition (engineering relevance) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **B-stage** | Intermediate cure state of a thermoset resin in which it is tack-free at room temperature but flowable under heat — the *storage state* of prepreg. |
| 2 | **Debulking** | Application of vacuum (and sometimes mild heat) between ply groups during layup to compact the stack and remove entrapped air. |
| 3 | **Breather** | A felt-like fabric placed inside the vacuum bag to provide a uniform vacuum path and absorb excess resin. |
| 4 | **Bleeder** | A porous fabric placed outside the release film to absorb and vent excess resin during cure, controlling resin content. |
| 5 | **Gel time** | Time elapsed (at a given temperature) for a thermoset resin to reach its gel point, where viscosity rises sharply and flow ceases. |
| 6 | **Exotherm** | Self-heating of a curing resin from the exothermic cross-linking reaction — can cause thermal overshoot and matrix degradation in thick laminates. |
| 7 | **$T_g$ (Glass Transition Temperature)** | Temperature at which a cured polymer transitions from rigid glassy to rubbery state; a primary indicator of **degree of cure** and maximum service temperature. |

---

**Q.B(b)** Describe, with a neat sketch (use a labelled block diagram), the **complete cure cycle of a 3501-6 epoxy prepreg in an autoclave**, indicating ramp rates, dwell temperatures, dwell times, applied pressures, and the role of vacuum. Discuss the consequences of **incomplete cure** on $T_g$ and on mechanical properties. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Cure Cycle Description (4 marks):**

A standard 3501-6 cure cycle consists of:

1. **Vacuum application** at room temperature, held throughout the heat-up phase (to remove volatiles and trapped air). Vacuum = **−1 bar (gauge)**.
2. **First ramp:** 1.5–3 $^\circ$C/min from 22 $^\circ\text{C}$ to **80 $^\circ\text{C}$** — an intermediate dwell at 80 $^\circ\text{C}$ for 30–60 min allows resin viscosity to drop and flow to occur.
3. **Second ramp:** 1.5–3 $^\circ$C/min to **177 $^\circ\text{C}$** — the main cure temperature.
4. **Dwell at 177 $^\circ\text{C}$ for 120 min** under **autoclave pressure of 7 bar (100 psi)**.
5. **Cool-down:** 1.5–3 $^\circ$C/min, with pressure held until temperature drops below 60 $^\circ\text{C}$ to prevent porosity from trapped volatiles.

**Block diagram (text representation, 1 mark):**

$$
\text{Vacuum ON} \rightarrow \text{Ramp } (1.5-3\,^\circ\text{C/min}) \rightarrow 80\,^\circ\text{C dwell} \rightarrow \text{Ramp} \rightarrow 177\,^\circ\text{C dwell (120 min, 7 bar)} \rightarrow \text{Cool-down with P held}
$$

**Consequences of Incomplete Cure (2 marks):**
- $T_g$ is **lower** than the specified 190 $^\circ\text{C}$ (often by 10–30 $^\circ\text{C}$), measured by DSC or DMA.
- Mechanical properties degrade: **reduced interlaminar shear strength (ILSS)**, **lower flexural modulus**, **lower hot/wet strength**, and **higher creep** at service temperature.
- The part is **rejected** under most aerospace specifications (e.g., per CMH-17 or Boeing/Airbus process specs).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Cure Cycle:** Do NOT write "heat to 177 °C and hold for 2 hours." The intermediate **80 °C dwell** and the **vacuum** before pressure application are *essential* steps that examiners explicitly test. Skipping them costs 1–2 marks per omission. Also, **ramp rate must be stated in $^\circ$C/min** — not "slow heating."

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this checklist the night before the KTU exam for Module 2.**

- ✅ **Prepreg** = fibres pre-impregnated with **B-stage** resin; stored at **$-18\,^\circ\text{C}$**.
- ✅ **B-stage** = partially cured, tacky at room temp, flowable under heat.
- ✅ **C-stage** = fully cured, infusible, insoluble — final state.
- ✅ **Shelf life** = total time in freezer; **Out-life** = total time at room temp.
- ✅ **Layup** = placing plies in a defined **stacking sequence** and **ply orientation**.
- ✅ **Debulking** = vacuum compaction between ply groups; **Bagging** = sealing the stack for cure.
- ✅ **Breather** = uniform vacuum path; **Bleeder** = absorbs excess resin; **Release film** = prevents sticking.
- ✅ **Cure cycle** = ramp + dwell + cool-down, characterized by **temperature, time, pressure** profile.
- ✅ **Exotherm** = internal heat from cure reaction; can overshoot set-point in thick parts.
- ✅ **Gel point** = resin ceases to flow; **gel time** = time to reach gel point at a given $T$.
- ✅ **$T_g$** = glass transition temperature; primary metric of cure quality.
- ✅ **Volume balance:** $V_f + V_m + V_v = 1$ (KTU's most-tested identity).
- ✅ **Fibre volume fraction:** $V_f = \dfrac{\rho_c W_f}{\rho_f}$.
- ✅ **Theoretical density:** $\rho_{th} = \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{W_f}{\rho_f} + \dfrac{W_m}{\rho_m}}$.
- ✅ **Void content:** $V_v = \dfrac{\rho_{th} - \rho_c}{\rho_{th}}$.
- ✅ **Aerospace void criterion:** $V_v < 1\%$; **Aerospace $V_f$ target:** $0.55 \leq V_f \leq 0.62$.
- ✅ **Tooling materials:** aluminum, Invar, steel, carbon/epoxy; **Release agents:** wax, silicone, PTFE.
- ✅ **Autoclave pressure range:** 7–15 bar; **Vacuum level:** −1 bar (gauge) during heat-up.
- ✅ **Cure temperature for 3501-6:** $177 \pm 5\,^\circ\text{C}$ with $T_g \approx 190\,^\circ\text{C}$.
- ✅ **Negative $V_v$** in a numerical = experimental error; re-test, never report as such.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Board Tip:** For any 14-mark question on this module, **always** present the *definition* (2 marks), the *formula* (2 marks), the *derivation* (5 marks), the *substitution* (3 marks), and the *physical interpretation* (2 marks). This 2+2+5+3+2 mark-split mirrors the KTU 2024 valuation key for Module 2 numerical problems.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Special Tooling Considerations Required for Composite Manufacturing; Basic Concepts of Stiffness, Strength, Thermal and Moisture Expansion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT744 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Special Tooling Considerations Required for Composite Manufacturing; Basic Concepts of Stiffness, Strength, Thermal and Moisture Expansion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Special Tooling Considerations for Composite Manufacturing

**Formal Definition:**
Tooling in composite manufacturing refers to the molds, mandrels, fixtures, and auxiliary structures used to shape, consolidate, and cure composite laminates. Unlike metallic manufacturing, composite tooling must simultaneously address **thermal expansion mismatch**, **pressure uniformity**, **surface finish replication**, and **demoldability** because the part cures at elevated temperatures (typically **120 °C – 180 °C** for epoxy systems and **>350 °C** for polyimide/bismaleimide systems) while the tool itself undergoes thermal cycling.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Tooling is the silent hero of composite part quality. A perfectly designed laminate can be ruined by a tool that expands differently, distorts under autoclave pressure, or sticks to the part during demolding.

**Intuitive Analogy – "The Baking Cake Pan"**
Think of composite tooling exactly like a **baking cake pan**:
- The **pan shape** defines the final geometry of the cake (composite part).
- If the pan is made of a different material than what the baker expects, the cake may shrink differently and **crack** upon cooling (this is *CTE mismatch*).
- The pan must be **non-stick** (release agent) or the cake gets torn apart on removal.
- A thin aluminum pan may **buckle** under oven pressure just as a weak tool buckles under autoclave pressure.

A composite tool expands, conducts heat, resists pressure, and releases the part — all at the same time. Hence it is a "smart" tooling problem.

---

### 1.2 Basic Concepts of Stiffness, Strength, Thermal & Moisture Expansion

**Stiffness (Rigidity):**
Stiffness is the resistance of a material to elastic deformation under an applied load. It is governed primarily by the **Young's Modulus (E)** measured in **GPa** and is a function of the **fiber volume fraction (V_f)**, **fiber orientation (θ)**, and the **moduli of the constituent phases**.

**Strength:**
Strength is the maximum stress a material can sustain before failure. For composites, several strengths exist: **tensile (σ_t)**, **compressive (σ_c)**, **interlaminar shear (ILSS)**, and **in-plane shear (τ_12)**. Because composites are anisotropic, strength is also **direction-dependent**.

**Thermal Expansion (CTE):**
The **Coefficient of Thermal Expansion (α)** quantifies the fractional change in length per unit change in temperature. In composites, α differs **parallel** and **perpendicular** to the fibers because fibers (e.g., carbon) often have *negative* or near-zero CTE while the polymer matrix has a *positive* CTE.

**Moisture Expansion (CME):**
The **Coefficient of Moisture Expansion (β)** quantifies dimensional change due to absorbed moisture. Polymers absorb water (epoxy can absorb **1–3 % by weight**), and this water plasticizes the matrix and causes **swelling**, generating internal hygroscopic stresses.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition Box:**
> - **Specific Stiffness** = E / ρ (units: GPa·cm³/g) — measures structural efficiency.
> - **Specific Strength** = σ / ρ (units: MPa·cm³/g) — measures load-bearing efficiency per unit weight.
> - These are *the* reasons composites dominate aerospace: carbon/epoxy has E/ρ ≈ **10× aluminum** and σ/ρ ≈ **5× aluminum**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Special Tooling Considerations — Detailed Logic

#### A. Tooling Material Selection
The right tooling material balances **CTE, thermal conductivity, machinability, durability, and cost**.

**Common Tooling Materials for Composites:**

| Tool Material | CTE (×10⁻⁶ /°C) | Typical Use | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Aluminum (7075, 6061)** | 23 – 24 | Prototype/short-run tools | Easy to machine, high thermal conductivity | High CTE, low life |
| **Steel (P20, H13)** | 11 – 13 | Production metal tools | Durable, stable | Heavy, hard to machine |
| **Invar (Fe-36Ni)** | **1.2** | High-tolerance aerospace tools | Near-zero CTE, matches carbon/epoxy | Expensive, low conductivity |
| **Carbon Fiber/Epoxy Tool** | 0 – 2 (controlled) | Large autoclave tools | Tailorable CTE, lightweight | Skilled layup needed |
| **Castable Ceramic / Syntactic** | 4 – 8 | High-temp cures | Heat resistant | Brittle |
| **Nickel-shell (electroformed)** | 13 | Reusable female molds | Excellent surface finish | Costly, limited size |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The CTE-Matching Principle:** For high-precision autoclave-cured parts, the tool's CTE should match the part's CTE in the cure direction. **Invar** is preferred for carbon/epoxy because both have α ≈ **0–2 × 10⁻⁶/°C** in the fiber direction.

#### B. Pressure Distribution & Deflection
Under autoclave pressure (**0.3 – 0.7 MPa**), tools deflect. The deflection of a simply supported rectangular plate is:

$$
\delta = \frac{k \cdot P \cdot a^4}{E_t \cdot t^3}
$$

where **P** = pressure, **a** = span, **E_t** = tool modulus, **t** = tool thickness, **k** = geometry factor.

**Why this matters:** A tool that deflects more than the part's tolerance (often **±0.1 mm** for aerospace skins) will produce a scrapped part. **Stiffer tool → thicker backing or ribs.**

#### C. Thermal Management
- Tools must **heat uniformly** to avoid cure gradients.
- **Aluminum** heats fast (high thermal conductivity ≈ 167 W/m·K).
- **Invar** heats slowly (≈ 17 W/m·K) — this may extend cure cycle times.
- **Carbon/epoxy tools** offer the best balance: tailorable CTE and reasonable conductivity.

#### D. Surface Preparation & Release Systems
- **Release agents**: Frekote, Semiperm, or sacrificial film (Teflon).
- **Sealants**: Prevent resin from penetrating porous tool surfaces.
- **Surface finish**: Tool surface roughness (Ra) directly transfers to part surface. For aerodynamic surfaces, **Ra < 0.4 μm** is required.

#### E. Tool Geometry & Draft Angles
- Minimum **draft angle: 1° – 3°** for easy demolding.
- **Corner radii** ≥ 3× laminate thickness to avoid fiber bridging and wrinkles.

#### F. Reinforcement & Backing Structures
For large tools, **egg-crate backing structures**, **ribbed stiffeners**, or **tubular steel frames** are used to reduce deflection and weight.

---

### 2.2 Basic Concepts — Stiffness, Strength, Thermal & Moisture Expansion

#### A. Stiffness — Micromechanics Foundation

For a **unidirectional (UD) lamina** loaded along fibers (Longitudinal, **L-direction**), the **Rule of Mixtures (ROM)** applies:

$$
E_L = V_f E_f + V_m E_m
$$

For loading **perpendicular to fibers (Transverse, T-direction)**, the **Inverse Rule of Mixtures** applies (iso-strain assumption fails):

$$
\frac{1}{E_T} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **ROM gives the UPPER bound** (Voigt model). The **Inverse ROM gives the LOWER bound** (Reuss model). Real transverse modulus lies between these bounds.

A more accurate transverse modulus uses the **Halpin–Tsai equation**:

$$
E_T = E_m \left( \frac{1 + 2\eta V_f}{1 - \eta V_f} \right), \quad \eta = \frac{\left( E_f / E_m \right) - 1}{\left( E_f / E_m \right) + 2}
$$

#### B. Strength Concepts

**Longitudinal Tensile Strength (ROM):**

$$
\sigma_{L}^{ts} = V_f \sigma_{f}^{ts} + V_m \sigma_{m}^{ts}
$$

**Transverse Tensile Strength** is matrix-dominated:

$$
\sigma_{T}^{ts} \approx \sigma_{m}^{ts} \left[ 1 - \left( \sqrt{V_f} - V_f \right) \left( 1 - E_m / E_f \right) \right]
$$

#### C. Thermal Expansion — Micromechanics

**Longitudinal CTE of UD lamina (Schapery's model):**

$$
\alpha_L = \frac{E_f \alpha_f V_f + E_m \alpha_m V_m}{E_f V_f + E_m V_m}
$$

**Transverse CTE:**

$$
\alpha_T = V_f \alpha_f + V_m \alpha_m + \left[ \frac{V_f V_m (\alpha_f - \alpha_m)(E_f - E_m)}{V_f E_f + V_m E_m} \right]
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why α_L is so low:** Because stiff fibers constrain the matrix. For carbon fiber (α_f ≈ −0.5 × 10⁻⁶/°C) in epoxy (α_m ≈ 60 × 10⁻⁶/°C), the longitudinal CTE of a 60 % V_f carbon/epoxy can be **near zero** — even *negative* for high-modulus carbon. This is why carbon/epoxy tools and parts are dimensionally stable.

#### D. Moisture Expansion — Micromechanics

Analogous to thermal expansion, with moisture content **M** (fraction):

**Longitudinal CME:**

$$
