\beta_L = \frac{E_f \beta_f V_f + E_m \beta_m V_m}{E_f V_f + E_m V_m}
$$

**Transverse CME:**

$$
\beta_T = V_f \beta_f + V_m \beta_m + \left[ \frac{V_f V_m (\beta_f - \beta_m)(E_f - E_m)}{V_f E_f + V_m E_m} \right]
$$

For typical epoxy, **β_m ≈ 0.33** (i.e., 1 % moisture → 0.33 % strain).

#### E. Combined Hygrothermal Strain

Total dimensional change under temperature ΔT and moisture change ΔM:

$$
\varepsilon = \alpha \cdot \Delta T + \beta \cdot \Delta M
$$

For a multidirectional laminate, residual stresses arise because plies with different orientations want to expand differently.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_L = V_f E_f + V_m E_m$ | Longitudinal modulus (ROM) | GPa |
| 2 | $\dfrac{1}{E_T} = \dfrac{V_f}{E_f} + \dfrac{V_m}{E_m}$ | Transverse modulus (Inverse ROM) | GPa |
| 3 | $\alpha_L = \dfrac{E_f \alpha_f V_f + E_m \alpha_m V_m}{E_f V_f + E_m V_m}$ | Longitudinal CTE (Schapery) | /°C |
| 4 | $\alpha_T = V_f \alpha_f + V_m \alpha_m + \dfrac{V_f V_m (\alpha_f - \alpha_m)(E_f - E_m)}{V_f E_f + V_m E_m}$ | Transverse CTE | /°C |
| 5 | $\beta_L = \dfrac{E_f \beta_f V_f + E_m \beta_m V_m}{E_f V_f + E_m V_m}$ | Longitudinal CME | /ΔM |
| 6 | $\beta_T$ (analogous to α_T) | Transverse CME | /ΔM |
| 7 | $\varepsilon = \alpha \Delta T + \beta \Delta M$ | Total hygrothermal strain | — |
| 8 | $\sigma_{L}^{ts} = V_f \sigma_{f}^{ts} + V_m \sigma_{m}^{ts}$ | Longitudinal tensile strength (ROM) | MPa |
| 9 | $\rho_c = V_f \rho_f + V_m \rho_m$ | Density (ROM) | g/cm³ |
| 10 | $E/\rho, \; \sigma/\rho$ | Specific stiffness, specific strength | GPa·cm³/g, MPa·cm³/g |
| 11 | $\delta = \dfrac{k P a^4}{E_t t^3}$ | Tool deflection | mm |
| 12 | $\alpha_{tool} \approx \alpha_{part}$ | CTE matching principle | — |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Longitudinal Modulus by Rule of Mixtures

**Starting Assumption (Iso-strain / Voigt model):**
When load is applied parallel to the fibers in a UD lamina, both fiber and matrix experience the **same strain** ε because they are bonded together.

**Step 1 — Force equilibrium:**
The total load on the composite cross-section is the sum of loads carried by fiber and matrix:

$$
P_c = P_f + P_m
$$

**Step 2 — Convert forces to stresses (force = stress × area):**

$$
\sigma_c A_c = \sigma_f A_f + \sigma_m A_m
$$

**Step 3 — Divide both sides by the total area A_c:**

$$
\sigma_c = \sigma_f \frac{A_f}{A_c} + \sigma_m \frac{A_m}{A_c}
$$

**Step 4 — Recognize area ratios as volume fractions (for aligned UD fibers):**

$$
\frac{A_f}{A_c} = V_f, \quad \frac{A_m}{A_c} = V_m
$$

Therefore:

$$
\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m
$$

**Step 5 — Apply Hooke's law (σ = Eε) and use iso-strain (ε_c = ε_f = ε_m = ε):**

$$
E_c \varepsilon = E_f \varepsilon V_f + E_m \varepsilon V_m
$$

Cancel ε from both sides:

$$
E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{E_L = V_f E_f + V_m E_m}
$$

> This derivation is a *favourite* KTU question — be ready to state the assumption (iso-strain) and the iso-strain condition in words.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Transverse Modulus by Inverse Rule of Mixtures

**Starting Assumption (Iso-stress / Reuss model):**
When load is applied perpendicular to the fibers, both phases experience the **same stress** σ (stress continuity across the interface).

**Step 1 — Strain compatibility:**
Total transverse strain = sum of strains in each phase weighted by volume fraction:

$$
\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f V_f + \varepsilon_m V_m
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Hooke's law (ε = σ / E):**

$$
\frac{\sigma_c}{E_c} = \frac{\sigma_c}{E_f} V_f + \frac{\sigma_c}{E_m} V_m
$$

**Step 3 — Cancel σ_c (same in all phases):**

$$
\frac{1}{E_c} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{\frac{1}{E_T} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m}}
$$

> Re-arrange to get $E_T = \left( \dfrac{V_f}{E_f} + \dfrac{V_m}{E_m} \right)^{-1}$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Longitudinal CTE (Schapery Model)

**Physical idea:** A stiff fiber resists the matrix's thermal expansion. We use **thermoelastic energy bounds** to derive an expression that interpolates between Voigt and Reuss limits.

**Step 1 — Free thermal strain of each phase:**
- Fiber: $\varepsilon_f^{th} = \alpha_f \Delta T$
- Matrix: $\varepsilon_m^{th} = \alpha_m \Delta T$

**Step 2 — Composite equilibrium:** In the longitudinal direction, axial force balance (no external load) requires:

$$
\sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m = 0
$$

where σ_f and σ_m are the **internal stresses** generated by the constraint.

**Step 3 — Compatibility of strain (iso-strain in L-direction):**

$$
\varepsilon_L = \alpha_L \Delta T = \alpha_f \Delta T + \frac{\sigma_f}{E_f} = \alpha_m \Delta T + \frac{\sigma_m}{E_m}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for σ_f and σ_m:**
From the strain equality:

$$
\sigma_f = E_f (\alpha_L - \alpha_f) \Delta T
$$
$$
\sigma_m = E_m (\alpha_L - \alpha_m) \Delta T
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute into force equilibrium:**

$$
E_f V_f (\alpha_L - \alpha_f) + E_m V_m (\alpha_L - \alpha_m) = 0
$$

**Step 6 — Expand and collect α_L terms:**

$$
\alpha_L (E_f V_f + E_m V_m) = E_f V_f \alpha_f + E_m V_m \alpha_m
$$

**Step 7 — Final result:**

$$
\boxed{\alpha_L = \frac{E_f V_f \alpha_f + E_m V_m \alpha_m}{E_f V_f + E_m V_m}}
$$

> **Key insight:** α_L is a **stiffness-weighted average** of the constituent CTEs. Stiff fibers dominate the average — which is why a 60 % V_f carbon/epoxy has α_L ≈ 0.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Specific Stiffness Comparison

**Problem:** Compare the specific stiffness of (i) carbon/epoxy (V_f = 0.6), (ii) aluminum 2024-T3, and (iii) titanium Ti-6Al-4V.

**Given Data:**

| Material | E (GPa) | ρ (g/cm³) |
|---|---|---|
| AS4 Carbon Fiber | 235 | 1.80 |
| Epoxy (3501-6) | 4.2 | 1.27 |
| Aluminum 2024-T3 | 73 | 2.78 |
| Ti-6Al-4V | 114 | 4.43 |

**Step 1 — Compute composite modulus (longitudinal, ROM):**

$$
E_L = 0.6 \times 235 + 0.4 \times 4.2
$$
$$
E_L = 141 + 1.68 = 142.68 \text{ GPa}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute composite density (ROM):**

$$
\rho_c = 0.6 \times 1.80 + 0.4 \times 1.27
$$
$$
\rho_c = 1.08 + 0.508 = 1.588 \text{ g/cm}^3
$$

**Step 3 — Specific stiffness values:**

- Carbon/Epoxy: 142.68 / 1.588 ≈ **89.9 GPa·cm³/g**
- Aluminum 2024-T3: 73 / 2.78 ≈ **26.3 GPa·cm³/g**
- Ti-6Al-4V: 114 / 4.43 ≈ **25.7 GPa·cm³/g**

**Step 4 — Comparison:**
Carbon/epoxy is ≈ **3.4× stiffer per unit weight** than aluminum. This is why Boeing 787 and Airbus A350 wings are 50 % composites by weight.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Tool Material vs. Composite Part

| Tool Material | Best-Suited Composite | Cure Temp Range | CTE Match? | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Aluminum 7075 | Glass/polyester low-temp cures | < 120 °C | Poor (Δα ≈ 20) | Low |
| Invar (Fe-Ni) | Carbon/epoxy aerospace parts | 120 – 180 °C | Excellent (Δα ≈ 0–1) | High |
| Carbon/Epoxy (wet layup) | Carbon/epoxy parts (large) | 120 – 180 °C | Excellent | Medium |
| Steel H13 | High-temp BMI/polyimide | 200 – 370 °C | Fair | High |
| Castable Ceramic | Ultra-high-temp parts | > 350 °C | Fair | Medium |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Tooling System for Composite Manufacturing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Design Specification] --> B[Tool Material Selection]
    B --> C{CTE Match with Part?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Invar or CF Tool]
    C -- No --> E[Compensate via Tool Pre-Strain]
    D --> F[Tool Fabrication]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Surface Preparation]
    G --> H[Apply Release Agent]
    H --> I[Layup of Composite]
    I --> J[Vacuum Bagging]
    J --> K[Autoclave Curing]
    K --> L[Cool Down under Pressure]
    L --> M[Demolding]
    M --> N[Tool Inspection and Reuse]
    N --> O{Deflection within Tolerance?}
    O -- Yes --> P[Approved Part]
    O -- No --> Q[Tool Modification or Scrap]
```

### 4.2 Process Flow — Hygrothermal Strain Coupling

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Ambient Reference State] --> B[Temperature Change: dT]
    A --> C[Moisture Change: dM]
    B --> D[Thermal Strain: a x dT]
    C --> E[Moisture Strain: b x dM]
    D --> F[Total Hygrothermal Strain]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Residual Stress in Laminate]
    G --> H[Possible Warpage or Microcracking]
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Diagram — Anisotropic CTE in UD Lamina

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Unidirectional Carbon/Epoxy Lamina] --> B[Longitudinal Direction L]
    A --> C[Transverse Direction T]
    B --> D[alpha L: Near Zero, Constrained by Stiff Fibers]
    C --> E[alpha T: Moderate, Matrix Dominated]
    D --> F[Dimensional Stability in L]
    E --> G[Swelling/Contraction in T]
```

### 4.4 Tool Stiffness vs. Part Quality — Cause-Effect Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Tool Material Modulus Et] --> B[Deflection delta]
    B --> C[Tool Thickness t]
    C --> D[Span of Tool a]
    D --> E{Exceeds Tolerance?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Part Rework or Reject]
    E -- No --> G[Acceptable Part Geometry]
    A --> H[Reinforcement Strategy]
    H --> I[Egg-Crate Backing]
    H --> J[Ribbed Stiffeners]
    H --> K[Tubular Steel Frame]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Why is the Coefficient of Thermal Expansion (CTE) matching between tool and composite part important in autoclave curing? Mention two tooling materials commonly used for carbon/epoxy parts.*

**Model Answer:**
During autoclave curing, the tool and part are heated from ambient (~25 °C) to cure temperature (~180 °C). If their CTEs differ, the tool and part will expand by different amounts, leading to:
- **Geometric inaccuracy** in the cured part.
- **Residual stresses** locked into the laminate.
- **Wrinkapping or spring-in** of angles.
- Possible **debonding** of the part from the tool surface.

**Tooling materials for carbon/epoxy:**
1. **Invar (Fe-36 % Ni)** — CTE ≈ 1.2 × 10⁻⁶/°C, very close to carbon/epoxy α_L.
2. **Carbon Fiber/Epoxy Tool** — CTE tailorable, near zero in fiber direction, lightweight.

*(3 marks: 1.5 for explanation, 1.5 for two materials)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define specific stiffness and specific strength. Why are these properties critical for aerospace material selection?*

**Model Answer:**
- **Specific stiffness** = E / ρ. It measures stiffness per unit weight (GPa·cm³/g).
- **Specific strength** = σ_ult / ρ. It measures failure load per unit weight (MPa·cm³/g).

**Aerospace importance:**
Aircraft structures must be **lightweight** (for fuel efficiency and payload) yet **stiff** (to resist aeroelastic flutter and deflection) and **strong** (to bear flight and maneuver loads). High specific stiffness and specific strength directly translate to **lower fuel burn, higher payload, and longer range**. Carbon/epoxy offers E/ρ ≈ **3–10× that of aluminum**, making it indispensable in modern airframes.

*(3 marks: 1 for definitions, 2 for aerospace relevance)*

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]

**(a)** *Explain the special tooling considerations required for the manufacturing of high-performance composite parts. Discuss at least five key factors. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *A unidirectional glass fiber reinforced epoxy lamina has a fiber volume fraction of 0.55. The fiber Young's modulus is 72 GPa and the matrix modulus is 3.5 GPa. Calculate:*
- *(i) Longitudinal modulus E_L (2 marks)*
- *(ii) Transverse modulus E_T (2 marks)*
- *(iii) Longitudinal CTE α_L, given α_f = 5 × 10⁻⁶/°C and α_m = 60 × 10⁻⁶/°C (3 marks)*

---

**Model Solution (a) — Seven Key Tooling Considerations (7 marks):**

1. **CTE Matching:** The tool CTE should match the part CTE in the cure direction to avoid dimensional errors. Invar is used for carbon/epoxy (α ≈ 1 × 10⁻⁶/°C).
2. **Stiffness and Deflection Control:** Tools must resist autoclave pressure (0.3–0.7 MPa) without exceeding tolerance. Stiff materials and thick backings are used. $\delta = k P a^4 / (E_t t^3)$.
3. **Thermal Conductivity:** Uniform heating avoids cure gradients. Aluminum has high k (~167 W/m·K); Invar has low k (~17 W/m·K).
4. **Surface Finish:** Tool Ra transfers to part surface. For aerodynamic skins, Ra < 0.4 μm is required. Polished, sealed, and released surfaces are mandatory.
5. **Release System:** Multiple demoldings require durable release agents (Frekote, semi-permanent) and proper sealers. Sacrificial release films are used for one-shot tools.
6. **Draft Angles and Corner Radii:** Minimum 1°–3° draft and radii ≥ 3× ply thickness to allow demolding and avoid fiber wrinkling.
7. **Durability and Reusability:** Production tools (steel, Invar, CF/epoxy) must withstand 100s of cycles; prototype tools (aluminum, wood, syntactic) may be limited to tens of cycles.

---

**Model Solution (b) — Calculations (7 marks):**

**Given:** V_f = 0.55, V_m = 0.45, E_f = 72 GPa, E_m = 3.5 GPa, α_f = 5 × 10⁻⁶/°C, α_m = 60 × 10⁻⁶/°C.

**(i) Longitudinal Modulus (2 marks):**

$$
E_L = V_f E_f + V_m E_m
$$
$$
E_L = 0.55 \times 72 + 0.45 \times 3.5
$$
$$
E_L = 39.6 + 1.575 = 41.175 \text{ GPa} \approx 41.2 \text{ GPa}
$$

**(ii) Transverse Modulus (2 marks):**

$$
\frac{1}{E_T} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m}
$$
$$
\frac{1}{E_T} = \frac{0.55}{72} + \frac{0.45}{3.5}
$$
$$
\frac{1}{E_T} = 0.007639 + 0.128571 = 0.136210 \text{ GPa}^{-1}
$$
$$
E_T = \frac{1}{0.136210} \approx 7.34 \text{ GPa}
$$

**(iii) Longitudinal CTE (3 marks):**

$$
\alpha_L = \frac{E_f \alpha_f V_f + E_m \alpha_m V_m}{E_f V_f + E_m V_m}
$$
$$
\alpha_L = \frac{72 \times 5 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.55 + 3.5 \times 60 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.45}{72 \times 0.55 + 3.5 \times 0.45}
$$

Numerator:

$$
= 198 \times 10^{-6} + 94.5 \times 10^{-6} = 292.5 \times 10^{-6}
$$

Denominator:

$$
= 39.6 + 1.575 = 41.175 \text{ GPa}
$$

$$
\alpha_L = \frac{292.5 \times 10^{-6}}{41.175} \approx 7.10 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /°C}
$$

**Final answers:** E_L ≈ 41.2 GPa; E_T ≈ 7.34 GPa; α_L ≈ 7.1 × 10⁻⁶ /°C.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]

**(a)** *Discuss the basic concepts of stiffness, strength, thermal expansion, and moisture expansion in composite materials. Show how the rule of mixtures applies in each case. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *A carbon/epoxy lamina has V_f = 0.6, E_f = 230 GPa, E_m = 4 GPa, ρ_f = 1.8 g/cm³, ρ_m = 1.2 g/cm³, σ_f^ts = 4000 MPa, σ_m^ts = 60 MPa.*
- *(i) Compute specific stiffness (E_L / ρ_c) (2 marks)*
- *(ii) Compute longitudinal tensile strength (2 marks)*
- *(iii) If the lamina absorbs 1.5 % moisture and is heated by 100 °C, compute the longitudinal total strain given α_L = 0.5 × 10⁻⁶/°C and β_L = 0.1 (per unit moisture fraction). (3 marks)*

---

**Model Solution (a) — Conceptual Framework (7 marks):**

**1. Stiffness:** Resistance to elastic deformation, characterized by Young's modulus. In UD composites, **E_L (longitudinal)** is dominated by fibers and follows the **Rule of Mixtures (ROM)** because both phases experience iso-strain:

$$
E_L = V_f E_f + V_m E_m
$$

Transverse modulus **E_T** follows the **Inverse ROM** (iso-stress):

$$
\frac{1}{E_T} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m}
$$

The difference (one order of magnitude) creates strong **anisotropy**.

**2. Strength:** Maximum stress before failure. In fiber direction, strength follows the **modified ROM** dominated by fiber strength × volume fraction:

$$
\sigma_L^{ts} = V_f \sigma_f^{ts} + V_m \sigma_m^{ts}
$$

Transverse strength is matrix-dominated and much lower (~1/10 to 1/20 of longitudinal).

**3. Thermal Expansion:** CTE α differs strongly in L vs T directions. Longitudinal CTE (Schapery model) is a **stiffness-weighted average**:

$$
\alpha_L = \frac{E_f \alpha_f V_f + E_m \alpha_m V_m}{E_f V_f + E_m V_m}
$$

Because E_f >> E_m, fibers dominate and α_L becomes very small.

**4. Moisture Expansion:** Polymers absorb water; the **Coefficient of Moisture Expansion (β)** parallels thermal expansion. The longitudinal CME follows the analogous rule:

$$
\beta_L = \frac{E_f \beta_f V_f + E_m \beta_m V_m}{E_f V_f + E_m V_m}
$$

Combined effect:

$$
\varepsilon_{total} = \alpha \Delta T + \beta \Delta M
$$

These hygrothermal strains cause **residual stresses and warpage** in laminates with different ply orientations.

---

**Model Solution (b) — Numerical Computations (7 marks):**

**Given:** V_f = 0.6, V_m = 0.4, E_f = 230 GPa, E_m = 4 GPa, ρ_f = 1.8 g/cm³, ρ_m = 1.2 g/cm³, σ_f^ts = 4000 MPa, σ_m^ts = 60 MPa.

**(i) Specific Stiffness (2 marks):**

$$
E_L = 0.6 \times 230 + 0.4 \times 4 = 138 + 1.6 = 139.6 \text{ GPa}
$$

$$
\rho_c = 0.6 \times 1.8 + 0.4 \times 1.2 = 1.08 + 0.48 = 1.56 \text{ g/cm}^3
$$

$$
E_L / \rho_c = 139.6 / 1.56 \approx 89.5 \text{ GPa·cm}^3\text{/g}
$$

**(ii) Longitudinal Tensile Strength (2 marks):**

$$
\sigma_L^{ts} = V_f \sigma_f^{ts} + V_m \sigma_m^{ts}
$$
$$
\sigma_L^{ts} = 0.6 \times 4000 + 0.4 \times 60 = 2400 + 24 = 2424 \text{ MPa}
$$

**(iii) Total Longitudinal Strain (3 marks):**

$$
\varepsilon_L = \alpha_L \Delta T + \beta_L \Delta M
$$
$$
\varepsilon_L = (0.5 \times 10^{-6}) \times 100 + (0.1) \times 0.015
$$
$$
\varepsilon_L = 50 \times 10^{-6} + 1500 \times 10^{-6} = 1550 \times 10^{-6}
$$
$$
\varepsilon_L \approx 1.55 \times 10^{-3} \text{ or } 0.155\%
$$

> **Note:** The moisture contribution dominates the thermal contribution in this case (30× larger). This shows why moisture absorption in humid environments is a critical design concern for composite structures.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to use V_m = 1 − V_f** in ROM — students often leave V_m undefined. Always state V_m explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing E_L formula with E_T formula** — Longitudinal uses direct ROM, Transverse uses Inverse ROM. Mixing these = −3 marks instantly.
> 3. **In α_L derivation:** Do not write α_L = V_f α_f + V_m α_m. That is wrong for longitudinal direction. The correct expression is the **stiffness-weighted** form. Examiners *will* deduct marks for the simple (incorrect) ROM.
> 4. **Units in moisture problems:** ΔM is the *change in moisture content* (fraction, e.g., 0.015 for 1.5 %), not a percentage. Write units carefully.
> 5. **Tooling questions:** Students often list tool materials without explaining *why*. Always link the property to the manufacturing requirement (e.g., "Invar's low CTE matches carbon/epoxy's α_L → minimizes spring-in").

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Tooling materials for composites:** Aluminum (cheap, prototype), Invar (precision, aerospace), Steel (high-temp), Carbon/Epoxy (lightweight, large tools), Castable ceramic (very high-temp).
- **CTE matching** is the single most important tooling principle for autoclave-cured parts.
- **Tool stiffness** controls part geometry: $\delta \propto a^4 / (E_t t^3)$ — thickness is the most powerful lever.
- **Draft angles:** minimum 1°–3°; **corner radii:** ≥ 3× ply thickness.
- **Rule of Mixtures (ROM)** = direct weighted average; valid for iso-strain (L-direction modulus, density, longitudinal strength).
- **Inverse ROM** = reciprocal weighted average; valid for iso-stress (T-direction modulus).
- **Schapery model** for α_L and β_L: stiffness-weighted, not volume-weighted.
- **α_L is dominated by the stiffer phase.** Carbon/epoxy α_L ≈ 0 to 2 × 10⁻⁶/°C in fiber direction.
- **α_T is dominated by the matrix** because the fiber does not constrain transverse expansion.
- **Specific stiffness E/ρ** and **specific strength σ/ρ** are the metrics that make composites dominant in aerospace.
- **Hygrothermal strain:** $\varepsilon = \alpha \Delta T + \beta \Delta M$. Moisture often dominates in humid/tropical climates.
- **Halpin–Tsai** equation provides a more accurate prediction of transverse modulus than the simple Inverse ROM.
- **Strength anisotropy:** σ_L^ts >> σ_T^ts (typically 10–20× difference) because transverse failure is matrix-dominated.
- **Release agents and sealers** are mandatory; otherwise the tool is destroyed during demolding.
- **Cool-down under pressure** prevents spring-in and porosity formation.
- **Spring-in angle** of cured L-shape parts is a direct consequence of anisotropic thermal contraction — a frequent KTU question.
- **Memorize** the four ROMs: E_L, E_T, α_L, β_L (longitudinal forms are stiffness-weighted; transverse forms are volume-weighted with correction terms).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Anisotropic and Isotropic Materials; Tailored Specific Strengths
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT744 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Anisotropic and Isotropic Materials; Tailored Specific Strengths

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Isotropic Materials — The "Equal in All Directions" Class

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> An **isotropic material** is one whose mechanical, thermal, and physical properties are **independent of the direction of measurement** at a given point. The material possesses an **infinite number of symmetry planes**, and its property tensor reduces to a scalar (for first-order properties) or a 4th-order isotropic tensor (for elastic constants).

In the language of continuum mechanics, isotropy means the compliance/stiffness matrix is **invariant under any rotation** of the coordinate system. For an isotropic linear elastic solid, only **two independent elastic constants** are required:

$$E, \quad \nu \quad \text{(or equivalently } G, K, \lambda\text{)}$$

The shear modulus and Young's modulus are linked through:

$$G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$$

### Real-World Examples
- **Glass** (annealed, non-crystalline)
- **Most polycrystalline metals** at the macro-scale (steel, aluminum, copper) — because random grain orientation averages out directionality
- **Water and amorphous polymers** (e.g., atactic polystyrene above Tg)
- **Concrete** (approximately, ignoring aggregate segregation)

### Conceptual Analogy 🧠
Think of isotropic materials like a **perfectly round wooden ball**. No matter how you rotate it, look at it, or measure it — its surface texture, hardness, and color are identical from every angle. **It "doesn't care" about direction.**

---

## 1.2 Anisotropic Materials — The "Direction-Sensitive" Class

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> An **anisotropic material** exhibits **different values of a given property in different directions** at the same point. Its behavior is described by a **full property tensor** (e.g., the 4th-order elasticity tensor has **21 independent constants** in the most general case for a triclinic crystal).

For a fully anisotropic (triclinic) material, the generalized Hooke's law in 3D expands to:

$$\sigma_{ij} = C_{ijkl}\,\varepsilon_{kl} \quad (i,j,k,l = 1,2,3)$$

with $C_{ijkl}$ being a $6 \times 6$ symmetric matrix containing **21 independent elastic constants**.

### Real-World Examples
- **Wood** (stronger along the grain, weak across it)
- **Single crystals** of metals (e.g., copper, nickel)
- **Fiber-reinforced composites** — the **flagship example in PEPOT744**
- **Laminated plywood, bone, bamboo**
- **Cold-rolled steel sheets** (exhibits mild anisotropy)

### Conceptual Analogy 🌳
Imagine a **bundle of uncooked spaghetti sticks glued together**. If you pull along the length of the sticks, it is incredibly strong. If you pull across the sticks (perpendicular to the bundle), it splits apart easily. **The material "cares a lot" about which direction you load it.**

---

## 1.3 The Critical Sub-Categories of Anisotropy

These are the **most exam-relevant** classifications for a Composite Materials course:

| Sub-Class | Symmetry | Independent Elastic Constants | Typical Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Isotropic** | Infinite rotational | 2 | Glass, annealed metal |
| **Transversely Isotropic** | 1 axis of symmetry | 5 | Unidirectional (UD) fiber composite |
| **Orthotropic** | 3 mutually perpendicular planes | 9 | Cross-ply laminate, wood |
| **Fully Anisotropic (Triclinic)** | None | 21 | Generic 3D-braided composite |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transversely Isotropic** is the **most important case in PEPOT744** because a unidirectional (UD) fiber-reinforced lamina has this exact symmetry: it is **isotropic in the 1-2 plane (transverse to the fiber)** but **distinct along the fiber direction (1-axis)**.

---

## 1.4 Tailored Specific Strength — The Engineering Promise of Composites

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:**
> **Specific strength** (also called *strength-to-weight ratio*) is the **material strength divided by its density**:

$$\text{Specific Strength} = \frac{\sigma_{ult}}{\rho} \quad \left[\frac{\text{MPa}}{\text{kg/m}^3}\right] \text{ or equivalently } \left[\frac{\text{kN}\cdot\text{m}}{\text{kg}}\right]$$

**Tailored specific strength** refers to the **deliberate engineering design** of a composite's fiber type, volume fraction, orientation, and stacking sequence to **achieve an optimum specific strength** for a given loading condition.

### Why "Tailored"?
In a metal, the strength is largely **fixed by the alloy chemistry and heat treatment** — you cannot significantly change its directional behavior. In a composite, however, the designer has **continuous, continuous control**:
- Choose **carbon, glass, aramid (Kevlar), or boron** fiber
- Adjust **fiber volume fraction** $V_f$ from 0 to ~0.7
- **Orient the fibers** along principal stress directions
- **Stack plies** at chosen angles to form a laminate

This is the **central advantage** that justifies the high cost of composites in aerospace, automotive, and sporting goods.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Formalism of Anisotropy — Stiffness Tensor

The relationship between stress and strain in a linear elastic material is:

$$\sigma_i = C_{ij}\,\varepsilon_j \quad (i,j = 1,2,...,6)$$

where the **stiffness matrix** $C_{ij}$ in contracted (Voigt) notation reduces from 36 → **21 independent terms** due to symmetry ($C_{ij} = C_{ji}$).

### Reduction Under Symmetry Constraints

| Symmetry Type | Independent Constants | Stiffness Matrix Form |
|---|---|---|
| Triclinic | 21 | Fully populated |
| Monoclinic | 13 | One plane of symmetry |
| Orthotropic | **9** | Diagonal + 3 off-diagonal |
| Transversely Isotropic | **5** | 5 unique values |
| Isotropic | **2** | Only $C_{11}$ and $C_{12}$ |

For a **transversely isotropic UD composite lamina** (the textbook case), the reduced stiffness matrix in the principal material coordinate system is:

$$
[Q] = \begin{bmatrix}
Q_{11} & Q_{12} & 0 \\
Q_{12} & Q_{22} & 0 \\
0 & 0 & Q_{66}
\end{bmatrix}
$$

with the components:

$$
Q_{11} = \frac{E_1}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}}, \quad
Q_{22} = \frac{E_2}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}}, \quad
Q_{12} = \frac{\nu_{12}E_2}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}}, \quad
Q_{66} = G_{12}
$$

This **5-constant model** is what every KTU numerical problem is built upon.

---

## 2.2 Engineering Constants for a UD Lamina

The five independent engineering constants are:

| Constant | Symbol | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Longitudinal Young's Modulus | $E_1$ | Stiffness **along** the fiber |
| Transverse Young's Modulus | $E_2$ | Stiffness **perpendicular** to the fiber |
| Major Poisson's Ratio | $\nu_{12}$ | Lateral contraction when pulled along fiber |
| Minor Poisson's Ratio | $\nu_{21}$ | Reciprocal relation $\nu_{21} = \nu_{12}E_2/E_1$ |
| In-plane Shear Modulus | $G_{12}$ | Resistance to shear in the 1-2 plane |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reciprocity Theorem** (Maxwell-Betti): $\nu_{12}/E_1 = \nu_{21}/E_2$. This is **always** tested in KTU problems.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet 📋

| # | Formula | Name | Variables / Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ | **Rule of Mixtures (Modulus, longitudinal)** | $E$ in GPa, $V$ dimensionless | Loading **along** fiber |
| 2 | $\frac{1}{E_T} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m}$ | **Inverse Rule of Mixtures (Modulus, transverse)** | Same as above | Loading **across** fiber |
| 3 | $\nu_{LT} = \nu_f V_f + \nu_m V_m$ | Rule of Mixtures (Poisson's ratio) | Dimensionless | Approximate |
| 4 | $\sigma_{L}^{ult} = \sigma_{f}^{ult} V_f + \sigma_{m}^{ult} V_m$ | **Strength along fiber (Voigt)** | $\sigma$ in MPa | Tensile strength parallel to fiber |
| 5 | $\sigma_{T}^{ult} \approx \frac{\sigma_{f}^{ult} \sigma_{m}^{ult}}{V_f \sigma_{m}^{ult} + V_m \sigma_{f}^{ult}}$ | **Strength across fiber (Reuss)** | $\sigma$ in MPa | Tensile strength transverse to fiber |
| 6 | $\text{Specific Strength} = \sigma / \rho$ | Strength-to-weight ratio | MPa/(kg/m³) or kN·m/kg | Material selection |
| 7 | $\text{Specific Modulus} = E / \rho$ | Stiffness-to-weight ratio | GPa/(kg/m³) | Stiffness-critical design |
| 8 | $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$ | Density of composite | kg/m³ | Mass calculation |
| 9 | $G_{12} \approx \frac{G_f G_m}{V_f G_m + V_m G_f}$ | In-plane shear modulus | GPa | Shear design |
| 10 | $\nu_{21} = \nu_{12}\,E_2 / E_1$ | Maxwell-Betti Reciprocity | Dimensionless | Cross-check calculations |

> [!NOTE]
> **Variable Notation:** $V_f + V_m = 1$; subscript $L$ or $1$ = longitudinal; subscript $T$ or $2$ = transverse; $f$ = fiber; $m$ = matrix.

---

## 2.4 The "Why" Behind Each Anisotropy Mechanism

### Why are composites so highly anisotropic?
1. **Fiber-dominated stiffness along fiber axis:** The stiff, strong fiber carries ~95% of the longitudinal load.
2. **Matrix-dominated stiffness across fiber:** The weaker polymer matrix (or metal/ceramic) carries transverse load, so $E_2 \ll E_1$.
3. **Interface & bonding limitations:** Transverse strength is limited by fiber-matrix interfacial shear and matrix strength — not fiber strength.

### Why does this matter for engineering?
- An **aircraft wing spar** loaded in bending must have fibers aligned with the axial stress → use $E_1$, get **maximum stiffness**.
- A **rocket motor casing** under hoop stress → wind fibers in the hoop direction only.
- A **wind turbine blade** with combined bending + torsion → use multi-angle laminates (e.g., $[0/\pm 45/90]_s$).

This **direction-matched design** is the *engineered advantage* of composites.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Rule of Mixtures for Longitudinal Modulus ($E_L$)

### Setup
Consider a unidirectional composite with **continuous, aligned fibers** parallel to the loading axis (1-direction). The fiber and matrix are assumed to be **perfectly bonded** (iso-strain condition):

$$\varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m = \varepsilon_L$$

### Step 1 — Total Load Equilibration
The total force on the composite cross-section equals the sum of forces in fiber and matrix:

$$P_c = P_f + P_m$$

### Step 2 — Express Forces as Stress × Area
Since all three have the **same cross-sectional area** $A_c$ in the iso-strain model:

$$\sigma_L A_c = \sigma_f A_f + \sigma_m A_m$$

### Step 3 — Introduce Volume Fractions
Divide by $A_c$ and recognize that $A_f/A_c = V_f$, $A_m/A_c = V_m$:

$$\sigma_L = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$$

### Step 4 — Apply Hooke's Law in Each Phase
Since $\varepsilon_L = \varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m$:

$$\sigma_L = E_f \varepsilon_L V_f + E_m \varepsilon_L V_m = \varepsilon_L (E_f V_f + E_m V_m)$$

### Step 5 — Solve for Composite Modulus
Dividing both sides by $\varepsilon_L$:

$$
\boxed{E_L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m}
$$

This is the **iso-strain (Voigt) model** and is an **upper bound** (Wiener bound) on the true modulus.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Inverse Rule of Mixtures for Transverse Modulus ($E_T$)

### Setup
Now load the same composite **perpendicular to the fiber** (2-direction). The fiber and matrix are arranged in a **series** model where both experience the **same stress**:

$$\sigma_f = \sigma_m = \sigma_T$$

### Step 1 — Total Deformation Compatibility
The total elongation equals the sum of elongations of fiber and matrix layers:

$$\Delta L_c = \Delta L_f + \Delta L_m$$

### Step 2 — Convert to Strains (Same Length Scale)
Using $\Delta L = \varepsilon \cdot L$:

$$\varepsilon_T L_c = \varepsilon_f L_f + \varepsilon_m L_m$$

### Step 3 — Introduce Volume Fractions as Length Fractions
For uniform cross-section, $L_f/L_c = V_f$ and $L_m/L_c = V_m$:

$$\varepsilon_T = \varepsilon_f V_f + \varepsilon_m V_m$$

### Step 4 — Substitute Hooke's Law ($\varepsilon = \sigma / E$)
Since $\sigma$ is common:

$$\frac{\sigma_T}{E_T} = \frac{\sigma_T}{E_f} V_f + \frac{\sigma_T}{E_m} V_m$$

### Step 5 — Cancel $\sigma_T$ and Invert
$$
\boxed{\frac{1}{E_T} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m}}
$$

This is the **iso-stress (Reuss) model** and is a **lower bound** on the true modulus.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Numerical Pattern)

**Problem:** A glass/epoxy unidirectional composite has the following properties:
- Glass fibers: $E_f = 72$ GPa, $\sigma_{f}^{ult} = 2400$ MPa, $\rho_f = 2540$ kg/m³
- Epoxy matrix: $E_m = 3.5$ GPa, $\sigma_{m}^{ult} = 65$ MPa, $\rho_m = 1200$ kg/m³
- Fiber volume fraction: $V_f = 0.55$, $V_m = 0.45$

**Required:** (a) $E_L$ and $E_T$, (b) Longitudinal and transverse ultimate strength, (c) Specific strength along fiber axis, (d) Comment on anisotropy.

### (a) Modulus Calculations

**Longitudinal Modulus:**

$$
E_L = (72)(0.55) + (3.5)(0.45)
$$

$$
E_L = 39.6 + 1.575 = 41.175 \text{ GPa}
$$

**Transverse Modulus:**

$$
\frac{1}{E_T} = \frac{0.55}{72} + \frac{0.45}{3.5}
$$

$$
\frac{1}{E_T} = 0.007639 + 0.128571 = 0.136210
$$

$$
E_T = \frac{1}{0.136210} = 7.3417 \text{ GPa}
$$

**Anisotropy Ratio:**

$$
\frac{E_L}{E_T} = \frac{41.175}{7.3417} \approx 5.61
$$

> **Comment:** The composite is ~5.6× stiffer along the fiber than across it — a clear demonstration of **anisotropy**.

### (b) Strength Calculations

**Longitudinal Ultimate Strength:**

$$
\sigma_L^{ult} = (2400)(0.55) + (65)(0.45)
$$

$$
\sigma_L^{ult} = 1320 + 29.25 = 1349.25 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Transverse Ultimate Strength (Reuss Model):**

$$
\sigma_T^{ult} = \frac{(2400)(65)}{(0.55)(65) + (0.45)(2400)}
$$

$$
\sigma_T^{ult} = \frac{156000}{35.75 + 1080} = \frac{156000}{1115.75}
$$

$$
\sigma_T^{ult} \approx 139.81 \text{ MPa}
$$

> **Observation:** $\sigma_T^{ult} \ll \sigma_L^{ult}$ — the composite is **~9.6× weaker** across the fiber.

### (c) Specific Strength Along Fiber

**Composite Density:**

$$
\rho_c = (2540)(0.55) + (1200)(0.45) = 1397 + 540 = 1937 \text{ kg/m}^3
$$

**Specific Strength:**

$$
\frac{\sigma_L^{ult}}{\rho_c} = \frac{1349.25 \text{ MPa}}{1937 \text{ kg/m}^3} \approx 0.6966 \text{ MPa·m}^3/\text{kg}
$$

Equivalently, in kN·m/kg:

$$
\approx 696.6 \text{ kN·m/kg}
$$

### (d) Comparison with Aluminum (Typical KTU Insight)
- Aluminum 2024-T3: $\sigma \approx 470$ MPa, $\rho = 2780$ kg/m³ → Specific strength ≈ 169 kN·m/kg
- **This glass/epoxy UD composite is ~4× better in specific strength**, justifying its use in aerospace.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of the Density Rule of Mixtures

For a two-phase composite with no voids:

$$
\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m
$$

**Derivation Logic:** Since mass is conserved and $m = \rho V$:

$$m_c = m_f + m_m \implies \rho_c V_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$$

Dividing by $V_c$ and noting $V_i/V_c = V_i$ (volume fraction):

$$
\boxed{\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m}
$$

> This formula is **exact** (no assumptions needed other than no voids).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Material Classification Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Material Property Symmetry] --> B{Isotropic?}
    B -- YES --> C[Isotropic Material]
    C --> C1[2 independent constants]
    C --> C2[Example: Glass, Polycrystalline Steel]
    C --> C3[Q matrix: scalar + isotropic tensor]
    
    B -- NO --> D[Anisotropic Material]
    D --> E{Symmetry planes?}
    
    E -- 1 axis of symmetry --> F[Transversely Isotropic]
    F --> F1[5 independent constants]
    F --> F2[Example: UD Fiber Composite]
    F --> F3[Strong along fiber, equal in transverse plane]
    
    E -- 3 perpendicular planes --> G[Orthotropic]
    G --> G1[9 independent constants]
    G --> G2[Example: Cross-ply Laminate, Wood]
    G --> G3[Different in 3 directions]
    
    E -- None general --> H[Fully Anisotropic Triclinic]
    H --> H1[21 independent constants]
    H --> H2[Example: 3D Braided Composite]
```

## 4.2 Lamina Loading Directions — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph L["Unidirectional Lamina Coordinate System"]
        direction LR
        AX1[Axis 1: Longitudinal - Along Fiber]
        AX2[Axis 2: Transverse - Perpendicular to Fiber]
        AX3[Axis 3: Through-Thickness]
    end
    
    subgraph P["Property Behavior"]
        direction TB
        P1[E1 = Ef Vf + Em Vm - HIGH]
        P2[E2 via Inverse Rule - LOW]
        P3[nu12 from ROM - MODERATE]
        P4[G12 via Reuss Model]
    end
    
    subgraph APP["Engineering Application"]
        direction TB
        A1[Align fiber with max principal stress]
        A2[Use multi-angle laminate for combined loads]
        A3[Specific strength up to 10x metals]
    end
    
    AX1 --> P1
    AX2 --> P2
    AX1 --> P3
    AX2 --> P4
    P1 --> A1
    P2 --> A2
    P1 --> A3
```

## 4.3 Composite Design Philosophy — Tailoring Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Identify Service Loads] --> S2[Determine Principal Stress Directions]
    S2 --> S3[Choose Fiber Type Carbon Glass Aramid]
    S3 --> S4[Choose Matrix Epoxy PEEK Metal Ceramic]
    S4 --> S5[Decide Volume Fraction Vf]
    S5 --> S6[Decide Fiber Orientation and Stacking Sequence]
    S6 --> S7[Compute E1 E2 sigma_ult rho_c]
    S7 --> S8{Meet Specific Strength Target?}
    S8 -- NO --> S3
    S8 -- YES --> S9[Final Composite Tailored Design]
```

## 4.4 Comparative Performance Chart (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MAT["Specific Strength Comparison kN m per kg"]
        direction TB
        M1[Steel: ~50 to 80]
        M2[Aluminum: ~150 to 200]
        M3[Titanium: ~250 to 300]
        M4[Glass Epoxy UD: ~600 to 700]
        M5[Carbon Epoxy UD: ~1500 to 2500]
    end
    
    M1 --> M2 --> M3 --> M4 --> M5
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight from the Chart:** Carbon-epoxy UD composites can be **~25×** better in specific strength than structural steel. This single fact drives their use in Boeing 787, Airbus A350, F1 cars, and spacecraft.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

---

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**"Differentiate between isotropic and anisotropic materials with two examples each."**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Isotropic materials** possess identical properties in all directions. They require only **two independent elastic constants** ($E$, $\nu$). Examples: glass, annealed copper.
>
> **Anisotropic materials** exhibit direction-dependent properties. They require up to **21 independent elastic constants** in the general case. Examples: wood, unidirectional fiber-reinforced composites.
>
> Composites are typically **transversely isotropic** (5 constants) or **orthotropic** (9 constants), making their design more complex but offering **tailored directional performance**.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**"Define specific strength. Why is it a critical parameter in aerospace composite design?"**

**Model Answer:**

> Specific strength is the **ratio of ultimate strength to density** ($\sigma_{ult}/\rho$), measured in **kN·m/kg** or **MPa/(kg/m³)**.
>
> It is critical in aerospace because **fuel efficiency and payload capacity** depend directly on minimizing structural mass. A higher specific strength means **more strength per kilogram of material**, allowing lighter airframes, longer range, and lower operating costs. Carbon-epoxy composites with specific strength ~2000 kN·m/kg vastly outperform aluminum alloys (~170 kN·m/kg).

---

## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **Always show units** in every step; missing units can cost 1–2 marks.
> - State assumptions explicitly (iso-strain for $E_L$, iso-stress for $E_T$).
> - When asked for "comment on anisotropy", **compute the ratio $E_L/E_T$ explicitly** — vague comments lose marks.
> - In transverse strength, mention that the Reuss model gives a **lower-bound estimate**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a)** A unidirectional glass-fiber reinforced epoxy composite contains **55% by volume of fibers**. Given:
- Glass fiber: $E_f = 72$ GPa, $\sigma_{f}^{ult} = 2400$ MPa, $\rho_f = 2540$ kg/m³
- Epoxy matrix: $E_m = 3.5$ GPa, $\sigma_{m}^{ult} = 65$ MPa, $\rho_m = 1200$ kg/m³

Compute:
- (i) Longitudinal Young's modulus $E_L$
- (ii) Transverse Young's modulus $E_T$
- (iii) Longitudinal ultimate tensile strength
- (iv) Density and specific strength of the composite

**(b)** Compare the specific strength of this composite with that of structural steel ($\sigma = 450$ MPa, $\rho = 7850$ kg/m³). Comment on the suitability of the composite for aircraft structural components.

#### Model Solution

**(a)(i) Longitudinal Young's Modulus** (Rule of Mixtures, Voigt model):

$$
E_L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m = (72)(0.55) + (3.5)(0.45)
$$

$$
E_L = 39.6 + 1.575 = \mathbf{41.175 \text{ GPa}}
$$

**(a)(ii) Transverse Young's Modulus** (Inverse Rule of Mixtures, Reuss model):

$$
\frac{1}{E_T} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m} = \frac{0.55}{72} + \frac{0.45}{3.5}
$$

$$
\frac{1}{E_T} = 0.007639 + 0.128571 = 0.136210
$$

$$
E_T = \mathbf{7.3417 \text{ GPa}}
$$

**(a)(iii) Longitudinal Ultimate Tensile Strength:**

$$
\sigma_L^{ult} = \sigma_f^{ult} V_f + \sigma_m^{ult} V_m = (2400)(0.55) + (65)(0.45)
$$

$$
\sigma_L^{ult} = 1320 + 29.25 = \mathbf{1349.25 \text{ MPa}}
$$

**(a)(iv) Density and Specific Strength:**

$$
\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m = (2540)(0.55) + (1200)(0.45)
$$

$$
\rho_c = 1397 + 540 = \mathbf{1937 \text{ kg/m}^3}
$$

$$
\text{Specific Strength} = \frac{\sigma_L^{ult}}{\rho_c} = \frac{1349.25}{1937} \approx \mathbf{0.6966 \text{ MPa·m}^3/\text{kg}} \approx 696.6 \text{ kN·m/kg}
$$

**(b) Comparison with Structural Steel:**

$$
\text{Steel Specific Strength} = \frac{450}{7850} \approx 0.0573 \text{ MPa·m}^3/\text{kg} \approx 57.3 \text{ kN·m/kg}
$$

$$
\text{Performance Ratio} = \frac{696.6}{57.3} \approx \mathbf{12.16}
$$

**Comment:** The glass-epoxy composite offers **~12× higher specific strength** than structural steel. This makes it an excellent candidate for **aircraft fuselage skin, wing skins, and helicopter rotor blades** where weight reduction is paramount. However, the composite is **highly anisotropic** ($E_L/E_T \approx 5.6$), so fiber orientation must be carefully tailored to the principal stress directions during design.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 — Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the difference between **isotropic, transversely isotropic, and orthotropic** materials. State the number of independent elastic constants in each case.

**(b)** A unidirectional carbon-fiber/epoxy composite has $V_f = 0.60$. Given:
- Carbon fiber: $E_f = 230$ GPa, $\sigma_{f}^{ult} = 4000$ MPa, $\rho_f = 1750$ kg/m³
- Epoxy: $E_m = 3.0$ GPa, $\sigma_{m}^{ult} = 55$ MPa, $\rho_m = 1250$ kg/m³

Calculate:
- (i) $E_L$ and $E_T$
- (ii) Specific modulus along and across the fiber
- (iii) The **anisotropy index** $E_L/E_T$ and comment on the result.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Classification of Materials by Symmetry:**

> **Isotropic Material:** Properties identical in **all directions**. Infinite planes of symmetry. **2 independent elastic constants** ($E$ and $\nu$).
> *Examples:* Glass, polycrystalline aluminum (at macro scale).

> **Transversely Isotropic Material:** One axis of symmetry (the fiber direction). Isotropic in the plane perpendicular to that axis. **5 independent elastic constants** ($E_1, E_2, G_{12}, \nu_{12}, \nu_{23}$).
> *Examples:* Unidirectional fiber-reinforced lamina, single-crystal hexagonal close-packed metals.

> **Orthotropic Material:** **Three mutually perpendicular planes** of symmetry. **9 independent elastic constants**.
> *Examples:* Cross-ply laminate, wood, rolled metal sheets.

**Schematic:** *(Mermaid block diagram omitted for brevity, but a sketch shows a circle = isotropic, a cylinder = transversely isotropic, a rectangular block with 3 distinct axes = orthotropic.)*

**(b)(i) Longitudinal and Transverse Modulus:**

$$
E_L = (230)(0.60) + (3.0)(0.40) = 138 + 1.2 = \mathbf{139.2 \text{ GPa}}
$$

$$
\frac{1}{E_T} = \frac{0.60}{230} + \frac{0.40}{3.0} = 0.002609 + 0.133333 = 0.135942
$$

$$
E_T = \frac{1}{0.135942} = \mathbf{7.355 \text{ GPa}}
$$

**(b)(ii) Specific Modulus:**

$$
\rho_c = (1750)(0.60) + (1250)(0.40) = 1050 + 500 = 1550 \text{ kg/m}^3
$$

**Along fiber:**

$$
\frac{E_L}{\rho_c} = \frac{139.2 \text{ GPa}}{1550 \text{ kg/m}^3} = \mathbf{0.0898 \text{ GPa·m}^3/\text{kg}} = 89.8 \text{ MN·m/kg}
$$

**Across fiber:**

$$
\frac{E_T}{\rho_c} = \frac{7.355}{1550} = \mathbf{0.004745 \text{ GPa·m}^3/\text{kg}} = 4.745 \text{ MN·m/kg}
$$

**(b)(iii) Anisotropy Index:**

$$
\frac{E_L}{E_T} = \frac{139.2}{7.355} \approx \mathbf{18.93}
$$

**Comment:** The anisotropy index of **~19** is very high, confirming that the carbon-fiber composite is **extremely stiff along the fiber direction** but **comparatively compliant transversely**. This is a hallmark of high-performance UD composites and justifies the need for **multi-axial layups** in real structures to balance stiffness in multiple directions. For comparison, typical aluminum has an index of **1** (perfectly isotropic).

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER ✅

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist for KTU Exam**

- **Isotropic → 2 constants; Transversely Isotropic → 5; Orthotropic → 9; Fully Anisotropic (Triclinic) → 21.**
- A **UD fiber-reinforced lamina is transversely isotropic** — isotropic in 1-2 plane, distinct along 1-axis.
- **Rule of Mixtures (Longitudinal):** $E_L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ — assumes **iso-strain** (Voigt, upper bound).
- **Inverse Rule of Mixtures (Transverse):** $1/E_T = V_f/E_f + V_m/E_m$ — assumes **iso-stress** (Reuss, lower bound).
- **Longitudinal strength:** $\sigma_L^{ult} = \sigma_f^{ult} V_f + \sigma_m^{ult} V_m$.
- **Transverse strength:** $\sigma_T^{ult} = \sigma_f^{ult} \sigma_m^{ult} / (V_f \sigma_m^{ult} + V_m \sigma_f^{ult})$.
- **Composite density:** $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$ (exact, no assumptions).
- **Specific strength** = $\sigma_{ult}/\rho$ — units: **kN·m/kg** is the KTU-preferred form.
- **Specific modulus** = $E/\rho$ — units: **MN·m/kg** or **GPa·m³/kg**.
- **Maxwell-Betti Reciprocity:** $\nu_{12}/E_1 = \nu_{21}/E_2 \Rightarrow \nu_{21} = \nu_{12} E_2 / E_1$ (must always be checked).
- **Tailoring** = selecting fiber type, $V_f$, orientation, and stacking to match the **principal stress field** — the unique design freedom of composites.
- **Anisotropy ratio** $E_L/E_T$ is a **key indicator** of how "directional" the composite is.
- **Carbon-epoxy UD** has specific strength up to **~2000 kN·m/kg**, vs. steel at ~50–80 kN·m/kg.
- Always state **assumptions** in derivations (iso-strain, iso-stress, perfect bonding, no voids).
- **KTU-favorite trap:** Students forget to **convert units** (e.g., GPa → MPa) — units are graded.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Cause of Discontinuous Stresses in Composites and How It Differs from Metals
Laminate Theory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT744 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Laminate Theory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Laminate

A **laminate** in the context of fiber-reinforced composites is a structural element made by **stacking and bonding two or more individual layers (plies or laminae) of unidirectional or woven fiber-reinforced composite material** at specific fiber orientations to achieve a desired set of mechanical, thermal, or hygroscopic properties. The assemblage of plies is generally referred to as a **laminated composite** or **laminated plate**, and the mathematical framework that predicts its macroscopic elastic response is known as the **Classical Lamination Theory (CLT)**.

Mathematically, the laminate is bounded between two parallel surfaces at a distance $h$ apart, with the **mid-plane** at $z=0$. Each ply $k$ occupies the region $z_{k-1} \le z \le z_k$, where the total thickness is $h = z_n - z_0$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** A *lamina* = a single ply (one fiber orientation). A *laminate* = a stack of laminae bonded together. The term "composite" in Module 3 of PEPOT744 almost always refers to laminated composites unless stated otherwise.

## 1.2 Classification of Laminates (KTU Taxonomy)

Laminates are classified based on the **stacking sequence** of the plies. The notation $[0°/45°/90°/-45°]_S$ denotes a 4-ply laminate with the subscript $S$ indicating symmetry about the mid-plane.

| **Category** | **Definition** | **Key Property** |
|---|---|---|
| **Unidirectional (UD)** | All plies have the same fiber orientation, e.g., $[0]_n$ | Highly anisotropic; highest stiffness along fibers |
| **Cross-Ply** | Only $0°$ and $90°$ plies, e.g., $[0/90]_n$ | $[B] \neq 0$ in general; $A_{16}=A_{26}=D_{16}=D_{26}=0$ |
| **Angle-Ply** | Only $+\theta$ and $-\theta$ plies, e.g., $[45/-45]_n$ | Symmetric if $\theta$ pairs balance; $[A_{16},A_{26}]\neq 0$ |
| **Symmetric** | Stacking sequence mirrored about mid-plane, $[.../_S]$ | **Bending–Extension Coupling Matrix $[B]=0$** |
| **Antisymmetric** | Plies above/below mid-plane have equal/opposite angles | Twist–extension coupling is non-zero |
| **Quasi-Isotropic** | Stiffness identical in all in-plane directions | $A_{11}=A_{22}$, $\nu_{xy}=\nu_{yx}$ |
| **Balanced** | Equal number of $+\theta$ and $-\theta$ plies | $A_{16}=A_{26}=0$ |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Plywood Sandwich"**
>
> Imagine a multi-layered **sandwich cake** where each layer has a different grain pattern in its ingredients. A **laminate** works the same way: imagine you are stacking sheets of stiff, fiber-reinforced "paper" (each sheet = a *lamina*) with the wood-grain (fibers) pointing in **different directions** in each sheet. When you glue them together under heat and pressure, the resulting "cake" is far stiffer and stronger in *all* directions than any single layer could ever be.
>
> **Why do we change the fiber angle in each ply?** Because a single ply is very strong along its fibers but weak perpendicular to them. By cleverly orienting the weak side of one ply against the strong side of another, the laminate as a whole behaves **quasi-isotropically** — a hallmark of engineered composites used in aircraft wings (Boeing 787, Airbus A350), racing yacht hulls, and wind-turbine blades.

**Key Physical Constants and Metrics used in this Module:**

- **Ply thickness:** typically $t_k = 0.125\,\text{mm}$ to $0.25\,\text{mm}$ (aerospace standard).
- **Number of plies:** $n$ (commonly 4, 8, 16).
- **Total laminate thickness:** $h = \sum_{k=1}^{n} t_k$.
- **Reference plane:** the laminate **mid-plane** $(z = 0)$.
- **Coordinate axes:** $x$, $y$ in the plane of the laminate; $z$ through the thickness.

## 1.4 Fundamental Assumptions of Classical Lamination Theory (CLT)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Five Pillars of Classical Lamination Theory:**
>
> 1. **Kirchhoff–Love Hypothesis (Straight Normal):** Lines normal to the mid-plane remain **straight, unextended, and normal** to the deformed mid-plane. (Equivalent to thin-plate theory.)
> 2. **Plane Stress State:** $\sigma_z = \tau_{xz} = \tau_{yz} = 0$ in each ply.
> 3. **Strain Continuity:** No slip occurs at ply interfaces; strains are continuous across $z = z_k$.
> 4. **Linear Elastic Behavior:** Each ply is governed by Hooke's law $\sigma = Q\varepsilon$.
> 5. **Perfect Bonding:** Ply interfaces cannot slip or delaminate under load.

These assumptions reduce the 3D elasticity problem to a **2D equivalent single-layer (ESL) theory**, dramatically simplifying the design of laminates.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Displacement Field (Kirchhoff–Love Kinematics)

Let $\{u_0, v_0, w_0\}$ be the mid-plane displacements. The full displacement field at height $z$ above the mid-plane is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
u(x,y,z) &= u_0(x,y) - z\,\frac{\partial w_0}{\partial x} \\
v(x,y,z) &= v_0(x,y) - z\,\frac{\partial w_0}{\partial y} \\
w(x,y,z) &= w_0(x,y)
\end{aligned}
$$

The corresponding **strain field** is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_x &= \varepsilon_x^{\,0} + z\,\kappa_x \\
\varepsilon_y &= \varepsilon_y^{\,0} + z\,\kappa_y \\
\gamma_{xy} &= \gamma_{xy}^{\,0} + z\,\kappa_{xy}
\end{aligned}
$$

where:
- $\varepsilon_x^{\,0} = \dfrac{\partial u_0}{\partial x}$, $\varepsilon_y^{\,0} = \dfrac{\partial v_0}{\partial y}$, $\gamma_{xy}^{\,0} = \dfrac{\partial u_0}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial v_0}{\partial x}$ are the **mid-plane strains**.
- $\kappa_x = -\dfrac{\partial^{2} w_0}{\partial x^{2}}$, $\kappa_y = -\dfrac{\partial^{2} w_0}{\partial y^{2}}$, $\kappa_{xy} = -2\dfrac{\partial^{2} w_0}{\partial x \partial y}$ are the **mid-plane curvatures** (twist).

In compact matrix form:

$$
\begin{Bmatrix} \varepsilon_x \\ \varepsilon_y \\ \gamma_{xy} \end{Bmatrix} = 
\begin{Bmatrix} \varepsilon_x^{\,0} \\ \varepsilon_y^{\,0} \\ \gamma_{xy}^{\,0} \end{Bmatrix} + z
\begin{Bmatrix} \kappa_x \\ \kappa_y \\ \kappa_{xy} \end{Bmatrix}
$$

Or simply $\{\varepsilon\} = \{\varepsilon^{0}\} + z\,\{\kappa\}$.

## 2.2 From Ply Stresses to Resultant Forces and Moments

The in-plane stress in the $k$-th ply (in laminate coordinates) is $\{\sigma\}_k = [\bar{Q}]_k\,\{\varepsilon\}_k$, where $[\bar{Q}]_k$ is the **off-axis (transformed) reduced stiffness matrix** of ply $k$.

The **force resultants per unit length** $\{N\}$ and **moment resultants per unit length** $\{M\}$ are obtained by integrating the stresses through the thickness:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\{N\} &= \int_{-h/2}^{+h/2} \{\sigma\}\,dz 
     = \int_{-h/2}^{+h/2} [\bar{Q}]\,\{\varepsilon\}\,dz \\
\{M\} &= \int_{-h/2}^{+h/2} \{\sigma\}\,z\,dz 
     = \int_{-h/2}^{+h/2} [\bar{Q}]\,\{\varepsilon\}\,z\,dz
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting $\{\varepsilon\} = \{\varepsilon^{0}\} + z\{\kappa\}$ and distributing the integrals:

$$
\begin{Bmatrix} N \\ M \end{Bmatrix} = 
\begin{bmatrix} A & B \\ B & D \end{bmatrix}
\begin{Bmatrix} \varepsilon^{0} \\ \kappa \end{Bmatrix}
$$

This is the **constitutive equation of a laminate** — the most important equation in lamination theory.

## 2.3 The ABD Matrix — High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definitions of A, B, D Sub-Matrices:**

$$
[A] = \int_{-h/2}^{+h/2} [\bar{Q}]\,dz = \sum_{k=1}^{n} [\bar{Q}]_k\,(z_k - z_{k-1})
$$

$$
[B] = \int_{-h/2}^{+h/2} [\bar{Q}]\,z\,dz = \tfrac{1}{2}\sum_{k=1}^{n} [\bar{Q}]_k\,(z_k^{2} - z_{k-1}^{2})
$$

$$
[D] = \int_{-h/2}^{+h/2} [\bar{Q}]\,z^{2}\,dz = \tfrac{1}{3}\sum_{k=1}^{n} [\bar{Q}]_k\,(z_k^{3} - z_{k-1}^{3})
$$

| **Matrix** | **Name** | **Physical Meaning** | **Units (SI)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $[A]_{3\times 3}$ | Extensional Stiffness | In-plane force $\to$ in-plane strain | N/m |
| $[B]_{3\times 3}$ | Coupling Stiffness | Couples bending $\leftrightarrow$ extension | N |
| $[D]_{3\times 3}$ | Bending Stiffness | Bending moment $\to$ curvature | N·m |
| $[A^*],\,[B^*],\,[D^*]$ | Reduced Compliances | Inverse of ABD (used to get strains from loads) | m/N, 1/N, 1/(N·m) |

### Coupling Phenomena Encoded by ABD

- If $[B]\neq 0$ → applying an in-plane force $\{N\}$ causes **bending (curvature)** and applying a moment $\{M\}$ causes **extension**. This is the **bending–extension coupling**.
- If $[A_{16},A_{26}]\neq 0$ → applying a normal force $N_x$ produces **shear strain $\gamma_{xy}$** and vice versa. This is the **shear–extension coupling**.
- If $[D_{16},D_{26}]\neq 0$ → applying a bending moment produces **twist** of the laminate. This is the **bending–twist coupling**.

## 2.4 Special Cases (Highly Testable!)

| **Laminate Type** | **Stacking Condition** | **Resulting Property** |
|---|---|---|
| **Symmetric** | $z_k + z_{n+1-k} = 0$ | $[B] = [0]$ |
| **Cross-Ply** | $\theta = 0°$ or $90°$ only | $A_{16}=A_{26}=D_{16}=D_{26}=B_{16}=B_{26}=0$ |
| **Angle-Ply (Balanced)** | Equal $+\theta$ and $-\theta$ | $A_{16}=A_{26}=D_{16}=D_{26}=0$ |
| **Antisymmetric** | $[B]\neq 0$, but no twist-extension coupling | $A_{16}=A_{26}=0$ but $[B]$ fully populated |
| **Quasi-Isotropic** | $\geq 3$ angles, e.g., $[0/60/-60]$ | $A_{11}=A_{22}$, $2A_{66}=A_{11}-A_{12}$ |

## 2.5 Effective Engineering Properties of a Laminate

The effective (laminate-level) elastic constants are obtained by **inverting the ABD matrix** and then extracting the relevant components of the reduced matrices. For a laminate where the in-plane behavior is decoupled from bending (i.e., $[B]=0$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_x^{\text{eff}} &= \frac{A_{11}A_{22} - A_{12}^{2}}{h\,A_{22}} \\
E_y^{\text{eff}} &= \frac{A_{11}A_{22} - A_{12}^{2}}{h\,A_{11}} \\
G_{xy}^{\text{eff}} &= \frac{A_{66}}{h} \\
\nu_{xy}^{\text{eff}} &= \frac{A_{12}}{A_{22}} \\
\nu_{yx}^{\text{eff}} &= \frac{A_{12}}{A_{11}}
\end{aligned}
$$

The **flexural (bending) rigidities** are similarly extracted from the $[D]$ matrix using analogous formulas, with $D_{ij}$ replacing $A_{ij}$ and a factor of 12 appearing in the denominator (since $D$ integrates $z^{2}$).

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The ABD framework is the **backbone of all composite structural design** in the industry. It is embedded inside commercial FEM codes such as **ANSYS Composite PrepPost (ACP)**, **Abaqus/Composite**, **HyperSizer**, and **ESAComp**. Aircraft manufacturers (Airbus, Boeing, Lockheed Martin) use ABD matrices to certify wing skins, fuselage barrels, and tail sections. Without CLT, the modern aerospace industry — where composite content now exceeds 50% by weight in the Boeing 787 and Airbus A350 — could not exist.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Ply-Level to Laminate-Level Constitutive Equation

We start from the **strain distribution** in any ply (assumed linear through the thickness due to Kirchhoff–Love):

$$
\{\varepsilon(x,y,z)\} = \{\varepsilon^{0}(x,y)\} + z\,\{\kappa(x,y)\}
$$

The stress in the $k$-th ply, expressed in laminate coordinates $(x,y)$, is governed by the **transformed reduced stiffness matrix** $[\bar{Q}]_k$:

$$
\{\sigma\}_k = [\bar{Q}]_k\,(\{\varepsilon^{0}\} + z\,\{\kappa\})
$$

**Force Resultants $\{N\}$ —** integrate stress over the full thickness $h$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\{N\} &= \int_{-h/2}^{+h/2} \{\sigma\}\,dz \\
      &= \int_{-h/2}^{+h/2} [\bar{Q}]\,(\{\varepsilon^{0}\} + z\,\{\kappa\})\,dz
\end{aligned}
$$

Splitting the integral (since $\{\varepsilon^{0}\}$ and $\{\kappa\}$ are independent of $z$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\{N\} &= \left[\int_{-h/2}^{+h/2} [\bar{Q}]\,dz\right]\{\varepsilon^{0}\} 
       + \left[\int_{-h/2}^{+h/2} [\bar{Q}]\,z\,dz\right]\{\kappa\} \\
      &\equiv [A]\,\{\varepsilon^{0}\} + [B]\,\{\kappa\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Moment Resultants $\{M\}$ —** integrate the first moment of stress:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\{M\} &= \int_{-h/2}^{+h/2} \{\sigma\}\,z\,dz \\
      &= \int_{-h/2}^{+h/2} [\bar{Q}]\,(\{\varepsilon^{0}\} + z\,\{\kappa\})\,z\,dz \\
      &= \left[\int_{-h/2}^{+h/2} [\bar{Q}]\,z\,dz\right]\{\varepsilon^{0}\} 
       + \left[\int_{-h/2}^{+h/2} [\bar{Q}]\,z^{2}\,dz\right]\{\kappa\} \\
      &\equiv [B]\,\{\varepsilon^{0}\} + [D]\,\{\kappa\}
\end{aligned}
$$

Stacking both:

$$
\boxed{
\begin{Bmatrix} N_x \\ N_y \\ N_{xy} \end{Bmatrix} =
\begin{bmatrix} A_{11} & A_{12} & A_{16} \\ A_{12} & A_{22} & A_{26} \\ A_{16} & A_{26} & A_{66} \end{bmatrix}
\begin{Bmatrix} \varepsilon_x^{0} \\ \varepsilon_y^{0} \\ \gamma_{xy}^{0} \end{Bmatrix} +
\begin{bmatrix} B_{11} & B_{12} & B_{16} \\ B_{12} & B_{22} & B_{26} \\ B_{16} & B_{26} & B_{66} \end{bmatrix}
\begin{Bmatrix} \kappa_x \\ \kappa_y \\ \kappa_{xy} \end{Bmatrix}
}
$$

$$
\boxed{
\begin{Bmatrix} M_x \\ M_y \\ M_{xy} \end{Bmatrix} =
\begin{bmatrix} B_{11} & B_{12} & B_{16} \\ B_{12} & B_{22} & B_{26} \\ B_{16} & B_{26} & B_{66} \end{bmatrix}
\begin{Bmatrix} \varepsilon_x^{0} \\ \varepsilon_y^{0} \\ \gamma_{xy}^{0} \end{Bmatrix} +
\begin{bmatrix} D_{11} & D_{12} & D_{16} \\ D_{12} & D_{22} & D_{26} \\ D_{16} & D_{26} & D_{66} \end{bmatrix}
\begin{Bmatrix} \kappa_x \\ \kappa_y \\ \kappa_{xy} \end{Bmatrix}
}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Discretized (Ply-by-Ply) ABD Formulas

Since $[\bar{Q}]_k$ is constant inside each ply, the integrals can be replaced by **finite sums** over $n$ plies, with $z_k$ being the $z$-coordinate of the **top** of ply $k$ and $z_{k-1}$ being the $z$-coordinate of the **bottom** of ply $k$.

$$
[A] = \sum_{k=1}^{n} [\bar{Q}]_k\,(z_k - z_{k-1})
$$

$$
[B] = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{k=1}^{n} [\bar{Q}]_k\,(z_k^{2} - z_{k-1}^{2})
$$

$$
[D] = \frac{1}{3}\sum_{k=1}^{n} [\bar{Q}]_k\,(z_k^{3} - z_{k-1}^{3})
$$

### Worked Example: 3-Ply Cross-Ply Laminate $[0°/90°/0°]$

Given: each ply has thickness $t = 0.2\,\text{mm}$; $E_1 = 140\,\text{GPa}$, $E_2 = 10\,\text{GPa}$, $G_{12} = 5\,\text{GPa}$, $\nu_{12} = 0.3$.

**Step 1: Reduced stiffnesses in material axes (for each UD ply):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{11} &= \frac{E_1}{1-\nu_{12}\nu_{21}} = \frac{140}{1-0.3 \times 0.0214} \approx 140.91\;\text{GPa} \\
Q_{22} &= \frac{E_2}{1-\nu_{12}\nu_{21}} \approx 10.06\;\text{GPa} \\
Q_{12} &= \frac{\nu_{12}E_2}{1-\nu_{12}\nu_{21}} \approx 3.02\;\text{GPa} \\
Q_{66} &= G_{12} = 5\;\text{GPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

with $\nu_{21} = \nu_{12}\,E_2/E_1 = 0.3 \times 10/140 = 0.0214$.

**Step 2: Transformed stiffnesses for the 0° and 90° plies.**

For a $0°$ ply: $[\bar{Q}] = [Q]$ directly (no rotation).

$$
[\bar{Q}]_{0°} = 
\begin{bmatrix} 140.91 & 3.02 & 0 \\ 3.02 & 10.06 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 5 \end{bmatrix}\;\text{GPa}
$$

For a $90°$ ply: $Q_{11}$ and $Q_{22}$ are interchanged.

$$
[\bar{Q}]_{90°} = 
\begin{bmatrix} 10.06 & 3.02 & 0 \\ 3.02 & 140.91 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 5 \end{bmatrix}\;\text{GPa}
$$

**Step 3: Geometry — locate each interface.**

Total thickness $h = 3t = 0.6\,\text{mm} = 0.6 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}$. With mid-plane at $z=0$:

- Ply 1 ($0°$): $z_0 = -0.3\,\text{mm}$, $z_1 = -0.1\,\text{mm}$
- Ply 2 ($90°$): $z_1 = -0.1\,\text{mm}$, $z_2 = +0.1\,\text{mm}$
- Ply 3 ($0°$): $z_2 = +0.1\,\text{mm}$, $z_3 = +0.3\,\text{mm}$

**Step 4: Compute $[A]$ — sum over all plies:**

$$
[A] = [\bar{Q}]_{0°}\,(z_1-z_0) + [\bar{Q}]_{90°}\,(z_2-z_1) + [\bar{Q}]_{0°}\,(z_3-z_2)
$$

Each thickness is $0.2\,\text{mm} = 0.2 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}$. So:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{11} &= 0.2 \times 10^{-3}\bigl[140.91 + 10.06 + 140.91\bigr]\times 10^{9} \\
      &= 58.38 \times 10^{6}\;\text{N/m} \\
A_{22} &= 0.2 \times 10^{-3}\bigl[10.06 + 140.91 + 10.06\bigr]\times 10^{9} \\
      &= 32.21 \times 10^{6}\;\text{N/m} \\
A_{12} &= 0.2 \times 10^{-3}\bigl[3.02 + 3.02 + 3.02\bigr]\times 10^{9} \\
      &= 1.81 \times 10^{6}\;\text{N/m} \\
A_{66} &= 0.2 \times 10^{-3}\bigl[5 + 5 + 5\bigr]\times 10^{9} \\
      &= 3.0 \times 10^{6}\;\text{N/m} \\
A_{16}=A_{26} &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Compute $[B]$ — the coupling matrix.**

For a **symmetric** laminate (stacking $[0/90/0]$), the geometry is symmetric about $z=0$, and since only $A_{ij}$ are involved, **all terms of $[B]$ vanish**:

$$
[B] = [0]_{3\times 3}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **defining feature** of symmetric laminates: the bending–extension coupling disappears. The laminate can carry axial loads and bending loads **independently**, which is why $99\%$ of practical composite structures use symmetric stacking sequences.

**Step 6: Compute $[D]$ — the bending stiffness matrix.**

Using the sum $\sum [\bar{Q}]_k (z_k^3 - z_{k-1}^3)$ divided by 3:

For ply 1 ($0°$): $(z_1^3 - z_0^3) = ((-0.1)^3 - (-0.3)^3)\times 10^{-9} = (-0.001 + 0.027)\times 10^{-9} = 0.026 \times 10^{-9}\,\text{m}^{3}$

For ply 2 ($90°$): $(z_2^3 - z_1^3) = ((0.1)^3 - (-0.1)^3)\times 10^{-9} = 0.002 \times 10^{-9}\,\text{m}^{3}$

For ply 3 ($0°$): $(z_3^3 - z_2^3) = ((0.3)^3 - (0.1)^3)\times 10^{-9} = 0.026 \times 10^{-9}\,\text{m}^{3}$

Then:

$$
D_{11} = \tfrac{1}{3}\bigl[140.91(0.026) + 10.06(0.002) + 140.91(0.026)\bigr]\times 10^{9} \times 10^{-9}
$$

$$
D_{11} = \tfrac{1}{3}\times 7.43 \approx 2.48\;\text{N·m}
$$

(similar calculations for $D_{22}, D_{12}, D_{66}$ yield the full $[D]$ matrix).

**Step 7: Effective in-plane properties.**

$$
E_x^{\text{eff}} = \frac{A_{11}A_{22} - A_{12}^{2}}{h\,A_{22}} = \frac{(58.38)(32.21) - (1.81)^{2}}{0.6 \times 10^{-3} \times 32.21}\times 10^{6}
$$

$$
E_x^{\text{eff}} \approx \frac{1877.6}{19.33 \times 10^{-3}} \times 10^{-6} \approx 97.1\;\text{GPa}
$$

Similarly $E_y^{\text{eff}} \approx 53.6\;\text{GPa}$, and $\nu_{xy}^{\text{eff}} = A_{12}/A_{22} = 1.81/32.21 \approx 0.056$.

> [!TIP]
> Notice that $E_x > E_y$ because there are two $0°$ plies for every one $90°$ ply — the laminate is **stiffer along the fiber direction** of the majority plies. This is a classic KTU-level design insight.

## 3.3 Derivation: Why $[B] = 0$ for a Symmetric Laminate

For a **symmetric** laminate, the ply at $z$ and the ply at $-z$ are **identical** in material and orientation. Then for every contribution $[\bar{Q}]_{k} \cdot z_k \cdot dz$ in the upper half, there exists an equal contribution $[\bar{Q}]_{k} \cdot (-z) \cdot dz$ in the lower half, summing to zero. Formally:

$$
[B] = \int_{-h/2}^{+h/2} [\bar{Q}(z)]\,z\,dz = \int_{0}^{+h/2} \bigl([\bar{Q}(z)]z - [\bar{Q}(-z)]z\bigr)\,dz = 0
$$

The condition $[\bar{Q}(z)] = [\bar{Q}(-z)]$ (symmetry in material) makes the integrand identically zero.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Laminate Architecture & CLT Data Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fiber Reinforcement + Matrix] --> B[Single Lamina Ply]
    B --> C[Stack of Plies]
    C --> D[Laminate]
    D --> E{Ply Orientations}
    E --> F[0 deg Plies]
    E --> G[45 deg Plies]
    E --> H[90 deg Plies]
    E --> I[Minus 45 deg Plies]
    F --> J[Q Bar Matrices per Ply]
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[ABD Matrix Assembly]
    K --> L[A Extensional 3x3]
    K --> M[B Coupling 3x3]
    K --> N[D Bending 3x3]
    L --> O[Force Resultants N]
    M --> O
    M --> P[Moment Resultants M]
    N --> P
    O --> Q[Governing Equation]
    P --> Q
    Q --> R[Strain epsilon0 and Curvature kappa]
    R --> S[Engineering Properties E nu G]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block: Ply Stacking Sequence Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Top Surface z equals plus h over 2] --> A2[Ply 1 theta 1]
    A2 --> A3[Ply 2 theta 2]
    A3 --> A4[Ply 3 theta 3]
    A4 --> A5[Mid Plane z equals 0]
    A5 --> A6[Ply n minus 2]
    A6 --> A7[Ply n minus 1]
    A7 --> A8[Ply n theta n]
    A8 --> A9[Bottom Surface z equals minus h over 2]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block: Stress–Strain–Resultant Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Ply Level Stress sigma Q Bar times epsilon] --> P2[Through Thickness Integration]
    P2 --> P3[Force Resultant N per unit length]
    P2 --> P4[Moment Resultant M per unit length]
    P3 --> P5[ABD Constitutive Law]
    P4 --> P5
    P5 --> P6[Inverse ABD]
    P6 --> P7[Mid Plane Strain epsilon0]
    P6 --> P8[Curvature kappa]
    P7 --> P9[Ply by Ply Stress Recovery]
    P8 --> P9
    P9 --> P10[Failure Analysis Tsai Wu or Hashin]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block: Coupling Phenomena Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A16[A16 and A26 nonzero] --> C1[Shear Extension Coupling]
    B16[B16 and B26 nonzero] --> C2[Bending Twist Coupling]
    BGeneral[B matrix nonzero in general] --> C3[Bending Extension Coupling]
    D16[D16 and D26 nonzero] --> C4[Bending Twist Coupling]
    C1 --> X[Predict using full ABD]
    C2 --> X
    C3 --> X
    C4 --> X
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above diagrams map the **flow of information** in CLT. In actual design, the engineer starts from the desired loading $\{N, M\}$, inverts the ABD matrix to get $\{\varepsilon^0, \kappa\}$, reconstructs ply-level strains via $\{\varepsilon\}_k = \{\varepsilon^0\} + z_k\{\kappa\}$, and finally applies a **failure criterion** (Tsai–Hill, Tsai–Wu, Hashin, Puck) at the ply level.
