# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q1. Define a laminate. Differentiate between a symmetric and an antisymmetric laminate with a suitable example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **laminate** is a structural element made by bonding together two or more laminae (plies) of fiber-reinforced composite material, with each ply potentially oriented at a different fiber angle, to achieve tailored mechanical properties.

| **Symmetric Laminate** | **Antisymmetric Laminate** |
|---|---|
| Stacking sequence is mirrored about the mid-plane | Plies are arranged antisymmetrically about the mid-plane |
| Example: $[0/45/90/90/45/0]$ or $[0/90]_S$ | Example: $[0/90/-90/0]$ or $[0/45/-45/0]$ |
| Bending–extension coupling matrix $[B] = 0$ | $[B] \neq 0$ in general |
| Pure in-plane loads cause no bending | In-plane loads induce bending/twisting |

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**Q2. State the physical significance of the A, B, and D matrices in Classical Lamination Theory.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **$[A]$ – Extensional Stiffness Matrix:** Relates the in-plane force resultants $\{N\}$ to the mid-plane strains $\{\varepsilon^{0}\}$. It governs the **in-plane (membrane) response** of the laminate.
- **$[B]$ – Coupling Stiffness Matrix:** Couples in-plane forces to curvatures, and moments to mid-plane strains. It represents the **bending–extension coupling**. It vanishes for symmetric laminates.
- **$[D]$ – Bending Stiffness Matrix:** Relates moment resultants $\{M\}$ to curvatures $\{\kappa\}$. It governs the **flexural (bending) response** of the laminate.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse the *units* of A, B, D. Remember: $[A]$ has units of N/m, $[B]$ has units of N, and $[D]$ has units of N·m. Losing this distinction in a numerical problem is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**A four-ply cross-ply laminate $[0°/90°/90°/0°]$ is made of glass/epoxy with each ply thickness $t = 0.15\,\text{mm}$. The reduced stiffnesses of a unidirectional ply in material coordinates are: $Q_{11} = 38.6\,\text{GPa}$, $Q_{22} = 8.27\,\text{GPa}$, $Q_{12} = 2.56\,\text{GPa}$, $Q_{66} = 4.14\,\text{GPa}$.**

**(a)** Determine the elements of the $[A]$, $[B]$, and $[D]$ matrices for the laminate. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the effective in-plane engineering properties $E_x$, $E_y$, $G_{xy}$, and $\nu_{xy}$ of the laminate. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Q.A

**Part (a) — ABD Matrices (7 marks)**

**Step 1:** Identify the $[\bar{Q}]$ for the $0°$ and $90°$ plies. For $0°$, $[\bar{Q}]_0 = [Q]$:

$$
[\bar{Q}]_{0°} = \begin{bmatrix} 38.6 & 2.56 & 0 \\ 2.56 & 8.27 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 4.14 \end{bmatrix}\,\text{GPa}
$$

For $90°$, $Q_{11}$ and $Q_{22}$ swap:

$$
[\bar{Q}]_{90°} = \begin{bmatrix} 8.27 & 2.56 & 0 \\ 2.56 & 38.6 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 4.14 \end{bmatrix}\,\text{GPa}
$$

**Step 2:** Set up the $z$-coordinates. Total thickness $h = 4 \times 0.15 = 0.6\,\text{mm}$. Mid-plane at $z=0$:

- Ply 1 ($0°$): $z_0 = -0.30\,\text{mm}$, $z_1 = -0.15\,\text{mm}$
- Ply 2 ($90°$): $z_1 = -0.15\,\text{mm}$, $z_2 = 0\,\text{mm}$
- Ply 3 ($90°$): $z_2 = 0\,\text{mm}$, $z_3 = +0.15\,\text{mm}$
- Ply 4 ($0°$): $z_3 = +0.15\,\text{mm}$, $z_4 = +0.30\,\text{mm}$

**Step 3:** Compute $[A]$ — the laminate is **symmetric**, with two $0°$ plies (top + bottom) and two $90°$ plies (middle):

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{11} &= t\bigl[Q_{11}(0°) + Q_{11}(90°) + Q_{11}(90°) + Q_{11}(0°)\bigr] \\
      &= 0.15 \times 10^{-3}\,[38.6 + 8.27 + 8.27 + 38.6] \times 10^{9} \\
      &= 14.06 \times 10^{6}\;\text{N/m} \\
A_{22} &= 0.15 \times 10^{-3}\,[8.27 + 38.6 + 38.6 + 8.27] \times 10^{9} \\
      &= 14.06 \times 10^{6}\;\text{N/m} \\
A_{12} &= 0.15 \times 10^{-3} \times 4 \times 2.56 \times 10^{9} = 1.54 \times 10^{6}\;\text{N/m} \\
A_{66} &= 0.15 \times 10^{-3} \times 4 \times 4.14 \times 10^{9} = 2.48 \times 10^{6}\;\text{N/m} \\
A_{16} = A_{26} &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute $[B]$ — for a **symmetric** stacking, $[B] = [0]$.

**Step 5:** Compute $[D]$ — apply $D_{ij} = \tfrac{1}{3}\sum [\bar{Q}]_{ij,k}(z_k^3 - z_{k-1}^3)$:

For plies 1 and 4 (both $0°$): $(z_1^3 - z_0^3) = (z_4^3 - z_3^3) = ((-0.15)^3 - (-0.30)^3) = 0.023625\,\text{mm}^3$

For plies 2 and 3 (both $90°$): $(z_2^3 - z_1^3) = (z_3^3 - z_2^3) = (0 - (-0.15)^3) = 0.003375\,\text{mm}^3$

So:

$$
D_{11} = \tfrac{1}{3}\bigl[38.6(0.023625) + 8.27(0.003375) + 8.27(0.003375) + 38.6(0.023625)\bigr]\times 10^{9} \times 10^{-9}
$$

$$
D_{11} \approx \tfrac{1}{3}\times 1.880 \approx 0.627\;\text{N·m}
$$

By the same logic (since the laminate is symmetric and balanced):

$$
D_{22} = D_{11} = 0.627\;\text{N·m},\quad D_{12} \approx 0.0416\;\text{N·m},\quad D_{66} \approx 0.067\;\text{N·m}
$$

(All in N·m, with $D_{16}=D_{26}=0$.)

**Part (b) — Effective Engineering Properties (7 marks)**

For a symmetric laminate with $[B] = 0$, the effective in-plane moduli are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_x &= \frac{A_{11}A_{22} - A_{12}^{2}}{h\,A_{22}} = \frac{(14.06)^{2} - (1.54)^{2}}{0.6 \times 10^{-3} \times 14.06} \times 10^{6} \\
E_x &= \frac{197.7 - 2.37}{8.44 \times 10^{-3}} \times 10^{6} \approx 23.1\;\text{GPa} \\
E_y &= \frac{(14.06)^{2} - (1.54)^{2}}{0.6 \times 10^{-3} \times 14.06} \times 10^{6} = E_x = 23.1\;\text{GPa} \\
G_{xy} &= \frac{A_{66}}{h} = \frac{2.48 \times 10^{6}}{0.6 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 4.14\;\text{GPa} \\
\nu_{xy} &= \frac{A_{12}}{A_{22}} = \frac{1.54}{14.06} \approx 0.109
\end{aligned}
$$

The laminate is **quasi-isotropic in-plane** because the cross-ply arrangement with equal 0° and 90° plies makes $A_{11} = A_{22}$ and $\nu_{xy} = \nu_{yx}$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**A four-ply angle-ply laminate $[+45°/-45°/-45°/+45°]$ is made of carbon/epoxy. Each ply has thickness $t = 0.2\,\text{mm}$. The transformed reduced stiffnesses for the $+\theta$ and $-\theta$ plies are given by:**

$$
[\bar{Q}]_{+\theta} = \begin{bmatrix} 25.4 & 18.7 & 17.3 \\ 18.7 & 25.4 & 17.3 \\ 17.3 & 17.3 & 21.6 \end{bmatrix}\,\text{GPa},\quad
[\bar{Q}]_{-\theta} = \begin{bmatrix} 25.4 & 18.7 & -17.3 \\ 18.7 & 25.4 & -17.3 \\ -17.3 & -17.3 & 21.6 \end{bmatrix}\,\text{GPa}
$$

**(a)** Compute the $[A]$, $[B]$, and $[D]$ matrices for the laminate. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the physical significance of the resulting $[A]$, $[B]$, and $[D]$ matrices. State why this laminate is classified as **balanced** and **symmetric**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Q.B

**Part (a) — ABD Computation (7 marks)**

**Step 1:** Establish $z$-coordinates. Total $h = 4 \times 0.2 = 0.8\,\text{mm}$.

- Ply 1 ($+45°$): $z_0 = -0.4\,\text{mm}$, $z_1 = -0.2\,\text{mm}$
- Ply 2 ($-45°$): $z_1 = -0.2\,\text{mm}$, $z_2 = 0$
- Ply 3 ($-45°$): $z_2 = 0$, $z_3 = +0.2\,\text{mm}$
- Ply 4 ($+45°$): $z_3 = +0.2\,\text{mm}$, $z_4 = +0.4\,\text{mm}$

**Step 2:** $[A]$ matrix — each ply contributes $t = 0.2 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}$:

$$
A_{ij} = 0.2 \times 10^{-3} \sum_{k=1}^{4} \bar{Q}_{ij,k} \times 10^{9}
$$

For $A_{11}$: $4 \times 25.4 = 101.6$

For $A_{12}$: $4 \times 18.7 = 74.8$

For $A_{16}$: $\bar{Q}_{16}$ from $[+45°, -45°, -45°, +45°]$ is $[17.3, -17.3, -17.3, 17.3] \Rightarrow \sum = 0$

For $A_{66}$: $4 \times 21.6 = 86.4$

$$
[A] = 0.2 \times 10^{-3} \times 10^{9}
\begin{bmatrix} 101.6 & 74.8 & 0 \\ 74.8 & 101.6 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 86.4 \end{bmatrix} = 10^{6}
\begin{bmatrix} 20.32 & 14.96 & 0 \\ 14.96 & 20.32 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 17.28 \end{bmatrix}\;\text{N/m}
$$

**Step 3:** $[B]$ matrix — laminate is **symmetric** ($[+45/-45/-45/+45]$ is mirror-symmetric about $z=0$), so:

$$
[B] = [0]_{3\times 3}
$$

**Step 4:** $[D]$ matrix — using $D_{ij} = \tfrac{1}{3}\sum \bar{Q}_{ij,k}\,(z_k^3 - z_{k-1}^3)$:

For plies 1 and 4 ($+45°$): $(z_1^3 - z_0^3) = (z_4^3 - z_3^3) = 0.056\,\text{mm}^3$

For plies 2 and 3 ($-45°$): $(z_2^3 - z_1^3) = (z_3^3 - z_2^3) = 0.008\,\text{mm}^3$

Note: $\bar{Q}_{16}$ and $\bar{Q}_{26}$ in $[D]$ will cancel by antisymmetry (sign reversal across mid-plane), and so will any $[B]$-type effects — confirming $[B] = 0$. The remaining nonzero components are:

$$
D_{11} = \tfrac{1}{3}\times 10^{9} \times 10^{-9}\bigl[25.4(0.056 + 0.008 + 0.008 + 0.056)\bigr] = \tfrac{1}{3}\times 25.4 \times 0.128 \approx 1.084\;\text{N·m}
$$

Similarly, $D_{22} = 1.084\,\text{N·m}$, $D_{12} = \tfrac{1}{3}\times 18.7 \times 0.128 \approx 0.798\,\text{N·m}$, $D_{66} = \tfrac{1}{3}\times 21.6 \times 0.128 \approx 0.922\,\text{N·m}$, and $D_{16} = D_{26} = 0$.

$$
[D] \approx 
\begin{bmatrix} 1.084 & 0.798 & 0 \\ 0.798 & 1.084 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0.922 \end{bmatrix}\;\text{N·m}
$$

**Part (b) — Physical Significance & Classification (7 marks)**

**Physical Significance:**

- **$[A]$** describes the **in-plane stiffness** of the laminate. The non-zero $A_{11}, A_{22}$ and $A_{12}$ show that the laminate resists in-plane normal and shear-extension loads. The values $A_{11} = A_{22}$ indicate **in-plane isotropy** in stiffness (a hallmark of angle-ply balanced laminates with equal $+\theta$ and $-\theta$ plies).
- **$[B]$** is identically zero, meaning **no bending–extension coupling**. An axial load $\{N\}$ will *not* cause the laminate to bend or twist.
- **$[D]$** represents the **bending rigidity**. The non-zero diagonal entries mean the laminate resists bending in both $x$ and $y$ directions, while $D_{66}$ resists twist. The vanishing of $D_{16}, D_{26}$ implies **no bending–twist coupling**.

**Why Balanced and Symmetric:**

- **Balanced:** The laminate has an **equal number of $+\theta$ and $-\theta$ plies** (here, two $+45°$ and two $-45°$). This makes $A_{16} = A_{26} = 0$ because the off-diagonal terms of $[\bar{Q}]$ have opposite signs for $+\theta$ and $-\theta$ and cancel upon summation.
- **Symmetric:** The stacking sequence is a **mirror image** about the mid-plane (Ply 1 = Ply 4 = $+45°$; Ply 2 = Ply 3 = $-45°$). This makes the integrand of $[B]$ an odd function of $z$, hence $B_{ij} = 0$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit conversions:** $z$-coordinates must be in **meters** when computing $[A]$, $[B]$, $[D]$. A common error is mixing mm and m, which produces an $[A]$ matrix off by a factor of $10^3$ or $10^6$. Always write $z_k$ in **meters** before plugging in.
> 2. **Symmetry check on $[A]$:** Students frequently forget that $[A]$ is symmetric ($A_{ij} = A_{ji}$). If your computed $[A]$ is non-symmetric, you have an arithmetic error.
> 3. **Symmetric ≠ Balanced:** A laminate can be **symmetric** without being **balanced** (e.g., $[0/45/0]$) and **balanced** without being **symmetric** (e.g., $[0/45/-45/90]$). KTU problems test this distinction directly. Lose **1 mark** if you confuse the two.
> 4. **Effective property formula trap:** For effective $E_x$, $E_y$, etc., the formula is valid **only** when $[B] = 0$. If $[B]\neq 0$, you must first invert the full $6\times 6$ ABD matrix and extract the engineering constants from the reduced $3\times 3$ block — KTU often tests this caveat.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Laminate Theory**
>
> - **Lamina vs. Laminate:** Lamina = single ply; Laminate = stack of plies.
> - **Classical Lamination Theory (CLT)** is based on the **Kirchhoff–Love** straight-normal assumption and **plane stress** in each ply.
> - **Displacement field:** $u = u_0 - z\,\partial w_0/\partial x$, etc. — every point on a normal line moves rigidly with the mid-plane.
> - **Strain field:** $\{\varepsilon\} = \{\varepsilon^{0}\} + z\{\kappa\}$, where $\{\varepsilon^{0}\}$ are mid-plane strains and $\{\kappa\}$ are curvatures.
> - **Force resultants $\{N\}$ and Moment resultants $\{M\}$** are obtained by integrating stress through the thickness.
> - **ABD Matrix Equation:**
>   $\begin{Bmatrix} N \\ M \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} A & B \\ B & D \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} \varepsilon^{0} \\ \kappa \end{Bmatrix}$
> - **$[A] = \sum [\bar{Q}]_k (z_k - z_{k-1})$** — extensional stiffness (units: N/m).
> - **$[B] = \tfrac{1}{2}\sum [\bar{Q}]_k (z_k^2 - z_{k-1}^2)$** — coupling stiffness (units: N).
> - **$[D] = \tfrac{1}{3}\sum [\bar{Q}]_k (z_k^3 - z_{k-1}^3)$** — bending stiffness (units: N·m).
> - **Symmetric laminate ⇒ $[B] = 0$** (no bending–extension coupling).
> - **Balanced laminate ⇒ $A_{16} = A_{26} = 0$** (no shear–extension coupling).
> - **Cross-ply ⇒ $A_{16}=A_{26}=D_{16}=D_{26}=0$** (all coupling vanishes except possibly $[B]$).
> - **Quasi-isotropic laminate ⇒ $A_{11} = A_{22}$, $2A_{66} = A_{11} - A_{12}$** (in-plane isotropy in stiffness).
> - **Effective in-plane properties** (valid when $[B]=0$):
>   $E_x = (A_{11}A_{22} - A_{12}^2)/(h A_{22})$,
>   $E_y = (A_{11}A_{22} - A_{12}^2)/(h A_{11})$,
>   $G_{xy} = A_{66}/h$,
>   $\nu_{xy} = A_{12}/A_{22}$.
> - **Bending–extension coupling** is a **unique feature of composite laminates** — it does not exist in isotropic metal plates. This is why composite design is fundamentally different from metallic design.
> - **Always** use SI units consistently (N, m, Pa). Mixed units are the #1 cause of calculation errors in KTU board exams.
> - **Inversion of ABD** is required to get $\{\varepsilon^0, \kappa\}$ from $\{N, M\}$. The inverse has 4 sub-matrices $[A^*], [B^*], [C^*], [D^*]$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Micromechanical Behaviour of a Ply; Out-of-Plane Effects; Hooke's Law Applied to Unidirectional Composites
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT744 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Micromechanical Behaviour of a Ply; Out-of-Plane Effects; Hooke's Law Applied to Unidirectional Composites

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Micromechanical Behaviour of a Ply?

**Micromechanics of a ply** is the branch of composite mechanics that establishes a **constitutive relationship between the individual constituent properties** (fiber + matrix) and the **effective homogenized engineering constants** of a single unidirectional (UD) ply. The ply is treated as a **transversely isotropic** continuum whose in-plane behaviour is governed by five independent elastic constants.

Formally, if $E_f$, $\nu_f$, $V_f$ are the Young's modulus, Poisson's ratio, and volume fraction of the fiber, and $E_m$, $\nu_m$, $V_m$ are the corresponding properties of the matrix (with $V_f + V_m = 1$), then micromechanics predicts:

$$
E_1,\ E_2,\ G_{12},\ \nu_{12},\ G_{23}\quad \text{(independent)};\qquad G_{13}=G_{12},\ \nu_{21}=\nu_{12}\frac{E_2}{E_1}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A UD composite is **NOT isotropic** and **NOT fully orthotropic**. It is **transversely isotropic** — meaning it has an axis of symmetry (the fiber direction, 1-axis) and is isotropic in the 2-3 plane. Hence only **5 independent elastic constants** are needed, not 9.

## 1.2 Out-of-Plane Effects

**Out-of-plane (through-thickness) effects** refer to the mechanical response of the laminate/ply along the **3-direction** (perpendicular to the lamina plane). These include:

- **Interlaminar stresses** $\tau_{13}$, $\tau_{23}$, $\sigma_3$ (free-edge effects).
- **Through-thickness modulus** $E_3$, **transverse shear moduli** $G_{13}$, $G_{23}$.
- **Delamination and matrix cracking** initiation phenomena.

> [!NOTE]
> Classical laminated plate theory (CLPT) assumes $\sigma_3 = 0$, $\tau_{13} = \tau_{23} = 0$ — this is an **idealization**. Real laminates exhibit significant out-of-plane stresses near **free edges, ply drops, holes, and impact sites**.

## 1.3 Hooke's Law for a Unidirectional Composite

For a UD lamina, the **generalized Hooke's law** in the principal material coordinate system (1-2-3) is:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \sigma_1 \\ \sigma_2 \\ \sigma_3 \\ \tau_{23} \\ \tau_{13} \\ \tau_{12} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} C_{11} & C_{12} & C_{12} & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ C_{12} & C_{22} & C_{23} & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ C_{12} & C_{23} & C_{22} & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & \tfrac{C_{22}-C_{23}}{2} & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & C_{66} & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & C_{66} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \varepsilon_1 \\ \varepsilon_2 \\ \varepsilon_3 \\ \gamma_{23} \\ \gamma_{13} \\ \gamma_{12} \end{bmatrix}
$$

where $C_{66} = G_{12}$ and the off-diagonal symmetry reflects transverse isotropy in the 2-3 plane.

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The Bamboo Shaft

Imagine a **bamboo shaft** (fiber) embedded in a **resin paste** (matrix). When you pull along the bamboo direction, the bamboo takes almost all the load because it is much stiffer → **E₁ is dominated by fiber**. When you pull sideways (perpendicular to bamboo), the weak resin transfers load to bamboo through shear → **E₂ is dominated by matrix**. When you twist the shaft, the bamboo-resin interface shear controls twisting → **G₁₂ is governed by matrix shear + interface**. This is exactly the *physical intuition* behind micromechanical formulas.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic Rule of Thumb (Board Examiner's Favourite):**
> - **Along fiber** → **Fiber rules** (iso-strain, Voigt model).
> - **Across fiber** → **Matrix rules** (iso-stress, Reuss model).
> - **In shear** → **Matrix dominates** (fibers don't carry transverse shear well).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Logical Framework of Micromechanics

The analysis flow is structured into **five progressive layers**:

1. **Constituent Definition Layer:** Identify $E_f, E_m, \nu_f, \nu_m, V_f, V_m$ from material datasheets.
2. **Kinematic Assumption Layer:** Apply either iso-strain (parallel) or iso-stress (series) kinematic hypothesis.
3. **Volume-Averaging Layer:** Compute effective stress and strain via volume averaging.
4. **Property Extraction Layer:** Solve averaged equations to extract $E_1, E_2, \nu_{12}, G_{12}$.
5. **Validation Layer:** Check thermodynamic bounds (Voigt ≥ property ≥ Reuss) and physical consistency.

## 2.2 The Iso-Strain Model (Rule of Mixtures for E₁)

**Assumption:** Fiber and matrix experience the **same axial strain** $\varepsilon_1$ (parallel springs).

$$
\sigma_1 = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m = E_f \varepsilon_1 V_f + E_m \varepsilon_1 V_m
$$

Dividing by $\varepsilon_1$:

$$
E_1 = E_f V_f + E_m V_m \quad \text{(Voigt upper bound)}
$$

## 2.3 The Iso-Stress Model (Rule of Mixtures for E₂)

**Assumption:** Fiber and matrix experience the **same transverse stress** $\sigma_2$ (series springs).

$$
\varepsilon_2 = \varepsilon_f V_f + \varepsilon_m V_m = \frac{\sigma_2}{E_f} V_f + \frac{\sigma_2}{E_m} V_m
$$

Inverting:

$$
\frac{1}{E_2} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m} \quad \Rightarrow \quad E_2 = \frac{E_f E_m}{E_m V_f + E_f V_m}
$$

## 2.4 Poisson's Ratio (Major Poisson's Ratio ν₁₂)

$$
\nu_{12} = \nu_f V_f + \nu_m V_m
$$

The **minor Poisson's ratio** $\nu_{21}$ is obtained from the **reciprocal relation**:

$$
\nu_{21} = \nu_{12} \cdot \frac{E_2}{E_1}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In composites, $\nu_{12} \neq \nu_{21}$. This is **NEVER** the case in isotropic metals, where $\nu$ is a single scalar. Examiners frequently test this distinction.

## 2.5 Transverse Shear Modulus (Halpin-Tsai Semi-Empirical)

A pure rule-of-mixtures underestimates $G_{12}$. The **Halpin-Tsai equation** with shape factor $\xi = 1$ (circular fibers) gives:

$$
G_{12} = G_m \cdot \frac{G_f + G_m}{G_m V_f + G_f V_m} \quad \text{at } \xi = 1
$$

where $G_f = \dfrac{E_f}{2(1+\nu_f)}$, $\quad G_m = \dfrac{E_m}{2(1+\nu_m)}$.

Generalized form with shape factor $\xi$:

$$
\frac{M}{M_m} = \frac{1 + \xi \eta V_f}{1 - \eta V_f},\quad \eta = \frac{M_f/M_m - 1}{M_f/M_m + \xi}
$$

## 2.6 Out-of-Plane Engineering Constants

| Property | Formula / Source | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| $E_3$ | $E_3 = E_2$ | Transverse isotropy in 2-3 plane |
| $G_{23}$ | $\dfrac{E_2}{2(1+\nu_{23})}$ | In-plane-of-transverse shear |
| $G_{13}$ | $G_{13} = G_{12}$ | Transverse isotropy in 2-3 plane |
| $\nu_{23}$ | $\approx 0.3 - 0.4$ | Empirical, dominated by matrix |
| $\nu_{31}$ | $\nu_{12}\dfrac{E_2}{E_1}$ | Reciprocal relation |
| $\nu_{32}$ | $\nu_{23}\dfrac{E_2}{E_3} = \nu_{23}$ | Reciprocal in 2-3 |

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet — Complete Cheat Sheet

| # | Engineering Constant | Micromechanical Formula | Validity |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_1$ (Longitudinal) | $E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ | Exact (Voigt) |
| 2 | $E_2$ (Transverse) | $\dfrac{E_f E_m}{E_m V_f + E_f V_m}$ | Reuss lower bound |
| 3 | $E_2$ (Improved) | Halpin-Tsai with $\xi = 2$ | Accurate |
| 4 | $\nu_{12}$ | $\nu_f V_f + \nu_m V_m$ | Voigt rule |
| 5 | $\nu_{21}$ | $\nu_{12}\dfrac{E_2}{E_1}$ | Reciprocal |
| 6 | $G_{12}$ | $\dfrac{G_m (G_f + G_m) + V_f(G_f - G_m)}{G_m + V_f(G_f - G_m)}$ | Halpin-Tsai, $\xi=1$ |
| 7 | $G_{23}$ | $\dfrac{G_m (1 + V_f)}{(1 - V_f) + \dfrac{G_m}{G_f}(1+V_f)}$ | Halpin-Tsai |
| 8 | $E_3$ | $E_2$ | Transverse isotropy |
| 9 | $G_{13}$ | $G_{12}$ | Transverse isotropy |
| 10 | $\nu_{23}$ | $\approx \nu_m (1-V_f) + \nu_f V_f$ | Approximate |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These five constants ($E_1, E_2, G_{12}, \nu_{12}, G_{23}$) completely define the **in-plane + out-of-plane elastic response** of a UD ply. They are the **input** to the $[Q]$ reduced stiffness matrix and ultimately to classical laminate theory (CLT).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Longitudinal Young's Modulus $E_1$ (Iso-Strain / Voigt Model)

### Step 1 — Define the Representative Volume Element (RVE)

Consider a UD composite with fibers aligned along the **1-axis**. Let a uniform axial stress $\sigma_1$ be applied.

### Step 2 — Apply Kinematic Constraint

Both phases undergo identical axial strain because the fiber-matrix interface is assumed perfectly bonded and the cross-section remains plane:

$$
\varepsilon_1^{(f)} = \varepsilon_1^{(m)} = \varepsilon_1
$$

### Step 3 — Express Stress in Each Phase

By Hooke's law applied to each homogeneous constituent:

$$
\sigma_1^{(f)} = E_f \varepsilon_1, \qquad \sigma_1^{(m)} = E_m \varepsilon_1
$$

### Step 4 — Apply Force Equilibrium on Cross-Section

The total axial force equals the sum of forces carried by each phase over their respective areas:

$$
F_{tot} = \sigma_f A_f + \sigma_m A_m = \sigma_1 A_{tot}
$$

### Step 5 — Introduce Volume Fractions

For a unidirectional composite with uniform fiber distribution:

$$
V_f = \frac{A_f}{A_{tot}}, \quad V_m = \frac{A_m}{A_{tot}}, \quad V_f + V_m = 1
$$

### Step 6 — Substitute and Simplify

$$
\sigma_1 = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m
$$

$$
\sigma_1 = E_f \varepsilon_1 V_f + E_m \varepsilon_1 V_m
$$

$$
\boxed{E_1 = E_f V_f + E_m V_m}
$$

This is the **Voigt upper bound** and is exact when fibers are continuous, parallel, and perfectly aligned with the load.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Transverse Young's Modulus $E_2$ (Iso-Stress / Reuss Model)

### Step 1 — Apply Stress Constraint

The transverse stress is uniform across phases because they are arranged in **series** with respect to the 2-direction:

$$
\sigma_2^{(f)} = \sigma_2^{(m)} = \sigma_2
$$

### Step 2 — Express Strain in Each Phase

$$
\varepsilon_2^{(f)} = \frac{\sigma_2}{E_f}, \qquad \varepsilon_2^{(m)} = \frac{\sigma_2}{E_m}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply Strain Compatibility (Total Displacement)

$$
\Delta_2^{(tot)} = \Delta_2^{(f)} + \Delta_2^{(m)}
$$

In a unit-cell of total length $L_{tot}$:

$$
\varepsilon_2 L_{tot} = \varepsilon_f L_f + \varepsilon_m L_m
$$

### Step 4 — Convert to Volume Fractions

With $V_f = L_f / L_{tot}$, $V_m = L_m / L_{tot}$:

$$
\varepsilon_2 = \varepsilon_f V_f + \varepsilon_m V_m = \frac{\sigma_2 V_f}{E_f} + \frac{\sigma_2 V_m}{E_m}
$$

### Step 5 — Invert for $E_2$

$$
\frac{\varepsilon_2}{\sigma_2} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m}
$$

$$
\boxed{\frac{1}{E_2} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m}}
$$

$$
\boxed{E_2 = \frac{E_f E_m}{E_m V_f + E_f V_m}}
$$

This is the **Reuss lower bound**. Real composites fall **between** Voigt (E₁) and Reuss (E₂) bounds.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Major Poisson's Ratio $\nu_{12}$

### Step 1 — Definition

$$
\nu_{12} = -\frac{\varepsilon_2^{(ax)}}{\varepsilon_1^{(ax)}}
$$

### Step 2 — Lateral Strain in Each Phase

When axial strain $\varepsilon_1$ is applied, both phases contract laterally:

$$
\varepsilon_2^{(f)} = -\nu_f \varepsilon_1, \quad \varepsilon_2^{(m)} = -\nu_m \varepsilon_1
$$

### Step 3 — Volume-Average Lateral Strain

$$
\varepsilon_2 = \varepsilon_2^{(f)} V_f + \varepsilon_2^{(m)} V_m = -\nu_f \varepsilon_1 V_f - \nu_m \varepsilon_1 V_m
$$

### Step 4 — Solve

$$
\boxed{\nu_{12} = \nu_f V_f + \nu_m V_m}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Transverse Shear Modulus $G_{12}$ (Halpin-Tsai)

### Step 1 — Apply Pure Shear $\tau_{12}$

Both phases experience equal shear stress:

$$
\tau_{12}^{(f)} = \tau_{12}^{(m)} = \tau_{12}
$$

### Step 2 — Shear Strain in Each Phase

$$
\gamma_{12}^{(f)} = \frac{\tau_{12}}{G_f}, \quad \gamma_{12}^{(m)} = \frac{\tau_{12}}{G_m}
$$

### Step 3 — Energy Approach (Halpin-Tsai Form)

Halpin and Tsai (1969) modified the Reuss model by introducing an empirical **strengthening parameter** $\xi$ that accounts for fiber geometry:

$$
\frac{G_{12}}{G_m} = \frac{1 + \xi \eta V_f}{1 - \eta V_f}
$$

$$
\eta = \frac{G_f/G_m - 1}{G_f/G_m + \xi}
$$

For **circular fibers** in shear, $\xi = 1$. Substituting:

$$
\frac{G_{12}}{G_m} = \frac{(1 + \eta V_f)}{(1 - \eta V_f)} = \frac{G_f + G_m}{G_m V_f + G_f V_m}
$$

### Step 4 — Final Simplified Form

$$
\boxed{G_{12} = \frac{G_m (G_f + G_m) + V_f G_m (G_f - G_m)}{G_m + V_f(G_f - G_m)} = \frac{G_m(G_f + G_m + V_f(G_f - G_m))}{G_m + V_f(G_f - G_m)}}
$$

For practical use:

$$
\boxed{G_{12} \approx G_m \cdot \frac{1 + V_f}{1 - V_f}} \quad \text{(when } G_f \gg G_m \text{)}
$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Reduced Stiffness Matrix $[Q]$ from Engineering Constants

### Step 1 — Begin with 3D Compliance Matrix $[S]$

$$
S_{11} = \frac{1}{E_1},\quad S_{22} = \frac{1}{E_2},\quad S_{33} = \frac{1}{E_3} = \frac{1}{E_2}
$$

$$
S_{12} = -\frac{\nu_{21}}{E_2} = -\frac{\nu_{12}}{E_1},\quad S_{13} = S_{12}
$$

$$
S_{23} = -\frac{\nu_{32}}{E_3} = -\frac{\nu_{23}}{E_2}
$$

$$
S_{44} = \frac{1}{G_{23}},\quad S_{55} = \frac{1}{G_{13}} = \frac{1}{G_{12}},\quad S_{66} = \frac{1}{G_{12}}
$$

### Step 2 — Invert $[S]$ to Get $[C]$

For transverse isotropy, the stiffness matrix in contracted notation:

$$
[C] = \begin{bmatrix} C_{11} & C_{12} & C_{12} & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ C_{12} & C_{22} & C_{23} & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ C_{12} & C_{23} & C_{22} & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & \tfrac{C_{22}-C_{23}}{2} & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & C_{66} & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & C_{66} \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 3 — Extract Reduced Stiffness $[Q]$ (Plane Stress, $\sigma_3 = 0$)

For **plane stress** state ($\sigma_3 = \tau_{23} = \tau_{13} = 0$), the **reduced stiffness matrix** for a UD ply is:

$$
[Q] = \begin{bmatrix} Q_{11} & Q_{12} & 0 \\ Q_{12} & Q_{22} & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & Q_{66} \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 4 — Express Q in Engineering Constants

$$
Q_{11} = \frac{E_1}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}}
$$

$$
Q_{22} = \frac{E_2}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}}
$$

$$
Q_{12} = \frac{\nu_{12} E_2}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}} = \frac{\nu_{21} E_1}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}}
$$

$$
Q_{66} = G_{12}
$$

$$
\boxed{[\sigma]_{12} = [Q][\varepsilon]_{12} \quad \text{where } \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_1 \\ \sigma_2 \\ \tau_{12} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} Q_{11} & Q_{12} & 0 \\ Q_{12} & Q_{22} & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & Q_{66} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \varepsilon_1 \\ \varepsilon_2 \\ \gamma_{12} \end{bmatrix}}
$$

---

## 3.6 Derivation: Out-of-Plane Property Bounds

### Step 1 — Through-Thickness Modulus $E_3$

By **transverse isotropy**, properties in 2 and 3 directions are identical in any plane containing the fiber axis:

$$
\boxed{E_3 = E_2}
$$

### Step 2 — In-Plane-of-Transverse Shear $G_{23}$

Using Halpin-Tsai with shape factor $\xi$ that reflects the packing geometry (square $\xi = 1$, hexagonal $\xi \approx 1.5$):

$$
G_{23} = G_m \cdot \frac{1 + \xi \eta V_f}{1 - \eta V_f}, \quad \eta = \frac{G_f/G_m - 1}{G_f/G_m + \xi}
$$

For square packing, $\xi = 1$:

$$
\boxed{G_{23} = \frac{G_f G_m}{G_f V_m + G_m V_f}} \quad \text{(a Reuss-type lower bound)}
$$

### Step 3 — Transverse Poisson's Ratio $\nu_{23}$

$$
\boxed{\nu_{23} = 1 - \nu_{12} - \frac{E_1}{E_2}\nu_{21}} \quad \text{(from thermodynamic constraint } \det[S] > 0\text{)}
$$

Or equivalently, with transverse isotropy:

$$
\nu_{32} = \nu_{23}\frac{E_3}{E_2} = \nu_{23}
$$

---

## 3.7 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Standard)

**Given:** Carbon fiber / Epoxy UD ply.
$E_f = 230\ \text{GPa},\ \nu_f = 0.20$
$E_m = 3.5\ \text{GPa},\ \nu_m = 0.35$
$V_f = 0.60$

**Compute** all five engineering constants.

### Solution:

**Step 1 — Longitudinal Modulus:**
$$
E_1 = (230)(0.60) + (3.5)(0.40) = 138 + 1.4 = \mathbf{139.4\ \text{GPa}}
$$

**Step 2 — Major Poisson's Ratio:**
$$
\nu_{12} = (0.20)(0.60) + (0.35)(0.40) = 0.12 + 0.14 = \mathbf{0.26}
$$

**Step 3 — Transverse Modulus (Reuss):**
$$
E_2 = \frac{(230)(3.5)}{(3.5)(0.60) + (230)(0.40)} = \frac{805}{2.10 + 92.0} = \frac{805}{94.1} = \mathbf{8.55\ \text{GPa}}
$$

**Step 4 — Minor Poisson's Ratio:**
$$
\nu_{21} = 0.26 \times \frac{8.55}{139.4} = \mathbf{0.01595}
$$

**Step 5 — Shear Moduli:**
$$
G_f = \frac{230}{2(1+0.20)} = 95.83\ \text{GPa}
$$
$$
G_m = \frac{3.5}{2(1+0.35)} = 1.296\ \text{GPa}
$$
$$
G_{12} = \frac{1.296(95.83 + 1.296) + 0.60 \cdot 1.296 (95.83 - 1.296)}{1.296 + 0.60(95.83 - 1.296)} = \mathbf{3.32\ \text{GPa}}
$$

**Step 6 — Reduced Stiffness Matrix $[Q]$:**
$$
1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21} = 1 - (0.26)(0.01595) = 0.99585
$$
$$
Q_{11} = \frac{139.4}{0.99585} = \mathbf{140.0\ \text{GPa}}
$$
$$
Q_{22} = \frac{8.55}{0.99585} = \mathbf{8.585\ \text{GPa}}
$$
$$
Q_{12} = \frac{0.26 \times 8.55}{0.99585} = \mathbf{2.233\ \text{GPa}}
$$
$$
Q_{66} = 3.32\ \text{GPa}
$$

---

    B --> B1[ISO STRAIN Parallel Springs]

    C1 --> D[VOLUME AVERAGING]
    C2 --> D
    D --> D1[nu_12 Voigt Rule]
    D --> D2[nu_21 via Reciprocal]
    D --> D3[G_12 via Halpin Tsai]

    D3 --> E
    E --> F[CONSTRUCT Q MATRIX]

```

```

The **1-axis** is along the fiber, **2-axis** lies in the plane of the ply perpendicular to fiber, and **3-axis** is the through-thickness direction.
