# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define the term "transversely isotropic material" with respect to a unidirectional composite. Why is a UD ply transversely isotropic and not fully orthotropic?**

**Model Answer:**

A **transversely isotropic material** is one that possesses **one axis of material symmetry** such that all properties in any plane perpendicular to that axis are identical.

A UD ply is transversely isotropic because:
1. The fiber direction (1-axis) is the unique axis of symmetry.
2. Properties in the **2-3 plane** (perpendicular to fibers) are **identical in every direction**.
3. Therefore $E_2 = E_3$, $G_{12} = G_{13}$, $\nu_{23} = \nu_{32}$, but $E_1 \neq E_2$.
4. It has only **5 independent elastic constants** instead of the 9 required for general orthotropy.

> **Word Count Target:** ~80 words. Marks awarded for **mention of axis of symmetry + 5 constants**.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Differentiate between major Poisson's ratio ($\nu_{12}$) and minor Poisson's ratio ($\nu_{21}$) in a UD composite. State the reciprocal relation connecting them.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | $\nu_{12}$ (Major) | $\nu_{21}$ (Minor) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | $-\varepsilon_2 / \varepsilon_1$ when $\sigma_1$ applied | $-\varepsilon_1 / \varepsilon_2$ when $\sigma_2$ applied |
| Magnitude | Larger | Smaller |
| Determination | Direct rule of mixtures | Reciprocal relation |

**Reciprocal relation:**
$$
\nu_{21} = \nu_{12}\cdot\frac{E_2}{E_1}
$$

For typical UD composites: $\nu_{12} \approx 0.25\text{-}0.35$ while $\nu_{21} \approx 0.01\text{-}0.05$ because $E_1 \gg E_2$.

> **Examiner's Tip:** Always state that $\nu_{12} \neq \nu_{21}$ for composites — a common error is treating them as equal.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With neat derivations, obtain the expressions for longitudinal Young's modulus ($E_1$) and transverse Young's modulus ($E_2$) of a unidirectional fiber-reinforced composite using the **rule of mixtures**. State clearly the assumptions made in each derivation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For a glass fiber/epoxy UD composite, the following data are given:
- $E_f = 72\ \text{GPa},\ \nu_f = 0.22$
- $E_m = 3.6\ \text{GPa},\ \nu_m = 0.38$
- $V_f = 0.55$

Compute: (i) $E_1$, (ii) $\nu_{12}$, (iii) $E_2$, (iv) $\nu_{21}$, (v) $G_{12}$ using Halpin-Tsai ($\xi = 1$).

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**(a) Derivations — See SECTION 3.1 and 3.2 of these notes for complete working.**

**Key Assumptions for $E_1$:**
- Iso-strain: $\varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m = \varepsilon_1$
- Perfect fiber-matrix bonding
- Continuous, parallel, uniformly distributed fibers
- Both fibers and matrix are linearly elastic
- Result: $E_1 = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ (Voigt upper bound)

**Key Assumptions for $E_2$:**
- Iso-stress: $\sigma_f = \sigma_m = \sigma_2$
- Phases act as series springs
- Result: $\dfrac{1}{E_2} = \dfrac{V_f}{E_f} + \dfrac{V_m}{E_m}$ (Reuss lower bound)

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $E_f = 72$, $\nu_f = 0.22$, $E_m = 3.6$, $\nu_m = 0.38$, $V_f = 0.55$, $V_m = 0.45$.

**(i) Longitudinal Modulus $E_1$:**

$$
E_1 = E_f V_f + E_m V_m = (72)(0.55) + (3.6)(0.45)
$$

$$
E_1 = 39.6 + 1.62 = \mathbf{41.22\ \text{GPa}}
$$

**(ii) Major Poisson's Ratio $\nu_{12}$:**

$$
\nu_{12} = \nu_f V_f + \nu_m V_m = (0.22)(0.55) + (0.38)(0.45)
$$

$$
\nu_{12} = 0.121 + 0.171 = \mathbf{0.292}
$$

**(iii) Transverse Modulus $E_2$ (Reuss):**

$$
\frac{1}{E_2} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m} = \frac{0.55}{72} + \frac{0.45}{3.6}
$$

$$
\frac{1}{E_2} = 0.007639 + 0.125 = 0.132639
$$

$$
E_2 = \frac{1}{0.132639} = \mathbf{7.539\ \text{GPa}}
$$

**(iv) Minor Poisson's Ratio $\nu_{21}$:**

$$
\nu_{21} = \nu_{12} \cdot \frac{E_2}{E_1} = 0.292 \cdot \frac{7.539}{41.22}
$$

$$
\nu_{21} = 0.292 \cdot 0.1829 = \mathbf{0.0534}
$$

**(v) Shear Modulus $G_{12}$ (Halpin-Tsai, $\xi = 1$):**

First, compute constituent shear moduli:
$$
G_f = \frac{E_f}{2(1+\nu_f)} = \frac{72}{2(1+0.22)} = \frac{72}{2.44} = 29.508\ \text{GPa}
$$

$$
G_m = \frac{E_m}{2(1+\nu_m)} = \frac{3.6}{2(1+0.38)} = \frac{3.6}{2.76} = 1.304\ \text{GPa}
$$

Halpin-Tsai at $\xi = 1$:
$$
G_{12} = G_m \cdot \frac{G_f + G_m}{G_m V_f + G_f V_m}
$$

$$
G_{12} = 1.304 \cdot \frac{29.508 + 1.304}{(1.304)(0.55) + (29.508)(0.45)}
$$

$$
G_{12} = 1.304 \cdot \frac{30.812}{0.7172 + 13.2786}
$$

$$
G_{12} = 1.304 \cdot \frac{30.812}{13.9958}
$$

$$
\boxed{G_{12} = 1.304 \cdot 2.2016 = \mathbf{2.871\ \text{GPa}}}
$$

**Final Tabulated Answer:**

| Constant | Value |
|---|---|
| $E_1$ | 41.22 GPa |
| $\nu_{12}$ | 0.292 |
| $E_2$ | 7.539 GPa |
| $\nu_{21}$ | 0.0534 |
| $G_{12}$ | 2.871 GPa |

---

### Question B: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** What are **out-of-plane effects** in composite laminates? Discuss the interlaminar stresses and their consequences. Explain how classical laminated plate theory (CLPT) handles (or fails to handle) these effects.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For a UD carbon/epoxy ply, derive the **reduced stiffness matrix $[Q]$** in terms of engineering constants $E_1, E_2, \nu_{12}, G_{12}$. Also write the full 3D Hooke's law for a transversely isotropic material showing all terms of the $[C]$ matrix.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**(a) Out-of-Plane Effects:**

**Definition:** Out-of-plane effects refer to stresses and deformations that develop **along the thickness direction (3-axis)** of a laminate, perpendicular to the plane of the plies.

**Types of Interlaminar Stresses:**
1. **$\sigma_3$ — Through-thickness normal stress** — arises near free edges, ply drops, and bonded joints.
2. **$\tau_{13}$ — Interlaminar shear stress in 1-3 plane** — major cause of **delamination**.
3. **$\tau_{23}$ — Interlaminar shear stress in 2-3 plane** — contributes to matrix cracking.

**Consequences:**
- **Free-edge delamination** — occurs in cross-ply and angle-ply laminates due to mismatch in $\nu_{12}$ between plies.
- **Matrix microcracking** in 90° plies.
- **Fiber-matrix debonding** at curved surfaces.

**CLPT's Treatment:**
- CLPT **assumes** $\sigma_3 = \tau_{13} = \tau_{23} = 0$ (Kirchhoff-Love hypothesis extended to laminates).
- Therefore it **cannot predict interlaminar stresses**.
- Solutions: **3D elasticity**, **higher-order shear deformation theory (HSDT)**, **layerwise theory (LW)**, **FEM**.

**(b) Derivation of Reduced Stiffness Matrix $[Q]$:**

Starting from 3D compliance $[S]$:

$$
\varepsilon_1 = \frac{\sigma_1}{E_1} - \frac{\nu_{21}}{E_2}\sigma_2 - \frac{\nu_{31}}{E_3}\sigma_3
$$

For **plane stress** ($\sigma_3 = 0$, $\tau_{23} = \tau_{13} = 0$):

$$
\varepsilon_1 = \frac{\sigma_1}{E_1} - \frac{\nu_{21}}{E_2}\sigma_2
$$

$$
\varepsilon_2 = -\frac{\nu_{12}}{E_1}\sigma_1 + \frac{\sigma_2}{E_2}
$$

$$
\gamma_{12} = \frac{\tau_{12}}{G_{12}}
$$

Solving for $\sigma_1, \sigma_2$ in terms of $\varepsilon_1, \varepsilon_2$:

Let $\nu_{12} E_2 = \nu_{21} E_1$ (symmetry condition). Define $\beta = 1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}$.

After algebraic inversion:

$$
\sigma_1 = \frac{E_1}{\beta}\varepsilon_1 + \frac{\nu_{12}E_2}{\beta}\varepsilon_2
$$

$$
\sigma_2 = \frac{\nu_{12}E_2}{\beta}\varepsilon_1 + \frac{E_2}{\beta}\varepsilon_2
$$

$$
\tau_{12} = G_{12}\gamma_{12}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{[Q] = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{E_1}{1-\nu_{12}\nu_{21}} & \dfrac{\nu_{12}E_2}{1-\nu_{12}\nu_{21}} & 0 \\[8pt] \dfrac{\nu_{12}E_2}{1-\nu_{12}\nu_{21}} & \dfrac{E_2}{1-\nu_{12}\nu_{21}} & 0 \\[8pt] 0 & 0 & G_{12} \end{bmatrix}}
$$

**Full 3D Hooke's Law (Transversely Isotropic):**

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \sigma_1 \\ \sigma_2 \\ \sigma_3 \\ \tau_{23} \\ \tau_{13} \\ \tau_{12} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} C_{11} & C_{12} & C_{12} & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ C_{12} & C_{22} & C_{23} & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ C_{12} & C_{23} & C_{22} & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & \tfrac{C_{22}-C_{23}}{2} & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & C_{66} & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & C_{66} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \varepsilon_1 \\ \varepsilon_2 \\ \varepsilon_3 \\ \gamma_{23} \\ \gamma_{13} \\ \gamma_{12} \end{bmatrix}
$$

with $C_{66} = G_{12} = G_{13}$ (transverse isotropy in 2-3 plane).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit errors:** Forgetting to convert E in MPa vs GPa causes **−1 mark per sub-question**.
> 2. **Confusing $\nu_{12}$ and $\nu_{21}$:** Always use the **reciprocal relation** — losing this shows lack of concept ($-2$ marks).
> 3. **Using Voigt for $E_2$:** This is the most common error. $E_2$ uses **Reuss**, not Voigt. Cost: $-2$ to $-3$ marks.
> 4. **Forgetting shape factor in Halpin-Tsai:** State the value of $\xi$ explicitly. Examiner will deduct $-1$ mark if not mentioned.
> 5. **Writing wrong dimension of $[Q]$:** It is a **3×3 matrix** for plane stress, not 6×6. Many students write the full 6×6 $[C]$ by mistake.
> 6. **Not stating assumptions:** In derivation questions, always list **iso-strain OR iso-stress** as a preamble ($-1$ mark if missing).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] A **UD composite ply is transversely isotropic**, requiring only **5 independent elastic constants**: $E_1, E_2, G_{12}, \nu_{12}, G_{23}$ (with $E_3 = E_2$, $G_{13} = G_{12}$, $\nu_{21}$ via reciprocal).
- [ ] **$E_1$ (longitudinal)** uses the **Voigt rule** (iso-strain): $E_1 = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ — exact.
- [ ] **$E_2$ (transverse)** uses the **Reuss rule** (iso-stress): $1/E_2 = V_f/E_f + V_m/E_m$ — lower bound.
- [ ] **$\nu_{12}$** uses Voigt rule; **$\nu_{21}$** uses reciprocal relation $\nu_{21} = \nu_{12} E_2/E_1$.
- [ ] **$G_{12}$** uses **Halpin-Tsai** equation (not simple rule of mixtures).
- [ ] **Out-of-plane effects** ($\sigma_3, \tau_{13}, \tau_{23}$) are **neglected in CLPT** but are real and cause delamination.
- [ ] The **reduced stiffness matrix $[Q]$** is **3×3** for plane stress; full $[C]$ is **6×6**.
- [ ] Volume fractions satisfy $V_f + V_m = 1$ — always check.
- [ ] **Rule of thumb:** $E_1$ dominated by **fiber**, $E_2$ dominated by **matrix**, $G_{12}$ dominated by **matrix**.
- [ ] Carbon/epoxy typical values: $E_1 \approx 130\text{-}140\ \text{GPa}$, $E_2 \approx 8\text{-}10\ \text{GPa}$, $G_{12} \approx 5\text{-}7\ \text{GPa}$.
- [ ] **Free-edge delamination** in cross-ply laminates is the classical out-of-plane failure mode.
- [ ] Thermodynamic constraint: $\det[S] > 0$, requiring $-1 < \nu_{ij} < 1$ for stability.
- [ ] For **Hooke's law in 1-2 plane**: $[\sigma] = [Q][\varepsilon]$; in 3D: $[\sigma] = [C][\varepsilon]$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Stress-Strain Relations of a Unidirectional Composite Subjected to Mechanical, Thermal and Moisture Loads
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT744 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** The Stress-Strain Relations of a Unidirectional Composite Subjected to Mechanical, Thermal and Moisture Loads # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **Unidirectional (UD) Composite Lamina** is a single ply of fiber-reinforced material in which all fibers are aligned in a single direction (the **1-direction** or fiber direction) and are embedded in a matrix. When such a lamina is treated as a homogeneous, **orthotropic continuum**, the **stress-strain relations** describe how the internal stresses $\{\sigma_1, \sigma_2, \sigma_3, \tau_{23}, \tau_{13}, \tau_{12}\}$ are related to the total strains $\{\varepsilon_1, \varepsilon_2, \varepsilon_3, \gamma_{23}, \gamma_{13}, \gamma_{12}\}$ when the lamina is loaded by **mechanical forces, thermal gradients ($\Delta T$) and moisture/hygroscopic absorption ($\Delta M$)** simultaneously. Mathematically, the constitutive (Hooke's) law for a UD composite in the **material principal coordinates (1-2-3)** is written as: $$ \{\sigma\} = [Q]\{\varepsilon^{mech}\} = [Q]\Big(\{\varepsilon\} - \{\varepsilon^{T}\} - \{\varepsilon^{M}\}\Big) $$ where $\{\varepsilon^T\}$ is the free thermal strain vector and $\{\varepsilon^M\}$ is the free moisture (swelling) strain vector. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** A unidirectional lamina is **not isotropic**; it is **transversely isotropic** (symmetric about the fiber axis). It therefore has **5 independent elastic constants** in 3-D: $E_1, E_2, G_{12}, \nu_{12}, \nu_{23}$ (or equivalently, $E_1, E_2, G_{12}, \nu_{12}, G_{23}$). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Bamboo-Reinforced Mud Wall"** > Imagine a thin wall made of mud reinforced with parallel bamboo sticks (this is exactly how traditional Kerala "kavu walls" or modern GFRP laminates work). If you push the wall along the bamboo direction (1-axis), the bamboos carry almost all the load — the wall feels **very stiff** ($E_1$ is high). But if you push it sideways (2-axis), only the soft mud resists — the wall feels **weak and compliant** ($E_2$ is low). > > Now imagine the wall is heated by the sun: the mud wants to swell **more** than the bamboo, creating **internal stresses** even with no external load. If it also absorbs monsoon moisture, the mud swells even more. The **stress-strain relations** are the "master equation" that tells you: *given how much I push, heat, or soak the wall, what are the resulting stresses and deformations?* ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Symbol | Quantity | Typical Range (CFRP/Epoxy) | Units | |:---:|:---|:---:|:---:| | $E_1$ | Young's modulus along fiber | **140 – 200 GPa** | GPa | | $E_2$ | Young's modulus transverse to fiber | **8 – 12 GPa** | GPa | | $G_{12}$ | In-plane shear modulus | **4 – 7 GPa** | GPa | | $\nu_{12}$ | Major Poisson's ratio | **0.25 – 0.35** | — | | $\nu_{21}$ | Minor Poisson's ratio | **0.02 – 0.05** | — | | $\alpha_1$ | CTE along fiber | **−0.5 to 1 × 10⁻⁶ /°C** | /°C | | $\alpha_2$ | CTE transverse to fiber | **20 – 30 × 10⁻⁶ /°C** | /°C | | $\beta_1$ | Moisture expansion coeff. (fiber) | **0 – 0.5** | /% wt | | $\beta_2$ | Moisture expansion coeff. (matrix) | **0.3 – 0.6** | /% wt | --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Framework The derivation of the full constitutive relation is a **four-stage cascade**. Each stage is essential to understand and is routinely examined by KTU. ### Stage 1 — Hooke's Law in 3-D Material Coordinates (1-2-3) A unidirectional lamina is **orthotropic** (three orthogonal planes of material symmetry). The most general 3-D Hooke's law in compliance form is: $$ \{\varepsilon\} = [S]\{\sigma\} $$ Expanding the six strain components: $$ \begin{aligned} \varepsilon_1 &= S_{11}\sigma_1 + S_{12}\sigma_2 + S_{13}\sigma_3 \\ \varepsilon_2 &= S_{21}\sigma_1 + S_{22}\sigma_2 + S_{23}\sigma_3 \\ \varepsilon_3 &= S_{31}\sigma_1 + S_{32}\sigma_2 + S_{33}\sigma_3 \\ \gamma_{23} &= S_{44}\tau_{23} \\ \gamma_{13} &= S_{55}\tau_{13} \\ \gamma_{12} &= S_{66}\tau_{12} \end{aligned} $$ The compliance matrix $[S]$ is symmetric, so $S_{ij}=S_{ji}$, leaving **9 independent constants** in 3-D for a fully orthotropic material. ### Stage 2 — Reduction to Plane Stress (Lamina is Thin) A single ply is usually treated as a **thin lamina** lying in the 1-2 plane. The two faces (top/bottom) are traction-free, so: $$ \sigma_3 = \tau_{23} = \tau_{13} = 0 $$ The 3-D equations collapse to the **reduced 2-D compliance form**: $$ \begin{aligned} \varepsilon_1 &= S_{11}\sigma_1 + S_{12}\sigma_2 \\ \varepsilon_2 &= S_{21}\sigma_1 + S_{22}\sigma_2 \\ \gamma_{12} &= S_{66}\tau_{12} \end{aligned} $$ In matrix form: $$ \begin{Bmatrix} \varepsilon_1 \\ \varepsilon_2 \\ \gamma_{12} \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} S_{11} & S_{12} & 0 \\ S_{21} & S_{22} & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & S_{66} \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} \sigma_1 \\ \sigma_2 \\ \tau_{12} \end{Bmatrix} $$ ### Stage 3 — Express Compliance in Terms of Engineering Constants The reduced compliances are mapped directly to measurable engineering constants: $$ S_{11} = \frac{1}{E_1}, \quad S_{22} = \frac{1}{E_2}, \quad S_{12} = S_{21} = -\frac{\nu_{21}}{E_2} = -\frac{\nu_{12}}{E_1}, \quad S_{66} = \frac{1}{G_{12}} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reciprocity check:** $\dfrac{\nu_{12}}{E_1} = \dfrac{\nu_{21}}{E_2}$ — this is the **Maxwell-Betti reciprocity theorem**, always demanded in KTU derivations. ### Stage 4 — Inversion to Stiffness Matrix [Q] Inverting the reduced compliance gives the **reduced stiffness matrix** $[Q]$: $$ \{\sigma\} = [Q]\{\varepsilon^{mech}\}, \quad [Q] = [S]^{-1} $$ Performing the inversion yields: $$ \begin{aligned} Q_{11} &= \frac{E_1}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}} \\ Q_{22} &= \frac{E_2}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}} \\ Q_{12} = Q_{21} &= \frac{\nu_{12}E_2}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}} = \frac{\nu_{21}E_1}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}} \\ Q_{66} &= G_{12} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > In material coordinates, $[Q]$ is **diagonal except for the coupling term $Q_{12}$**, but **there is no $Q_{16}$ or $Q_{26}$** — this is what makes the 1-2 axes "principal" axes. This fact is a **favourite KTU true/false question**. ## 2.2 Adding Thermal & Moisture (Hygroscopic) Loads When the lamina undergoes a temperature change $\Delta T$ and a moisture change $\Delta M$ (in % weight or saturation fraction), the **free (unrestrained) strains** are: $$ \{\varepsilon^T\} = \begin{Bmatrix} \alpha_1 \\ \alpha_2 \\ 0 \end{Bmatrix}\Delta T, \quad \{\varepsilon^M\} = \begin{Bmatrix} \beta_1 \\ \beta_2 \\ 0 \end{Bmatrix}\Delta M $$ The mechanical strain is the *difference* between the total strain and the free expansion: $$ \{\varepsilon^{mech}\} = \{\varepsilon\} - \{\varepsilon^T\} - \{\varepsilon^M\} $$ Substituting into the stiffness law gives the **generalized constitutive law**: $$ \boxed{\;\begin{Bmatrix} \sigma_1 \\ \sigma_2 \\ \tau_{12} \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} Q_{11} & Q_{12} & 0 \\ Q_{12} & Q_{22} & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & Q_{66} \end{bmatrix} \left[ \begin{Bmatrix} \varepsilon_1 \\ \varepsilon_2 \\ \gamma_{12} \end{Bmatrix} - \begin{Bmatrix} \alpha_1 \\ \alpha_2 \\ 0 \end{Bmatrix}\Delta T - \begin{Bmatrix} \beta_1 \\ \beta_2 \\ 0 \end{Bmatrix}\Delta M \right]\;} $$ Or, in compliance form (more useful for force-resultant analysis): $$ \{\varepsilon\} = [S]\{\sigma\} + \{\alpha\}\Delta T + \{\beta\}\Delta M $$ ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Use | |:---:|:---|:---|:---| | 1 | $S_{11}=1/E_1$, $S_{22}=1/E_2$ | Direct compliances | Stage 2 | | 2 | $S_{12}=-\nu_{21}/E_2 = -\nu_{12}/E_1$ | Coupling compliance | Stage 2 | | 3 | $S_{66}=1/G_{12}$ | Shear compliance | Stage 2 | | 4 | $Q_{11}=E_1/(1-\nu_{12}\nu_{21})$ | Longitudinal stiffness | Stage 4 | | 5 | $Q_{22}=E_2/(1-\nu_{12}\nu_{21})$ | Transverse stiffness | Stage 4 | | 6 | $Q_{12}=\nu_{12}E_2/(1-\nu_{12}\nu_{21})$ | Coupling stiffness | Stage 4 | | 7 | $Q_{66}=G_{12}$ | Shear stiffness | Stage 4 | | 8 | $\varepsilon_i^{T} = \alpha_i \Delta T$ | Free thermal strain | Thermal load | | 9 | $\varepsilon_i^{M} = \beta_i \Delta M$ | Free moisture strain | Hygro load | | 10 | $\{\sigma\}=[Q]\big(\{\varepsilon\}-\{\alpha\}\Delta T - \{\beta\}\Delta M\big)$ | **Master equation** | Final answer | > **Note:** All absolute-value or magnitude bars (e.g., $\vert\Delta T\vert$) are written as `\vert` / `\mid` to prevent markdown table breakage. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility These relations are the **backbone of modern aerospace and automotive design**. Boeing 787 and Airbus A350 fuselages are **50%+ composite by weight**; the residual thermal and hygroscopic stresses computed from these equations determine whether a wing skin **delaminates** during a tropical flight (cabin at -55°C at 35,000 ft vs. +50°C on a humid tarmac). Ignoring moisture absorption has historically caused **naval GRP hulls to crack** in tropical waters. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: From Engineering Constants to Reduced Stiffness Matrix [Q] **Statement to prove:** Given the reduced compliance matrix $[S]$ expressed in engineering constants, derive the four independent entries of $[Q]$. ### Step 1 — Write the reduced stress-strain relation in compliance form We start from the Stage 2 relation: $$ \begin{Bmatrix} \varepsilon_1 \\ \varepsilon_2 \\ \gamma_{12} \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} S_{11} & S_{12} & 0 \\ S_{12} & S_{22} & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & S_{66} \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} \sigma_1 \\ \sigma_2 \\ \tau_{12} \end{Bmatrix} $$ **Logic:** The matrix is symmetric and decoupled (no $\tau_{12}$ appears in the normal-strain equations and vice-versa) because of the orthotropic symmetry of the UD lamina. ### Step 2 — Substitute the engineering constants Replacing each $S_{ij}$ with the corresponding engineering constant: $$ \begin{Bmatrix} \varepsilon_1 \\ \varepsilon_2 \\ \gamma_{12} \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1/E_1 & -\nu_{21}/E_2 & 0 \\ -\nu_{12}/E_1 & 1/E_2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1/G_{12} \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} \sigma_1 \\ \sigma_2 \\ \tau_{12} \end{Bmatrix} $$ **Logic:** The normal strains $\varepsilon_1, \varepsilon_2$ contain both a direct term and a Poisson coupling term; the engineering constants provide the physical meaning of each coefficient. ### Step 3 — Invert the 2×2 block of normal-strain equations The $3\times 3$ matrix is block-diagonal. We can invert the $2\times 2$ upper-left block and the $[1\times 1]$ lower-right block **separately**. For the upper-left block, let $a = 1/E_1$, $b = -\nu_{21}/E_2$, $c = -\nu_{12}/E_1$, $d = 1/E_2$. The determinant is: $$ \Delta = ad - bc = \frac{1}{E_1 E_2} - \left(\frac{-\nu_{21}}{E_2}\right)\!\left(\frac{-\nu_{12}}{E_1}\right) = \frac{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}}{E_1 E_2} $$ **Logic:** Subtracting the product of the off-diagonals from the product of the diagonals is the standard 2×2 matrix inversion rule. ### Step 4 — Apply the 2×2 inversion formula The inverse of a 2×2 matrix $\begin{bmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{bmatrix}$ is $\dfrac{1}{ad-bc}\begin{bmatrix} d & -b \\ -c & a \end{bmatrix}$: $$ \begin{bmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{bmatrix}^{-1} = \frac{1}{\Delta}\begin{bmatrix} d & -b \\ -c & a \end{bmatrix} = \frac{E_1 E_2}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}}\begin{bmatrix} 1/E_2 & \nu_{21}/E_2 \\ \nu_{12}/E_1 & 1/E_1 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Logic:** We have flipped the diagonal and changed the sign of the off-diagonals, then divided by the determinant. ### Step 5 — Simplify each entry Multiplying the matrix by the scalar pre-factor: $$ \frac{E_1 E_2}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}} \cdot \frac{1}{E_2} = \frac{E_1}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}} $$ $$ \frac{E_1 E_2}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}} \cdot \frac{\nu_{21}}{E_2} = \frac{\nu_{21}E_1}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}} $$ The full inverted (stiffness) matrix becomes: $$ [Q] = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{E_1}{1-\nu_{12}\nu_{21}} & \dfrac{\nu_{21}E_1}{1-\nu_{12}\nu_{21}} & 0 \\[8pt] \dfrac{\nu_{12}E_2}{1-\nu_{12}\nu_{21}} & \dfrac{E_2}{1-\nu_{12}\nu_{21}} & 0 \\[8pt] 0 & 0 & G_{12} \end{bmatrix} $$ **Logic:** Because $[Q]$ must be symmetric ($Q_{12} = Q_{21}$), we have two equivalent forms of the same coupling term: $\nu_{12}E_2 = \nu_{21}E_1$. The shear term inverts trivially since $1/(1/G_{12}) = G_{12}$. ### Step 6 — Compact notation (standard KTU form) The **standard KTU reduced stiffness matrix** is finally written as: $$ \boxed{\;[Q] = \begin{bmatrix} Q_{11} & Q_{12} & 0 \\ Q_{12} & Q_{22} & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & Q_{66} \end{bmatrix}\;} $$ with $$ Q_{11} = \frac{E_1}{1-\nu_{12}\nu_{21}}, \quad Q_{22} = \frac{E_2}{1-\nu_{12}\nu_{21}}, \quad Q_{12} = \frac{\nu_{12}E_2}{1-\nu_{12}\nu_{21}}, \quad Q_{66} = G_{12} $$ > **Q.E.D.** ## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Generalized Constitutive Law with Thermal & Moisture Loads **Statement to prove:** Show that the *total strain* $\{\varepsilon\}$ in a UD lamina loaded by mechanical, thermal, and moisture fields can be decomposed as the sum of mechanical, thermal, and hygroscopic parts. ### Step 1 — Define each load mode independently **(a) Pure mechanical load (no temperature, no moisture):** $$ \{\varepsilon^{mech}\} = [S]\{\sigma\} $$ **(b) Pure thermal load (free expansion, no external stress):** $$ \{\varepsilon^T\} = \{\alpha\}\Delta T = \begin{Bmatrix} \alpha_1 \\ \alpha_2 \\ 0 \end{Bmatrix}\Delta T $$ **(c) Pure moisture load (free swelling, no external stress):** $$ \{\varepsilon^M\} = \{\beta\}\Delta M = \begin{Bmatrix} \beta_1 \\ \beta_2 \\ 0 \end{Bmatrix}\Delta M $$ **Logic:** Each cause produces its own strain. In the linear, small-deformation regime (which is the KTU assumption), we can superpose them. ### Step 2 — Apply the principle of superposition The **total strain** observed in the lamina is the sum of the mechanical strain and the free expansion strains: $$ \{\varepsilon\} = \{\varepsilon^{mech}\} + \{\varepsilon^T\} + \{\varepsilon^M\} $$ **Logic:** The fibers and matrix expand freely when heated or wet, and the mechanical response is independent of how much they expanded. This is the **classical lamination theory (CLT) assumption** used in KTU Module 4 as well. ### Step 3 — Solve for mechanical strain Rearranging: $$ \{\varepsilon^{mech}\} = \{\varepsilon\} - \{\varepsilon^T\} - \{\varepsilon^M\} $$ ### Step 4 — Substitute into the mechanical stiffness law The mechanical stress is related to the mechanical strain via the stiffness matrix: $$ \{\sigma\} = [Q]\{\varepsilon^{mech}\} $$ Inserting the expression from Step 3: $$ \{\sigma\} = [Q]\Big(\{\varepsilon\} - \{\alpha\}\Delta T - \{\beta\}\Delta M\Big) $$ ### Step 5 — Expand into component form (verification) For the longitudinal (1) direction: $$ \sigma_1 = Q_{11}(\varepsilon_1 - \alpha_1\Delta T - \beta_1\Delta M) + Q_{12}(\varepsilon_2 - \alpha_2\Delta T - \beta_2\Delta M) $$ For the transverse (2) direction: $$ \sigma_2 = Q_{12}(\varepsilon_1 - \alpha_1\Delta T - \beta_1\Delta M) + Q_{22}(\varepsilon_2 - \alpha_2\Delta T - \beta_2\Delta M) $$ For shear: $$ \tau_{12} = Q_{66}\gamma_{12} $$ **Logic:** Note that **shear is decoupled** from thermal/moisture loads because the CTEs and CMEs in shear are assumed zero (no torsional hygro/thermal expansion for transversely isotropic UD lamina). This is a KTU-exam favourite. ### Step 6 — Invert to obtain compliance form with hygrothermal loads Multiplying both sides by $[S] = [Q]^{-1}$: $$ \{\varepsilon\} = [S]\{\sigma\} + \{\alpha\}\Delta T + \{\beta\}\Delta M $$ **Final form of the general constitutive law:** $$ \boxed{\; \begin{Bmatrix} \varepsilon_1 \\ \varepsilon_2 \\ \gamma_{12} \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} S_{11} & S_{12} & 0 \\ S_{12} & S_{22} & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & S_{66} \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} \sigma_1 \\ \sigma_2 \\ \tau_{12} \end{Bmatrix} + \begin{Bmatrix} \alpha_1 \\ \alpha_2 \\ 0 \end{Bmatrix}\Delta T + \begin{Bmatrix} \beta_1 \\ \beta_2 \\ 0 \end{Bmatrix}\Delta M\;} $$ > **Q.E.D.** ## 3.3 Equivalent (Hygrothermal) Force Resultants for a Lamina of Thickness $h$ For later use in laminate analysis (Module 4), the **thermal and moisture force resultants** are defined by integrating the free strains through the thickness: $$ \{N^T\} = \int_{-h/2}^{h/2} [Q]\{\alpha\}\Delta T \, dz, \quad \{N^M\} = \int_{-h/2}^{h/2} [Q]\{\beta\}\Delta M \, dz $$ For a **single lamina** with uniform $\Delta T, \Delta M$ through thickness: $$ \{N^T\} = h[Q]\{\alpha\}\Delta T, \quad \{N^M\} = h[Q]\{\beta\}\Delta M $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > The same equations apply if one defines a **combined hygrothermal load** $\Delta T^* = \Delta T + \Delta M \cdot (\beta/\alpha)$ (only valid when $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are both non-zero in the same direction). --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Constitutive Law Framework ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeInput1[/"Mechanical Loads
sigma_1, sigma_2, tau_12"/] nodeInput2[/"Thermal Load
Delta T"/] nodeInput3[/"Moisture Load
Delta M"/] nodeMat["Material Coordinates (1-2-3)
Fiber along 1-axis"] nodeS["Compliance Matrix S
3x3 Symmetric"] nodeQ["Stiffness Matrix Q
3x3 Symmetric"] nodeAlpha["Thermal Vector alpha
[alpha1, alpha2, 0]^T"] nodeBeta["Moisture Vector beta
[beta1, beta2, 0]^T"] nodeMech["Mechanical Strain
epsilon_mech = S . sigma"] nodeTherm["Free Thermal Strain
epsilon_T = alpha . DeltaT"] nodeMoist["Free Moisture Strain
epsilon_M = beta . DeltaM"] nodeSum((Sum: Total Strain
epsilon = epsilon_mech + epsilon_T + epsilon_M)) nodeStress(["Output: Stress vector
sigma = Q . (epsilon - alpha.DeltaT - beta.DeltaM)"]) nodeInput1 --> nodeS nodeS --> nodeMech nodeInput2 --> nodeAlpha nodeAlpha --> nodeTherm nodeInput3 --> nodeBeta nodeBeta --> nodeMoist nodeMech --> nodeSum nodeTherm --> nodeSum nodeMoist --> nodeSum nodeSum --> nodeStress nodeMat -. governs .-> nodeQ nodeQ -. inverts .-> nodeS nodeQ --> nodeStress ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Transformation Cascade for Off-Axis Lamina When the fibers are oriented at angle $\theta$ to the global x-axis, the stiffness matrix must be rotated. This shows the engineering workflow. ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA[/"Material Axes (1-2)
Fiber along 1"/] nodeB[/"Global Axes (x-y)
Load applied along x"/] nodeAngle["Off-Axis Angle
theta (degrees)"] nodeT["Transformation Matrix T
Reuter Matrix 3x3"] nodeQbar["Transformed Stiffness Qbar
6 independent entries"] nodeLoad[/"Applied Global Stress
sigma_x, sigma_y, tau_xy"/] nodeStrain[/"Global Strain
epsilon_x, epsilon_y, gamma_xy"/] nodeA -->|"rotate by theta"| nodeT nodeAngle --> nodeT nodeT --> nodeQbar nodeA -->|"Q in material axes"| nodeQbar nodeQbar --> nodeStrain nodeLoad --> nodeStrain nodeB -->|"sigma_x,y,xy applied"| nodeLoad ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Load Decomposition Logic ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeTotal[/"Total External Stimulus"/] nodeMech2["Mechanical sigma"] nodeTherm2["Thermal Delta T"] nodeMoist2["Moisture Delta M"] nodeFree["Free Expansion Strain
epsilon_free = alpha.DeltaT + beta.DeltaM"] nodeMechStrain["Mechanical Strain
epsilon_mech = S . sigma"] nodeTotalStrain((Total Strain epsilon)) nodeStressOut(["Resulting Stress sigma"])) nodeTotal --> nodeMech2 nodeTotal --> nodeTherm2 nodeTotal --> nodeMoist2 nodeMech2 --> nodeMechStrain nodeTherm2 --> nodeFree nodeMoist2 --> nodeFree nodeMechStrain --> nodeTotalStrain nodeFree --> nodeTotalStrain nodeTotalStrain --> nodeStressOut ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each) ### **Question A1** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **"Define a unidirectional composite lamina. Why is the principal material coordinate system (1-2) preferred for writing the stress-strain relations?"** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A unidirectional (UD) composite lamina is a single ply consisting of continuous, parallel fibers of one type embedded in a matrix material, with all fibers aligned in a common direction (typically the **1-direction**). The lamina is treated as a homogeneous, orthotropic continuum (3 marks — full description). The **1-2 principal material coordinate system** is preferred because: - The 1-axis lies along the fibers (the stiffest direction with the highest modulus $E_1$). - The 2-axis is perpendicular to the fibers (the weakest direction with $E_2 \ll E_1$). - In this system, the reduced stiffness matrix $[Q]$ is **decoupled**: $Q_{16} = Q_{26} = 0$, which simplifies all subsequent transformation and lamination calculations. - The off-diagonal coupling term $Q_{12}$ is the **only** coupling — it arises from the Poisson effect, not from shear-extension coupling. (Any 2 of the above 4 points = 3 marks) --- ### **Question A2** [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **"Distinguish between the reduced stiffness matrix [Q] and the transformed reduced stiffness matrix [Q̄]. Under what condition are they identical?"** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **$[Q]$** is the **reduced stiffness matrix in the principal material coordinates (1-2)** of the lamina; it has only **4 independent entries** ($Q_{11}, Q_{12}, Q_{22}, Q_{66}$) and is symmetric with $Q_{16}=Q_{26}=0$ (1 mark). - **$[\bar{Q}]$** is the **transformed (rotated) reduced stiffness matrix** in the **global/structural coordinates (x-y)** when the fibers are at an angle $\theta$ to the x-axis; it has **6 independent entries** ($\bar{Q}_{11}, \bar{Q}_{12}, \bar{Q}_{22}, \bar{Q}_{16}, \bar{Q}_{26}, \bar{Q}_{66}$) and exhibits **shear–extension coupling** (1 mark). - They are **identical** only when the fibers are aligned with the global x-axis, i.e., when $\theta = 0°$ (or $\theta = 90°$, which is just a relabelling of axes). In this case, no rotation is needed and the off-diagonal shear-coupling terms vanish (1 mark). --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks each — Module Internal Choice) ### **Question B-A (Choice 1)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **(a)** *Derive the reduced stiffness matrix $[Q]$ for a unidirectional composite lamina in terms of the engineering constants $E_1, E_2, G_{12}, \nu_{12}, \nu_{21}$. Clearly show the compliance-to-stiffness inversion steps.* **(7 marks)** **(b)** *A unidirectional T300/5208 graphite/epoxy lamina has $E_1 = 181$ GPa, $E_2 = 10.3$ GPa, $G_{12} = 7.17$ GPa, $\nu_{12} = 0.28$. Determine all four components of $[Q]$. If the lamina is cooled by $\Delta T = -100°C$ with $\alpha_1 = 0$, $\alpha_2 = 22.5 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C, find the residual thermal stresses when the lamina is constrained from contracting in both directions ($\varepsilon_1 = \varepsilon_2 = 0$).* **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution — B-A (a)** — 7 marks **Step 1:** Start from reduced compliance in engineering constants (1 mark): $$ [S] = \begin{bmatrix} 1/E_1 & -\nu_{21}/E_2 & 0 \\ -\nu_{12}/E_1 & 1/E_2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1/G_{12} \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 2:** Note the Maxwell-Betti reciprocity $\nu_{21} = \nu_{12}E_2/E_1$ (0.5 mark). **Step 3:** Determinant of the 2×2 normal-strain block (1.5 marks): $$ \Delta = \frac{1}{E_1 E_2} - \frac{\nu_{12}\nu_{21}}{E_1 E_2} = \frac{1-\nu_{12}\nu_{21}}{E_1 E_2} $$ **Step 4:** Inversion of the 2×2 block (2 marks): $$ [Q] = \frac{E_1 E_2}{1-\nu_{12}\nu_{21}}\begin{bmatrix} 1/E_2 & \nu_{21}/E_2 \\ \nu_{12}/E_1 & 1/E_1 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 5:** Final entries and shear inversion (1.5 marks): $$ Q_{11} = \frac{E_1}{1-\nu_{12}\nu_{21}}, \quad Q_{22} = \frac{E_2}{1-\nu_{12}\nu_{21}}, \quad Q_{12} = \frac{\nu_{12}E_2}{1-\nu_{12}\nu_{21}}, \quad Q_{66} = G_{12} $$ **Step 6:** Note $Q_{16} = Q_{26} = 0$ in material coordinates (0.5 mark). --- #### **Model Solution — B-A (b)** — 7 marks **Step 1:** Compute $\nu_{21}$ (0.5 mark): $$ \nu_{21} = \frac{\nu_{12}E_2}{E_1} = \frac{0.28 \times 10.3}{181} = 0.01593 $$ **Step 2:** Compute the denominator (0.5 mark): $$ 1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21} = 1 - (0.28)(0.01593) = 1 - 0.004461 = 0.99554 $$ **Step 3:** Compute each $Q_{ij}$ (2 marks): $$ Q_{11} = \frac{181}{0.99554} = 181.81 \text{ GPa} $$ $$ Q_{22} = \frac{10.3}{0.99554} = 10.346 \text{ GPa} $$ $$ Q_{12} = \frac{0.28 \times 10.3}{0.99554} = 2.897 \text{ GPa} $$ $$ Q_{66} = G_{12} = 7.17 \text{ GPa} $$ **Step 4:** Apply the constrained condition $\varepsilon_1 = \varepsilon_2 = 0$ in the master equation (1 mark): $$ \{\sigma\} = -[Q]\left(\{\alpha\}\Delta T\right) = -[Q]\begin{Bmatrix} 0 \\ 22.5 \times 10^{-6} \\ 0 \end{Bmatrix}(-100) $$ **Step 5:** Compute residual thermal stresses (2 marks): $$ \sigma_1 = -Q_{12}\alpha_2\Delta T = -(2.897)(22.5 \times 10^{-6})(-100) = +6.518 \text{ MPa} $$ $$ \sigma_2 = -Q_{22}\alpha_2\Delta T = -(10.346)(22.5 \times 10^{-6})(-100) = +23.28 \text{ MPa} $$ $$ \tau_{12} = 0 $$ **Step 6:** Physical interpretation (1 mark): Both stresses are **tensile** (positive), as expected when a lamina with positive CTE is cooled while being prevented from contracting — the matrix "pulls" the fibers into tension. --- ### **Question B-B (Choice 2)** [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **(a)** *Starting from first principles, derive the generalized stress-strain relation for a unidirectional lamina subjected to a combined mechanical, thermal ($\Delta T$) and moisture ($\Delta M$) loading. Clearly state the assumptions.* **(7 marks)** **(b)** *For a UD glass/epoxy lamina, the following data are given: $E_1 = 38.6$ GPa, $E_2 = 8.27$ GPa, $G_{12} = 4.14$ GPa, $\nu_{12} = 0.26$, $\alpha_1 = 7.0 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C, $\alpha_2 = 22.5 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C, $\beta_1 = 0$, $\beta_2 = 0.6$ per %wt. The lamina is heated by $\Delta T = +80°C$ and absorbs $\Delta M = 1.0$ %wt. If the lamina is **stress-free** ($\sigma_1 = \sigma_2 = \tau_{12} = 0$), determine the resulting free thermal and moisture strains in the 1 and 2 directions. Comment on the physical significance of $\beta_1 = 0$.* **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution — B-B (a)** — 7 marks **Step 1: Assumptions (1.5 marks)** - Lamina is **thin** (plane stress: $\sigma_3 = \tau_{23} = \tau_{13} = 0$). - Material is **homogeneous and orthotropic** (3 mutually perpendicular planes of symmetry). - **Linear elastic** behaviour (small strains, Hooke's law valid). - **Superposition is valid** — thermal, moisture, and mechanical effects are independent and additive (linear regime). - Temperature and moisture changes are **uniform** through the thickness. **Step 2: Mechanical-only relation (1 mark)** $$ \{\sigma\} = [Q]\{\varepsilon^{mech}\} $$ **Step 3: Define free hygrothermal strains (1 mark)** $$ \{\varepsilon^T\} = \{\alpha\}\Delta T, \quad \{\varepsilon^M\} = \{\beta\}\Delta M $$ **Step 4: Superposition principle (1 mark)** $$ \{\varepsilon\} = \{\varepsilon^{mech}\} + \{\varepsilon^T\} + \{\varepsilon^M\} $$ **Step 5: Solve for mechanical strain and substitute (1.5 marks)** $$ \{\varepsilon^{mech}\} = \{\varepsilon\} - \{\alpha\}\Delta T - \{\beta\}\Delta M $$ $$ \{\sigma\} = [Q]\Big(\{\varepsilon\} - \{\alpha\}\Delta T - \{\beta\}\Delta M\Big) $$ **Step 6: Write in expanded form (1 mark)** $$ \sigma_1 = Q_{11}(\varepsilon_1 - \alpha_1\Delta T - \beta_1\Delta M) + Q_{12}(\varepsilon_2 - \alpha_2\Delta T - \beta_2\Delta M) $$ $$ \sigma_2 = Q_{12}(\varepsilon_1 - \alpha_1\Delta T - \beta_1\Delta M) + Q_{22}(\varepsilon_2 - \alpha_2\Delta T - \beta_2\Delta M) $$ $$ \tau_{12} = Q_{66}\gamma_{12} $$ --- #### **Model Solution — B-B (b)** — 7 marks **Step 1: Identify the stress-free condition** (0.5 mark). When $\sigma_1 = \sigma_2 = \tau_{12} = 0$, the generalized law reduces to: $$ [Q]\Big(\{\varepsilon\} - \{\alpha\}\Delta T - \{\beta\}\Delta M\Big) = \{0\} $$ Since $[Q]$ is non-singular, the parenthesis must vanish: $$ \{\varepsilon\} = \{\alpha\}\Delta T + \{\beta\}\Delta M $$ **Step 2: Compute thermal strains** (1.5 marks): $$ \varepsilon_1^T = \alpha_1 \Delta T = (7.0 \times 10^{-6})(80) = 5.6 \times 10^{-4} $$ $$ \varepsilon_2^T = \alpha_2 \Delta T = (22.5 \times 10^{-6})(80) = 18.0 \times 10^{-4} $$ **Step 3: Compute moisture strains** (1.5 marks): $$ \varepsilon_1^M = \beta_1 \Delta M = (0)(1.0) = 0 $$ $$ \varepsilon_2^M = \beta_2 \Delta M = (0.6)(1.0) = 0.6 $$ **Step 4: Total free strains** (1 mark): $$ \varepsilon_1 = \varepsilon_1^T + \varepsilon_1^M = 5.6 \times 10^{-4} + 0 = 5.6 \times 10^{-4} $$ $$ \varepsilon_2 = \varepsilon_2^T + \varepsilon_2^M = 18.0 \times 10^{-4} + 0.6 = 0.0018 + 0.6 = 0.6018 $$ **Step 5: Physical interpretation of $\beta_1 = 0$** (1.5 marks): > The coefficient $\beta_1$ represents the moisture expansion of the **fibers themselves** along their length. Since most engineering fibers (carbon, glass, aramid/Kevlar) are **ceramic or polymer chains with very low water absorption** along their highly oriented molecular axis, they essentially do not swell longitudinally. Therefore, the **fibers carry no hygroscopic strain** along their axis — the moisture effect is purely a **transverse matrix phenomenon**. This is why $\beta_1 \approx 0$ for nearly all commercial UD composites, and it is the matrix-dominated direction (2-axis) that experiences the majority of swelling. **Step 6: Sanity check** (1 mark): The transverse total strain ($\approx 0.6$ or 60%) is dominated by moisture because $\beta_2 \cdot \Delta M$ is enormous compared to $\alpha_2 \Delta T$. This highlights that **moisture effects can dominate thermal effects** in polymer-matrix composites, especially in marine or tropical environments. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls — KTU 2024 Scheme** > 1. **Sign convention confusion:** KTU uses the **standard mechanics convention** — tensile stresses and strains are **positive**, and the master equation is $\{\sigma\} = [Q]\big(\{\varepsilon\} - \{\alpha\}\Delta T - \{\beta\}\Delta M\big)$. Forgetting the **negative sign** on the free expansion terms costs **1–2 marks** instantly. > 2. **Mixing up $\nu_{12}$ and $\nu_{21}$:** The **major** Poisson's ratio $\nu_{12}$ refers to transverse strain due to longitudinal loading. The relation $\nu_{12}E_2 = \nu_{21}E_1$ **must be used** whenever only one Poisson's ratio is given. Errors here cascade through $Q_{11}, Q_{22}, Q_{12}$. > 3. **Forgetting the determinant $(1-\nu_{12}\nu_{21})$:** Students often write $Q_{11} = E_1$ (wrong!) or $Q_{11} = E_1(1-\nu_{12}\nu_{21})$ (still wrong — sign error). The correct form is $Q_{11} = E_1/(1-\nu_{12}\nu_{21})$. > 4. **Treating $\Delta T$ as positive for cooling:** "Cooled by 100°C" means $\Delta T = -100°C$. A negative $\Delta T$ produces **tensile** thermal stresses when CTE is positive. > 5. **Forgetting units of $\beta$:** $\beta$ is in **per %wt** (or per unit moisture content), **not** a dimensionless ratio. Multiplying $\beta_2$ by $\Delta M$ (in %wt) directly gives a strain. > 6. **Unit mismatch between $\alpha$ and $\Delta T$:** $\alpha$ is in /°C and $\Delta T$ is in °C — the product is dimensionless strain. Mixing $\alpha$ in /K and $\Delta T$ in °C is OK (offset cancels) but mixing per °F causes silent errors. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - [x] A **UD lamina** is treated as a **homogeneous orthotropic continuum** with 5 independent 3-D elastic constants. - [x] In the **principal material coordinates (1-2)**, the reduced stiffness matrix $[Q]$ has only **4 independent entries** ($Q_{11}, Q_{12}, Q_{22}, Q_{66}$). - [x] The **compliance-stiffness relations** are: $S_{11}=1/E_1$, $S_{22}=1/E_2$, $S_{12}=-\nu_{12}/E_1=-\nu_{21}/E_2$, $S_{66}=1/G_{12}$. - [x] The **stiffness entries** are: $Q_{11}=E_1/(1-\nu_{12}\nu_{21})$, $Q_{22}=E_2/(1-\nu_{12}\nu_{21})$, $Q_{12}=\nu_{12}E_2/(1-\nu_{12}\nu_{21})$, $Q_{66}=G_{12}$. - [x] **Maxwell-Betti reciprocity:** $\nu_{12}/E_1 = \nu_{21}/E_2$. - [x] The **generalized constitutive law** (mechanical + thermal + moisture) is: $\{\sigma\} = [Q]\big(\{\varepsilon\} - \{\alpha\}\Delta T - \{\beta\}\Delta M\big)$. - [x] **Shear is decoupled** from hygrothermal loads: $\tau_{12} = Q_{66}\gamma_{12}$ (no $\alpha_6$ or $\beta_6$). - [x] **Free thermal/moisture strains** are additive: $\varepsilon^{free} = \alpha \Delta T + \beta \Delta M$. - [x] The **transformation** to off-axis (x-y) coordinates is via the Reuter matrix: $[\bar{Q}] = [T]^{-1}[Q][T]^{-T}$. - [x] For a **stress-free** lamina, total strain equals free hygrothermal strain: $\varepsilon = \alpha \Delta T + \beta \Delta M$. - [x] $\beta_1 \approx 0$ for most fibers (longitudinal moisture expansion is negligible). - [x] $\alpha_1 \ll \alpha_2$ (fibers suppress longitudinal CTE; matrix dominates transverse CTE). - [x] Residual thermal stresses after curing/cooling: $\{\sigma_R\} = -[Q]\{\alpha\}\Delta T$ (for a fully constrained ply). - [x] The **equivalent hygrothermal load** concept: $\Delta T^* = \Delta T + (\beta/\alpha)\Delta M$ (use with caution — only when $\alpha \neq 0$ in all relevant directions). ---

topics.title: Stress/Strain/Curvature of a Laminate Under Constant Axial Forces and Bending Moments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT744 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Stress/Strain/Curvature of a Laminate Under Constant Axial Forces and Bending Moments

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In **Classical Lamination Theory (CLT)**, a laminate is a stack of *N* bonded laminae (plies), each of thickness *t_k*, oriented at prescribed fibre angles. When the laminate is subjected to constant in-plane **axial forces** {N} = {N_x, N_y, N_xy}ᵀ (units: **N/m** or lb/in) and **bending moments** {M} = {M_x, M_y, M_xy}ᵀ (units: **N·m/m** or lb·in/in) per unit width, the laminate responds by developing:

- **Mid-plane strains** {ε⁰} = {ε_x⁰, ε_y⁰, γ_xy⁰}ᵀ (dimensionless)
- **Mid-plane curvatures** {κ} = {κ_x, κ_y, κ_xy}ᵀ (units: **1/m** or 1/in)

The bridge between the applied loads {N, M} and the kinematic response {ε⁰, κ} is the **ABD stiffness matrix**, a 6×6 symmetric matrix that couples in-plane and flexural behaviour.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** The governing load–deformation relation is
> $$\begin{Bmatrix} N \\ M \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} A & B \\ B & D \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} \varepsilon^0 \\ \kappa \end{Bmatrix}$$
> where **[A]** is the *extensional stiffness*, **[B]** is the *coupling stiffness*, and **[D]** is the *bending stiffness* matrix.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Multi-Layer Cake"

Imagine a **bakery's layered cake** with alternating vanilla and chocolate sponges, each sponge baked at a different tilt (representing different fibre angles). If you push down on the top of the cake with one hand (a force) and simultaneously bend the tray with the other hand (a moment):

- The whole cake **stretches** (mid-plane strain).
- The top surface becomes **curved** (curvature).
- Because the sponges are tilted, pushing down on the cake also makes the tray **twist sideways** — this twisting is the **B-coupling** between extension and bending.

This twist is *unique to laminates*; a homogeneous metal plate would not exhibit it. The ABD matrix mathematically captures exactly how much each layer (sponger) contributes to the cake's (laminate's) overall stiffness and coupling.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants/Metrics Used in This Module**
> - **h** = total laminate thickness (m)
> - **z_k** = distance of ply *k* from laminate mid-plane (m)
> - **Q̄_ij^(k)** = transformed reduced stiffness of ply *k* (Pa or psi)
> - **ν_12, E_1, E_2, G_12** = orthotropic ply elastic constants

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Logic Chain — From Ply Stress to Laminate Loads

The derivation flows in a clear **bottom-up** hierarchy:

1. **Ply Level (Constitutive):** Each orthotropic ply *k* has its own material coordinate system (1–2). Through **stress transformation**, the off-axis reduced stiffnesses Q̄_ij^(k) are obtained.
2. **Strain Field (Kinematics):** Assuming *Kirchhoff–Love plate hypothesis* (straight, inextensible normals), the strain at distance *z* from the mid-plane is:
   $$\{\varepsilon(z)\} = \{\varepsilon^0\} + z\,\{\kappa\}$$
3. **Stress Field (Constitutive × Kinematics):** Substitute the strain field into the constitutive law of ply *k* to obtain σ_x, σ_y, τ_xy at any depth.
4. **Resultant Loads (Equilibrium):** Integrate ply stresses across the laminate thickness to get in-plane forces N and moments M.
5. **ABD Construction:** Collect all integration terms into a single 6×6 stiffness matrix.

### 2.2 The Constitutive Strain Field

The displacement field of a plate (u, v, w) gives rise to a strain field that is **linear in z**:

$$\varepsilon_x = \varepsilon_x^0 + z\,\kappa_x$$
$$\varepsilon_y = \varepsilon_y^0 + z\,\kappa_y$$
$$\gamma_{xy} = \gamma_{xy}^0 + z\,\kappa_{xy}$$

In matrix form: $\{\varepsilon\} = \{\varepsilon^0\} + z\{\kappa\}$.

### 2.3 Ply Stress at a Distance *z* Inside the Laminate

The stress in the *k*th ply (with mid-plane at *z_k*) is obtained by substituting the strain field into the off-axis constitutive equation $\{\sigma\}^{(k)} = [\bar Q]^{(k)} \{\varepsilon\}^{(k)}$:

$$\begin{aligned} \sigma_x^{(k)} &= \bar Q_{11}^{(k)}(\varepsilon_x^0 + z\kappa_x) + \bar Q_{12}^{(k)}(\varepsilon_y^0 + z\kappa_y) + \bar Q_{16}^{(k)}(\gamma_{xy}^0 + z\kappa_{xy}) \\ \sigma_y^{(k)} &= \bar Q_{21}^{(k)}(\varepsilon_x^0 + z\kappa_x) + \bar Q_{22}^{(k)}(\varepsilon_y^0 + z\kappa_y) + \bar Q_{26}^{(k)}(\gamma_{xy}^0 + z\kappa_{xy}) \\ \tau_{xy}^{(k)} &= \bar Q_{61}^{(k)}(\varepsilon_x^0 + z\kappa_x) + \bar Q_{62}^{(k)}(\varepsilon_y^0 + z\kappa_y) + \bar Q_{66}^{(k)}(\gamma_{xy}^0 + z\kappa_{xy}) \end{aligned}$$

### 2.4 Resultant Force and Moment Resultants

**Resultant in-plane forces (N_x, N_y, N_xy)** are obtained by integrating the stresses from *-h/2* to *+h/2*:

$$\{N\} = \int_{-h/2}^{h/2} \{\sigma\}\,dz$$

**Resultant bending moments (M_x, M_y, M_xy)** are obtained by integrating the *moment-arm* (z × stress):

$$\{M\} = \int_{-h/2}^{h/2} \{\sigma\}\,z\,dz$$

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **single most important reference** for solving KTU Module 3 numerical problems. Memorise the ABD integration limits.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|----------|---------|-------|------------------|
| 1 | Extensional Stiffness **A_ij** | $A_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^{N} \bar Q_{ij}^{(k)}(h_k - h_{k-1})$ | N/m | Resistance to in-plane stretching |
| 2 | Coupling Stiffness **B_ij** | $B_{ij} = \tfrac{1}{2}\sum_{k=1}^{N} \bar Q_{ij}^{(k)}(h_k^{2} - h_{k-1}^{2})$ | N | Coupling between stretch & bending |
| 3 | Bending Stiffness **D_ij** | $D_{ij} = \tfrac{1}{3}\sum_{k=1}^{N} \bar Q_{ij}^{(k)}(h_k^{3} - h_{k-1}^{3})$ | N·m | Resistance to bending & twisting |
| 4 | Force Resultant | $N_i = \int_{-h/2}^{h/2} \sigma_i\,dz$ | N/m | In-plane load per unit width |
| 5 | Moment Resultant | $M_i = \int_{-h/2}^{h/2} \sigma_i\,z\,dz$ | N·m/m | Moment per unit width |
| 6 | Ply Stress (compact) | $\{\sigma\}^{(k)} = [\bar Q]^{(k)}(\{\varepsilon^0\} + z\{\kappa\})$ | Pa | Stress in *k*th ply |
| 7 | Inverse ABD | $\begin{Bmatrix} \varepsilon^0 \\ \kappa \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} A & B \\ B & D \end{bmatrix}^{-1}\begin{Bmatrix} N \\ M \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} A^\* & B^\* \\ C^\* & D^\* \end{bmatrix}\begin{Bmatrix} N \\ M \end{Bmatrix}$ | 1/(N/m), 1/(N·m/m) | Compliance form |
| 8 | Symmetric laminate | $B_{ij} = 0$ for all *i, j* | – | No stretch-bend coupling |
| 9 | Cross-ply laminate | $\bar Q_{16} = \bar Q_{26} = 0$ | Pa | Only 0° and 90° plies |
| 10 | Strain at distance z | $\{\varepsilon(z)\} = \{\varepsilon^0\} + z\{\kappa\}$ | – | Kirchhoff–Love hypothesis |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The ABD formulation is the workhorse of the aerospace industry. It is used by **NASA, Boeing, and Airbus** in tools like **Nastran/ABAQUS** to size composite wing skins, helicopter rotor blades, and pressure vessels.

### 2.6 Why the ABD Matrix is Symmetric

Because the strain energy density of a linear elastic laminate is a positive-definite quadratic form, the stiffness tensor must satisfy **Maxwell–Betti reciprocity**, leading to $A_{ij} = A_{ji}$, $D_{ij} = D_{ji}$, and $B_{ij} = B_{ji}$. This symmetry reduces the number of independent coefficients from 36 to 21.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Ply Stress to the ABD Matrix

**Step 1 — Ply Stress in Compact Form**

For the *k*th ply, the off-axis constitutive equation is:

$$\{\sigma\}^{(k)} = [\bar Q]^{(k)} \{\varepsilon\}^{(k)}$$

where $\{\sigma\}^{(k)} = \{\sigma_x, \sigma_y, \tau_{xy}\}^{T(k)}$ and $[\bar Q]^{(k)}$ is the 3×3 transformed reduced stiffness matrix.

**Step 2 — Substitute the Linear Strain Field**

The Kirchhoff–Love hypothesis gives $\{\varepsilon\}^{(k)} = \{\varepsilon^0\} + z\{\kappa\}$.

Therefore:

$$\{\sigma\}^{(k)} = [\bar Q]^{(k)} \{\varepsilon^0\} + z\,[\bar Q]^{(k)} \{\kappa\}$$

**Step 3 — Define the In-Plane Force Resultant {N}**

$$\{N\} = \int_{-h/2}^{h/2} \{\sigma\}\,dz = \int_{-h/2}^{h/2} \bigl([\bar Q]\{\varepsilon^0\} + z[\bar Q]\{\kappa\}\bigr)\,dz$$

Splitting ply-by-ply (since [Q̄] is piecewise constant):

$$\{N\} = \Bigl[\sum_{k=1}^{N} [\bar Q]^{(k)} \int_{h_{k-1}}^{h_k} dz\Bigr]\{\varepsilon^0\} + \Bigl[\sum_{k=1}^{N} [\bar Q]^{(k)} \int_{h_{k-1}}^{h_k} z\,dz\Bigr]\{\kappa\}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the Thickness Integrals**

For ply *k*:

$$\int_{h_{k-1}}^{h_k} dz = h_k - h_{k-1}$$

$$\int_{h_{k-1}}^{h_k} z\,dz = \tfrac{1}{2}\bigl(h_k^{2} - h_{k-1}^{2}\bigr)$$

**Step 5 — Identify the Stiffness Matrices A and B**

$$\{N\} = [A]\{\varepsilon^0\} + [B]\{\kappa\}$$

with:

$$A_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^{N} \bar Q_{ij}^{(k)}\,(h_k - h_{k-1})$$

$$B_{ij} = \tfrac{1}{2}\sum_{k=1}^{N} \bar Q_{ij}^{(k)}\,(h_k^{2} - h_{k-1}^{2})$$

**Step 6 — Define the Moment Resultant {M} and Identify D**

$$\{M\} = \int_{-h/2}^{h/2} \{\sigma\}\,z\,dz = \int_{-h/2}^{h/2} \bigl(z[\bar Q]\{\varepsilon^0\} + z^{2}[\bar Q]\{\kappa\}\bigr)\,dz$$

Ply-by-ply:

$$\{M\} = \Bigl[\sum_{k=1}^{N} [\bar Q]^{(k)} \int_{h_{k-1}}^{h_k} z\,dz\Bigr]\{\varepsilon^0\} + \Bigl[\sum_{k=1}^{N} [\bar Q]^{(k)} \int_{h_{k-1}}^{h_k} z^{2}\,dz\Bigr]\{\kappa\}$$

Evaluating the second moment integral:

$$\int_{h_{k-1}}^{h_k} z^{2}\,dz = \tfrac{1}{3}\bigl(h_k^{3} - h_{k-1}^{3}\bigr)$$

Hence:

$$\{M\} = [B]\{\varepsilon^0\} + [D]\{\kappa\}$$

with:

$$D_{ij} = \tfrac{1}{3}\sum_{k=1}^{N} \bar Q_{ij}^{(k)}\,(h_k^{3} - h_{k-1}^{3})$$

**Step 7 — Assemble the Global Equation**

Combining the two resultants into a single 6×6 system:

$$\begin{Bmatrix} N_x \\ N_y \\ N_{xy} \\ \hdashline M_x \\ M_y \\ M_{xy} \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} A_{11} & A_{12} & A_{16} & B_{11} & B_{12} & B_{16} \\ A_{12} & A_{22} & A_{26} & B_{12} & B_{22} & B_{26} \\ A_{16} & A_{26} & A_{66} & B_{16} & B_{26} & B_{66} \\ \hdashline B_{11} & B_{12} & B_{16} & D_{11} & D_{12} & D_{16} \\ B_{12} & B_{22} & B_{26} & D_{12} & D_{22} & D_{26} \\ B_{16} & B_{26} & B_{66} & D_{16} & D_{26} & D_{66} \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} \varepsilon_x^0 \\ \varepsilon_y^0 \\ \gamma_{xy}^0 \\ \hdashline \kappa_x \\ \kappa_y \\ \kappa_{xy} \end{Bmatrix}$$

This single matrix is the **ABD matrix** of the laminate.

### 3.2 Derivation: Solving for Strains and Curvatures (Inverse Form)

**Step 1 — Recognise the Symmetric 6×6 System**

The ABD matrix is symmetric and (for a stable laminate) positive-definite. It is therefore invertible.

**Step 2 — Partitioned Inversion**

Using the *2×2 block matrix inversion identity*:

$$\begin{bmatrix} A & B \\ B & D \end{bmatrix}^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} A^\* & B^\* \\ C^\* & D^\* \end{bmatrix}$$

the explicit sub-matrices are:

$$A^\* = (A - B D^{-1} B)^{-1}$$

$$B^\* = -A^\* B D^{-1}$$

$$C^\* = -D^\* B A^\*\,\,\,\text{(equivalently } -(D - B A^{-1} B)^{-1} B A^{-1}\text{)}$$

$$D^\* = (D - B A^{-1} B)^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Write the Kinematic Response**

$$\begin{Bmatrix} \varepsilon^0 \\ \kappa \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} A^\* & B^\* \\ C^\* & D^\* \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} N \\ M \end{Bmatrix}$$

In expanded form:

$$\varepsilon_x^0 = A_{11}^\* N_x + A_{12}^\* N_y + A_{16}^\* N_{xy} + B_{11}^\* M_x + B_{12}^\* M_y + B_{16}^\* M_{xy}$$

$$\kappa_x = C_{11}^\* N_x + C_{12}^\* N_y + C_{16}^\* N_{xy} + D_{11}^\* M_x + D_{12}^\* M_y + D_{16}^\* M_{xy}$$

(and analogous equations for ε_y⁰, γ_xy⁰, κ_y, κ_xy).

**Step 4 — Ply Stress Recovery**

Once ε⁰ and κ are known, the stress in ply *k* at any *z* is:

$$\{\sigma\}^{(k)}(z) = [\bar Q]^{(k)} \bigl(\{\varepsilon^0\} + z\{\kappa\}\bigr)$$

In expanded form (for general angle-ply laminates):

$$\begin{aligned} \sigma_x^{(k)} &= \bar Q_{11}^{(k)}(\varepsilon_x^0 + z\kappa_x) + \bar Q_{12}^{(k)}(\varepsilon_y^0 + z\kappa_y) + \bar Q_{16}^{(k)}(\gamma_{xy}^0 + z\kappa_{xy}) \\ \sigma_y^{(k)} &= \bar Q_{12}^{(k)}(\varepsilon_x^0 + z\kappa_x) + \bar Q_{22}^{(k)}(\varepsilon_y^0 + z\kappa_y) + \bar Q_{26}^{(k)}(\gamma_{xy}^0 + z\kappa_{xy}) \\ \tau_{xy}^{(k)} &= \bar Q_{16}^{(k)}(\varepsilon_x^0 + z\kappa_x) + \bar Q_{26}^{(k)}(\varepsilon_y^0 + z\kappa_y) + \bar Q_{66}^{(k)}(\gamma_{xy}^0 + z\kappa_{xy}) \end{aligned}$$

These are evaluated at the **top and bottom** of each ply (z = ±t_k/2) for first-ply failure analysis (Tsai–Wu / Tsai–Hill criteria).

### 3.3 Special Cases (High-Yield for KTU)

| Case | Condition | Consequence |
|------|-----------|-------------|
| **Symmetric Laminate** | Stack is mirror-symmetric about z = 0 | $B_{ij} = 0$ — no stretch–bend coupling |
| **Cross-Ply Laminate** | Plies at 0° and 90° only | $\bar Q_{16} = \bar Q_{26} = 0$ — no extension–shear or bend–twist coupling |
| **Balanced Laminate** | For every +θ ply there is a –θ ply | $A_{16} = A_{26} = 0$ |
| **Quasi-Isotropic** | Equal fractions of 0°, ±60°, 90° | $A_{ij} \propto \delta_{ij}$ (isotropic in-plane) |
| **Unsymmetric, Unbalanced** | General case | All A, B, D terms non-zero; full 6×6 coupling |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of Classical Lamination Theory

The following block diagram maps the **complete computational workflow** from raw ply data to recovered ply stresses — the exact sequence a KTU student must follow in a 14-mark derivation question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Ply Data: E1, E2, G12, nu12, theta, tk, zk] --> B[Compute Q11, Q22, Q12, Q66]
    B --> C[Transform: Qbar_ij from Qij and theta]
    C --> D[Loop over plies k=1 to N]
    D --> E[Compute Aij, Bij, Dij via thickness integrals]
    E --> F[Assemble 6x6 ABD Matrix]
    F --> G[Apply loads Nx Ny Nxy Mx My Mxy]
    G --> H[Invert ABD to get A* B* C* D*]
    H --> I[Solve epsilon0 and kappa]
    I --> J[Recover sigma in each ply at z = plus or minus tk/2]
    J --> K[Apply Failure Criterion: Tsai-Wu or Tsai-Hill]
    K --> L[Report First Ply Failure Load]
```

### 4.2 Laminate Stacking & Coordinate System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Ply N top: plus thetaN, z = h/2] --> P2[Ply N minus 1]
    P2 --> P3[Ply 3]
    P3 --> P4[Ply 2]
    P4 --> P5[Ply 1 bottom: plus theta1, z = minus h/2]
    AX[z axis: through thickness] -.-> P1
    AX -.-> P5
    MID[Mid plane z = 0] -.-> P3
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram of the ABD Matrix Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ABD[ABD Matrix 6x6 Symmetric] --> TOP[Top Left 3x3: A Extensional]
    ABD --> BOT[Bottom Right 3x3: D Bending]
    ABD --> OFF[Off Diagonal 3x3: B Coupling]
    TOP --> A1[In plane N to epsilon0]
    BOT --> D1[Moment M to curvature kappa]
    OFF --> B1[N causes bending AND M causes stretching]
    B1 --> EX[Physical Coupling: bending twisting warping]
```

### 4.4 Cause-Effect Chain: Load → Deformation → Stress

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L[Applied Loads: Nx Ny Nxy and Mx My Mxy] --> R[ABD Matrix]
    R --> K[Mid Plane Strains epsilon0 and Curvatures kappa]
    K --> S[Ply Stress: sigma x, sigma y, tau xy as functions of z]
    S --> F[Failure Check at top and bottom of each ply]
    F --> O[Safe or First Ply Failure Indication]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Aid:** Every box in these diagrams corresponds to a **single equation** in Section 3. When a KTU question asks "Derive the stress in a ply", you are essentially walking through **Boxes A → I → S** above.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper PEPOT744]**
**Define the A, B, and D matrices of a laminate. State the physical significance of each with their units.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **A matrix (Extensional Stiffness):** $A_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^{N} \bar Q_{ij}^{(k)}(h_k - h_{k-1})$
  - Relates in-plane force resultants {N} to mid-plane strains {ε⁰}.
  - Units: **N/m** (force per unit length). **(1 mark)**

- **B matrix (Coupling Stiffness):** $B_{ij} = \tfrac{1}{2}\sum_{k=1}^{N} \bar Q_{ij}^{(k)}(h_k^{2} - h_{k-1}^{2})$
  - Couples in-plane forces with curvatures and moments with mid-plane strains.
  - For a **symmetric** laminate, B = 0.
  - Units: **N** (force). **(1 mark)**

- **D matrix (Bending Stiffness):** $D_{ij} = \tfrac{1}{3}\sum_{k=1}^{N} \bar Q_{ij}^{(k)}(h_k^{3} - h_{k-1}^{3})$
  - Relates moment resultants {M} to mid-plane curvatures {κ}.
  - Units: **N·m** (moment). **(1 mark)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, PEPOT744]**
**What is meant by "stretch–bend coupling" in a laminate? Under what stacking condition does it vanish?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Stretch–bend coupling is the phenomenon in which an **in-plane force** produces not only mid-plane strain but also **curvature** (bending), and conversely a **bending moment** produces both curvature and mid-plane strain. **(1.5 marks)**

Mathematically, it arises from the off-diagonal sub-matrix **[B]** in the ABD matrix. The governing coupled equation is:

$$\{N\} = [A]\{\varepsilon^0\} + [B]\{\kappa\}$$

$$\{M\} = [B]\{\varepsilon^0\} + [D]\{\kappa\}$$

It vanishes (**B_ij = 0**) when the laminate is **symmetric about its mid-plane**, i.e., for every ply at +z there is an identical ply at –z with the same orientation. **(1.5 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**  [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper]

**(a)** Derive the load–deformation relationship for a multi-ply laminate, clearly defining the A, B, and D stiffness matrices. State the Kirchhoff–Love hypotheses used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A symmetric cross-ply laminate has the stacking sequence **[0°/90°/90°/0°]**, each ply of thickness **0.125 mm**. The material properties are:
$E_1 = 138\,\text{GPa}$, $E_2 = 9\,\text{GPa}$, $G_{12} = 6.9\,\text{GPa}$, $\nu_{12} = 0.30$.

Determine the **A and D matrices** for the laminate. Take $\nu_{21} = \nu_{12} \cdot E_2 / E_1$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution – Question A (a)** [7 marks]

**Step 1: Kirchhoff–Love Hypotheses (1 mark)**

1. Straight, inextensible normals remain straight and perpendicular to the mid-surface after deformation.
2. The transverse normal stress σ_z ≈ 0 (plane stress).
3. No shear deformation in the transverse direction (γ_xz = γ_yz = 0).

**Step 2: Strain Field (1.5 marks)**

$$\{\varepsilon(z)\} = \{\varepsilon^0\} + z\{\kappa\}$$

**Step 3: Stress in Ply k (1.5 marks)**

$$\{\sigma\}^{(k)} = [\bar Q]^{(k)}\bigl(\{\varepsilon^0\} + z\{\kappa\}\bigr)$$

**Step 4: Resultant Forces and Moments (1.5 marks)**

$$\{N\} = \int_{-h/2}^{h/2}\{\sigma\}\,dz, \quad \{M\} = \int_{-h/2}^{h/2}\{\sigma\}\,z\,dz$$

**Step 5: Identify A, B, D (1.5 marks)**

$$A_{ij} = \sum \bar Q_{ij}^{(k)}(h_k - h_{k-1}), \quad B_{ij} = \tfrac{1}{2}\sum \bar Q_{ij}^{(k)}(h_k^{2} - h_{k-1}^{2}), \quad D_{ij} = \tfrac{1}{3}\sum \bar Q_{ij}^{(k)}(h_k^{3} - h_{k-1}^{3})$$

Final equation:

$$\begin{Bmatrix} N \\ M \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} A & B \\ B & D \end{bmatrix}\begin{Bmatrix} \varepsilon^0 \\ \kappa \end{Bmatrix}$$

---

#### **Model Solution – Question A (b)** [7 marks]

**Step 1: Reduced Stiffnesses Q_ij (0.5 mark)**

$$\nu_{21} = 0.30 \times 9/138 = 0.01957$$

$$Q_{11} = \frac{E_1}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}} = \frac{138}{1 - (0.30)(0.01957)} = 138.81\,\text{GPa}$$

$$Q_{22} = \frac{E_2}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}} = \frac{9}{0.9941} = 9.053\,\text{GPa}$$

$$Q_{12} = \frac{\nu_{12} E_2}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}} = 2.716\,\text{GPa}$$

$$Q_{66} = G_{12} = 6.9\,\text{GPa}$$

**Step 2: For 0° Ply** (transformation: $\bar Q_{ij} = Q_{ij}$)

$\bar Q_{11} = 138.81$, $\bar Q_{22} = 9.053$, $\bar Q_{12} = 2.716$, $\bar Q_{66} = 6.9$ GPa, $\bar Q_{16} = \bar Q_{26} = 0$.

**Step 3: For 90° Ply** (transformation: swap indices 1 ↔ 2, flip sign of Q₁₂)

$\bar Q_{11} = 9.053$, $\bar Q_{22} = 138.81$, $\bar Q_{12} = 2.716$, $\bar Q_{66} = 6.9$ GPa, $\bar Q_{16} = \bar Q_{26} = 0$.

**Step 4: A Matrix (2 marks)**

Since 0° and 90° plies each occupy 50% of the thickness, $A_{ij} = \bar Q_{ij}^{0°} \cdot h/2 + \bar Q_{ij}^{90°} \cdot h/2$, with $h = 4 \times 0.125 = 0.5$ mm = 0.0005 m.

$$A_{11} = (138.81 + 9.053)/2 \times 0.0005 \times 10^9 = 73.93 \times 10^6\,\text{N/m}$$

$$A_{22} = (9.053 + 138.81)/2 \times 0.0005 \times 10^9 = 73.93 \times 10^6\,\text{N/m}$$

$$A_{12} = 2.716 \times 0.0005 \times 10^9 = 1.358 \times 10^6\,\text{N/m}$$

$$A_{66} = 6.9 \times 0.0005 \times 10^9 = 3.45 \times 10^6\,\text{N/m}$$

$$A_{16} = A_{26} = 0$$

$$[A] = 10^6 \begin{bmatrix} 73.93 & 1.358 & 0 \\ 1.358 & 73.93 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 3.45 \end{bmatrix}\,\text{N/m}$$

**Step 5: D Matrix (2 marks)**

For a symmetric cross-ply laminate with stacking [0/90/90/0]:

Ply interfaces: z = –0.25, –0.125, 0, +0.125, +0.25 mm (in m: –2.5e-4, –1.25e-4, 0, +1.25e-4, +2.5e-4).

| Ply | Range (m) | Material |
|-----|-----------|----------|
| 1 (bottom 0°) | –2.5e-4 → –1.25e-4 | 0° |
| 2 (90°) | –1.25e-4 → 0 | 90° |
| 3 (90°) | 0 → +1.25e-4 | 90° |
| 4 (top 0°) | +1.25e-4 → +2.5e-4 | 0° |

$$D_{11} = \tfrac{1}{3}\sum \bar Q_{11}^{(k)}(h_k^{3} - h_{k-1}^{3})$$

For each ply: $(h_k^{3} - h_{k-1}^{3}) = (0.000125)^{3} - (0)^{3} = 1.953 \times 10^{-12}\,\text{m}^{3}$ (and similarly for symmetric plies).

$$D_{11} = \tfrac{1}{3}\bigl[138.81 \times 1.953\text{e-}12 \times 2 + 9.053 \times 1.953\text{e-}12 \times 2\bigr] \times 10^9$$

$$D_{11} = \tfrac{1}{3}\times 1.953\text{e-}12 \times 295.73 \times 10^9 = 192.5\,\text{N·m}$$

$$D_{22} = 192.5\,\text{N·m} \quad (\text{by symmetry of stacking})$$

$$D_{12} = \tfrac{1}{3}\times 2.716 \times 10^9 \times 4 \times 1.953\text{e-}12 = 7.07\,\text{N·m}$$

$$D_{66} = \tfrac{1}{3}\times 6.9 \times 10^9 \times 4 \times 1.953\text{e-}12 = 17.97\,\text{N·m}$$

$$[D] = \begin{bmatrix} 192.5 & 7.07 & 0 \\ 7.07 & 192.5 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 17.97 \end{bmatrix}\,\text{N·m}$$

**B = 0** due to symmetric stacking. ✓

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**  [KTU University Exam – June 2024 / PEPOT744 Model]

**(a)** With the help of neat sketches, explain the physical significance of the **[A], [B], and [D]** matrices. Differentiate between a **symmetric** and an **antisymmetric** laminate with reference to the B matrix. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A four-ply laminate has the stacking sequence **[+45°/–45°/+45°/–45°]** with each ply of thickness **0.2 mm**. Material: $E_1 = 140\,\text{GPa}$, $E_2 = 10\,\text{GPa}$, $G_{12} = 5\,\text{GPa}$, $\nu_{12} = 0.3$.

Compute the **transformed reduced stiffnesses** $\bar Q_{ij}$ for ±45° and the **B matrix**. State whether the laminate is symmetric. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution – Question B (a)** [7 marks]

**Physical Significance (4.5 marks):**

- **[A] — Extensional Stiffness:** Determines the in-plane force resultants required to produce unit mid-plane strains. A high $A_{11}$ means the laminate strongly resists stretching in the x-direction. It is computed as a *first moment* of stiffness across thickness. **(1.5 marks)**

- **[B] — Coupling Stiffness:** Couples stretching and bending. A non-zero B means an applied axial force will *also* bend the plate (and a moment will *also* stretch the mid-plane). Computed as a *second moment*. **(1.5 marks)**

- **[D] — Bending Stiffness:** Determines the moment resultants required to produce unit curvatures. A high $D_{11}$ means the laminate strongly resists bending about the y-axis. Computed as a *third moment*. **(1.5 marks)**

**Symmetric vs Antisymmetric (2.5 marks):**

- **Symmetric laminate:** Stacking is a mirror image about the geometric mid-plane. Example: [0/45/45/0]. For each ply at +z there is an identical ply at –z. Therefore $\sum z_k \, \bar Q_{ij}^{(k)} = 0$, which makes $B_{ij} = 0$. The plate bends without twisting or warping when stretched.

- **Antisymmetric laminate:** Ply orientations are mirrored *and* sign-flipped (e.g., [0/45/–45/0] or [0/90/0/90]). The B matrix is non-zero, producing the **warping** and **bend–twist coupling** effects used in **twist morphing wings** of modern UAVs.

---

#### **Model Solution – Question B (b)** [7 marks]

**Step 1: Q_ij (0.5 mark)**

$1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21} = 1 - (0.3)(0.3 \times 10/140) = 0.9936$

$Q_{11} = 140/0.9936 = 140.90$ GPa, $Q_{22} = 10/0.9936 = 10.06$ GPa, $Q_{12} = (0.3 \times 10)/0.9936 = 3.02$ GPa, $Q_{66} = 5$ GPa.

**Step 2: Transformed Q̄ for +45° (1.5 marks)**

Using $c = s = \cos 45° = \sin 45° = 1/\sqrt{2}$, $c^{2} = s^{2} = 0.5$, $cs = 0.5$:

$$\bar Q_{11} = Q_{11}c^{4} + 2(Q_{12} + 2Q_{66})c^{2}s^{2} + Q_{22}s^{4}$$
$$= 140.90(0.0625) + 2(3.02 + 10)(0.25) + 10.06(0.0625)$$
$$= 8.806 + 6.51 + 0.629 = 15.95\,\text{GPa}$$

$$\bar Q_{22} = Q_{11}s^{4} + 2(Q_{12} + 2Q_{66})c^{2}s^{2} + Q_{22}c^{4} = 15.95\,\text{GPa}$$

$$\bar Q_{12} = (Q_{11} + Q_{22} - 4Q_{66})c^{2}s^{2} + Q_{12}(c^{4} + s^{4})$$
$$= (140.90 + 10.06 - 20)(0.25) + 3.02(0.5) = 32.74 + 1.51 = 34.25\,\text{GPa}$$

(Wait — recalculate: $c^{2}s^{2} = 0.25$, $c^{4} + s^{4} = 0.5$.)

$$\bar Q_{12} = (140.90 + 10.06 - 4 \times 5)(0.25) + 3.02(0.5) = 130.96 \times 0.25 + 1.51 = 32.74 + 1.51 = 34.25$$

(Correction: $Q_{11} + Q_{22} - 4Q_{66} = 140.90 + 10.06 - 20 = 130.96$.)

$$\bar Q_{66} = (Q_{11} + Q_{22} - 2Q_{12} - 2Q_{66})c^{2}s^{2} + Q_{66}(c^{4} + s^{4})$$
$$= (140.90 + 10.06 - 6.04 - 10)(0.25) + 5(0.5) = 134.92 \times 0.25 + 2.5 = 33.73 + 2.5 = 36.23\,\text{GPa}$$

$$\bar Q_{16} = (Q_{11} - Q_{12} - 2Q_{66})c^{3}s + (Q_{12} - Q_{22} + 2Q_{66})cs^{3}$$
$$= (140.90 - 3.02 - 10)(0.3536) + (3.02 - 10.06 + 10)(0.3536) = 127.88 \times 0.3536 + 2.96 \times 0.3536 = 45.23 + 1.05 = 46.28\,\text{GPa}$$

$$\bar Q_{26} = (Q_{11} - Q_{12} - 2Q_{66})cs^{3} + (Q_{12} - Q_{22} + 2Q_{66})c^{3}s = 46.28\,\text{GPa}$$

So for **+45°**:
$\bar Q_{11} = \bar Q_{22} = 15.95$, $\bar Q_{12} = 34.25$, $\bar Q_{66} = 36.23$, $\bar Q_{16} = \bar Q_{26} = 46.28$ GPa.

**Step 3: Transformed Q̄ for –45° (1 mark)**

Replacing θ by –θ flips the sign of $\bar Q_{16}$ and $\bar Q_{26}$ only:

$\bar Q_{11} = 15.95$, $\bar Q_{22} = 15.95$, $\bar Q_{12} = 34.25$, $\bar Q_{66} = 36.23$, $\bar Q_{16} = \bar Q_{26} = -46.28$ GPa.

**Step 4: B Matrix (3 marks)**

Ply coordinates (total thickness h = 4 × 0.2 = 0.8 mm = 8e-4 m; z measured from mid-plane):

| Ply | z_bottom (m) | z_top (m) | Material |
|-----|--------------|-----------|----------|
| 1 | –4e-4 | –2e-4 | +45° |
| 2 | –2e-4 | 0 | –45° |
| 3 | 0 | +2e-4 | +45° |
| 4 | +2e-4 | +4e-4 | –45° |

$$B_{ij} = \tfrac{1}{2}\sum_{k=1}^{4} \bar Q_{ij}^{(k)}(z_k^{2} - z_{k-1}^{2})$$

Each ply: $(z_k^{2} - z_{k-1}^{2}) = 4 \times 10^{-8} - 1.6 \times 10^{-7} = ...$. Let us recompute:

Ply 1: $z_1^{2} - z_0^{2} = (2e-4)^{2} - (4e-4)^{2} = 4e-8 - 16e-8 = -12e-8\,\text{m}^{2}$
Ply 2: $z_2^{2} - z_1^{2} = 0 - 4e-8 = -4e-8\,\text{m}^{2}$
Ply 3: $z_3^{2} - z_2^{2} = 4e-8 - 0 = 4e-8\,\text{m}^{2}$
Ply 4: $z_4^{2} - z_3^{2} = 16e-8 - 4e-8 = 12e-8\,\text{m}^{2}$

**B₁₁:** $\tfrac{1}{2}[15.95(-12\text{e-}8) + 15.95(-4\text{e-}8) + 15.95(4\text{e-}8) + 15.95(12\text{e-}8)] \times 10^9$
$= \tfrac{1}{2} \times 15.95 \times 0 \times 10 \times 10^9 = 0$

**B₁₂:** Similarly = 0 (since $\bar Q_{12}$ is the same for +45° and –45°).

**B₁₆:** $\tfrac{1}{2}[46.28(-12\text{e-}8) + (-46.28)(-4\text{e-}8) + 46.28(4\text{e-}8) + (-46.28)(12\text{e-}8)] \times 10^9$
$= \tfrac{1}{2} \times 46.28 \times 10^9 \times 10^{-8} \times [-12 + 4 + 4 - 12] = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 46.28 \times 10 \times (-16)$
$= -3702.4\,\text{N}$ (magnitude non-zero)

**B₂₆:** Same as B₁₆ = –3702.4 N.

**B₆₆:** $\tfrac{1}{2}[36.23(-12\text{e-}8) + 36.23(-4\text{e-}8) + 36.23(4\text{e-}8) + 36.23(12\text{e-}8)] \times 10^9 = 0$

**B₂₂:** Same as B₁₁ = 0.

**Final B Matrix (1 mark):**

$$[B] = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & -3702.4 \\ 0 & 0 & -3702.4 \\ -3702.4 & -3702.4 & 0 \end{bmatrix}\,\text{N}$$

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):** The laminate is **symmetric** (mirror stack [+45/–45/–45/+45] in terms of z-positions) but **not balanced**; B is non-zero only in the extension–shear coupling terms, characteristic of a balanced but unsymmetric lay-up.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Sign error in B₁₆:** When computing $[B]$, students often forget that for **–θ** plies, only $\bar Q_{16}$ and $\bar Q_{26}$ flip sign. Forgetting this gives a wrong B matrix. (–2 marks)
> 2. **Unit mismatch in A vs D:** A is in **N/m**, B in **N**, D in **N·m**. Mixing units in the inverse-form problem causes the answer to be off by a factor of **h** or **h²**. (–2 marks)
> 3. **Forgetting that B = 0 for symmetric laminates:** Always state the stacking symmetry first — it is worth **1 mark** by itself.
> 4. **Strain-field assumption:** Examiners award the first mark for clearly stating the **Kirchhoff–Love hypotheses** before writing $\{\varepsilon\} = \{\varepsilon^0\} + z\{\kappa\}$. Skipping this loses 1 mark immediately.
> 5. **Ply stress sign error:** σ_x in the top ply uses **+z = +h/2**; bottom ply uses **z = –h/2**. Confusing these gives the wrong sign of curvature contribution. (–1 mark)

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Must Memorise for KTU ESE**

- ☐ The ABD master equation: $\{N, M\}^T = [A, B; B, D] \{\varepsilon^0, \kappa\}^T$ — **one-line definition worth 1 mark**.
- ☐ All three integration formulas for **A, B, D** with thickness powers **1, 2, 3** and prefactors **1, 1/2, 1/3** — frequently asked in Part A.
- ☐ **Kirchhoff–Love plate hypotheses** — write them before any derivation.
- ☐ Strain field $\{\varepsilon\} = \{\varepsilon^0\} + z\{\kappa\}$ — the central kinematic equation of the module.
- ☐ **Symmetric laminate ⇒ B = 0**; **Balanced laminate ⇒ A₁₆ = A₂₆ = 0**; **Cross-ply ⇒ Q̄₁₆ = Q̄₂₆ = 0** — these three single-line results are tested every semester.
- ☐ ABD matrix is **6×6 symmetric**; hence 21 independent constants.
- ☐ Units: **[A] = N/m, [B] = N, [D] = N·m** — remember the order (extensional, coupling, bending).
- ☐ Inverse ABD: $A^* = (A - BD^{-1}B)^{-1}$, $D^* = (D - BA^{-1}B)^{-1}$, $B^* = -A^*BD^{-1}$, $C^* = -D^*BA^{-1}$.
- ☐ Ply stress recovery: $\{\sigma\}^{(k)} = [\bar Q]^{(k)}(\{\varepsilon^0\} + z\{\kappa\})$ — evaluated at **z = ±t_k/2** for failure analysis.
- ☐ **Bend–twist coupling** in antisymmetric angle-ply laminates is exploited in **morphing aircraft wings** (real-world application).
- ☐ The ABD matrix is positive-definite for a stable laminate ⇒ its inverse always exists.
- ☐ For **quasi-isotropic** lay-ups (0°/±60°/90° with equal fractions), $A_{11} = A_{22}$, $A_{16} = A_{26} = 0$, and $A_{66} = (A_{11} - A_{12})/2$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Unusual Behaviours Which May Occur in Laminates Such as Bending/Stretching Coupling and Stretching-Shear Coupling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT744 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** The Unusual Behaviours Which May Occur in Laminates Such as Bending/Stretching Coupling and Stretching-Shear Coupling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 — The Unusual Behaviours in Laminates: Coupling Effects

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (Classical Lamination Theory — CLT Framework)

A **laminate** is a stack of bonded unidirectional or fabric plies (called *laminae*). Under the assumptions of **Classical Lamination Theory (CLT)**, the laminate is described by the **constitutive equation**:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} N \\ M \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} A & B \\ B & D \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \varepsilon^{0} \\ \kappa \end{bmatrix}
$$

Where:
- $[N]$ = **Resultant in-plane force vector** (N/m) — $\{N_x, N_y, N_{xy}\}^T$
- $[M]$ = **Resultant moment vector** (N·m/m) — $\{M_x, M_y, M_{xy}\}^T$
- $[\varepsilon^0]$ = **Mid-plane strains** — $\{\varepsilon_x^0, \varepsilon_y^0, \gamma_{xy}^0\}^T$
- $[\kappa]$ = **Mid-plane curvatures and twist** — $\{\kappa_x, \kappa_y, \kappa_{xy}\}^T$

The three sub-matrices have specific physical meanings:
- **$[A]$ — Extension Stiffness Matrix** (units: N/m)
- **$[B]$ — Coupling Stiffness Matrix (Bending–Stretching)** (units: N)
- **$[D]$ — Bending Stiffness Matrix** (units: N·m)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **"Unusual behaviours"** in laminates refer to **coupling effects** — phenomena where applying one type of load (e.g., axial force) produces a *different* type of response (e.g., bending, twisting, or shear deformation) that would never occur in a homogeneous isotropic material like steel or aluminium. These couplings are **direct consequences of the heterogeneous, anisotropic, multi-ply architecture** of composite laminates.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Why Do Couplings Occur?

Imagine a stack of **playing cards glued together with rubber cement**, where every alternate card is painted at **45°** to the next.

- If you **stretch** this stack by pulling on its ends, the stack **bends to one side** because the off-axis plies try to shear and rotate as they elongate. This is **Bending–Stretching Coupling**.
- If you **twist** the stack (like wringing a towel), the stack also **bends out of plane**. This is **Bending–Twist Coupling** (related, but governed by the D-matrix).
- If you **stretch** the stack along its length, it **also shears sideways** — the originally rectangular cross-section deforms into a parallelogram. This is **Stretching–Shear Coupling**.

> [!NOTE]
> In a **homogeneous isotropic material** (e.g., a steel sheet), these couplings are **mathematically forbidden by symmetry**. The presence of off-diagonal terms in $[A]$, $[B]$, or $[D]$ matrices is the **mathematical signature** of the unusual behaviour.

### 1.3 The Three Principal Coupling Phenomena (Syllabus Highlight)

| # | Coupling Type | Governing Sub-matrix | Triggering Load | Resulting Response |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Bending–Stretching Coupling** | $[B]$ (off-diagonals or diagonals) | Axial force $N_x$ **or** Bending moment $M_x$ | Curvature $\kappa_x$ **and** mid-plane strain $\varepsilon_x^0$ simultaneously |
| 2 | **Stretching–Shear Coupling** | $[A_{16}]$, $[A_{26}]$ (off-diagonals of $[A]$) | Normal force $N_x$ or $N_y$ | Shear strain $\gamma_{xy}^0$ also develops |
| 3 | **Bending–Twist Coupling** | $[D_{16}]$, $[D_{26}]$ (off-diagonals of $[D]$) | Bending moment $M_x$ or $M_y$ | Twist curvature $\kappa_{xy}$ also develops |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The user's topic explicitly asks about **Bending/Stretching Coupling** and **Stretching-Shear Coupling**. Bending–Twist Coupling is mentioned only for completeness and to contrast it.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->
