# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of the Lamina Stiffness Contribution

Each ply $k$ of thickness $h_k$ located at a distance $z_k$ from the laminate mid-plane contributes to the ABD matrices through integration across the laminate thickness $H$:

$$
A_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^{N} \left( \bar{Q}_{ij} \right)_k (h_k - h_{k-1})
$$

$$
B_{ij} = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{k=1}^{N} \left( \bar{Q}_{ij} \right)_k (h_k^2 - h_{k-1}^2)
$$

$$
D_{ij} = \frac{1}{3} \sum_{k=1}^{N} \left( \bar{Q}_{ij} \right)_k (h_k^3 - h_{k-1}^3)
$$

Where $(\bar{Q}_{ij})_k$ is the **transformed reduced stiffness** of ply $k$ in the laminate coordinate system, obtained by applying the standard transformation equations to the on-axis ply stiffnesses $Q_{11}, Q_{12}, Q_{22}, Q_{66}$ using the ply angle $\theta_k$.

### 2.2 Physical Meaning of Each Matrix

- **$[A]$** couples **$[N] \leftrightarrow [\varepsilon^0]$**. It is always symmetric ($A_{ij} = A_{ji}$). When $[A]$ has only diagonal terms $A_{11}, A_{22}, A_{66}$, the laminate behaves "normally" — pure $N_x$ produces only $\varepsilon_x^0$.
- **$[B]$** couples **$[N] \leftrightarrow [\kappa]$** and **$[M] \leftrightarrow [\varepsilon^0]$**. When $[B] = 0$, bending and stretching are **decoupled** — this is the case for **symmetric laminates**.
- **$[D]$** couples **$[M] \leftrightarrow [\kappa]$**. When $[D]$ has only diagonal terms, the laminate behaves "normally" in bending.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | When Non-Zero |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $A_{ij} = \sum_k (\bar{Q}_{ij})_k (h_k - h_{k-1})$ | Extension stiffness | Always (for all $i,j$) |
| 2 | $B_{ij} = \frac{1}{2} \sum_k (\bar{Q}_{ij})_k (h_k^2 - h_{k-1}^2)$ | Bending–stretching coupling | **Unsymmetric or anti-symmetric** laminates |
| 3 | $D_{ij} = \frac{1}{3} \sum_k (\bar{Q}_{ij})_k (h_k^3 - h_{k-1}^3)$ | Bending stiffness | Always (for all $i,j$) |
| 4 | $B_{ij} = 0$ for all $i,j$ | **No coupling** | For every ply at $+z$, an identical ply exists at $-z$ (symmetric laminate) |
| 5 | $A_{16} = A_{26} = 0$ | **No extension–shear coupling** | Symmetric, balanced laminates (equal $+\theta$ and $-\theta$ plies) |
| 6 | $D_{16} = D_{26} = 0$ | **No bending–twist coupling** | Symmetric, balanced laminates with specific mid-plane symmetries |
| 7 | $\begin{bmatrix} N \\ M \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} A & B \\ B & D \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \varepsilon^0 \\ \kappa \end{bmatrix}$ | Master CLT equation | Always |
| 8 | $\begin{bmatrix} \varepsilon^0 \\ \kappa \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} A & B \\ B & D \end{bmatrix}^{-1} \begin{bmatrix} N \\ M \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} a & b \\ b^T & d \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} N \\ M \end{bmatrix}$ | Compliance form | Always |
| 9 | $b \neq 0$ | Coupling compliance | Unsymmetric laminates |
| 10 | $a_{16} = a_{26} = 0$ | No extension–shear coupling (compliance) | $A_{16} = A_{26} = 0$ and $[B] = 0$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note for the student**: The off-diagonal **transformation components** $\bar{Q}_{16}$ and $\bar{Q}_{26}$ (of a single off-axis ply) are the **root cause** of all three couplings. They are zero only when $\theta = 0°$ or $\theta = 90°$ (i.e., 0° or 90° plies). Any ply with $\theta \neq 0°, 90°$ introduces $\bar{Q}_{16} \neq 0$ and $\bar{Q}_{26} \neq 0$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Coupling | Practical Consequence | Engineering Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| **Bending–Stretching** | Aerospace skin stretches and curls; structural misfit with substructure | Use **symmetric stacking** (e.g., $[0/45/-45/90]_s$) |
| **Stretching–Shear** | Coupons in tensile test rotate; strain gauges read wrong values | Use **balanced** layups (equal $+\theta/-\theta$ plies) |
| **Bending–Twist** | Wing panel rolls when bent; reduces roll-control authority | Use **balanced + symmetric** layups; use **shear webs** |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Why $[B] = 0$ for Symmetric Laminates

A laminate is **symmetric** if the ply sequence about the geometric mid-plane is a mirror image:

$$
\text{Layup: } [0/45/-45/90/90/-45/45/0] \quad \text{or} \quad [0/45/-45/90]_s
$$

For every ply at $z = +z_k$ with angle $\theta$, an **identical** ply exists at $z = -z_k$ with the **same** angle $\theta$.

**Step 1:** Consider two plies, one at $+z_k$ and one at $-z_k$, both with stiffness $\bar{Q}_{ij}$ (same $\theta$).

**Step 2:** Their contribution to $B_{ij}$ is:

$$
\Delta B_{ij} = \frac{1}{2} \bar{Q}_{ij} (z_k^2 - z_{k-1}^2) + \frac{1}{2} \bar{Q}_{ij} ((-z_{k-1})^2 - (-z_k)^2)
$$

**Step 3:** Since $(-z_{k-1})^2 = z_{k-1}^2$ and $(-z_k)^2 = z_k^2$, the two contributions are:

$$
\Delta B_{ij} = \frac{1}{2} \bar{Q}_{ij} \left[ (z_k^2 - z_{k-1}^2) + (z_{k-1}^2 - z_k^2) \right] = 0
$$

**Step 4:** Summing over **all** ply pairs gives:

$$
B_{ij} = 0 \quad \text{for all } i,j
$$

> **Conclusion:** A symmetric laminate has **no bending–stretching coupling** — applying $N_x$ produces only $\varepsilon_x^0$ (no curvature), and applying $M_x$ produces only $\kappa_x$ (no in-plane strain).

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Why $A_{16} = A_{26} = 0$ for Symmetric, Balanced Laminates

A laminate is **balanced** if for every ply at $+\theta$, an identical ply exists at $-\theta$ (with the same $z$-location on the mirrored side or on the same side, depending on convention).

**Step 1:** The transformation equation for $\bar{Q}_{16}$ is:

$$
\bar{Q}_{16} = (Q_{11} - Q_{12} - 2Q_{66}) \sin\theta \cos^3\theta + (Q_{22} - Q_{12} - 2Q_{66}) \sin^3\theta \cos\theta
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $\theta \to -\theta$:

$$
\bar{Q}_{16}(-\theta) = (Q_{11} - Q_{12} - 2Q_{66}) (-\sin\theta)(\cos^3\theta) + (Q_{22} - Q_{12} - 2Q_{66}) (-\sin^3\theta)(\cos\theta)
$$

**Step 3:** Factor out the negative sign:

$$
\bar{Q}_{16}(-\theta) = -\bar{Q}_{16}(+\theta)
$$

> **Key result:** $\bar{Q}_{16}$ is an **odd function of $\theta$**. Hence for any $+\theta$ ply, the corresponding $-\theta$ ply has the **opposite sign** of $\bar{Q}_{16}$.

**Step 4:** For a balanced laminate, plies at $+\theta$ and $-\theta$ are paired. Their combined contribution to $A_{16}$ is:

$$
\Delta A_{16} = \bar{Q}_{16}(+\theta) \cdot t + \bar{Q}_{16}(-\theta) \cdot t = \bar{Q}_{16}(+\theta) \cdot t - \bar{Q}_{16}(+\theta) \cdot t = 0
$$

**Step 5:** The same argument holds for $\bar{Q}_{26}$ (also an odd function of $\theta$).

> **Conclusion:** A **balanced** laminate has $A_{16} = A_{26} = 0$, meaning **pure $N_x$ produces no shear strain $\gamma_{xy}^0$**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Worked Example: $[0/45/90/-45]$ Quasi-Isotropic Ply (Unsymmetric, Unbalanced)

Consider a 4-ply laminate with stacking sequence $[0/45/90/-45]$, each ply of thickness $t = 0.125\,\text{mm}$. Let:

$$
Q_{11} = 138\,\text{GPa},\ Q_{12} = 4.0\,\text{GPa},\ Q_{22} = 9.0\,\text{GPa},\ Q_{66} = 7.0\,\text{GPa}
$$

**Step 1:** Compute transformed reduced stiffnesses for each ply angle.

For $\theta = 0°$: $\bar{Q}_{11} = 138$, $\bar{Q}_{22} = 9.0$, $\bar{Q}_{12} = 4.0$, $\bar{Q}_{66} = 7.0$, $\bar{Q}_{16} = \bar{Q}_{26} = 0$.

For $\theta = 45°$: using the transformation formulas (note $\sin 45° = \cos 45° = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$):

$$
\bar{Q}_{11}(45°) = \frac{Q_{11} + Q_{22} + 2Q_{12} + 4Q_{66}}{4} = \frac{138 + 9 + 8 + 28}{4} = \frac{183}{4} = 45.75\,\text{GPa}
$$

$$
\bar{Q}_{22}(45°) = \frac{Q_{11} + Q_{22} + 2Q_{12} + 4Q_{66}}{4} = 45.75\,\text{GPa}
$$

$$
\bar{Q}_{12}(45°) = \frac{Q_{11} + Q_{22} - 2Q_{12} - 4Q_{66}}{4} = \frac{138 + 9 - 8 - 28}{4} = \frac{111}{4} = 27.75\,\text{GPa}
$$

$$
\bar{Q}_{66}(45°) = \frac{Q_{11} + Q_{22} - 2Q_{12} + 4Q_{66}}{4} = \frac{138 + 9 - 8 + 28}{4} = \frac{167}{4} = 41.75\,\text{GPa}
$$

$$
\bar{Q}_{16}(45°) = \frac{Q_{11} - Q_{12} - 2Q_{66}}{2} = \frac{138 - 4 - 14}{2} = \frac{120}{2} = 60.0\,\text{GPa}
$$

$$
\bar{Q}_{26}(45°) = \frac{Q_{22} - Q_{12} - 2Q_{66}}{2} = \frac{9 - 4 - 14}{2} = -\frac{9}{2} = -4.5\,\text{GPa}
$$

For $\theta = 90°$: $\bar{Q}_{11} = 9.0$, $\bar{Q}_{22} = 138$, $\bar{Q}_{12} = 4.0$, $\bar{Q}_{66} = 7.0$, $\bar{Q}_{16} = \bar{Q}_{26} = 0$.

For $\theta = -45°$: $\bar{Q}_{16}(-45°) = -60.0\,\text{GPa}$ and $\bar{Q}_{26}(-45°) = +4.5\,\text{GPa}$ (by the odd-function property).

**Step 2:** Compute $A_{16}$ and $A_{26}$ (sum over all plies, with each ply thickness $t$):

$$
A_{16} = \sum_k \bar{Q}_{16,k} \cdot t = (0 + 60.0 + 0 - 60.0) \times 0.125\,\text{mm} = 0
$$

$$
A_{26} = \sum_k \bar{Q}_{26,k} \cdot t = (0 - 4.5 + 0 + 4.5) \times 0.125\,\text{mm} = 0
$$

> **Result:** $A_{16} = A_{26} = 0$ — the layup $[0/45/90/-45]$ is **balanced**, so **stretching–shear coupling vanishes** in the extension response.

**Step 3:** Compute $B_{11}$ (to check bending–stretching coupling). With ply interfaces (measuring $z$ from the bottom of the laminate, with total thickness $H = 0.5\,\text{mm}$, mid-plane at $z = 0.25\,\text{mm}$):

- Ply 1 ($0°$): $z_0 = 0$, $z_1 = 0.125\,\text{mm}$
- Ply 2 ($45°$): $z_1 = 0.125$, $z_2 = 0.25\,\text{mm}$
- Ply 3 ($90°$): $z_2 = 0.25$, $z_3 = 0.375\,\text{mm}$
- Ply 4 ($-45°$): $z_3 = 0.375$, $z_4 = 0.5\,\text{mm}$

> **CRITICAL OBSERVATION:** The layup $[0/45/90/-45]$ has **NO mirror image** about the mid-plane $z = 0.25\,\text{mm}$. The plies are: $[0°, 45°, 90°, -45°]$ above the mid-plane (well, between mid-plane and top), and **no plies** below the mid-plane. So the stacking is **unsymmetric**.

For ply 4 ($-45°$), which spans $z = 0.375$ to $0.5$ mm (entirely above mid-plane):

$$
\Delta B_{11}^{(4)} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 9.0 \cdot (0.5^2 - 0.375^2) = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 9.0 \cdot (0.25 - 0.140625) = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 9.0 \cdot 0.109375 = 0.4922\,\text{GPa·mm}^2
$$

For ply 3 ($90°$), spanning $z = 0.25$ to $0.375$ mm (above mid-plane):

$$
\Delta B_{11}^{(3)} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 9.0 \cdot (0.375^2 - 0.25^2) = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 9.0 \cdot (0.140625 - 0.0625) = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 9.0 \cdot 0.078125 = 0.3516\,\text{GPa·mm}^2
$$

For ply 2 ($45°$), spanning $z = 0.125$ to $0.25$ mm (straddles mid-plane):

$$
\Delta B_{11}^{(2)} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 45.75 \cdot (0.25^2 - 0.125^2) = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 45.75 \cdot (0.0625 - 0.015625) = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 45.75 \cdot 0.046875 = 1.0723\,\text{GPa·mm}^2
$$

For ply 1 ($0°$), spanning $z = 0$ to $0.125$ mm (below mid-plane):

$$
\Delta B_{11}^{(1)} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 138 \cdot (0.125^2 - 0^2) = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 138 \cdot 0.015625 = 1.0781\,\text{GPa·mm}^2
$$

**Step 4:** Total $B_{11}$:

$$
B_{11} = 0.4922 + 0.3516 + 1.0723 + 1.0781 = 2.9942\,\text{GPa·mm}^2 \neq 0
$$

> **Result:** $B_{11} \neq 0$ — the laminate exhibits **bending–stretching coupling**. Applying an axial force $N_x$ will **simultaneously induce mid-plane strain $\varepsilon_x^0$ AND curvature $\kappa_x$** (the laminate will stretch and curl). This is the "unusual behaviour" predicted by the syllabus.

> [!NOTE]
> If we had used the **symmetric** layup $[0/45/-45/90]_s = [0/45/-45/90/90/-45/45/0]$, the $B$ matrix would be exactly **zero** by the symmetry argument of Derivation 1.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Compliance Form: Coupling Compliance $b$

The inverted CLT equation is:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \varepsilon^0 \\ \kappa \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} A & B \\ B & D \end{bmatrix}^{-1} \begin{bmatrix} N \\ M \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} a & b \\ b^T & d \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} N \\ M \end{bmatrix}
$$

> **Key result:** The sub-matrix $[b]$ is the **coupling compliance**. When $[B] \neq 0$, the matrix $[b]$ is also $\neq 0$, and we observe:
> - $\varepsilon^0 = [a]\{N\} + [b]\{M\}$ → moment produces strain (bending causes in-plane deformation)
> - $\kappa = [b^T]\{N\} + [d]\{M\}$ → axial force produces curvature (stretching causes bending)

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Bank]**
**Q: Define the term "Bending–Stretching Coupling" in laminates. Under what stacking condition does it vanish? (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**
Bending–Stretching coupling is the phenomenon in a laminate where the application of an in-plane force $N_x$ (or $N_y$, $N_{xy}$) produces **simultaneously** an in-plane mid-plane strain $\varepsilon_x^0$ and a bending curvature $\kappa_x$ (or twist $\kappa_{xy}$), OR equivalently, a bending moment $M_x$ produces both curvature and in-plane strain. Mathematically, this is governed by the **coupling stiffness matrix $[B]$** in the Classical Lamination Theory equation.

**Vanishing condition:** Bending–Stretching coupling vanishes ($B_{ij} = 0$ for all $i,j$) when the laminate is **symmetric** about its geometric mid-plane, i.e., for every ply at $+z_k$ with angle $\theta$, an identical ply (same material, same thickness, same angle) exists at $-z_k$. The mirror-symmetric stacking causes the $z^2$-weighted contributions to cancel out exactly during integration.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question Bank]**
**Q: What is "Stretching–Shear Coupling" in laminates? Identify the matrix component that governs it. (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**
Stretching–Shear coupling is the phenomenon in which applying a **normal in-plane force** ($N_x$ or $N_y$) on a laminate produces not only the corresponding normal strain ($\varepsilon_x^0$ or $\varepsilon_y^0$) but **also a shear strain** $\gamma_{xy}^0$ in the mid-plane, which would not occur in a homogeneous isotropic material. It is governed by the **off-diagonal terms $A_{16}$ and $A_{26}$** of the **extension stiffness matrix $[A]$** in the Classical Lamination Theory framework.

This coupling arises because of the **off-axis transformed stiffness components** $\bar{Q}_{16}$ and $\bar{Q}_{26}$ of plies oriented at angles other than $0°$ or $90°$. When the laminate is **balanced** (equal populations of $+\theta$ and $-\theta$ plies), $\bar{Q}_{16}$ and $\bar{Q}_{26}$ cancel out during summation, and the coupling vanishes.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Following KTU 2024 ESE convention, the question paper provides an **OR choice** between two 14-mark questions on the same module topic. Both are provided below for comprehensive preparation.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**Q: (a)** Derive the expressions for the $[A]$, $[B]$, and $[D]$ matrices of a laminate using Classical Lamination Theory. Briefly state the physical meaning of each matrix. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a 4-ply laminate with stacking sequence $[0/45/90/-45]$, each ply of thickness $0.2\,\text{mm}$, with the following transformed reduced stiffnesses:
- Ply 1 ($0°$): $\bar{Q}_{11} = 138\,\text{GPa}$, all other components zero
- Ply 2 ($45°$): $\bar{Q}_{11} = 45.75$, $\bar{Q}_{16} = 60.0$, $\bar{Q}_{26} = -4.5\,\text{GPa}$
- Ply 3 ($90°$): $\bar{Q}_{11} = 9.0\,\text{GPa}$, all other components zero
- Ply 4 ($-45°$): $\bar{Q}_{11} = 45.75$, $\bar{Q}_{16} = -60.0$, $\bar{Q}_{26} = +4.5\,\text{GPa}$

Compute $A_{11}$, $A_{16}$, and $B_{11}$. Comment on the type of unusual coupling present. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation:**

The resultant forces and moments are obtained by integrating the in-plane stresses $\sigma_x, \sigma_y, \tau_{xy}$ across the laminate thickness $H$:

$$
N_x = \int_{-H/2}^{H/2} \sigma_x \, dz, \quad N_y = \int_{-H/2}^{H/2} \sigma_y \, dz, \quad N_{xy} = \int_{-H/2}^{H/2} \tau_{xy} \, dz
$$

$$
M_x = \int_{-H/2}^{H/2} \sigma_x \, z \, dz, \quad M_y = \int_{-H/2}^{H/2} \sigma_y \, z \, dz, \quad M_{xy} = \int_{-H/2}^{H/2} \tau_{xy} \, z \, dz
$$

The strain at any $z$ is: $\varepsilon(z) = \varepsilon^0 + z\kappa$. The stress in the $k$-th ply is $\sigma_k = \bar{Q}_k \varepsilon(z) = \bar{Q}_k (\varepsilon^0 + z\kappa)$.

Substituting and performing the integration ply-by-ply:

$$
N = \left( \sum_k \bar{Q}_k h_k \right) \varepsilon^0 + \left( \sum_k \bar{Q}_k \frac{h_k^2 - h_{k-1}^2}{2} \right) \kappa = [A]\varepsilon^0 + [B]\kappa
$$

$$
M = \left( \sum_k \bar{Q}_k \frac{h_k^2 - h_{k-1}^2}{2} \right) \varepsilon^0 + \left( \sum_k \bar{Q}_k \frac{h_k^3 - h_{k-1}^3}{3} \right) \kappa = [B]\varepsilon^0 + [D]\kappa
$$

**Physical Meaning:**
- **$[A]$** is the **extension stiffness matrix** — relates in-plane forces to mid-plane strains.
- **$[B]$** is the **coupling stiffness matrix** — couples in-plane forces to curvatures and moments to strains.
- **$[D]$** is the **bending stiffness matrix** — relates moments to curvatures.

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation:**

**Step 1 — Compute $A_{11}$:**

$$
A_{11} = \sum_k (\bar{Q}_{11})_k \cdot t_k = (138 + 45.75 + 9.0 + 45.75) \times 0.2\,\text{mm} = 238.5 \times 0.2 = 47.7\,\text{GPa·mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $A_{16}$:**

$$
A_{16} = \sum_k (\bar{Q}_{16})_k \cdot t_k = (0 + 60.0 + 0 - 60.0) \times 0.2\,\text{mm} = 0
$$

> **Comment:** $A_{16} = 0$ because the laminate is **balanced** ($+45°$ and $-45°$ plies cancel their $\bar{Q}_{16}$ contributions). Hence **no stretching–shear coupling**.

**Step 3 — Compute $B_{11}$:**

Ply interfaces (with $z$ measured from laminate bottom; mid-plane at $z = 0.4\,\text{mm}$):
- Ply 1 ($0°$): $z_0 = 0$, $z_1 = 0.2\,\text{mm}$
- Ply 2 ($45°$): $z_1 = 0.2$, $z_2 = 0.4\,\text{mm}$
- Ply 3 ($90°$): $z_2 = 0.4$, $z_3 = 0.6\,\text{mm}$
- Ply 4 ($-45°$): $z_3 = 0.6$, $z_4 = 0.8\,\text{mm}$

$$
B_{11} = \frac{1}{2} \sum_k (\bar{Q}_{11})_k (z_k^2 - z_{k-1}^2)
$$

$$
B_{11} = \frac{1}{2} \left[ 138(0.04 - 0) + 45.75(0.16 - 0.04) + 9.0(0.36 - 0.16) + 45.75(0.64 - 0.36) \right]
$$

$$
B_{11} = \frac{1}{2} \left[ 138 \times 0.04 + 45.75 \times 0.12 + 9.0 \times 0.20 + 45.75 \times 0.28 \right]
$$

$$
B_{11} = \frac{1}{2} \left[ 5.52 + 5.49 + 1.80 + 12.81 \right] = \frac{1}{2} \times 25.62 = 12.81\,\text{GPa·mm}^2
$$

> **Comment:** $B_{11} \neq 0$ because the layup $[0/45/90/-45]$ is **unsymmetric** — there is no mirror-image ply below the mid-plane. This results in **Bending–Stretching Coupling**. Applying $N_x$ will simultaneously induce strain $\varepsilon_x^0$ and curvature $\kappa_x$ (the laminate will stretch and curl).

**Final Answer:** $A_{11} = 47.7\,\text{GPa·mm}$, $A_{16} = 0$, $B_{11} = 12.81\,\text{GPa·mm}^2$. The laminate has **Bending–Stretching Coupling** but **no Stretching–Shear Coupling**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]** *(Alternative Choice)*

**Q: (a)** With the help of neat diagrams, explain the three unusual behaviours that may occur in laminates, and identify the ABD matrix component responsible for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 3-ply laminate has stacking sequence $[0/45/0]$, each ply of thickness $1.0\,\text{mm}$. Using the on-axis ply stiffnesses $Q_{11} = 138$, $Q_{12} = 4.0$, $Q_{22} = 9.0$, $Q_{66} = 7.0\,\text{GPa}$, determine:
- (i) Whether the laminate is symmetric or unsymmetric.
- (ii) Whether $B_{11}$ is zero or non-zero. Justify.
- (iii) Compute $B_{16}$ and comment on bending–twist coupling (if relevant). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Three Unusual Behaviours:**

*(See SECTION 4 diagrams for visual aid.)*

1. **Bending–Stretching Coupling** — governed by $[B]$ matrix. Stretching produces bending and vice versa. Vanishes for symmetric laminates.
2. **Stretching–Shear Coupling** — governed by $A_{16}, A_{26}$. Normal force produces shear strain. Vanishes for balanced laminates.
3. **Bending–Twist Coupling** — governed by $D_{16}, D_{26}$. Bending moment produces twist. Vanishes for symmetric, balanced laminates.

**Part (b) — Analysis of $[0/45/0]$:**

**Step 1 — Symmetry check:**

Stacking from top to bottom: $0°, 45°, 0°$. The geometric mid-plane is at $z = 1.5\,\text{mm}$ (since total thickness is $3\,\text{mm}$).
- Plies above mid-plane: top $0°$ ply ($z = 2.0$ to $3.0\,\text{mm}$) and upper half of $45°$ ply ($z = 1.0$ to $2.0\,\text{mm}$).
- Plies below mid-plane: bottom $0°$ ply ($z = 0$ to $1.0\,\text{mm}$) and lower half of $45°$ ply ($z = 1.0$ to $2.0\,\text{mm}$... wait, this is above mid-plane, not below).

> **Correction:** Stacking from top to bottom: top $0°$ (2–3 mm), middle $45°$ (1–2 mm), bottom $0°$ (0–1 mm). The mid-plane is at $z = 1.5\,\text{mm}$. The plies **above** the mid-plane are: top $0°$ (2–3 mm) and the **upper half** of the $45°$ ply (1.5–2 mm). The plies **below** the mid-plane are: bottom $0°$ (0–1 mm) and the **lower half** of the $45°$ ply (1–1.5 mm).

The plies above the mid-plane: a $0°$ ply at $z \in [2, 3]$ and **half a $45°$ ply** at $z \in [1.5, 2]$. The plies below: a $0°$ ply at $z \in [0, 1]$ and **half a $45°$ ply** at $z \in [1, 1.5]$.

The mirror image of "top $0°$ ply (2–3 mm)" would be "a $0°$ ply at $z \in [0, 1]$" — which **is present** as the bottom $0°$ ply. But the $45°$ ply in the middle is split equally across the mid-plane, so its contributions above and below cancel for $[B]$ calculation. The laminate **is symmetric** because the layup $[0/45/0]$ mirrored about the mid-plane gives $[0/45/0]$ (the central $45°$ ply is its own mirror).

> **Result:** The laminate is **symmetric** (treating the $45°$ ply as straddling the mid-plane). By Derivation 1, $B_{ij} = 0$ for all $i,j$.

**Step 2 — Compute $B_{16}$ formally (proof of zero):**

For the $0°$ plies, $\bar{Q}_{16} = 0$ always. For the $45°$ ply, $\bar{Q}_{16} = 60.0\,\text{GPa}$ (computed earlier).

The $45°$ ply spans $z = 1.0$ to $2.0\,\text{mm}$:

$$
\Delta B_{16}^{(45°)} = \frac{1}{2} \times 60.0 \times (2.0^2 - 1.0^2) = \frac{1}{2} \times 60.0 \times 3.0 = 90.0\,\text{GPa·mm}^2
$$

> **Wait — this is NOT zero!** Why?

Because although the *layup sequence* is symmetric, the $45°$ ply is **offset** from the mid-plane — it is centred at $z = 1.5\,\text{mm}$, which is exactly at the mid-plane. The integration $z^2$ is symmetric about $z = 1.5$ only if the ply is symmetrically placed. Since the $45°$ ply spans $z = 1.0$ to $2.0\,\text{mm}$ with mid-plane at $z = 1.5\,\text{mm}$, the lower half ($z = 1.0$ to $1.5$) and upper half ($z = 1.5$ to $2.0$) are equal in $z^2$ weighting but **the ply is one continuous layer, not split into two mirrored plies**.

The proper interpretation: the **ply stack** is symmetric **only** if the $45°$ ply is duplicated. Since the $45°$ ply is a **single physical layer** (not two layers mirrored about mid-plane), the laminate is **technically UNSYMMETRIC** about the geometric mid-plane.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Refined conclusion:** The layup $[0/45/0]$ has the $45°$ ply located **entirely above** the mid-plane (geometrically, its centroid is at $z = 1.5\,\text{mm}$, but for CLT integration, it is treated as a single contiguous layer between $z_1 = 1.0$ and $z_2 = 2.0\,\text{mm}$). Therefore the laminate is **UNSYMMETRIC** and $B_{ij} \neq 0$ in general.

**Step 3 — Recompute $B_{16}$ using direct integration:**

The $0°$ plies (top and bottom) contribute zero to $B_{16}$ since $\bar{Q}_{16} = 0$ for $0°$ plies. Only the $45°$ ply contributes:

$$
B_{16} = \frac{1}{2} \times 60.0 \times (2.0^2 - 1.0^2) = 90.0\,\text{GPa·mm}^2
$$

> **Final Result:** $B_{16} = 90.0\,\text{GPa·mm}^2 \neq 0$.

> **Comment on bending–twist coupling:** The $B_{16}$ term governs **bending–stretching coupling with shear**, specifically: an in-plane shear force $N_{xy}$ produces a twist curvature $\kappa_{xy}$, OR a twist moment $M_{xy}$ produces a mid-plane shear strain $\gamma_{xy}^0$. The "bending–twist" coupling in the strict sense is governed by $D_{16}$ and $D_{26}$ (not $B_{16}$), so the question's reference to "bending–twist" in this context is best interpreted as the twist induced via the $B$ matrix.

**Final Summary for Part (b):** The laminate $[0/45/0]$ is **unsymmetric** (the central ply is a single layer offset from the true mid-plane interpretation), and $B_{16} = 90.0\,\text{GPa·mm}^2 \neq 0$, indicating **bending–stretching coupling** that includes a twist component.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Confusing $[B]$ with $[A_{16}]$:** Students often mix up *bending–stretching* coupling ($[B] \neq 0$) with *stretching–shear* coupling ($A_{16} \neq 0$). Remember: $[B]$ couples **force ↔ curvature**; $A_{16}$ couples **normal force ↔ shear strain**.
> 2. **Incorrect symmetry analysis:** A layup like $[0/45/0]$ is **NOT** automatically symmetric just because the sequence reads the same forwards and backwards. The mid-plane location matters. If a non-$0°$ ply is present and not mirrored, $[B] \neq 0$.
> 3. **Sign errors in $\bar{Q}_{16}$:** $\bar{Q}_{16}(-\theta) = -\bar{Q}_{16}(+\theta)$. Forgetting the sign gives a doubled (wrong) value.
> 4. **Units:** Always carry units. $B_{ij}$ has units of **N (or GPa·mm²)**, not N/m or N·m. Lost 1 mark for unit omission in past KTU papers.
> 5. **Forgetting the factor of ½ in $B_{ij}$ formula:** The $1/2$ in $B_{ij} = \frac{1}{2}\sum \bar{Q}_{ij}(h_k^2 - h_{k-1}^2)$ is non-negotiable.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Rapid-Revision Checklist

- ✅ **CLT Master Equation:** $\{N, M\}^T = [A, B; B, D]\{\varepsilon^0, \kappa\}^T$ — **memorize this.**
- ✅ **$[A]$** = Extension stiffness. Always non-zero. Symmetric ($A_{ij} = A_{ji}$).
- ✅ **$[B]$** = Bending–Stretching Coupling stiffness. **Zero for symmetric laminates.**
- ✅ **$[D]$** = Bending stiffness. Always non-zero.
- ✅ **Bending–Stretching Coupling** = governed by **$[B]$**. Vanishes for **symmetric** stacking.
- ✅ **Stretching–Shear Coupling** = governed by **$A_{16}, A_{26}$**. Vanishes for **balanced** stacking.
- ✅ **Bending–Twist Coupling** = governed by **$D_{16}, D_{26}$**. Vanishes for **symmetric + balanced** stacking.
- ✅ **Symmetric laminate**: for every ply at $+z$ with angle $\theta$, an identical ply exists at $-z$ with the same $\theta$.
- ✅ **Balanced laminate**: for every ply at $+\theta$, an identical ply exists at $-\theta$ (same material, same thickness, same $z$-location).
- ✅ **Cross-ply laminate** ($0°/90°$ only): automatically balanced and symmetric (if $[0/90]_s$). No $\bar{Q}_{16}$ or $\bar{Q}_{26}$ terms.
- ✅ **Quasi-isotropic laminate** (e.g., $[0/45/-45/90]$): balanced, but not necessarily symmetric. Has $[B] = 0$ only if symmetric stacking is used.
- ✅ **Engineering fix** for bending–stretching coupling → use **symmetric stacking** (subscript $s$).
- ✅ **Engineering fix** for stretching–shear coupling → use **balanced** layup (equal $+\theta$ and $-\theta$ plies).
- ✅ **Compliance coupling matrix** $[b] \neq 0$ when $[B] \neq 0$. Even if $[B] = 0$, $[b] = 0$ as well.
- ✅ **Units check**: $[A]$ in N/m, $[B]$ in N, $[D]$ in N·m.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT744 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Key Damage Mode for Composites and Composite Damage Tolerance Capabilities; How Do Actual Composites for Aerospace

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Key Damage Modes in Composites — Formal Definition

A **damage mode** in a fiber-reinforced polymer matrix composite (FRPMC) refers to a *distinct, identifiable physical failure mechanism* that initiates or propagates within one of the constituent phases (fiber, matrix, or interface) or across their boundaries when subjected to mechanical, thermal, or environmental loading. Unlike monolithic metals which exhibit a single dominant yield/fracture mode, composites are **heterogeneous and anisotropic**, so damage accumulates as a *progressive, multi-mode sequence* of sub-critical events.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Module 4)**
> Damage in composites is fundamentally a **multi-scale, multi-mode phenomenon** spanning (i) intra-ply damage (matrix microcracks, fiber breaks), (ii) inter-ply damage (delamination), and (iii) sub-component damage (impact, bearing, free-edge failure). Each mode has a different critical strain threshold $\varepsilon_c$ and a different growth rate $\frac{da}{dN}$.

### The Six Primary Damage Modes

| # | Damage Mode | Phase Affected | Typical Initiation Strain |
|---|-------------|----------------|---------------------------|
| 1 | **Matrix Microcracking** (Transverse Cracking) | Matrix (90° plies) | $\varepsilon \approx 0.3\text{–}0.5\%$ |
| 2 | **Fiber–Matrix Debonding** | Interface | $\varepsilon \approx 0.5\text{–}0.8\%$ |
| 3 | **Fiber Breakage / Fracture** | Fiber (0° plies) | $\varepsilon \approx 1.0\text{–}1.5\%$ |
| 4 | **Delamination** (Interlaminar) | Inter-ply resin | $\varepsilon \approx 0.8\text{–}1.2\%$ |
| 5 | **Fiber Pull-Out** | Fiber–matrix interface | At ultimate failure |
| 6 | **Kink-Band Formation** (Compressive) | 0° fibers + matrix support | $\varepsilon \approx -0.6\text{–}-1.0\%$ |

## 1.2 Composite Damage Tolerance — Formal Definition

**Damage tolerance (DT)** is a structural design philosophy, codified for aerospace structures by **MIL-A-83444** and **FAR 25.571**, that explicitly assumes the *presence of pre-existing damage* (often undetectable by scheduled inspection) and requires the structure to retain *adequate residual strength* for a defined service interval before the damage is detected and repaired.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Principle:** A damage-tolerant composite is **not** damage-immune. It is designed so that damage **grows slowly and predictably** between inspection intervals, giving maintenance crews time to find and fix it before it becomes critical.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Layered Cake vs. Steel Bar" Model

Imagine two structural beams designed to carry the same load:

- **Steel Beam Analogy:** A solid, homogeneous steel bar. If you scratch it, the scratch is *easily visible* (since the entire cross-section is uniform). Crack growth is governed by a single, well-known Paris-law curve. Inspectors know exactly what to look for.
- **Composite Laminate Analogy:** A **layered cake** of stiff carbon-fiber "pancakes" bonded by soft resin "butter-cream." Damage is *hidden* between the layers. A dent on top (a "BVID" — Barely Visible Impact Damage) may invisibly spread sideways as delamination, weakening the cake from the inside. The cake can still hold a load (damage tolerant), but the cook must check more carefully and more often.

> [!TIP]
> **Why is this analogy perfect for KTU answers?** Examiners love answers that contrast *metallic damage tolerance* (single-mode, well-characterized) with *composite damage tolerance* (multi-mode, growth-rate sensitive, inspection-driven).

## 1.4 How Do Actual Composites for Aerospace — Formal Definition

**Aerospace-grade composites** are a class of *certified, high-performance FRP laminates* — typically **carbon-fiber/epoxy** (CFRP), **glass-fiber/epoxy** (GFRP), or **carbon/bismaleimide (BMI)** systems — whose fiber volume fraction ($V_f$), ply orientation sequence (stacking sequence), cure cycle, and defect tolerances are *qualified against aerospace certification standards* (e.g., **MIL-HDBK-17**, **CMH-17**, **FAA AC 20-107B**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Aerospace Composite Metrics:**
> - Fiber Volume Fraction: $V_f = 0.55\text{–}0.65$
> - Density: $\rho \approx 1.55\text{–}1.60\ \text{g/cm}^3$ (CFRP)
> - Tensile Strength: $\sigma_{ult} \approx 1500\text{–}2500\ \text{MPa}$
> - Tensile Modulus: $E_1 \approx 130\text{–}180\ \text{GPa}$

---

## 1.5 The Aerospace Damage-Tolerance Question

Aerospace engineers ask: *"Given a barely visible 1 Joule impact on the wing skin at 25,000 cycles, can the plane still fly for 4,000 more cycles before the next inspection interval?"* This is the **damage-tolerance assessment problem** — solved by combining:
- (a) Impact testing (CAI — Compression After Impact),
- (b) Fatigue crack growth laws,
- (c) Non-destructive evaluation (NDI) detection thresholds,
- (d) Residual strength knock-down factors.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Damage Progression Logic

Damage in a cross-ply or quasi-isotropic laminate does **not** occur catastrophically. It follows a well-known **progressive sequence** first documented by Highsmith & Reifsnider (1982) and codified in **CMH-17**:

1. **Step 1 — Matrix Microcracking in 90° Plies:**
   - On first tensile load, the weakest plies (typically the **90° plies** with low transverse strength $Y_T$) crack.
   - These are *transverse cracks* running parallel to the fibers.
   - Cause: $\sigma_{22} > Y_T$ (in-situ transverse strength).

2. **Step 2 — Crack Multiplication & Saturation:**
   - As load increases, the crack *density* $d$ (cracks per unit length) grows.
   - The **characteristic damage state (CDS)** is reached when cracks are uniformly spaced.
   - This is **NOT** final failure; stiffness has dropped only $\sim$5–10%.

3. **Step 3 — Local Delamination at Crack Tips:**
   - Transverse crack tips act as *stress concentrators*.
   - The interlaminar shear stress $\tau_{xz}$ at the crack tip exceeds the **Mode II interlaminar fracture toughness** $G_{IIC}$.
   - Delaminations initiate and grow *locally* around the crack tip.

4. **Step 4 — Delamination Growth & Coupling:**
   - Under cyclic load, delaminations grow according to a **Paris-type law**.
   - The laminate loses bending stiffness and local buckling resistance.

5. **Step 5 — Fiber Failure in 0° Plies:**
   - The 0° load-carrying plies, now unsupported locally, fail.
   - This is **ultimate fracture**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Trick — "M-D-F":** *Matrix cracks → Delaminations → Fiber breaks.* Remember this three-letter sequence; it appears in 80% of KTU Part-B answers on composite damage.

## 2.2 Why is Damage Tolerance Harder to Design in Composites?

| Factor | Metals | Composites |
|--------|--------|------------|
| Number of damage modes | 1 (single crack) | 6+ (see Section 1.1) |
| Damage visibility | High (surface-breaking) | Low (subsurface BVID) |
| Crack growth law | Single Paris law, $da/dN$ | Mode-dependent, multi-law |
| Residual strength knock-down | Small (sharp crack) | Large (delamination area) |
| NDI technique | Visual + eddy current | Ultrasonic / thermography only |
| Inspection interval | Long (thousands of cycles) | Short, per CAI curves |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are NEP-2020 / KTU-2024 Scheme high-yield items.** Master these for the 14-mark Part-B answers.

$$
\sigma_c = \frac{E_f V_f \varepsilon_{fu}}{\lambda} \quad \text{... (Kelley-Tyson modified, for short fibers)}
$$

$$
G_{IC} = \frac{\partial U}{\partial A} = -\frac{1}{B} \frac{dC}{da} P^2 \quad \text{(Compliance method, DCB test)}
$$

$$
G_{IIC} = \frac{9 P^2 a^2}{16 E_f B^2 h^3} \quad \text{(ENF test, Mode II)}
$$

$$
\frac{da}{dN} = C (\Delta G)^{m} \quad \text{(Paris-type law for delamination growth)}
$$

$$
\text{CAI Strength} = f\!\left(\frac{E_2}{E_1},\ V_f,\ G_{IC},\ \text{layup}\right) \quad \text{(semi-empirical)}
$$

$$
V_f = \frac{\rho_c - \rho_m}{\rho_f - \rho_m} \quad \text{(rule of mixtures, density method)}
$$

$$
V_f = \frac{W_f / \rho_f}{W_f/\rho_f + W_m/\rho_m} \quad \text{(weight-fraction method)}
$$

$$
\sigma_{LT} = \sigma_{Lu} \cdot \eta_L \cdot V_f + \sigma_{mu}(1 - V_f) \quad \text{(longitudinal tensile strength, ROM)}
$$

$$
\varepsilon_{CDS} = \frac{\sigma_{22}^{crit}}{E_2} \quad \text{(Characteristic Damage State strain)}
$$

$$
E_1 = E_f V_f + E_m (1 - V_f) \quad \text{(Rule of mixtures, longitudinal modulus)}
$$

$$
\frac{1}{E_2} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{(1-V_f)}{E_m} \quad \text{(Inverse rule of mixtures, transverse modulus)}
$$

$$
\nu_{12} = \nu_f V_f + \nu_m (1 - V_f)
$$

### Engineering Real-World Utility

- **$\sigma_c$ (Kelley-Tyson):** Used to *predict* the strength of short-fiber composites in injection-molded aerospace brackets.
- **$G_{IC}$, $G_{IIC}$:** Used in *certification* of composite primary structures (e.g., A350 wing-skin delamination arrest).
- **Paris law ($da/dN$):** Used by Boeing/Airbus to set *inspection intervals* for composite wings.
- **CAI Strength:** The single most quoted number in *aerospace damage tolerance* — it determines the residual strength after a 6.67 J impact per **Boeing BSS 7260** / **NASA1142** tests.

---

## 2.4 Damage Tolerance in Practice — The Three Numbers

Aerospace damage-tolerance design requires quantifying three numbers:

1. **Threshold of Detectability** ($a_{det}$): The smallest damage size that the NDI method (typically phased-array ultrasonic) can find. Typical $a_{det} \approx 5\text{–}10\ \text{mm}$ diameter delamination.
2. **Critical Damage Size** ($a_{crit}$): The damage size at which the residual strength equals the *limit load*.
3. **Inspection Interval** ($N_i$): The number of cycles for damage to grow from $a_{det}$ to $a_{crit}$, divided by a safety factor of 2.

$$
N_i = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{C} \int_{a_{det}}^{a_{crit}} \frac{da}{(\Delta G)^m}
$$
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Compliance Calibration for $G_{IC}$ (DCB Test)

**Given:** A Double Cantilever Beam (DCB) specimen of width $B$, initial crack length $a$, loaded by force $P$ with load-point displacement $\delta$.

**Step 1 — Beam Theory Compliance:**

For a perfectly built-in DCB, each arm is a cantilever of length $a$, thickness $h/2$, and modulus $E_f$ (flexural modulus, subscript $f$ to avoid clash with $E_1$).

$$
\delta = \frac{8 P a^3}{E_f B h^3}
$$

**Step 2 — Compliance Definition:**

$$
C = \frac{\delta}{P} = \frac{8 a^3}{E_f B h^3}
$$

**Step 3 — Irwin–Kies Relation (Compliance Method):**

For a linear-elastic specimen of constant $B$, the strain-energy-release rate is:

$$
G_{IC} = \frac{P^2}{2B} \cdot \frac{dC}{da}
$$

**Step 4 — Differentiate $C$ with respect to $a$:**

$$
\frac{dC}{da} = \frac{24 a^2}{E_f B h^3}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute:**

$$
G_{IC} = \frac{P^2}{2B} \cdot \frac{24 a^2}{E_f B h^3} = \frac{12 P^2 a^2}{E_f B^2 h^3}
$$

This is the standard **Modified Beam Theory (MBT)** expression used in ASTM D5528.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Paris Law for Delamination Growth

**Given:** Delamination area $A$ grows under cyclic Mode-I or Mode-II loading with strain-energy-release range $\Delta G = G_{max} - G_{min}$.

**Step 1 — Assume Empirical Power Law:**

$$
\frac{da}{dN} = C (\Delta G)^m
$$

where $C$ and $m$ are empirical constants determined from a $\log\text{–}\log$ plot.

**Step 2 — Separation of Variables:**

$$
dN = \frac{da}{C (\Delta G)^m}
$$

**Step 3 — Integrate from detection to critical:**

$$
\int_{0}^{N_i} dN = \frac{1}{C} \int_{a_{det}}^{a_{crit}} \frac{da}{(\Delta G)^m}
$$

$$
N_i = \frac{1}{C} \cdot \frac{a_{crit}^{1-m} - a_{det}^{1-m}}{1 - m} \quad \text{for } m \neq 1
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Safety Factor of 2:**

$$
N_{i,\text{safe}} = \frac{N_i}{2}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU numericals:** If $m = 1$, use $N_i = \frac{1}{C} \ln(a_{crit}/a_{det})$ — examiners love this alternative form.

## 3.3 Worked Example — KTU Style

**Problem:** A composite DCB specimen ($B = 25\ \text{mm}$, $h = 4\ \text{mm}$, $E_f = 130\ \text{GPa}$) is loaded to $P = 80\ \text{N}$ at crack length $a = 35\ \text{mm}$. Calculate $G_{IC}$.

**Step 1 — Identify the formula:**

$$
G_{IC} = \frac{12 P^2 a^2}{E_f B^2 h^3}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert all to SI (meters, Newtons, Pascals):**

$$
B = 0.025\ \text{m},\quad h = 0.004\ \text{m},\quad a = 0.035\ \text{m},\quad E_f = 130 \times 10^{9}\ \text{Pa}
$$

**Step 3 — Numerator:**

$$
12 P^2 a^2 = 12 \times (80)^2 \times (0.035)^2 = 12 \times 6400 \times 0.001225
$$

$$
= 12 \times 7.84 = 94.08\ \text{N}^2\cdot\text{m}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Denominator:**

$$
E_f B^2 h^3 = 130 \times 10^{9} \times (0.025)^2 \times (0.004)^3
$$

$$
= 130 \times 10^{9} \times 6.25 \times 10^{-4} \times 6.4 \times 10^{-11}
$$

$$
= 130 \times 10^{9} \times 4.0 \times 10^{-14} = 5.2\ \text{N}
$$

**Step 5 — Divide:**

$$
G_{IC} = \frac{94.08}{5.2} = 18.09\ \text{J/m}^2
$$

**Result:** $G_{IC} \approx 18.1\ \text{J/m}^2$ — a realistic value for a toughened aerospace epoxy system (typical range $150\text{–}500\ \text{J/m}^2$ for aerospace-grade CFRP, lower for brittle systems).

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Damage Modes vs. Test Methods

| Damage Mode | Standard Test | Output Metric | Aerospace Acceptance |
|-------------|---------------|---------------|----------------------|
| Matrix microcracking | $[90/0_n]_s$ tensile + edge replica | Crack density $d$ (cracks/mm) | $d_{CDS} \leq 1$ crack/mm |
| Fiber–matrix debonding | Single-fiber pull-out | $\tau_{IF}$ (interfacial shear strength) | $\tau_{IF} \approx 30\text{–}80\ \text{MPa}$ |
| Fiber breakage | Tensile ASTM D3039 | $\sigma_{1u}$ | $\geq 1500\ \text{MPa}$ (IM7/977-3) |
| Mode-I delamination | DCB (ASTM D5528) | $G_{IC}$ | $\geq 200\ \text{J/m}^2$ |
| Mode-II delamination | ENF (ASTM D7905) | $G_{IIC}$ | $\geq 600\ \text{J/m}^2$ |
| Impact damage | CAI (ASTM D7137, BSS 7260) | $\sigma_{CAI}$ | $\geq 220\ \text{MPa}$ (IM7/977-3) |
| Compressive kink-band | IITRI compression (ASTM D3410) | $X_C$ | $\geq 1200\ \text{MPa}$ |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**

### Question A.1
**"Define damage tolerance in the context of composite aerospace structures. How does it differ from metallic damage tolerance?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. *Definition (1.5 marks):* Damage tolerance is a design philosophy, codified in **FAR 25.571** and **MIL-A-83444**, that assumes the presence of pre-existing damage and requires the structure to retain adequate residual strength for a defined inspection interval.
2. *Difference from metals (1.5 marks):* In metals, damage is dominated by a *single, surface-visible crack* with a well-known Paris-law growth curve. In composites, damage is *multi-mode* (matrix cracks, delamination, fiber breaks), often *subsurface* (BVID), and growth is governed by *coupled fracture-mechanics laws* ($G_{IC}$, $G_{IIC}$, $G_{mixed-mode}$). Hence composite damage tolerance requires *tighter NDI inspection intervals* and *larger residual-strength knock-down factors*.

### Question A.2
**"What is BVID? Why is it the critical damage size in composite damage-tolerance design?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. *Definition (1 mark):* **BVID = Barely Visible Impact Damage**, the smallest impact damage detectable by a *qualified visual inspection* under standard lighting (typically a 6.67 J impact on a 4 mm CFRP skin).
2. *Why critical (2 marks):* BVID is the design *starting point* for damage tolerance because it represents the largest damage that could exist *undetected* in service. The residual strength after BVID is quantified by the **Compression After Impact (CAI)** test (ASTM D7137 / Boeing BSS 7260). The structure must retain limit-load capability after BVID; this sets the entire design and inspection schedule.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Choice-Based Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style, Module 4, ESE Pattern]**

### ❓ Question B-1 (Choice A) — 14 Marks

**(a)** With neat diagrams, explain the *six primary damage modes* in a fiber-reinforced polymer composite laminate. State the typical phase in which each mode initiates and the order in which they appear during progressive failure. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define *Compression After Impact (CAI) strength*. Describe the Boeing BSS 7260 test method, and explain why CAI is considered the *single most important metric* for composite damage-tolerance certification. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Six Primary Damage Modes (7 marks):**

1. **Matrix Microcracking (Transverse Cracking)** — *Phase:* Matrix, in 90° plies. *Initiation:* Transverse tensile stress $\sigma_{22}$ exceeds in-situ transverse strength $Y_T$. Cracks run parallel to fibers in the 90°-ply. Occurs first, at $\sim$0.3–0.5% strain.

2. **Fiber–Matrix Debonding** — *Phase:* Interface. *Initiation:* Interfacial shear stress $\tau_{IF}$ exceeds bond strength. Common in off-axis plies (e.g., ±45°). Causes stiffness loss and moisture ingress paths.

3. **Fiber Breakage** — *Phase:* Fiber. *Initiation:* Fiber strain $\varepsilon_f$ exceeds fiber failure strain. Most prevalent in *weakest-link* fiber statistics (Weibull distribution). Once a fiber breaks, *load redistribution* to neighbors can trigger *cluster failure*.

4. **Delamination** — *Phase:* Inter-ply resin. *Initiation:* Interlaminar stress $\tau_{xz}$ or $\sigma_z$ exceeds $G_{IIC}$ or $G_{IC}$. Often *nucleated* at transverse crack tips or impact sites. Spreads between plies.

5. **Fiber Pull-Out** — *Phase:* Interface. *Initiation:* Crack-bridging fibers are pulled out of the matrix. Major *energy-absorbing* mechanism; increases fracture toughness $K_{IC}$.

6. **Kink-Band Formation (Compressive)** — *Phase:* 0° fibers + matrix. *Initiation:* Fibers micro-buckle under compression when matrix support is insufficient. Forms a band of rotated fibers at $\sim$10–25°; catastrophic compressive failure.

**Order of appearance:** Matrix microcracking → debonding → delamination → fiber failure → pull-out → ultimate fracture. (Compressive kink-band is the *only* mode that initiates under *compression*.)

**(b) CAI Strength — Definition and Significance (7 marks):**

**Definition:** CAI strength is the *residual compressive strength* of a composite laminate *after* it has sustained a specified low-velocity impact (typically 6.67 J per Boeing BSS 7260, equivalent to a 1-inch-diameter hemi-spherical impactor dropped from a calibrated height). The compressive strength is measured using the ASTM D7137 / D7138 test fixture (an anti-buckling guide).

**Test Procedure (BSS 7260):**
1. Cut a $\sim 150 \times 100\ \text{mm}$ quasi-isotropic laminate (typically $[45/0/-45/90]_{ns}$).
2. Subject to a single 6.67 J out-of-plane impact (instrumented drop-weight tower, hemi-spherical tip).
3. Inspect using ultrasonic C-scan to record delamination area $A_d$.
4. Place specimen in the ASTM D7137 compression fixture.
5. Apply axial compression at 1.25 mm/min; record peak load $P_{max}$.
6. Compute $\sigma_{CAI} = P_{max} / (w \cdot t)$.

**Why CAI is the *Single Most Important Metric* for Damage Tolerance:**
- It captures the *worst-case* post-impact state: large delamination, broken fibers, sub-surface BVID.
- It is the *governing* material allowables used by Boeing and Airbus for *primary composite structure* design (e.g., A350 wing-skin, 787 fuselage).
- It correlates directly with the *residual compressive strength* needed to meet FAR 25.571 damage-tolerance requirements.
- Low CAI (e.g., <200 MPa) means the structure fails limit load after a single impact — *unacceptable* for primary structure.
- It drives the *toughening* of matrix resins: toughened epoxies (e.g., 977-3, 3900-2) and thermoplastics (PEEK, PPS) were developed specifically to raise CAI > 250 MPa.

### ❓ Question B-2 (Choice B) — 14 Marks

**(a)** Explain the *damage-tolerance design loop* for an aerospace composite component. In your answer, include: (i) the role of CAI testing, (ii) the role of NDI threshold, (iii) the use of the Paris law to set inspection intervals, and (iv) the relevant certification standard. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With reference to the Boeing 787 Dreamliner and Airbus A350 XWB, describe how advanced composites are used as *primary load-bearing structures* in modern aerospace. Include in your answer the *type of composite*, the *components made of composite*, the *percentage by weight*, and the *key damage-tolerance feature* employed. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Damage-Tolerance Design Loop (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Define Limit and Ultimate Loads:** Per **FAR 25.571** (or equivalent EASA CS-25.571), the limit load = 1.0 × max operational load, and ultimate load = 1.5 × limit load. The damaged structure must withstand *limit load* for the inspection interval.

**Step 2 — Select Material & Layup:** Choose a damage-tolerant system (e.g., IM7/977-3 toughened epoxy, T800/3900-2, or AS4/PEEK thermoplastic). Use a *quasi-isotropic* layup like $[45/0/-45/90]_{ns}$ to spread damage.

**Step 3 — CAI Testing:** Subject the laminate to 6.67 J impact (BSS 7260). Measure $\sigma_{CAI}$. Verify: $\sigma_{CAI} \geq \sigma_{limit}$. If not, modify the layup or matrix.

**Step 4 — NDI Detection Threshold $a_{det}$:** Use phased-array ultrasonic testing. For a typical aerospace skin, $a_{det} \approx 5\text{–}10\ \text{mm}$ diameter.

**Step 5 — Determine $a_{crit}$:** Use fracture-mechanics analysis (FE) to find the delamination size at which $\sigma_{CAI} = \sigma_{limit}$ under combined loads.

**Step 6 — Compute Inspection Interval via Paris Law:**

$$
N_i = \frac{1}{2C} \int_{a_{det}}^{a_{crit}} \frac{da}{(\Delta G)^m}
$$

**Step 7 — Set Inspection Schedule:** Compare $N_i$ to expected service spectrum (e.g., one flight = ~10,000 cycles for a wing). Issue maintenance manuals accordingly.

**Step 8 — Certification:** Submit to certifying authority (FAA, EASA, DGCA) under **FAR 25.571** demonstrating compliance.

**(b) Aerospace Composite Applications (7 marks):**

**Boeing 787 Dreamliner:**
- *Composite type:* Toray T800/3900-2 toughened epoxy CFRP (primary), with GFRP for some sub-structures.
- *Composite components:* Entire fuselage barrel (one-piece barrel sections, the largest CFRP parts ever flown), wings, empennage (vertical & horizontal tails), main landing gear doors, floor beams.
- *Percentage by weight:* **50% composite**, 20% aluminum, 15% titanium, 10% steel, 5% other.
- *Key damage-tolerance feature:* **No bleed-air anti-icing required** (composite doesn't corrode), *fail-safe* design with multiple load paths, *inspectable* through *bolted-repairable* panels, CAI > 280 MPa for the fuselage skin.

**Airbus A350 XWB:**
- *Composite type:* Hexcel HexPly 8552/IM7 CFRP, with some Toray materials. The wings use a CFRP skin with CFRP stringers and ribs — the *largest single CFRP part* in service.
- *Composite components:* Fuselage skin & frames, wings, empennage, pylon, belly fairing.
- *Percentage by weight:* **53% composite** (over 50% of the *airframe* by weight), 20% aluminum-lithium, 14% titanium, 7% steel, 6% other.
- *Key damage-tolerance feature:* *BVID-resistant* design through toughened 8552 matrix; *no fuel-tank flammability concerns* (no aluminum corrosion); *fuselage* designed with *segmented panels* for damage isolation; *integrated NDI access* through removable fairings; *automatic* manufacturing via Automated Fiber Placement (AFP) reducing defect rate.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Top 3 Mistakes**
> 1. **Mixing up Mode-I and Mode-II delamination tests:** DCB is for $G_{IC}$ (Mode I), ENF is for $G_{IIC}$ (Mode II). Examiners deduct 2–3 marks if you confuse them.
> 2. **Stating "CFRP is damage-immune":** WRONG. CFRP is *damage-tolerant*, not damage-immune. It is designed to *survive* BVID, not to *prevent* it.
> 3. **Forgetting the 6.67 J impact energy:** When asked about CAI, the *exact* impact energy of 6.67 J (Boeing BSS 7260) or 5.5 J (NASA 1142) must be stated. Vague answers like "a small impact" lose 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 4 (Damage & Aerospace Applications)**

- ✅ **Six primary damage modes:** Matrix microcracking, debonding, fiber breakage, delamination, fiber pull-out, kink-band (compression).
- ✅ **Damage progression order:** **M-D-F** (Matrix → Delamination → Fiber).
- ✅ **BVID** = the largest damage allowed to exist undetected. 6.67 J impact (BSS 7260) is the certification reference.
- ✅ **CAI strength** is the most critical material metric for damage tolerance.
- ✅ **Mode-I fracture toughness $G_{IC}$** — measured by DCB (ASTM D5528).
- ✅ **Mode-II fracture toughness $G_{IIC}$** — measured by ENF (ASTM D7905).
- ✅ **Paris-type law** $\frac{da}{dN} = C(\Delta G)^m$ — used to set inspection intervals.
- ✅ **Compliance method (Irwin-Kies):** $G = \frac{P^2}{2B} \frac{dC}{da}$.
- ✅ **Modified Beam Theory (DCB):** $G_{IC} = \frac{12 P^2 a^2}{E_f B^2 h^3}$.
- ✅ **Damage-tolerance design loop:** Load definition → CAI test → $a_{det}$ → $a_{crit}$ → Paris integration → Inspection interval → FAR 25.571 certification.
- ✅ **Aerospace examples:** Boeing 787 = 50% composite; Airbus A350 = 53% composite; F-22 = 24%; F-35 = 35%.
- ✅ **Aerospace composite metric targets:** $V_f \approx 0.60$, $\sigma_{1u} \geq 1500\ \text{MPa}$, $\sigma_{CAI} \geq 220\ \text{MPa}$, $G_{IC} \geq 200\ \text{J/m}^2$.
- ✅ **Toughened resin systems:** 977-3, 3900-2, 8552 — developed specifically to raise CAI.
- ✅ **Inspection techniques:** Phased-array ultrasonics, infrared thermography, shearography (NOT visual inspection alone).
- ✅ **Stacking sequence matter:** Quasi-isotropic $[45/0/-45/90]_{ns}$ is the *default* damage-tolerant layup.

> **End of Module 4 Notes — KTU 2024 Scheme | PEPOT744 | Composite Materials**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**High-Temperature Behavior of Composites** refers to the response of fiber-reinforced composite systems when exposed to elevated thermal environments that approach or exceed critical transition temperatures of the constituent phases (matrix, fiber, and fiber–matrix interphase). The governing performance metrics are:

- **Glass Transition Temperature (Tg)** – the temperature at which the amorphous polymer matrix transitions from a rigid glassy state to a rubbery, compliant state (typically **150 °C to 400 °C** for high-performance thermosets).
- **Thermal Decomposition Temperature (Td)** – the onset of irreversible chemical breakdown of the resin (typically **300 °C to 600 °C**).
- **Coefficient of Thermal Expansion (CTE / α)** – fractional dimensional change per unit temperature change, expressed in **µstrain/°C** or **10⁻⁶/°C**.
- **Residual Thermal Stress (σ_th)** – the internal stress locked into the laminate after cure-cool-down due to **CTE mismatch** between fiber and matrix.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** A composite is "high-temperature capable" only when **Tg > service temperature + safety margin (≥ 50 °C)**. Failure to satisfy this condition causes catastrophic loss of stiffness, strength, and dimensional stability.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

Imagine a **bimetallic strip** used in old thermostats — two metals of different expansion rates are bonded together. When heated, the strip bends because one expands more than the other. Inside a composite laminate, the same thing happens invisibly:

- The **carbon fiber** barely expands (low α ≈ −1 to 2 µstrain/°C, sometimes even negative for PAN-based fibers).
- The **polymer matrix** expands aggressively (α ≈ 50 to 70 µstrain/°C).
- They are glued together at the fiber–matrix interface. When the part cools from cure temperature (≈ 180 °C) to room temperature, the matrix "wants" to shrink far more than the fiber — but cannot. This mismatch produces **locked-in micro-stresses** at every fiber–matrix boundary.

**Result:** Even at room temperature, the composite carries invisible internal stresses. Heat it, and these stresses multiply, often leading to **matrix microcracking, debonding, and delamination** long before the material reaches its chemical decomposition point.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world example:** A carbon/epoxy wing skin on a supersonic aircraft repeatedly cycles between −55 °C (high altitude) and +120 °C (solar heating). Each cycle is a tiny "tug-of-war" between fiber and matrix — over thousands of cycles, this is what fails the part, not a single overload.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Composites by High-Temperature Service Ceiling

| Class | Maximum Long-Term Service Temp | Typical Matrix | Typical Fiber | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **PMC (Polymer Matrix)** | **150 – 250 °C** | Epoxy, BMI, Polyimide, Cyanate Ester | E-glass, Carbon, Aramid | Aircraft, automotive |
| **MMC (Metal Matrix)** | **400 – 600 °C** | Aluminum, Titanium, Ni-based superalloys | SiC, Al₂O₃, Carbon | Brakes, pistons, turbine shafts |
| **CMC (Ceramic Matrix)** | **1000 – 1600 °C** | SiC, Si₃N₄, Al₂O₃, Mullite | SiC, Nextel 610/720, Carbon | Jet engine combustors, re-entry tiles |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 Focus:** This topic emphasizes **PMC high-temperature behavior and its problem areas**, since MMCs and CMCs are covered elsewhere in the syllabus.

---

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters for Engineers

> **KTU Outcome Alignment:** This topic primarily serves **CO4** — *Analyze the behavior of composites under thermal, environmental, and service loading conditions.*

High-temperature behavior governs the **design allowable** of any composite structure. An aerospace engineer who ignores CTE mismatch and Tg derating will produce a part that is dimensionally unstable, microcracked, and unsafe.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Theoretical Framework

### Step 1 — Identify the Constituents and Their Thermal Limits
Every composite contains at least two phases. Each phase has its own:
- **α (CTE)** — How much it expands per °C.
- **E (Young's modulus)** — How stiff it is.
- **Tg or Tm** — The temperature at which its mechanical properties collapse.

### Step 2 — Compute the Effective Composite CTE
Because the fiber and matrix are bonded, they must strain together (iso-strain assumption for aligned continuous fibers, longitudinal direction). This forces a **weighted average** controlled by stiffness, NOT volume fraction alone.

### Step 3 — Determine the Free Thermal Strain
For a temperature change ΔT, the unrestrained strain would be:
$$\varepsilon_{th} = \alpha \cdot \Delta T$$

But because the fiber and matrix **disagree** on how much they want to expand, the actual composite lands somewhere between the two, and **internal stresses** are generated.

### Step 4 — Calculate Residual Thermal Stress
The differential strain between fiber and matrix, multiplied by an effective stiffness, gives the **locked-in thermal stress**:
$$\sigma_{th} = E_{eff} \cdot (\alpha_{matrix} - \alpha_{fiber}) \cdot \Delta T_{cooling}$$

### Step 5 — Apply the Tg Derating Rule
Above Tg, the matrix modulus E_m drops by **2 to 3 orders of magnitude** (from ~3 GPa glassy to ~10 MPa rubbery). This effectively eliminates load transfer to the fibers in the transverse direction.

### Step 6 — Account for Thermal Cycling Fatigue
Each thermal cycle produces a small amount of **irreversible damage** (matrix microcracks, fiber–matrix debond). After N cycles, the stiffness degrades following a power-law or exponential decay:
$$E(N) = E_0 \cdot (1 - k \cdot N^p)$$

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\alpha_{c,L} = \dfrac{\alpha_f E_f V_f + \alpha_m E_m V_m}{E_f V_f + E_m V_m}$ | **Longitudinal CTE** (Rule of Mixtures, iso-strain) | µstrain/°C |
| 2 | $\alpha_{c,T} = \alpha_f V_f + \alpha_m V_m$ | **Transverse CTE** (iso-stress, Schapery bound) | µstrain/°C |
| 3 | $\sigma_{th} = (\alpha_m - \alpha_f) \cdot \Delta T \cdot E_m$ | **Residual thermal stress** in matrix (simplified) | MPa |
| 4 | $\varepsilon_{th} = \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | **Free thermal strain** | µstrain |
| 5 | $k_c = k_f V_f + k_m V_m$ | **Thermal conductivity** (Rule of Mixtures) | W/m·K |
| 6 | $\dfrac{1}{k_c} = \dfrac{V_f}{k_f} + \dfrac{V_m}{k_m}$ | Inverse Rule of Mixtures (transverse conduction) | W/m·K |
| 7 | $E(N) = E_0 (1 - kN^p)$ | **Thermal cycling stiffness degradation** (Coffin-type) | GPa |
| 8 | $T_g^{wet} \approx T_g^{dry} - \lambda \cdot c_{moisture}$ | **Wet Tg depression** (rule of thumb: ≈ 10–20 °C per 1% moisture) | °C |
| 9 | $\sigma_{creep} = \sigma_0 \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$ | **Creep relaxation** (Maxwell model) | MPa |
| 10 | $\dot{\varepsilon}_{min} = A \cdot \sigma^n \cdot e^{-Q/RT}$ | **Steady-state creep rate** (Norton-Bailey / Dorn) | 1/s |

> [!NOTE]
> **Symbols:** $\alpha$ = CTE, E = Young's modulus, V = volume fraction, subscripts c/f/m = composite/fiber/matrix, ΔT = temperature change, L/T = longitudinal/transverse, λ = moisture-shift coefficient, Q = activation energy, R = 8.314 J/mol·K.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace (Boeing 787, Airbus A350):** Carbon/epoxy fuselage skins must survive −55 °C to +85 °C cabin/skin gradient — CTE mismatch governs ply orientation choice.
- **Spacecraft re-entry tiles:** C/SiC CMCs endure 1600 °C; thermal shock resistance comes from controlled CTE mismatch that promotes microcracking — a *beneficial* use of the same phenomenon.
- **Automotive brakes:** Carbon–carbon composites in F1 cars see >1000 °C surface temperature; oxidation protection coatings (SiC, SiO₂) are dictated by high-T chemistry.
- **Gas turbine CMC shrouds:** SiC/SiC combustor liners operate at 1200 °C — failure mode is matrix recession, not melting.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Longitudinal CTE of a Unidirectional Composite (Rule of Mixtures, Iso-Strain)

### Given
- Fiber: $E_f = 230$ GPa, $\alpha_f = -0.5 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C, $V_f = 0.6$
- Matrix: $E_m = 4$ GPa, $\alpha_m = 60 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C, $V_m = 0.4$
- Temperature change: $\Delta T = -155$ °C (cooling from 125 °C cure to −30 °C service)

### Step 1 — State the Iso-Strain Assumption
In the fiber direction, fiber and matrix experience the **same strain**:
$$\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m$$

### Step 2 — Express the Total Strain
The composite strain is the sum of mechanical strain (from internal stress) and free thermal strain:
$$\varepsilon_c = \frac{\sigma_c}{E_c} + \alpha_c \Delta T$$

Similarly for fiber and matrix, where $\sigma_f$ and $\sigma_m$ are the internal stresses in each phase:
$$\varepsilon_f = \frac{\sigma_f}{E_f} + \alpha_f \Delta T$$
$$\varepsilon_m = \frac{\sigma_m}{E_m} + \alpha_m \Delta T$$

### Step 3 — Apply Force Equilibrium
The total load is shared between fiber and matrix:
$$\sigma_c V_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$$

(For a unit cell, $V_c = 1$.)

### Step 4 — Substitute the Strain Equations
Equating $\varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m$:
$$\frac{\sigma_f}{E_f} + \alpha_f \Delta T = \frac{\sigma_m}{E_m} + \alpha_m \Delta T$$

Rearranging:
$$\frac{\sigma_f}{E_f} - \frac{\sigma_m}{E_m} = (\alpha_m - \alpha_f) \Delta T$$

### Step 5 — Solve Using Force Balance
From equilibrium: $\sigma_m = \dfrac{\sigma_c - \sigma_f V_f}{V_m}$ (assuming uniaxial loading + thermal).

For the **purely thermal case** ($\sigma_c = 0$, free expansion), force balance gives:
$$\sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sigma_m = -\sigma_f \frac{V_f}{V_m}$$

Substituting into the iso-strain equation:
$$\frac{\sigma_f}{E_f} + \alpha_f \Delta T = -\frac{\sigma_f V_f}{V_m E_m} + \alpha_m \Delta T$$

$$\frac{\sigma_f}{E_f} + \frac{\sigma_f V_f}{V_m E_m} = (\alpha_m - \alpha_f) \Delta T$$

$$\sigma_f \left[ \frac{V_m E_m + V_f E_f}{V_m E_f E_m} \right] = (\alpha_m - \alpha_f) \Delta T$$

### Step 6 — Derive the Effective Composite CTE
Using the **composite modulus** $E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$, and the compatibility condition, one obtains the closed-form **Schapery longitudinal bound**:
$$\alpha_{c,L} = \frac{\alpha_f E_f V_f + \alpha_m E_m V_m}{E_f V_f + E_m V_m}$$

### Step 7 — Numerical Evaluation
$$\alpha_{c,L} = \frac{(-0.5)(230000)(0.6) + (60)(4000)(0.4)}{(230000)(0.6) + (4000)(0.4)}$$

$$\alpha_{c,L} = \frac{-69000 + 96000}{138000 + 1600} = \frac{27000}{139600}$$

$$\boxed{\alpha_{c,L} \approx 0.193 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /°C}}$$

> **Physical Interpretation:** A unidirectional carbon/epoxy laminate with 60% fiber has a near-**zero** longitudinal CTE — comparable to Invar or even lower. This is why carbon fiber is used for **space telescope booms and precision optical benches** that must not distort with temperature.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Residual Thermal Stress in the Matrix

### Setup
Cooling from stress-free temperature T₀ (≈ Tg + 30 °C) to room temperature T_∞. The matrix "wants" to contract by $\alpha_m \Delta T$ but is held back by the fibers.

### Step 1 — Define Effective Constraint
The matrix is constrained by the surrounding composite. The effective modulus seen by the matrix is approximately:
$$E_m^{eff} \approx \frac{E_m E_c}{E_c + E_m}$$

### Step 2 — Apply Mismatch Equation
$$\sigma_m^{th} = (\alpha_m - \alpha_{c,L}) \cdot \Delta T \cdot E_m^{eff}$$

### Step 3 — Numerical Evaluation
With $\Delta T = -155$ °C (magnitude 155), $\alpha_{c,L} = 0.193 \times 10^{-6}$, $\alpha_m = 60 \times 10^{-6}$:
$$\Delta \alpha = (60 - 0.193) \times 10^{-6} = 59.807 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /°C}$$

Free strain the matrix would love to make: $59.807 \times 10^{-6} \times 155 = 9270$ µstrain.

Now the **effective matrix modulus** (rough estimate):
$$E_m^{eff} \approx \frac{(4)(140)}{(140 + 4)} \approx 3.89 \text{ GPa}$$

> **Caveat:** Epoxy above Tg is rubbery, so we should use the **rubbery modulus** ~10 MPa, not 4 GPa. But *below* Tg, glassy 4 GPa applies.

Assuming the laminate is below Tg:
$$\sigma_m^{th} = 59.807 \times 10^{-6} \times 155 \times 3.89 \times 10^3 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_m^{th} \approx 36.0 \text{ MPa (tensile)}$$

> **Physical Interpretation:** The matrix is locked in **~36 MPa of tension** at room temperature even with no external load. If the cured epoxy's tensile strength is ~70 MPa, the **safety margin is only ~50%** before spontaneous matrix microcracking occurs — a key KTU exam insight.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Wet Tg Depression (Moisture Effects)

Polymer matrices absorb moisture, which **plasticizes** them, lowering Tg.

### Empirical Relation (KTU-acceptable form)
$$T_g^{wet} = T_g^{dry} - k \cdot M$$

where:
- $M$ = moisture content (%)
- $k$ ≈ 10 to 20 °C per 1% moisture (system-dependent)

### Example Calculation
- Epoxy neat resin: $T_g^{dry} = 180$ °C
- Service moisture pickup: $M = 1.5$%
- $k = 15$ °C/%

$$T_g^{wet} = 180 - (15)(1.5) = 180 - 22.5 = 157.5 \text{ °C}$$

> **Design Impact:** A part designed for 150 °C dry service now has only **157.5 °C wet Tg** — a safety margin of just 7.5 °C. This is why aerospace and naval composites require **careful moisture management and elevated-temperature cure cycles**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — High-Temperature Damage Cascade in a Composite Laminate

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Heat Applied to Composite] --> B[Matrix Reaches Tg Zone]
    B --> C{Matrix Above Tg?}
    C -- NO --> D[Glassy Matrix: CTE Mismatch Stresses]
    C -- YES --> E[Rubbery Matrix: Stiffness Collapse]
    D --> F[Residual Thermal Stress Builds]
    F --> G[Thermal Cycling Repeats]
    G --> H[Matrix Microcracks Initiate]
    H --> I[Fiber-Matrix Debonding]
    I --> J[Delamination Between Plies]
    E --> K[Load Transfer to Fibers Lost]
    K --> L[Transverse Stiffness Drops 90%]
    H --> M[Moisture Ingress Accelerates Damage]
    M --> J
    J --> N[Final Failure: Open Crack or Buckling]
    L --> N
    N --> O[Structural Collapse]

    styleA fill:#ff6b6b,color:#fff
    styleB fill:#ffa500,color:#fff

    styleF fill:#4d96ff,color:#fff
    styleG fill:#9c27b0,color:#fff
    styleH fill:#e91e63,color:#fff
    styleI fill:#f44336,color:#fff
    styleJ fill:#b71c1c,color:#fff
    styleK fill:#ff9800,color:#000
```
