
> **Reading the diagram:** Each box is a damage state. Arrows show causal progression. The two parallel "death spirals" (right side = stiffness collapse, left side = microcrack growth) merge at the final failure node.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Comparative High-Temperature Performance of Composite Classes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PMCs[Polymer Matrix Composites - 150 to 250 C]
        P1[Epoxy - 150 C] --> P2[BMI - 200 C]
        P2 --> P3[Polyimide - 250 C]
    end

    subgraph MMCs[Metal Matrix Composites - 400 to 600 C]
        M1[Al-SiC - 400 C] --> M2[Ti-SiC - 500 C]
        M2 --> M3[Ni-Al2O3 - 600 C]
    end

    subgraph CMCs[Ceramic Matrix Composites - 1000 to 1600 C]
        C1[SiC-SiC - 1200 C] --> C2[C-C - 1500 C]
        C2 --> C3[Nextel-Al2O3 - 1600 C]
    end

    PMCs -->|Service Temp Increase| MMCs
    MMCs -->|Service Temp Increase| CMCs

    styleP1 fill:#a8d8ea
    styleP2 fill:#a8d8ea
    styleP3 fill:#a8d8ea
    styleM1 fill:#f4a261
    styleM2 fill:#f4a261
    styleM3 fill:#f4a261
    styleC1 fill:#e76f51
    styleC2 fill:#e76f51
    styleC3 fill:#e76f51
```

---

## 4.3 Problem-Area Mapping (Cause → Effect → Mitigation)

| # | Problem Area | Root Cause | Effect on Composite | Engineering Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Matrix softening at Tg** | Amorphous polymer phase transition | 2–3 orders of magnitude drop in E_m | Use higher-Tg resin (BMI, PI, cyanate ester) |
| 2 | **CTE mismatch** | Δα between fiber and matrix | Residual thermal stress, microcracking | Balanced/symmetric layup, low-α fibers |
| 3 | **Thermal cycling fatigue** | Repeated ΔT exposure | Progressive stiffness loss | Toughened matrices, particle-toughened interlayers |
| 4 | **Oxidation of carbon fibers** | O₂ attack at > 400 °C | Fiber thinning, strength loss | SiC coatings, glass-ceramic seal coats |
| 5 | **Moisture-induced Tg depression** | Hygroscopic matrix absorption | Lowered operating ceiling | Surface sealants, dry prepreg, vacuum bagging |
| 6 | **Matrix decomposition (Td)** | Pyrolysis, charring, ablation | Mass loss, porosity, strength loss | Phenolic resins (intentional char), ablatives |
| 7 | **Interphase degradation** | Fiber coating breakdown | Debonding, ILSS drop | Pyrocarbon / BN / SiC fiber coatings |
| 8 | **Creep rupture (MMC)** | Time-dependent metal flow | Permanent deformation, void growth | Refractory particle reinforcement, barrier layers |
| 9 | **Thermal shock (CMC)** | Sudden ΔT | Surface cracking, spallation | Low-thermal-expansion fibers, controlled porosity |
| 10 | **Differential aging** | Fiber and matrix age at different rates | Property drift over service life | Lifetime prediction via Arrhenius modeling |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**Define Glass Transition Temperature (Tg). Why is it a critical design parameter for polymer matrix composites used at elevated temperatures?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** Glass Transition Temperature (Tg) is the temperature at which an amorphous polymer matrix transitions from a rigid, glassy state to a soft, rubbery state due to the onset of large-scale segmental motion of polymer chains.
- **Why critical (1.5 marks):** Above Tg, the matrix modulus drops by 2–3 orders of magnitude (e.g., from ~3 GPa to ~10 MPa for epoxy). This causes (a) loss of load transfer to fibers, especially in transverse and shear modes, (b) drastic reduction in hot/wet strength and stiffness, and (c) increased creep and permanent deformation. Therefore, **service temperature must remain below Tg – safety margin (typically ≥ 50 °C)** to retain design properties.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Explain the phenomenon of CTE mismatch in fiber-reinforced composites. What residual stresses develop due to this mismatch?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Phenomenon (1.5 marks):** Coefficient of Thermal Expansion (CTE) mismatch refers to the difference in dimensional change per unit temperature change between the fiber (α_f ≈ −1 to 5 × 10⁻⁶/°C for carbon) and the matrix (α_m ≈ 50 to 70 × 10⁻⁶/°C for epoxy). When a composite cools from cure temperature to room temperature, the matrix wants to shrink far more than the fiber but is constrained by the bond at the interface.
- **Residual stresses (1.5 marks):** This constraint produces **residual thermal stresses** — the matrix is left in **tensile stress** (it is held back from contracting fully), while the fiber is in **compressive stress** (it is squeezed by the matrix). These stresses are "locked in" at every fiber–matrix interface and can reach 30–50 MPa even at room temperature.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **longitudinal coefficient of thermal expansion (α_c,L)** of a unidirectional fiber-reinforced composite using the iso-strain (rule of mixtures) assumption. State the assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A unidirectional carbon/epoxy laminate is cured at 175 °C and cooled to 25 °C. Given: $E_f = 230$ GPa, $\alpha_f = -0.5 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C, $V_f = 0.55$; $E_m = 3.5$ GPa, $\alpha_m = 65 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C, $V_m = 0.45$. Calculate:
  (i) the longitudinal CTE of the composite, and
  (ii) the residual thermal stress in the matrix.
**(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

**Assumptions (2 marks):**
1. Fibers are continuous, parallel, and perfectly aligned with the loading axis.
2. Perfect bonding at the fiber–matrix interface (no slip, no debonding).
3. Both phases experience the **same axial strain** (iso-strain / Voigt condition).
4. Material is homogeneous on the macro-scale.
5. Linear elastic behavior of both phases.
6. Uniform temperature change ΔT throughout the laminate.

**Derivation (5 marks):**

Let $\varepsilon_c$, $\varepsilon_f$, $\varepsilon_m$ be the total strains in the composite, fiber, and matrix respectively.

**Iso-strain condition:**
$$\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m = \varepsilon$$

**Total strain = mechanical strain + free thermal strain:**

For fiber:
$$\varepsilon = \frac{\sigma_f}{E_f} + \alpha_f \Delta T \quad \text{...(i)}$$

For matrix:
$$\varepsilon = \frac{\sigma_m}{E_m} + \alpha_m \Delta T \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Force equilibrium (no external load, unit cross-section):**
$$\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m \quad \text{...(iii)}$$

For a stress-free composite ($\sigma_c = 0$):
$$\sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sigma_m = -\sigma_f \frac{V_f}{V_m} \quad \text{...(iv)}$$

Equating (i) and (ii) and substituting (iv):
$$\frac{\sigma_f}{E_f} + \alpha_f \Delta T = -\frac{\sigma_f V_f}{V_m E_m} + \alpha_m \Delta T$$

$$\sigma_f \left[ \frac{1}{E_f} + \frac{V_f}{V_m E_m} \right] = (\alpha_m - \alpha_f) \Delta T$$

$$\sigma_f \left[ \frac{V_m E_m + V_f E_f}{V_m E_f E_m} \right] = (\alpha_m - \alpha_f) \Delta T$$

Solving for $\sigma_f$ and using the composite strain equation, the effective CTE is obtained as:
$$\boxed{\alpha_{c,L} = \frac{\alpha_f E_f V_f + \alpha_m E_m V_m}{E_f V_f + E_m V_m}}$$

This is the **Rule of Mixtures for longitudinal CTE** (Schapery's upper bound for aligned continuous fibers).

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $\Delta T = 25 - 175 = -150$ °C (magnitude 150 °C)
- $E_f = 230 \times 10^3$ MPa, $\alpha_f = -0.5 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C, $V_f = 0.55$
- $E_m = 3.5 \times 10^3$ MPa, $\alpha_m = 65 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C, $V_m = 0.45$

**(i) Longitudinal CTE (3.5 marks):**
$$\alpha_{c,L} = \frac{(-0.5)(230000)(0.55) + (65)(3500)(0.45)}{(230000)(0.55) + (3500)(0.45)}$$

Numerator:
$$= (-0.5 \times 230000 \times 0.55) + (65 \times 3500 \times 0.45)$$
$$= -63250 + 102375 = 39125$$

Denominator:
$$= (230000 \times 0.55) + (3500 \times 0.45) = 126500 + 1575 = 128075$$

$$\alpha_{c,L} = \frac{39125}{128075} = 0.3055 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /°C}$$

**(ii) Residual Thermal Stress in Matrix (3.5 marks):**
$$\Delta \alpha = (\alpha_m - \alpha_{c,L}) = (65 - 0.3055) \times 10^{-6} = 64.69 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /°C}$$

Free strain mismatch: $\Delta \alpha \cdot \Delta T = 64.69 \times 10^{-6} \times 150 = 9704$ µstrain

Effective matrix modulus (constrained by composite):
$$E_m^{eff} \approx \frac{E_m E_c}{E_m + E_c} = \frac{(3.5)(128.075)}{3.5 + 128.075} = \frac{448.26}{131.575} \approx 3.41 \text{ GPa}$$

Residual tensile stress in matrix:
$$\sigma_m^{th} = \Delta \alpha \cdot \Delta T \cdot E_m^{eff}$$
$$= 9704 \times 10^{-6} \times 3.41 \times 10^3 \text{ MPa}$$
$$= 33.1 \text{ MPa (tensile)}$$

$$\boxed{\alpha_{c,L} \approx 0.306 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /°C}, \quad \sigma_m^{th} \approx 33.1 \text{ MPa (tensile)}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Alternative]**

**(a)** Discuss the **major problem areas** encountered when polymer matrix composites are exposed to elevated temperatures. Explain the role of **Tg, CTE mismatch, and moisture absorption**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With neat diagrams, explain **thermal cycling damage** in composite laminates. How does the stiffness of the laminate degrade with the number of thermal cycles? Suggest **two engineering solutions** to extend thermal-cycling life. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution Outline

**(a) Problem areas (7 marks):**

1. **Glass Transition Temperature (Tg) issue (2 marks):** Above Tg, the polymer matrix transitions from glassy to rubbery state. E_m drops from ~3 GPa to ~10 MPa. The composite loses transverse and shear strength catastrophically. The design rule is: **T_service < Tg − 50 °C (dry) or Tg − 75 °C (wet)**.

2. **CTE Mismatch (2 marks):** Fibers (low α) and matrix (high α) expand differently on heating. This produces residual thermal stresses at the interface, causing **matrix microcracking, debonding, and ply delamination** during thermal cycling. Carbon/epoxy shows the worst mismatch; glass/epoxy is moderate; aramid/epoxy is the best matched.

3. **Moisture Absorption (2 marks):** Polymer matrices are hygroscopic. Absorbed moisture **plasticizes** the resin, depressing Tg by 10–20 °C per 1% moisture content. This is the **"hot/wet"** design condition that aerospace engineers must always check.

4. **Oxidation and Decomposition (1 mark):** Above ~300 °C in air, organic matrices begin to oxidize and char. Carbon fibers oxidize above ~400 °C in air without protective coatings.

**(b) Thermal cycling damage (7 marks):**

- **Mechanism (3 marks):** During each thermal cycle, the matrix expands and contracts more than the fiber. This produces cyclic shear stresses at the fiber–matrix interface. After many cycles, **fatigue microcracks** initiate in the matrix (typically in the transverse plies first, because they carry the highest matrix-dominated stress), then propagate along the fiber–matrix interface, and finally coalesce into **delaminations** between plies.

- **Stiffness Degradation Law (2 marks):** Empirical relation:
$$E(N) = E_0 (1 - k N^p)$$
where $E_0$ is the initial modulus, $N$ is the number of cycles, $k$ is a damage rate constant, and $p$ is an exponent (typically 0.3 to 0.7). After ~1000 cycles, the transverse modulus can drop by 30–50%.

- **Engineering Solutions (2 marks):**
  1. **Toughened matrices** (rubber-toughened or thermoplastic-toughened epoxies) that arrest microcracks.
  2. **3D-reinforced / stitched / z-pinned** preforms that resist delamination growth.
  3. **Symmetric and balanced layups** that minimize in-plane CTE mismatch.
  4. **Use of high-Tg resins** (BMI, cyanate ester, polyimide) to raise the operating ceiling.

---

## 5.1 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU exams:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch:** Forgetting to convert GPa to MPa (multiply by 10³) or α to consistent powers of 10⁻⁶. A calculation with $\alpha_f$ in /°C and $E_f$ in GPa can give answers off by **a factor of 1000**.
> 2. **Sign of ΔT:** When cooling, ΔT is **negative**. Many students drop the sign and get a *compressive* matrix stress instead of the correct *tensile* one. The matrix is always in **tensile** residual stress after cool-down (it is held back from shrinking).
> 3. **Using α_m alone, not Δα:** The driving force is the **mismatch** $(\alpha_m - \alpha_f)$, not the absolute CTE of the matrix.
> 4. **Ignoring moisture:** In aerospace problems, students often quote dry Tg. The examiner may deduct 1–2 marks if "hot/wet" condition is not considered.
> 5. **Mixing iso-strain and iso-stress formulas:** The longitudinal CTE uses iso-strain (Voigt, stiffness-weighted). The transverse CTE uses iso-stress (Reuss, compliance-weighted). Don't interchange them.
> 6. **Skipping assumptions in derivations:** KTU examiners award 1–2 marks for clearly stating the iso-strain assumption. Skipping this loses easy marks.

---

## 5.2 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Tg** is the single most important high-temperature limit for PMCs. Service temperature must be **>50 °C below Tg (dry)**.
- **CTE mismatch** between fiber and matrix produces **residual thermal stresses** proportional to Δα × ΔT × E.
- **Longitudinal CTE** uses the **iso-strain** rule of mixtures (stiffness-weighted).
- **Transverse CTE** uses the **iso-stress** rule of mixtures (volume-weighted, but with the Schapery bound for accuracy).
- **Moisture** plasticizes the matrix and **lowers Tg** by ~10–20 °C per 1% absorbed water.
- **Thermal cycling fatigue** is governed by $E(N) = E_0 (1 - kN^p)$ — stiffness degrades progressively.
- **Carbon fibers** can have **negative CTE** (especially PAN-based, high-modulus), which exacerbates mismatch with epoxy.
- **High-Tg resin hierarchy:** Epoxy (150 °C) < Vinyl Ester (110 °C) < Cyanate Ester (250 °C) < BMI (230 °C) < Polyimide (350 °C).
- **CMCs** solve the high-T problem by using ceramic matrices that don't have Tg in the conventional sense — failure is by **matrix recession and microcracking**, not glass transition.
- **Engineering mitigations** for high-T composites: (i) high-Tg/toughened resins, (ii) balanced/symmetric layups, (iii) tough interlayers, (iv) fiber coatings (PyC, BN, SiC), (v) environmental barrier coatings (EBCs), (vi) 3D reinforcement for thermal-cycling resistance.
- **Always state the iso-strain assumption** explicitly in derivations — it earns easy marks.
- **Hot/wet** is the worst-case design condition in aerospace composites.

> **End of Module 4 Topic Notes — High Temperature Applications Behave; Problem Areas**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

Influence of Structural Geometries; The Advantages and Disadvantages of Composites with Respect to Product Lifecycle Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT744 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Influence of Structural Geometries; The Advantages and Disadvantages of Composites with Respect to Product Lifecycle Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Structural Geometries in Composites

In the context of fibre-reinforced polymer (FRP) composites, **Structural Geometry** refers to the **spatial arrangement, orientation, stacking sequence, and morphological configuration** of the reinforcing phase (fibres, particles, whiskers) within the matrix material. It is the *single most important design variable* that governs the directional (anisotropic) mechanical, thermal, and environmental response of a composite laminate or part.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Structural geometry encompasses the macro-scale architecture of the reinforcement — including **ply orientation (θ), stacking sequence ([θ₁/θ₂/...θₙ]), fibre architecture (unidirectional, woven, braided, stitched), and core topology (in sandwich structures)** — which collectively determine the load-bearing capacity, damage tolerance, and manufacturability of the composite component.

The three principal geometric families studied under this module are:

| Geometry Class | Sub-Types | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Continuous Fibre Laminates** | Unidirectional (UD), Cross-ply, Angle-ply, Quasi-isotropic, Multidirectional | Aerospace skins, wind turbine blades, pressure vessels |
| **Textile / Interlock Geometries** | 2D Woven, 2D Braided, 3D Woven, 3D Braided, Stitched | Tubular structures, T-joints, rocket motor casings |
| **Sandwich / Core Geometries** | Honeycomb core, Foam core, Truss core, Balsa core | Floor panels, radomes, aircraft interiors |

## 1.2 Definition of Product Lifecycle Management (PLM) for Composites

**Product Lifecycle Management (PLM)** is an integrated, information-driven approach to managing a product from its **cradle (raw material extraction)** through its **grave (end-of-life disposal or recycling)**, encompassing four key phases:

1. **Design & Engineering** (CAD/CAE, material selection)
2. **Manufacturing & Fabrication** (lay-up, curing, machining)
3. **In-Service Use** (operation, maintenance, repair)
4. **End-of-Life (EoL)** (recycling, reuse, incineration, landfill)

> [!NOTE]
> Composites possess a **discontinuous, heterogeneous, and often thermoset-dominated** lifecycle, which is fundamentally different from monolithic metals. PLM for composites must therefore address **recyclability bottlenecks, non-destructive evaluation (NDE) requirements, and repair-versus-replace trade-offs** at every phase.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Building Block Logic"

Imagine you are building a **house from LEGO bricks** versus **pouring a concrete monolith**:

- **Monolithic metal** = the concrete pour → uniform, isotropic, but heavy and design-rigid.
- **Composite laminate** = the LEGO house → you choose *which colour brick (fibre orientation)* goes *where (ply position)*, and you *stack them (sequence)* to resist specific wind (load) directions.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Rule of Thumb:** If you know the *load vector*, you can **engineer the geometry** to put stiffness exactly where needed. This is called **tailoring** — and it is the *superpower* of composites that metals simply cannot offer.

The same analogy extends to PLM: a LEGO house can be **disassembled and rebuilt** (recyclable thermoplastic composites), whereas reinforced concrete is **destructive to demolish** (thermoset composites — difficult to recycle).

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Influence of Structural Geometries — Analytical Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Unidirectional (UD) Laminate Geometry
- All fibres aligned parallel to a single reference axis (typically the **0° direction / x-axis**).
- Exhibits **maximum stiffness and strength along the fibre direction** but **minimum transverse properties** (matrix-dominated).
- Governing stiffness (Classical Lamination Theory, CLT):

$$
E_{x} = E_{f} V_{f} + E_{m} (1 - V_{f}) \quad \text{(Rule of Mixtures — Longitudinal)}
$$

$$
\frac{1}{E_{y}} = \frac{V_{f}}{E_{f}} + \frac{(1 - V_{f})}{E_{m}} \quad \text{(Inverse Rule of Mixtures — Transverse)}
$$

- **Why:** Fibres carry axial load; matrix transfers shear; transverse direction is weak because load must "jump" from fibre to matrix via shear-lag.

### 2.1.2 Cross-Ply Laminate Geometry
- Alternating plies of 0° and 90° orientation: **[0/90]ₙ**.
- **Bimodal stiffness** — strong along both x and y axes; poor in shear.
- **Thermal residual stresses** arise due to mismatch in coefficient of thermal expansion (CTE) between 0° and 90° plies upon cooldown from cure temperature.

### 2.1.3 Angle-Ply Laminate Geometry
- Plies oriented at ±θ, e.g., **[+45/-45]ₙ**.
- Excellent for **shear-dominated loads** (e.g., torsional boxes, helicopter rotor blades).
- Off-axis stiffness follows the **Transformation Equation**:

$$
\frac{1}{E_{x}(\theta)} = \frac{\cos^{4}\theta}{E_{1}} + \left(\frac{1}{G_{12}} - \frac{2\nu_{12}}{E_{1}}\right)\sin^{2}\theta \cos^{2}\theta + \frac{\sin^{4}\theta}{E_{2}}
$$

### 2.1.4 Quasi-Isotropic Laminate Geometry
- Stacking sequence **[0/±45/90]ₙ** repeated to give *near-isotropic in-plane response*.
- Approximates: $E_{x} \approx E_{y} \approx E_{xy} \approx \frac{1}{3}E_{1}$ (with reasonable $V_f$).
- Used in **aircraft wing skins** where loading is multi-axial and unpredictable.

### 2.1.5 Woven & Braided Geometries
- **2D Woven:** Warp (0°) and weft (90°) interlaced; gives balanced in-plane properties and **improved impact resistance** due to fibre undulation (crimp).
- **2D Braided:** Helical interlock of multiple yarn systems over a mandrel; ideal for **axisymmetric parts** (drive shafts, prosthetics).
- **3D Woven/Braided:** Z-direction fibres through thickness → **delamination resistance**, near-net-shape manufacturing.

### 2.1.6 Sandwich Structure Geometry
- Two **high-stiffness face sheets** (composite laminates) bonded to a **low-density core** (honeycomb, foam, balsa).
- Bending stiffness per unit mass is orders of magnitude higher than monolithic panels:

$$
D_{\text{sandwich}} \approx \frac{E_{f} \, t_{f} \, d^{2}}{2}
$$

where $d$ is the distance between face-sheet centroids and $t_f$ is face-sheet thickness.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why geometry dictates performance:** A honeycomb sandwich panel with 1% of the solid cross-sectional weight can match the bending stiffness of an aluminium plate. This is the **bending-skin separation principle** — placing material far from the neutral axis maximises the second moment of area, $I$.

## 2.2 Product Lifecycle Management (PLM) — Phase-wise Analysis

| PLM Phase | Composite-Specific Considerations |
|---|---|
| **Material Selection** | Fibre-matrix compatibility, cure kinetics, shelf-life of prepreg, regulatory compliance (REACH) |
| **Design** | Tailored lay-up, ply drop rules, manufacturing constraints, joint design |
| **Manufacturing** | Lay-up, autoclave/oven cure, Resin Transfer Moulding (RTM), Filament Winding, pultrusion |
| **In-Service** | NDE (ultrasound, thermography), repair (scarf/flush patch), damage tracking |
| **End-of-Life** | Recycling (mechanical, thermal, chemical), energy recovery, landfill |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Legend | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{L} = E_{f} V_{f} + E_{m} V_{m}$ | $E_L$: longitudinal modulus, $V_f$: fibre vol. fraction | Predicts axial stiffness of UD composite |
| 2 | $\frac{1}{E_{T}} = \frac{V_{f}}{E_{f}} + \frac{V_{m}}{E_{m}}$ | $E_T$: transverse modulus | Predicts transverse stiffness of UD composite |
| 3 | $\nu_{LT} = \nu_{f} V_{f} + \nu_{m} V_{m}$ | $\nu_{LT}$: major Poisson's ratio | Strain coupling in UD lamina |
| 4 | $\rho_{c} = \rho_{f} V_{f} + \rho_{m} V_{m}$ | $\rho$: density | Weight estimation |
| 5 | $\frac{1}{E_{x}(\theta)} = \frac{c^{4}}{E_{1}} + \left(\frac{1}{G_{12}} - \frac{2\nu_{12}}{E_{1}}\right)s^{2}c^{2} + \frac{s^{4}}{E_{2}}$ | $c=\cos\theta, s=\sin\theta$ | Off-axis stiffness of angle-ply |
| 6 | $A_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^{N} \bar{Q}_{ij}^{(k)} (z_{k} - z_{k-1})$ | $A_{ij}$: extensional stiffness matrix | CLT for laminate analysis |
| 7 | $D_{ij} = \frac{1}{3} \sum_{k=1}^{N} \bar{Q}_{ij}^{(k)} (z_{k}^{3} - z_{k-1}^{3})$ | $D_{ij}$: bending stiffness matrix | Flexural rigidity of laminate |
| 8 | $D_{\text{sand}} = \frac{E_f t_f d^{2}}{2}$ | $d$: core thickness + face thickness | Bending stiffness of sandwich |
| 9 | $V_{f,\text{crit}} = \frac{\sigma_{m,u} - \sigma_{m}'}{\sigma_{f,u} - \sigma_{m}'}$ | Critical fibre volume for strength reversal | Minimum $V_f$ for reinforcement efficiency |
| 10 | $W_{f,\text{rec}} = \frac{m_{\text{recovered fibre}}}{m_{\text{input fibre}}}$ | $W_{f,\text{rec}}$: fibre reclamation yield | PLM recyclability metric |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace (Boeing 787, Airbus A350):** 50%+ composite volume → 20% fuel burn reduction; geometry: quasi-iso + sandwich.
- **Wind Energy:** 80+ m blades use **UD glass/carbon spar caps** + sandwich shells for high specific stiffness.
- **Automotive (BMW i3, Tesla battery trays):** Compression-moulded SMC/carbon SMC; geometry optimised for crash energy absorption via PLM-driven CAE.
- **Marine:** Sandwich hulls with balsa/PVC cores; geometry tuned for **buoyancy + impact tolerance**.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Longitudinal Modulus of a UD Composite (Rule of Mixtures)

We start from the **kinematic assumption** (iso-strain / Voigt model): under axial load parallel to the fibres, both fibre and matrix experience **equal strain**, i.e.,

$$
\varepsilon_{f} = \varepsilon_{m} = \varepsilon_{L}
$$

### Step 1: Force equilibrium on a representative volume element (RVE)
The total axial load $P_c$ is the sum of loads carried by fibre and matrix in parallel:

$$
P_{c} = P_{f} + P_{m}
$$

### Step 2: Express each load in terms of stress and area
Using $P = \sigma A$ and the fact that stresses and strains are uniform within each phase:

$$
\sigma_{c} A_{c} = \sigma_{f} A_{f} + \sigma_{m} A_{m}
$$

### Step 3: Divide by the total area $A_c$ to obtain composite stress

$$
\sigma_{c} = \sigma_{f} \left(\frac{A_{f}}{A_{c}}\right) + \sigma_{m} \left(\frac{A_{m}}{A_{c}}\right) = \sigma_{f} V_{f} + \sigma_{m} V_{m}
$$

### Step 4: Apply the iso-strain condition via Hooke's Law
With $\sigma_i = E_i \varepsilon_i$ and $\varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m = \varepsilon_L$:

$$
\sigma_{c} = E_{f} \varepsilon_{L} V_{f} + E_{m} \varepsilon_{L} V_{m} = \varepsilon_{L} (E_{f} V_{f} + E_{m} V_{m})
$$

### Step 5: Divide by the common strain to obtain the composite modulus

$$
\boxed{\,E_{L} = \frac{\sigma_{c}}{\varepsilon_{L}} = E_{f} V_{f} + E_{m} V_{m}\,}
$$

**Real-world interpretation:** This is the **upper bound** (Voigt bound) for composite stiffness and is accurate to within 2–3% for $V_f > 0.4$ UD CFRP — the reason it is the *first equation* taught in every KTU composite materials course.

## 3.2 Derivation: Transverse Modulus (Inverse Rule of Mixtures / Reuss Model)

For transverse loading (perpendicular to fibres), the **iso-stress assumption** applies: the same stress acts on both phases in series.

### Step 1: Stress equality (iso-stress / Reuss assumption)

$$
\sigma_{f} = \sigma_{m} = \sigma_{T}
$$

### Step 2: Strain compatibility — total displacement is the sum of phase displacements

$$
\Delta L_{c} = \Delta L_{f} + \Delta L_{m}
$$

### Step 3: Convert to strains using common length

$$
\varepsilon_{c} L = \varepsilon_{f} L_{f} + \varepsilon_{m} L_{m}
$$

### Step 4: Recognise $L_f/L = V_f$ and $L_m/L = V_m$ (volume fractions as length fractions in 1D)

$$
\varepsilon_{c} = \varepsilon_{f} V_{f} + \varepsilon_{m} V_{m}
$$

### Step 5: Apply Hooke's Law with iso-stress

$$
\frac{\sigma_{T}}{E_{T}} = \frac{\sigma_{T}}{E_{f}} V_{f} + \frac{\sigma_{T}}{E_{m}} V_{m}
$$

### Step 6: Cancel $\sigma_T$ and rearrange

$$
\boxed{\,\frac{1}{E_{T}} = \frac{V_{f}}{E_{f}} + \frac{V_{m}}{E_{m}}\,}
$$

This is the **lower bound** — accurate to within 5–10% and explains *why* transverse strength of UD composites is **matrix-limited**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Geometry → Property → PLM Impact

| Geometry | Dominant Property | Best-Case Use | PLM-Phase Most Affected |
|---|---|---|---|
| **UD [0]ₙ** | Max $E_L$, min $E_T$ | Spar caps, pressure vessels | Manufacturing (fibre alignment) |
| **Cross-ply [0/90]ₙ** | Bimodal in-plane stiffness | Printed circuit boards, panels | Design (orthotropic coupling) |
| **Angle-ply [±45]ₙ** | High shear stiffness | Torsion tubes, helicopter blades | In-service (fatigue of ±45 plies) |
| **Quasi-iso [0/±45/90]ₙ** | Near-isotropic in-plane | Aircraft wing skins | Design + NDE |
| **Woven (2D)** | Balanced + impact-resistant | Car bonnets, ballistic panels | End-of-life (fibre separation) |
| **Braided (2D/3D)** | Damage tolerance, net-shape | Drive shafts, prosthetics | Repair (geometry continuity) |
| **Sandwich** | High bending stiffness/mass | Floors, radomes, blades | EoL (core-face delamination) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** When asked "How does geometry influence composite behaviour?", the **expected answer** should reference at least *three* of: (i) anisotropy, (ii) stiffness/strength directionality, (iii) damage tolerance, and (iv) manufacturability. A single-parameter answer is **incomplete** and will be penalised.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A unidirectional glass-fibre/epoxy composite has $V_f = 0.55$, $E_f = 72$ GPa, $E_m = 3.5$ GPa. Compute $E_L$, $E_T$, and the **anisotropy ratio** $E_L/E_T$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute $V_m$:**
$$
V_{m} = 1 - V_{f} = 1 - 0.55 = 0.45
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $E_L$ (Rule of Mixtures):**
$$
E_{L} = (72)(0.55) + (3.5)(0.45) = 39.60 + 1.575 = 41.18 \text{ GPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $E_T$ (Inverse Rule of Mixtures):**
$$
\frac{1}{E_{T}} = \frac{0.55}{72} + \frac{0.45}{3.5} = 0.00764 + 0.12857 = 0.13621 \text{ GPa}^{-1}
$$

$$
E_{T} = \frac{1}{0.13621} = 7.34 \text{ GPa}
$$

**Step 4 — Anisotropy Ratio:**
$$
\frac{E_{L}}{E_{T}} = \frac{41.18}{7.34} \approx 5.61
$$

**Interpretation:** A typical UD glass/epoxy shows ~5–6× anisotropy — the *root cause* of why **geometry (stacking sequence) must be designed** to compensate for this directional bias.
