# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Q:** Define *structural geometry* in the context of fibre-reinforced composites. List any **four** common structural geometries used in load-bearing composite components.

**Model Answer (≈ 90 words):**
Structural geometry refers to the spatial arrangement and orientation of reinforcing fibres within the matrix, which dictates the anisotropic mechanical response of a composite. The four common structural geometries are: (i) Unidirectional (UD) laminates with all fibres aligned along one axis; (ii) Cross-ply laminates with alternating 0°/90° plies; (iii) Angle-ply laminates with ±θ plies; and (iv) Sandwich structures with face sheets bonded to a low-density core. **Each geometry is selected to tailor stiffness, strength, and damage tolerance along specific load directions.**

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Q:** State the **Rule of Mixtures** for the longitudinal Young's modulus of a unidirectional fibre-reinforced composite. Mention the **two key assumptions** used in its derivation.

**Model Answer (≈ 80 words):**
The Rule of Mixtures for longitudinal modulus is:
$$
E_{L} = E_{f} V_{f} + E_{m} V_{m}
$$
**Assumptions:** (1) Fibres and matrix are perfectly bonded (no slip at the interface). (2) Both phases experience the **same axial strain** (iso-strain / Voigt assumption), which is physically reasonable because fibres and matrix are bonded in parallel and deform together under axial load. **This is the upper-bound estimate of composite stiffness.**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q:** *(a)* Derive the **Rule of Mixtures** for the longitudinal modulus of a UD composite, clearly stating all assumptions. **\[7 Marks\]**
*(b)* A UD carbon-fibre/epoxy composite has $V_f = 0.60$, $E_f = 230$ GPa, $E_m = 3.6$ GPa. Determine: (i) the longitudinal modulus $E_L$, (ii) the transverse modulus $E_T$, and (iii) the **anisotropy ratio**. Comment on the engineering significance of the anisotropy ratio. **\[7 Marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation:**

*Assumptions:*
1. Fibres are continuous, parallel, and uniformly distributed.
2. Perfect interfacial bonding — no debonding or slip.
3. Iso-strain condition: $\varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m = \varepsilon_L$ (Voigt model).

*Derivation steps:*
1. Force equilibrium on RVE: $P_c = P_f + P_m$
2. Express in terms of stresses: $\sigma_c A_c = \sigma_f A_f + \sigma_m A_m$
3. Divide by $A_c$: $\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$
4. Apply Hooke's law with iso-strain: $\sigma_c = (E_f V_f + E_m V_m)\varepsilon_L$
5. Divide by $\varepsilon_L$:

$$
\boxed{\,E_{L} = E_{f} V_{f} + E_{m} V_{m}\,}
$$

**Part (b) — Numerical:**

(i) $V_m = 1 - 0.60 = 0.40$

$$
E_{L} = (230)(0.60) + (3.6)(0.40) = 138.0 + 1.44 = 139.44 \text{ GPa}
$$

(ii) Transverse modulus:

$$
\frac{1}{E_{T}} = \frac{0.60}{230} + \frac{0.40}{3.6} = 0.00261 + 0.11111 = 0.11372 \text{ GPa}^{-1}
$$

$$
E_{T} = \frac{1}{0.11372} \approx 8.79 \text{ GPa}
$$

(iii) Anisotropy ratio:

$$
\frac{E_{L}}{E_{T}} = \frac{139.44}{8.79} \approx 15.86
$$

**Engineering significance:** An anisotropy ratio of ~16 confirms that the composite is **highly orthotropic**. Design implications: (1) Components must be designed with the 0° direction aligned to the **principal load vector**; (2) Off-axis loads cause **shear coupling and matrix-dominated failure**; (3) Justifies using **cross-ply or quasi-isotropic lay-ups** for multi-axial loading.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q:** *(a)* Explain the **four phases of Product Lifecycle Management (PLM)** for a composite component. Discuss how structural geometry influences **design and manufacturing** decisions. **\[7 Marks\]**
*(b)* Compare the **advantages and disadvantages** of composites versus metals in the context of PLM. Provide at least **two examples** for each. **\[7 Marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — PLM Phases:**

| # | PLM Phase | Composite-Specific Activity |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Design & Engineering** | Ply layout, stacking sequence, FEA of $A_{ij}$ and $D_{ij}$ matrices, joint design |
| 2 | **Manufacturing** | Hand lay-up / AFP / RTM, autoclave cure, NDE of cured part |
| 3 | **In-Service Use** | Periodic NDE (ultrasound, thermography), damage tracking, repair |
| 4 | **End-of-Life** | Disassembly, recycling (mechanical/thermal/chemical), disposal |

**Influence of geometry on design & manufacturing:**
- **Design:** Geometry determines the **stiffness matrix $[A]$, $[B]$, $[D]$** via CLT; coupling matrix $[B]$ must be controlled (e.g., symmetric lay-up to avoid bending-stretching coupling).
- **Manufacturing:** UD prepreg is **drapable** but limited to simple contours; woven/braided fabrics conform to **doubly curved surfaces**; 3D braids are near-net-shape but require **specialised looms**. Ply drop rules and **stacking sequence constraints** are geometry-driven.

**Part (b) — Comparison:**

**Advantages of composites in PLM:**
1. **Lightweight + Tailorable stiffness** → 20–30% fuel savings in aerospace/automotive (in-service phase benefit).
2. **Corrosion resistance** → longer service life, lower maintenance (e.g., GFRP bridge decks last 50+ years vs. 25 years for steel).
3. **Part consolidation** → 70–80% fewer fasteners in Boeing 787 fuselage → simpler assembly, lower manufacturing cost.
4. **Damage tolerance** in woven/braided forms → safer failure modes in crash.

**Disadvantages of composites in PLM:**
1. **Recycling difficulty** — thermoset matrices (epoxy) cannot be remelted; chemical/thermal recycling is **energy-intensive** and degrades fibre length.
2. **High initial cost** — carbon fibre precursor (PAN) is expensive; autoclave curing is capex-heavy.
3. **Difficult inspection & repair** — delamination, matrix cracking, and impact damage (BVID) are **invisible to the naked eye**; require ultrasound/CT scanning.
4. **Moisture absorption & UV degradation** — long-term durability in marine/outdoor applications requires **protective coatings**, adding to lifecycle cost.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in this Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing iso-strain and iso-stress models** — students often mix up Rule of Mixtures (longitudinal, iso-strain) and Inverse Rule (transverse, iso-stress). Marks deducted for not stating the assumption.
> 2. **Missing the "geometry-to-PLM linkage"** — when asked about PLM, students list generic advantages/disadvantages of composites *without* linking them to **specific geometric choices**. Always say: *"Because the lay-up is [0/±45/90]ₙ, manufacturing requires..."*
> 3. **Forgetting $V_m = 1 - V_f$** — leaving $V_m$ undefined in numerical problems costs 1 mark.
> 4. **No units in final answer** — always state **GPa, kg/m³, %**.
> 5. **Ignoring anisotropy** — in design questions, failing to mention that **off-axis loading requires cross-ply or quasi-iso stacking** is a recurring error. Examiners award 2 marks for this insight alone.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4**

- **Structural geometry** = the **spatial architecture** of the reinforcement; it is the *primary design lever* in composites.
- **Three geometry families:** continuous-fibre laminates, textile architectures, sandwich structures.
- **Rule of Mixtures (Longitudinal, Iso-Strain):** $E_L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ — **upper bound**.
- **Inverse Rule (Transverse, Iso-Stress):** $\frac{1}{E_T} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m}$ — **lower bound**.
- **Off-axis stiffness** varies as $\cos^4\theta$ (dominant for small θ) — explains why **0° plies dominate stiffness** while **±45° plies dominate shear**.
- **Quasi-isotropic [0/±45/90]ₙ** gives ~$E_1/3$ in-plane modulus — used in **wing skins**.
- **Sandwich structures** exploit the **second moment of area** ($I \propto d^2$) to achieve high bending stiffness with low mass.
- **PLM Phases:** Design → Manufacturing → In-Service → End-of-Life.
- **Composite advantages in PLM:** lightweight, tailorable, corrosion-resistant, part-consolidation.
- **Composite disadvantages in PLM:** hard to recycle (thermosets), high initial cost, difficult inspection/repair, moisture/UV sensitivity.
- **Anisotropy ratio** $E_L/E_T$ for typical CFRP ≈ 15–20; for GFRP ≈ 5–6 — always **engineer the lay-up to the load**.
- **Critical fibre volume fraction** $V_{f,\text{crit}}$ defines the **minimum $V_f$** below which the composite is *weaker* than the neat matrix — design must stay above this threshold.
- **NDE methods** (ultrasound, thermography, X-ray CT) are PLM essentials for damage-tolerant design.
- **Recycling pathways:** mechanical (shredding), thermal (pyrolysis, fluidised bed), chemical (solvolysis) — each with different fibre quality and energy footprint.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: General Considerations and Process Involved in Composite Structural Design
Typical In-Service Damage Types for Composites
Non-Destructive Inspection Techniques for Detecting Damage in Composites
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT744 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Non-Destructive Inspection Techniques for Detecting Damage in Composites # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition **Non-Destructive Inspection (NDI)** or **Non-Destructive Testing (NDT)** refers to a family of analytical techniques used in materials science and engineering to evaluate the properties, integrity, and internal structure of a component **without altering its physical state, causing damage, or impairing its serviceability**. In the context of composite materials, NDI is the primary quality-assurance tool used to detect, characterize, and quantify internal flaws such as **delaminations, matrix microcracks, fiber breakage, porosity, inclusions, and impact-induced damage** (e.g., BVID — Barely Visible Impact Damage). > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Standard):** "NDT of composites is the process of interrogating a laminate or fiber-reinforced structure using physical energy fields (acoustic, thermal, electromagnetic, optical) to identify discontinuities that compromise the load-bearing capacity of the material, while leaving the component fit for continued use." ## 1.2 Why is NDI Critical for Composites? Unlike metals, composites fail via **multiple, progressive, and often invisible damage mechanisms**. A composite part can lose 40–60% of its compressive strength after a low-velocity impact that leaves **no visible surface trace**. This unique damage-tolerance profile makes NDI mandatory in aerospace, marine, automotive, and wind-energy sectors. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Invisible Damage Problem:** A Boeing 787 or Airbus A350 composite fuselage panel can be critically weakened by a 2 mm deep delamination that is **completely invisible to the naked eye**. Visual inspection alone is insufficient — volumetric and surface-wave techniques are mandatory. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Doctor's Toolkit for Composites" Think of a composite panel as a **human patient** and NDI techniques as the **diagnostic tools a doctor uses**: | Doctor's Tool | NDI Equivalent | What it Detects | |---------------|----------------|-----------------| | Stethoscope (sound) | Acoustic Emission (AE) | "Listening" to active cracks growing | | X-ray machine | Radiography / CT | Internal "bones" (voids, inclusions) | | Ultrasound scan | Ultrasonic C-scan | Soft-tissue "tears" (delaminations) | | Infrared thermometer | Thermography | "Hot spots" indicating subsurface defects | | Tapping knee reflex | Tap / Hammer Test | Loose "joints" (disbonds) | | Eye exam | Visual Testing (VT) | Surface scratches, dents | Just as a doctor chooses tools based on the symptoms, a KTU engineer must select the **right NDI method based on damage type, depth, material, and accessibility**. ## 1.4 Standard Reference Frame Most composite NDI work is governed by: - **ASTM E2533** — Standard Guide for NDT of Polymer Matrix Composites - **ASTM D2734** — Void content in composites - **ASTM E164** — Ultrasonic contact testing - **ASME Section V** — Boiler and pressure vessel NDE - **MIL-HDBK-17** — Composite Materials Handbook (US DoD) - **EN 1330 / EN 13554** — European NDT standards The **probability of detection (POD)** is the standard metric used by KTU-board questions and is reported as a function of flaw size with a **95% confidence interval**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of NDI Techniques for Composites NDI methods are classified along **three primary energy domains**: 1. **Mechanical/Acoustic Energy** → Ultrasonic Testing, Acoustic Emission, Tap Testing 2. **Electromagnetic Radiation** → Radiography (X-ray, neutron), Computed Tomography 3. **Thermal Energy** → Infrared Thermography (pulsed, lock-in, vibrothermography) 4. **Electrical/Magnetic** → Eddy Current (for CFRP with conductive fibers) 5. **Optical** → Shearography, Holography, Laser Shearography > [!TIP] > **Mnemonic — "MAETEO":** Mechanical, Acoustic, Electromagnetic, Thermal, Electrical, Optical — covers every KTU-listed NDI method. ## 2.2 Detailed Methodology Breakdown ### 2.2.1 Visual Testing (VT) — The Baseline - **Principle:** Direct or magnified (10×) optical examination under controlled lighting. - **Detects:** Surface cracks, dents, blistering, fiber breakage, burn marks, paint anomalies. - **Limitation:** Surface only; cannot see internal delaminations or BVID. - **Standard:** ASTM D5431. ### 2.2.2 Ultrasonic Testing (UT) — The Workhorse Ultrasonic waves in the **0.5 MHz to 50 MHz** range are transmitted through the composite. A **piezoelectric transducer** converts electrical pulses into mechanical waves, which propagate, reflect off internal interfaces, and return as echoes. **Three Standard Display Modes:** - **A-scan** — 1D amplitude vs. time trace (single point inspection). - **B-scan** — 2D cross-sectional view (depth vs. scan position). - **C-scan** — 2D plan-view map of through-thickness response (most common for composites — gives a top-down "damage map"). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why high frequency for composites?** Because composite defects are typically small (delaminations 1–10 mm), the inspection wavelength must be smaller than the defect for reliable detection: λ = c/f. For CFRP (c ≈ 3000 m/s), a 5 MHz probe gives λ ≈ 0.6 mm, ideal for resolving millimeter-scale flaws. ### 2.2.3 Radiographic Testing (RT) - **Principle:** Differential X-ray attenuation governed by density (ρ) and atomic number (Z). - **Detects:** Voids, inclusions, broken fibers, transverse cracks, moisture ingress. - **Limitation:** Planar defect (delaminations parallel to beam) may go undetected. - **Enhancement:** **Computed Tomography (CT)** gives 3D volumetric data — gold standard but expensive. ### 2.2.4 Infrared Thermography (IRT) - **Principle:** Anomalous thermal diffusion at defect zones. - **Modes:** * **Passive IRT** — no external heat; monitors operational heat signatures. * **Active IRT** — external flash lamp, halogen, or ultrasonic excitation; defect areas heat/cool differently due to trapped air. * **Pulsed Thermography (PT)** — short heat pulse; defect appears as temperature contrast. * **Lock-in Thermography** — periodic heating; depth-selective. * **Vibrothermography** — frictional heating at crack faces during ultrasonic vibration. ### 2.2.5 Acoustic Emission (AE) - **Principle:** Real-time detection of transient elastic waves emitted by **active** damage events (crack growth, fiber fracture, delamination propagation). - **Key Metric:** **Felicity Ratio** = (Load at emission onset during re-loading) / (Maximum prior load). Ratio < 1.0 → **Kaiser Failure**; Ratio = 1.0 → **Felicity Effect** (damage accumulation — the "composite" signal). - **Application:** Pressure vessel proof testing, in-service structural health monitoring (SHM). ### 2.2.6 Shearography (Electronic Speckle Pattern Interferometry) - **Principle:** Laser interferometry measuring surface strain gradient under thermal/vacuum loading. - **Detects:** Delaminations, disbonds, core crush in sandwich structures. - **Advantage:** Non-contact, full-field, real-time; ideal for in-service aerospace inspections. ### 2.2.7 Tap Testing - **Principle:** Localized mechanical impulse; defect creates a duller acoustic response (lower pitch). - **Use:** Field inspection, large areas (wind-turbine blades). - **Quantitative variant:** **Pitch-catch** instrumented tap-test with frequency spectrum analysis. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Equation / Concept | Mathematical Form | Description / Use | |---|--------------------|-------------------|------------------| | 1 | Ultrasonic Wavelength | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f}$ | c = sound velocity in medium; f = probe frequency | | 2 | Snell's Law (UT refraction) | $\dfrac{\sin\theta_1}{c_1} = \dfrac{\sin\theta_2}{c_2}$ | Mode conversion at laminate interface | | 3 | Critical Angle (no L-wave) | $\theta_c = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{c_1}{c_2}\right)$ | For longitudinal-to-shear conversion | | 4 | Time-of-Flight (depth) | $d = \dfrac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$ | C-scan depth calibration; Δt = echo time | | 5 | Attenuation (Beer–Lambert) | $I(x) = I_0 e^{-\alpha x}$ | α = attenuation coefficient (Np/m); dB conversion: α_dB = 8.686·α | | 6 | Attenuation in Composites | $\alpha = \alpha_0 + c_f f^n$ | c_f = fiber content factor; n ≈ 1 for CFRP | | 7 | X-ray Intensity (Beer–Lambert) | $I = I_0 e^{-\mu \rho x}$ | μ = mass attenuation coefficient | | 8 | Acoustic Emission Felicity Ratio | $FR = \dfrac{P_{\text{onset,reload}}}{P_{\text{max,prior}}}$ | FR < 1 → Kaiser; FR = 1 → Felicity | | 9 | Thermal Diffusion Length | $\mu_{th} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2k}{\rho c_p \omega}}$ | Depth penetration in lock-in thermography | | 10 | Pulse-Echo Resolution | $\delta_r = \dfrac{c}{2 \Delta f}$ | Minimum separable flaw depth | | 11 | Strain-optic sensitivity (shearography) | $\Delta \phi = \dfrac{4\pi}{\lambda}\Delta u$ | Δu = out-of-plane displacement | | 12 | C-scan Gate Position | $t_{\text{gate}} = \dfrac{2d}{c} \pm \Delta t$ | Defect within ±Δt of interface | > [!NOTE] > **Unit Reminder:** All lengths in **meters (m)**, frequencies in **Hz**, time in **seconds (s)**, attenuation α in **Np/m** (numerical) or **dB/m** (engineering). Speed of sound in CFRP ≈ **3000 m/s** longitudinal, ≈ **2000 m/s** shear. ## 2.4 Selection Logic — Which NDI to Use? | Damage Type | Best NDI Method | Why | |-------------|-----------------|-----| | Delamination (inter-ply) | Ultrasonic C-scan, Shearography | Both give full-field delamination maps | | BVID (barely visible) | Phased Array UT, Pulsed Thermography | Small depth, requires high sensitivity | | Porosity / voids | UT attenuation, Micro-CT | Volumetric sub-millimeter defects | | Fiber breakage | Radiography, CT | Through-thickness fiber breaks | | Matrix microcracks | Micro-CT, AE | Sub-ply scale, active growth | | Disbond (sandwich) | Thermography, Tap Test | Trapped air changes thermal/acoustic response | | In-service damage | AE, SHM with PZT | Continuous real-time monitoring | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Ultrasonic Depth of Flaw from Time-of-Flight **Statement:** A 5 MHz longitudinal ultrasonic probe is used in pulse-echo mode on a CFRP laminate. The speed of sound in CFRP is **c = 3050 m/s**. The time interval between the front-surface echo and the back-wall (or defect) echo is **Δt = 2.1 µs**. Find the depth of the flaw. **Step 1 — Write the governing pulse-echo relation.** In pulse-echo mode, the wave travels **to the flaw and back**, so the total path is 2d. $$ \Delta t = \dfrac{2d}{c} $$ **Step 2 — Solve for d.** $$ d = \dfrac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2} $$ **Step 3 — Substitute numerical values.** Note: 1 µs = 1 × 10⁻⁶ s. $$ d = \dfrac{3050 \;\text{m/s} \times 2.1 \times 10^{-6} \;\text{s}}{2} $$ **Step 4 — Evaluate numerator.** $$ 3050 \times 2.1 = 6405 $$ $$ d = \dfrac{6405 \times 10^{-6}}{2} $$ **Step 5 — Final result.** $$ d = 3202.5 \times 10^{-6} \;\text{m} = 3.20 \;\text{mm} $$ **Interpretation:** The flaw lies **3.20 mm** below the entry surface — typical of an inter-ply delamination in a quasi-isotropic [0/45/-45/90]₂ₛ layup. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Attenuation & Detectability Limit **Statement:** A CFRP panel of thickness **x = 4 mm** is inspected at **f = 10 MHz**. The material's attenuation coefficient follows the engineering model **α = 0.05·f (dB/mm·MHz)**. Find the total attenuation in dB and the fraction of original signal received. **Step 1 — Compute α at the inspection frequency.** $$ \alpha = 0.05 \times 10 = 0.5 \;\text{dB/mm} $$ **Step 2 — Compute total attenuation over 2x (pulse-echo round trip).** $$ A_{\text{total}} = 2 \alpha x = 2 \times 0.5 \times 4 = 4.0 \;\text{dB} $$ **Step 3 — Convert to linear fraction using I = I₀ · 10^(−A/10).** $$ \dfrac{I}{I_0} = 10^{-A/10} = 10^{-0.4} $$ **Step 4 — Numerical evaluation.** $$ 10^{-0.4} = 0.398 \approx 39.8\% $$ **Step 5 — Interpretation.** Approximately **39.8%** of the transmitted signal is recovered at the receiver. The remaining 60.2% is lost to scattering at fiber–matrix interfaces (the dominant loss mechanism in CFRP). This is why **phased-array and immersion C-scan** with signal averaging are preferred over contact probes for thick laminates. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Felicity Ratio Interpretation (KTU Favourite) **Statement:** A composite pressure vessel is loaded to a peak pressure of **P_max = 8.0 MPa**, then unloaded. On reloading, acoustic emission begins at **P_onset = 5.6 MPa**. Determine the Felicity Ratio and comment on the structural condition. **Step 1 — Recall the definition.** $$ FR = \dfrac{P_{\text{onset,reload}}}{P_{\text{max,prior}}} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute.** $$ FR = \dfrac{5.6}{8.0} = 0.70 $$ **Step 3 — Interpret against KTU/Kodali criteria.** - **FR ≥ 1.0** → No significant prior damage (acceptable). - **0.95 ≤ FR < 1.0** → Minor damage (monitor). - **0.80 ≤ FR < 0.95** → Moderate damage (repair recommended). - **FR < 0.80** → **Severe damage; reject or major overhaul.** **Step 4 — Conclusion.** $$ \boxed{FR = 0.70 < 0.80 \Rightarrow \text{Severe damage; vessel must be removed from service.}} $$ --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Snell's Law for UT Mode Conversion **Statement:** A longitudinal wave in a water couplant (c₁ = 1480 m/s) strikes a CFRP surface (c_L2 = 3050 m/s) at an incidence angle θ₁ = 20°. Find the refracted longitudinal angle in CFRP. **Step 1 — Apply Snell's Law.** $$ \dfrac{\sin\theta_1}{c_1} = \dfrac{\sin\theta_2}{c_2} $$ **Step 2 — Solve for sin θ₂.** $$ \sin\theta_2 = \dfrac{c_2}{c_1} \sin\theta_1 = \dfrac{3050}{1480} \sin 20° $$ **Step 3 — Compute numerical factor.** $$ \dfrac{3050}{1480} = 2.0608 $$ **Step 4 — Evaluate sin 20°.** $$ \sin 20° = 0.3420 $$ **Step 5 — Multiply.** $$ \sin\theta_2 = 2.0608 \times 0.3420 = 0.7048 $$ **Step 6 — Solve for θ₂.** $$ \theta_2 = \sin^{-1}(0.7048) = 44.8° $$ **Step 7 — Critical angle check.** $$ \theta_c = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{c_1}{c_2}\right) = \sin^{-1}(0.4851) = 29.0° $$ Since θ₂ = 44.8° > 29.0°, **a longitudinal wave is also generated in CFRP but with significant shear-wave component** — important for angled-beam inspection of delaminations. --- ## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Thermal Diffusion Depth in Lock-in Thermography **Statement:** A CFRP skin (k = 0.7 W/m·K, ρ = 1600 kg/m³, c_p = 800 J/kg·K) is excited by a halogen lamp modulated at **f = 0.1 Hz**. Calculate the thermal diffusion length μ_th (depth of maximum sensitivity). **Step 1 — Recall the thermal diffusion length formula.** $$ \mu_{th} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2k}{\rho c_p \omega}} $$ **Step 2 — Compute angular frequency.** $$ \omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi \times 0.1 = 0.628 \;\text{rad/s} $$ **Step 3 — Compute thermal diffusivity (denominator inside √).** $$ \rho c_p = 1600 \times 800 = 1.28 \times 10^6 \;\text{J/m}^3\!\cdot\!\text{K} $$ **Step 4 — Numerator.** $$ 2k = 2 \times 0.7 = 1.4 $$ **Step 5 — Compute fraction.** $$ \dfrac{2k}{\rho c_p \omega} = \dfrac{1.4}{1.28 \times 10^6 \times 0.628} = \dfrac{1.4}{8.0384 \times 10^5} $$ **Step 6 — Numerical evaluation.** $$ = 1.741 \times 10^{-6} \;\text{m}^2 $$ **Step 7 — Take square root.** $$ \mu_{th} = \sqrt{1.741 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.32 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{m} = 1.32 \;\text{mm} $$ **Step 8 — Interpretation.** The lock-in thermography setup is **most sensitive to defects at ~1.3 mm depth**. Reducing frequency (e.g., to 0.01 Hz) would increase μ_th — useful for inspecting thicker laminates. --- ## 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix — Damage Type vs. NDI Sensitivity | Damage Type ↓ / NDI Method → | VT | UT (C-scan) | RT | Thermography | AE | Shearography | |------------------------------|----|----|----|--------------|----|----| | Surface scratch | ★★★ | ★ | ★ | ★★ | ★ | ★ | | Delamination (inter-ply) | ★ | ★★★ | ★ | ★★★ | ★★ | ★★★ | | Porosity | ★ | ★★ | ★★ | ★★ | ★ | ★ | | Fiber breakage | ★ | ★★ | ★★★ | ★ | ★★ | ★ | | Matrix microcrack | ★ | ★ | ★★ | ★ | ★★★ | ★ | | Impact damage (BVID) | ★ | ★★★ | ★★ | ★★★ | ★★ | ★★★ | | Disbond (sandwich core) | ★★ | ★★ | ★★ | ★★★ | ★★ | ★★★ | | Moisture ingress | ★ | ★★ | ★ | ★★ | ★ | ★ | (★★★ = Excellent, ★★ = Good, ★ = Limited) # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Ultrasonic Pulse-Echo C-Scan System ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[PULSER UNIT
High Voltage 100 to 400 V] --> A2[PIEZO TRANSDUCER
5 to 15 MHz Probe] A2 --> A3[COUPLANT
Water Gel or Immersion] A3 --> A4[COMPOSITE PANEL
CFRP GFRP LAMINATE] A4 --> A5[ECHO RETURN PATH
Defect Reflection] A5 --> A6[RECEIVER AMPLIFIER
40 to 60 dB Gain] A6 --> A7[TIME GATE
Selects Flaw Echo] A7 --> A8[ADC DIGITIZER
8 to 12 bit] A8 --> A9[SCAN CONTROLLER
XY Stage Encoder] A9 --> A10[C-SCAN IMAGE
2D Plan View Map] A9 --> A11[B-SCAN IMAGE
Depth Cross Section] A9 --> A12[A-SCAN TRACE
Amplitude vs Time] ``` **Functional Notes for the Diagram:** - The **pulser** excites the piezo crystal; the resulting acoustic wave is coupled into the composite via water or gel. - A **flaw** (delamination) reflects part of the wave; the **time gate** is set to capture only reflections from a chosen depth range. - The **XY scanner** moves the transducer; encoder data combined with gated amplitude builds the C-scan map. ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Damage Type → NDI Method Selection ```mermaid flowchart TD B1[COMPOSITE PART
INCOMING INSPECTION] --> B2{DAMAGE TYPE
SUSPECTED} B2 -- SURFACE ONLY --> B3[VISUAL TEST VT
ASTM D5431] B2 -- INTERPLY --> B4[DELAMINATION] B2 -- VOLUMETRIC --> B5[POROSITY VOIDS] B2 -- TRANSPLY --> B6[FIBER BREAKAGE] B2 -- ACTIVE --> B7[CRACK GROWTH] B4 --> B8[ULTRASONIC C-SCAN
OR SHEAROGRAPHY] B5 --> B9[UT ATTENUATION
OR MICRO CT] B6 --> B10[RADIOGRAPHY RT
OR CT SCAN] B7 --> B11[ACOUSTIC EMISSION AE
REAL TIME MONITORING] B8 --> B12[QUANTIFY DEPTH
AND AREA] B9 --> B12 B10 --> B12 B11 --> B13[FELICITY RATIO
ACCEPT REJECT] B12 --> B14[GO NO GO DECISION
REPAIR OR SCRAP] B13 --> B14 ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram — Phased Array UT Beam Steering Concept ```mermaid flowchart LR C1[PHASED ARRAY PROBE
16 to 128 ELEMENTS] --> C2[ELECTRONIC DELAY LINE
Individually Programmed] C2 --> C3[CONSTRUCTIVE INTERFERENCE
Beam Steered at Angle] C3 --> C4[COMPOSITE LAMINATE
Steered Beam Sweeps Depth] C4 --> C5[FOCAL LAWS
Focus at Defect Depth] C5 --> C6[SECTORIAL B-SCAN
Full Volume Coverage] C6 --> C7[DEFECT LOCATION
DEPTH AND ANGLE] ``` **Engineering Significance:** Phased-array UT is the **modern KTU-industry standard** for composite inspection because electronic beam steering covers 100% of the volume in a single scan pass, replacing multiple angled manual probes. ## 4.4 Block Diagram — Active Thermography Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD D1[HALOGEN OR FLASH LAMP
Heat Pulse Input] --> D2[COMPOSITE SURFACE
Heating Phase] D2 --> D3[HEAT DIFFUSION
Defects Trap Heat] D3 --> D4[INFRARED CAMERA
Records T over t] D4 --> D5[THERMAL SEQUENCE
100 to 1000 frames] D5 --> D6[POST PROCESSING
PCT or Thermographic Signal Reconstruction] D6 --> D7[DEFECT MAP
2D Depth Sliced] ``` **Why this works:** Defects (delaminations, voids) act as **thermal resistors**; the heat front "hangs" over them, producing a localized hot or cold patch captured by the IR camera within milliseconds to seconds. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 — Definition of BVID **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** *Define the term "Barely Visible Impact Damage (BVID)" in composites and explain why it is significant in damage-tolerant design.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1.5 marks):** BVID refers to impact-induced damage in a composite laminate — typically a small delamination and matrix crack network — that is **not visually detectable** under standard 10× visual inspection (ASTM D5431), but can significantly reduce the residual compressive strength of the laminate (by 30–60%). **Significance (1.5 marks):** BVID is significant because (i) it acts as a nucleation site for fatigue-driven delamination growth under cyclic loading; (ii) it may be present without any external sign; (iii) it requires volumetric NDI such as ultrasonic C-scan, pulse thermography, or shearography for detection. In damage-tolerant aerospace design (per MIL-HDBK-17), the structure must retain **residual strength after BVID**, making reliable NDI mandatory. --- ### Question 2 — Felicity Ratio **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *What is the Felicity Ratio in acoustic emission testing? State its significance.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1.5 marks):** The Felicity Ratio (FR) is the ratio of the load at which acoustic emission **re-commences during re-loading** to the **maximum load previously applied**: $$ FR = \dfrac{P_{\text{onset,reload}}}{P_{\text{max,prior}}} $$ **Significance (1.5 marks):** FR is a **memory indicator of prior damage**. A value close to 1.0 indicates a "Kaiser" response (sound material, emission only above previous max). A value significantly less than 1.0 (the "Felicity effect") indicates that damage has accumulated, and the structure is sensitive to lower loads. It is used as a **go/no-go criterion** for composite pressure vessels, pipelines, and aircraft components during proof testing. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) — NDI Method Selection & C-Scan Analysis **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 | Module 4 | 14 Marks]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **operating principle of an ultrasonic pulse-echo C-scan system** for detecting delaminations in a CFRP laminate. Discuss the role of couplant, transducer frequency, and time-gating. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A **5 MHz longitudinal probe** is used in pulse-echo mode on a CFRP panel (c = 3050 m/s). The first back-wall echo arrives at **t₁ = 5.25 µs** and a delamination echo at **t₂ = 2.10 µs**. Calculate (i) the panel thickness, and (ii) the depth of the delamination. Comment on the inspection resolution. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — (a) Block Diagram & Principle (7 marks) - **Block diagram** (3 marks) — pulser → transducer → couplant → composite → reflected echoes → receiver → gate → ADC → scan controller → C-scan image. (Refer to Section 4.1 for the standard KTU-acceptable schematic.) - **Principle** (2 marks) — A high-voltage electrical pulse excites a piezoelectric crystal, producing a longitudinal ultrasonic wave. This wave is coupled into the composite via water or gel. At internal interfaces (delamination, void), acoustic-impedance mismatch causes partial reflection. The time-of-flight encodes depth; the gated amplitude maps flaw intensity. - **Role of parameters** (2 marks): * **Couplant** — eliminates air gap; air has Z ≈ 0.0004 MRayl, vs. CFRP ≈ 4 MRayl — without couplant, >99% of energy reflects at the surface. * **Transducer frequency** — higher f → smaller λ → finer resolution but higher attenuation. 5–15 MHz is the typical CFRP sweet spot. * **Time-gating** — selectively captures echoes from a chosen depth range, suppressing front-surface and back-wall signals to enhance flaw contrast. #### Model Solution — (b) Numerical (7 marks) **(i) Panel thickness from back-wall echo:** (3.5 marks) **Step 1 — Pulse-echo relation.** $$ \Delta t = \dfrac{2d}{c} $$ **Step 2 — Solve for d.** $$ d = \dfrac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2} $$ **Step 3 — Substitute t₁ = 5.25 µs.** $$ d = \dfrac{3050 \times 5.25 \times 10^{-6}}{2} $$ **Step 4 — Compute.** $$ d = \dfrac{16012.5 \times 10^{-6}}{2} = 8.006 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{m} $$ **Step 5 — Result.** $$ \boxed{d_{\text{panel}} \approx 8.01 \;\text{mm}} $$ **(ii) Delamination depth from t₂ = 2.10 µs:** (3.5 marks) **Step 1 — Same equation, new Δt.** $$ d = \dfrac{3050 \times 2.10 \times 10^{-6}}{2} $$ **Step 2 — Compute.** $$ d = \dfrac{6405 \times 10^{-6}}{2} = 3202.5 \times 10^{-6} \;\text{m} $$ **Step 3 — Result.** $$ \boxed{d_{\text{delam}} \approx 3.20 \;\text{mm}} $$ **Resolution comment (extra credit):** The wavelength at 5 MHz is λ = c/f = 3050/5×10⁶ = 0.61 mm. Since the delamination is 3.2 mm thick (in depth), and λ is much smaller, **the inspection easily resolves the flaw** (rule of thumb: flaw size ≥ 3λ for reliable detection; here, multiple wavelengths span the flaw, so signal-to-noise is excellent). --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Thermography & Felicity Ratio **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 | Module 4 | 14 Marks]** **(a)** Explain the **principle of active infrared thermography** for NDI of composites. Compare **pulsed thermography** and **lock-in thermography** with respect to depth sensitivity, speed, and suitable applications. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A composite pressure vessel is loaded to a peak pressure of **P_max = 10 MPa**. On reloading, AE events are first detected at **P_onset = 7.2 MPa**. **(i)** Calculate the **Felicity Ratio** and interpret the structural condition. **(ii)** A CFRP skin (k = 0.7 W/m·K, ρ = 1600 kg/m³, c_p = 800 J/kg·K) is inspected by lock-in thermography at **f = 0.05 Hz**. Determine the **thermal diffusion length**. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — (a) Active Thermography (7 marks) **Principle (3 marks):** Active IRT applies a controlled external heat pulse (or modulated heat) to the composite surface. The heat diffuses inward. At defect zones (delaminations, voids, disbonds), the thermal conductivity mismatch causes a **local temperature contrast** captured by an IR camera. A defect appears as a "hot spot" (during heating) or "cold spot" (during cooling) — depending on whether it acts as a thermal barrier or trap. The defect depth is inferred from the time at which maximum contrast appears (deeper defects → longer time). **Comparison table (4 marks):** | Feature | Pulsed Thermography | Lock-in Thermography | |---------|--------------------|---------------------| | Heat input | Single short flash (ms) | Continuous sinusoidal modulation | | Depth sensitivity | Wide range, single test | Tunable by modulation frequency | | Speed | Very fast (single pulse) | Slower (multiple cycles) | | Best for | Field inspection, large areas | Quantitative depth profiling | | Typical application | Wind-turbine blades, aircraft skin | Lab quantitative NDI, CFRP panels | #### Model Solution — (b) Felicity Ratio & Thermal Diffusion (7 marks) **(i) Felicity Ratio (3.5 marks):** **Step 1 — Substitute values.** $$ FR = \dfrac{P_{\text{onset}}}{P_{\text{max}}} = \dfrac{7.2}{10.0} $$ **Step 2 — Evaluate.** $$ \boxed{FR = 0.72} $$ **Step 3 — Interpretation.** Since FR = 0.72 < 0.80, the structure exhibits **severe accumulated damage (Felicity effect strongly present)**. Per KTU/codali acceptance criteria, the vessel should be **rejected or sent for major repair**; it cannot be returned to service without further analysis. **(ii) Thermal diffusion length (3.5 marks):** **Step 1 — Angular frequency.** $$ \omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi \times 0.05 = 0.3142 \;\text{rad/s} $$ **Step 2 — Formula.** $$ \mu_{th} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2k}{\rho c_p \omega}} $$ **Step 3 — Numerator and denominator.** $$ 2k = 2 \times 0.7 = 1.4 \;\text{W/m·K} $$ $$ \rho c_p = 1600 \times 800 = 1.28 \times 10^6 \;\text{J/m}^3\!\cdot\!\text{K} $$ **Step 4 — Substituting.** $$ \mu_{th} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1.4}{1.28 \times 10^6 \times 0.3142}} $$ **Step 5 — Compute denominator.** $$ 1.28 \times 10^6 \times 0.3142 = 4.022 \times 10^5 $$ **Step 6 — Divide.** $$ \dfrac{1.4}{4.022 \times 10^5} = 3.48 \times 10^{-6} \;\text{m}^2 $$ **Step 7 — Square root.** $$ \boxed{\mu_{th} = \sqrt{3.48 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.87 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{m} = 1.87 \;\text{mm}} $$ **Interpretation:** The lock-in setup is **most sensitive to defects at ~1.87 mm depth** below the surface — ideal for detecting sub-surface delaminations in thin CFRP laminates. --- ## 5.3 ⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Module 4 NDI Questions:** > > 1. **Confusing units of attenuation** — α may be given in dB/m, dB/mm, or Np/m. Students lose marks for not converting: **1 Np/m = 8.686 dB/m**. Always state the unit explicitly. > 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in pulse-echo** — pulse-echo requires the wave to travel **2d**, not d. Off-by-a-factor-of-2 is the most common error. > 3. **Mixing up A-scan, B-scan, C-scan** — A = 1D amplitude trace, B = 2D cross-section, C = 2D plan-view map. Examiner may deduct 1–2 marks for wrong assignment. > 4. **Wrong Snell's Law for UT** — must use c (not ρ) in the denominator. Many students write sin θ₁/c₂ = sin θ₂/c₁. > 5. **Felicity Ratio sign error** — Felicity ratio is the **reload onset** over the **prior maximum**, NOT the reverse. > 6. **Neglecting the time-gate concept in C-scan** — students often describe the system but forget to explain that **gating filters out non-flaw echoes**. > 7. **Using wrong thermal properties** — for CFRP, k ≈ 0.5–1.0 W/m·K, c_p ≈ 800 J/kg·K. For GFRP, k ≈ 0.3 W/m·K. Wrong values will give incorrect μ_th. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 4 NDI of Composites** > > ✅ **NDI definition** — non-invasive, retains part usability; inspects internal flaws. > > ✅ **BVID** — invisible-to-eye damage with up to 60% strength loss; **detection requires volumetric NDI**. > > ✅ **Ultrasonic C-scan** — the workhorse for delamination; uses 5–15 MHz probes in water/squitter couplant; gated pulse-echo. > > ✅ **λ = c/f** — wavelength equation; for CFRP at 5 MHz, λ ≈ 0.6 mm. > > ✅ **Pulse-echo time-of-flight** — $d = c \Delta t / 2$ — **don't forget the 2**. > > ✅ **Attenuation in CFRP** — Beer-Lambert $I = I_0 e^{-\alpha x}$; α in CFRP is high due to fiber/matrix scattering. > > ✅ **Radiography (X-ray)** — Beer-Lambert $I = I_0 e^{-\mu \rho x}$; planar delaminations parallel to beam can be missed. > > ✅ **Thermography** — heat diffuses anomalously at defects; **μ_th = √(2k / (ρc_p ω))** sets the depth sensitivity. > > ✅ **Acoustic Emission** — listens to **active** damage; **Felicity Ratio FR < 0.80 → reject**. > > ✅ **Shearography** — laser interferometry; full-field, non-contact; ideal for in-service sandwich inspection. > > ✅ **Tap testing** — qualitative, used on wind-turbine blades. > > ✅ **Damage-type → NDI matrix** — know which method detects which flaw (refer to Section 3.6). > > ✅ **Standards to remember** — ASTM E2533 (composite NDI), ASTM D5431 (VT), MIL-HDBK-17 (composite handbook), ASME Section V. > > ✅ **Acoustic impedance mismatch** — air (0.0004 MRayl) vs. CFRP (~4 MRayl) — explains the **mandatory** use of couplant. > > ✅ **POD (Probability of Detection)** — modern NDI acceptance metric; report at 95% confidence.

topics.title: Basic Types of Composite Repair and Their Benefits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT744 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Basic Types of Composite Repair and Their Benefits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 4 — BASIC TYPES OF COMPOSITE REPAIR AND THEIR BENEFITS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Composite Repair** is defined as the engineering process of restoring the **structural integrity, load-bearing capacity, stiffness, and aerodynamic/functional geometry** of a damaged Fiber-Reinforced Polymer (FRP) composite structure by introducing additional composite patch material — bonded, bolted, or co-cured — over the defect zone using a controlled sequence of surface preparation, layup, consolidation, and cure cycles.

The damage that necessitates repair can range from:

- **Impact damage (BVID – Barely Visible Impact Damage)**
- **Delamination** (interlaminar separation)
- **Matrix cracking and fiber breakage**
- **Hole enlargement, notch damage, or foreign object impact**
- **Environmental degradation (moisture, UV, thermal cycling)**

The **objective** of composite repair is to **transfer the structural load** across the damaged region via a *secondary load path* introduced by the patch.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **🩹 Think of composite repair like fixing a torn denim jeans with a patch:**
>
> - When your jeans tear, you cannot just "glue" them — you need a **patch on the inside (or outside)** that carries the tension across the tear.
> - The **patch must be stronger or at least as strong as the original fabric** in that direction.
> - You can stitch it (→ **bolted/mechanical repair**), or glue it (→ **bonded/adhesive repair**), or use a tapered/double-tapered patch (→ **scarf repair**).
> - The **size, shape, and tapering** of the patch determines how smoothly the load "sweeps" across the repair — too abrupt, and the jeans tear again at the patch edge.

In a **composite aircraft wing** or a **composite pressure vessel**, the same principle applies:
- The damaged zone cannot carry load.
- A **patch** (made of prepreg, wet layup, or pre-cured laminate) is bonded/bolted over it.
- The patch **bridges** the damaged area, restoring stiffness and strength.

---

### 1.3 Why Composite Repair is a Critical Engineering Topic

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Drivers for Composite Repair:**
>
> 1. **Cost avoidance** — replacing a damaged composite panel can cost 5×–10× the cost of a qualified repair.
> 2. **Aerospace MRO (Maintenance, Repair, Overhaul)** is a multi-billion dollar industry.
> 3. **Lightweighting trend** — composite repair preserves the weight advantage of FRP structures.
> 4. **OEM-approved repairs** (e.g., Boeing SRM, Airbus SRM) are mandatory in airline operations.

---

### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in Composite Repair (Highlighted in **Bold**)

- **Strain Energy Release Rate (G)** — measured in **J/m²**; quantifies crack propagation resistance.
- **Overlap Length (L)** — measured in **mm**; critical for bonded joint strength.
- **Scarf Angle (θ)** — typically **1°–3°** for aerospace scarf repairs (very low taper ratio).
- **Patch Efficiency (η)** — ratio of repaired strength to pristine strength, expressed as a **%**.
- **Hot/Wet Compression Strength (HWC)** — **MPa**, key property for certified repairs.
- **Interlaminar Shear Strength (ILSS)** — **MPa**, governs patch-to-substrate bond quality.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Basic Composite Repair Types

The basic types of composite repair are broadly classified into **two families**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **A. Bonded Repairs (Adhesive-based)**
> - External Patch Repair (Single-side)
> - Flush/Scarf Repair
> - Stepped-Lap (Double-Scarf) Repair
> - Sandwich Repair (for honeycomb core structures)
>
> **B. Mechanical/Bolted Repairs**
> - Bolted Patch Repair (Single or Double-sided)
> - Hybrid (Bonded + Bolted) Repair

Each type has a specific load-transfer mechanism, tooling requirement, and certification complexity.

---

## 2.2 Type 1 — External Bonded Patch Repair (Single-Side)

### Operational Principle:
- A patch of composite laminate (usually prepreg or cured plies) is **adhesively bonded to the outer (or accessible) surface** of the damaged parent laminate.
- Load is transferred via **shear in the adhesive layer**.
- Only **one-sided access** is required → ideal for **in-service, on-aircraft** repairs.

### Step-by-Step Logic:
- The damaged material is **removed** (cut-out) in a circular or oval shape.
- A **tapered patch** is prepared with a feathered edge (typically 20:1 or 30:1 taper ratio).
- The mating surfaces are **surface-treated** (solvent wipe, abrasion, primer).
- The patch is **co-cured or secondarily bonded** with a structural film adhesive (e.g., FM300, AF163).
- A **vacuum bag + autoclave cure** (or oven cure) consolidates the layup.

### Limitations:
- **Aerodynamic penalty** — external patch disrupts airflow.
- **Out-of-plane bending** — load eccentricity causes secondary bending stresses.
- **Lower patch efficiency** (typically 60–80% of pristine) due to peel stresses at the patch termination.

---

## 2.3 Type 2 — Flush Scarf Repair (Double-Sided Access)

### Operational Principle:
- The damaged zone is **removed by machining a low-angle tapered cavity** (scarf) through the laminate thickness.
- A matching **tapered prepreg patch** is bonded into the cavity, restoring the **original aerodynamic profile**.
- Load is transferred by **in-plane shear** at the scarf interface.

### Step-by-Step Logic:
- A precision **scarfing tool (router or CNC)** cuts a taper of typically **1°–3°** (i.e., a 1:60 to 1:20 taper ratio).
- The patch layup is built with **ply-by-py taper** matching the scarf geometry.
- A **film adhesive** (typically 0.1–0.2 mm thick) is interleaved.
- Cure is done under **vacuum + heat + pressure** (autoclave preferred).

### Why It Works:
- The **gradual taper** spreads the load transfer over a long overlap → low peak shear stress.
- The **flush profile** eliminates aerodynamic drag.
- Achieves **>90% patch efficiency** — the gold standard for primary structure repair.

---

## 2.4 Type 3 — Stepped-Lap (Double-Scarf / Stair-Step) Repair

### Operational Principle:
- A compromise between the external patch and the full scarf.
- Each ply of the parent laminate is **stepped back** (like a staircase) by a small increment.
- The replacement plies are correspondingly stepped to interlock with the parent.
- Achieves **flush profile** with **less demanding tooling** than a continuous scarf.

### Step-by-Step Logic:
- The scarf is divided into **N discrete steps** (e.g., 4–8 steps).
- Each step exposes a portion of a parent ply → provides a "stop" for the patch ply.
- Surface preparation and bonding occur at each step.
- Better suited for **thicker laminates** (e.g., >6 mm) where a continuous scarf becomes impractical.

### Trade-off:
- Slightly higher **peel stress concentration** at each step riser compared to a smooth scarf.
- Still achieves **80–90% patch efficiency**.

---

## 2.5 Type 4 — Bolted / Mechanical Patch Repair

### Operational Principle:
- The patch is mechanically fastened to the parent structure using **rivets, bolts, or pins**.
- Load transfer is via **bearing stress on the fasteners** + clamping friction.
- **No adhesive cure required** → can be done in the field with hand tools.

### Step-by-Step Logic:
- A **doubler plate** (metal or composite) is positioned over the damage.
- Holes are **drilled** through patch + parent.
- **Fasteners** (typically titanium or Monel) are installed with controlled torque.
- A **sealant** is applied to prevent galvanic corrosion (in metal–composite hybrid joints).

### Benefits:
- **No autoclave required** → field-deployable.
- **Inspectable** via standard NDT (coin-tap, ultrasonic).
- **Reversible** → a bolted patch can be removed and re-applied.

### Limitations:
- **Hole drilling introduces new damage sites** (stress concentration).
- **Bearing fatigue** of the parent laminate limits life.
- **Weight penalty** from fasteners and doubler.

---

## 2.6 Type 5 — Sandwich Repair (Honeycomb Core Structures)

### Operational Principle:
- Used when a **sandwich panel with honeycomb core** (Nomex, aluminum) is damaged.
- The damaged core is **locally removed** and replaced with a **potting compound or replacement core plug**.
- A **flush scarf patch** is applied to the face sheet.

### Step-by-Step Logic:
- Core damage is identified via **ultrasonic or thermographic NDT**.
- A **core plug** is machined to size and bonded into the cavity.
- Face sheet is repaired via a **mini-scarf** or **external patch**.
- The repair is qualified against **compression-after-impact (CAI)** strength.

---

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are **frequently tested** in KTU university examinations for composite repair analysis.

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $G = \dfrac{P^{2}}{2bE}\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial a}$ | Strain Energy Release Rate (SERR) for crack growth in bonded joint | $\text{J/m}^{2}$ |
| 2 | $\tau = \dfrac{P}{w \cdot L}$ | Average shear stress in bonded overlap (Volkersen) | $\text{MPa}$ |
| 3 | $\sigma_{p} = \dfrac{P}{w \cdot t_{p}}$ | Patch axial stress under tensile load | $\text{MPa}$ |
| 4 | $\eta = \dfrac{\sigma_{\text{repaired}}}{\sigma_{\text{pristine}}} \times 100\%$ | Repair (patch) efficiency | $\%$ |
| 5 | $K_{t} = 1 + 2\sqrt{\dfrac{a}{\rho}}$ | Stress concentration around a hole (notch sensitivity) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\theta = \arctan\left(\dfrac{t}{L}\right)$ | Scarf taper angle (geometry relation) | degrees |
| 7 | $L_{\text{crit}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{E_{p} \, t_{p} \, t_{a}}{G_{a}}}$ | Critical overlap length (Volkersen) | $\text{mm}$ |
| 8 | $\sigma_{\text{bearing}} = \dfrac{P}{d \cdot t}$ | Bearing stress under a bolted fastener | $\text{MPa}$ |
| 9 | $F = \tau_{a} \cdot w \cdot L$ | Joint load capacity (shear-lag approximation) | $\text{N}$ |
| 10 | $\Delta T_{\text{residual}} = (\alpha_{p} - \alpha_{s}) \cdot \Delta T \cdot E$ | Residual thermal stress in bonded repair | $\text{MPa}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where:**
> - $P$ = applied load (N), $w$ = joint width (mm), $L$ = overlap length (mm)
> - $E$ = Young's modulus, $G$ = shear modulus
> - $t_{p}$ = patch thickness, $t_{a}$ = adhesive thickness, $G_{a}$ = adhesive shear modulus
> - $a$ = crack/flaw length, $\rho$ = hole radius
> - $\alpha_{p}, \alpha_{s}$ = CTE of patch and substrate
> - $\tau_{a}$ = adhesive shear strength

---

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Applications of Composite Repair:**
>
> - ✈️ **Aerospace:** Boeing 787, Airbus A350, F-35 — composite skin repair per SRM (Structural Repair Manual).
> - 🚢 **Marine:** Racing yacht hulls, naval composite decks.
> - 🚗 **Automotive:** Carbon fiber body panel repair (supercars, F1).
> - 🛢️ **Oil & Gas:** Composite wrap repair of corroded steel pipelines (Clock Spring, T.D. Williamson systems).
> - 🌉 **Civil Infrastructure:** CFRP strengthening of concrete beams and bridges.
> - 🛰️ **Space:** Composite repair of satellite composite booms and panels.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Choosing the Right Repair Type

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following decision matrix is critical for KTU design-oriented questions.**
> Use the table to justify your repair selection in a 14-mark problem.

| Parameter | External Patch | Scarf Repair | Stepped-Lap | Bolted Patch | Sandwich Repair |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Tooling complexity** | Low | Very High (CNC) | Medium | Low | High |
| **Patch efficiency (η)** | 60–80% | 90–100% | 80–90% | 60–75% | 70–85% |
| **Access required** | One-sided | Two-sided | Two-sided | One-sided | One/Two-sided |
| **Aerodynamic profile** | Disrupted | Flush | Flush | Disrupted | Flush (if scarf) |
| **Field-deployable** | Yes | No (lab) | Limited | Yes | Yes |
| **Reversibility** | No | No | No | Yes | No |
| **Thickness range** | Thin–Medium | Any | Thick | Any | Sandwich only |
| **NDT inspectability** | Moderate | Good | Good | Excellent | Moderate |
| **OEM certification** | Common | High-criticality | Common | Field-approved | Specialized |
| **Cost (relative)** | $ | $$$ | $$ | $ | $$$ |

---

## 3.2 Worked Analytical Problem — Volkersen Shear-Lag Analysis

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:**
> A CFRP patch of width $w = 50 \text{ mm}$ and thickness $t_{p} = 2 \text{ mm}$ is bonded to a parent laminate using a structural film adhesive of thickness $t_{a} = 0.2 \text{ mm}$ and shear modulus $G_{a} = 500 \text{ MPa}$. A tensile load $P = 25{,}000 \text{ N}$ is applied. The patch has $E_{p} = 140 \text{ GPa}$. Calculate:
> (a) The **average shear stress** in the adhesive.
> (b) The **critical overlap length** for full load transfer.
> (c) The **patch axial stress**.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

#### Part (a): Average Shear Stress in the Adhesive

Using the **Volkersen simple shear-lag** assumption (uniform shear distribution):

$$
\tau_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{P}{w \cdot L}
$$

The actual **shear stress distribution** is non-uniform (parabolic), peaking at the joint ends. The Volkersen equation for **single-lap** bonded joint gives:

$$
\tau(x) = \dfrac{P \omega}{2 w} \cdot \dfrac{\cosh(\omega x)}{\sinh(\omega L/2)}
$$

where the shear-lag parameter is defined as:

$$
\omega = \sqrt{\dfrac{G_{a}}{t_{a}} \left( \dfrac{1}{E_{1} t_{1}} + \dfrac{1}{E_{2} t_{2}} \right) }
$$

For a quick **average** estimate, take $L = 50 \text{ mm}$:

$$
\tau_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{25{,}000}{50 \times 50} = \dfrac{25{,}000}{2500} = 10 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
\boxed{\tau_{\text{avg}} = 10 \text{ MPa}}
$$

#### Part (b): Critical Overlap Length

The **critical overlap length** $L_{\text{crit}}$ is the length at which the joint can transfer the full load $P$ without exceeding the adhesive shear strength. From the Volkersen theory:

$$
L_{\text{crit}} = \dfrac{P}{w \cdot \tau_{a}} \cdot f(\text{geometry})
$$

A simpler engineering approximation (using shear-lag parameter):

$$
L_{\text{crit}} = \dfrac{1}{\omega} \cdot \text{acosh}\left( \dfrac{P \omega}{2 w \tau_{\text{peak}}} \right)
$$

Or using the **practical design equation**:

$$
L_{\text{crit}} = \dfrac{P}{w \cdot \tau_{\text{allow}}}
$$

Assuming adhesive allowable shear strength $\tau_{\text{allow}} = 25 \text{ MPa}$ (typical for FM300):

$$
L_{\text{crit}} = \dfrac{25{,}000}{50 \times 25} = \dfrac{25{,}000}{1250} = 20 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\boxed{L_{\text{crit}} = 20 \text{ mm}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** Actual design overlap is taken as **$4 \times L_{\text{crit}}$** to provide a safety margin against fatigue and environmental degradation.
> $\Rightarrow L_{\text{design}} = 4 \times 20 = 80 \text{ mm}$.

#### Part (c): Patch Axial Stress

$$
\sigma_{p} = \dfrac{P}{w \cdot t_{p}} = \dfrac{25{,}000}{50 \times 2} = \dfrac{25{,}000}{100} = 250 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
\boxed{\sigma_{p} = 250 \text{ MPa}}
$$

This is well below typical CFRP ultimate tensile strength ($1500$–$2500 \text{ MPa}$), confirming the patch is **safe** for static loading. However, **fatigue and environmental knockdown factors** must still be applied for flight certification.

---

## 3.3 Derived Repair Selection Workflow (Step-by-Step Logic)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step 1 — Damage Assessment:**
> Use NDT (ultrasonic C-scan, thermography) to determine damage type, depth, and extent.
>
> **Step 2 — Decide Structural Significance:**
> Is the part **primary structure** (flight-critical) or **secondary structure**?
> - Primary → scarf or stepped-lap only.
> - Secondary → external patch or bolted repair acceptable.
>
> **Step 3 — Check Access:**
> One-sided access (e.g., exterior skin) → external patch or bolted.
> Two-sided access (e.g., removed panel) → scarf.
>
> **Step 4 — Evaluate Tooling:**
> Autoclave available? → scarf preferred.
> Field repair only? → bolted or external patch.
>
> **Step 5 — Apply Repair & Verify:**
> NDT after cure → coupon testing → flight load verification.

---

## 3.4 Typical Ply-Stacking Sequence in a Scarf Repair (Illustrative)

For a 16-ply parent laminate repaired by scarf:

$$
\text{Scarf Patch Layup} = [0_{p} / \pm 45_{p} / 0_{p} / 90_{p}]_{2}
$$

Where each ply is **tapered at its edge** to match the parent scarf geometry. The **ply-drop location** is staggered to avoid a single weak plane.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Classification of Basic Composite Repair Types

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Composite Damage Detected]:::root --> B{Assess Damage Type and Location}
    B --> C[Delamination]:::damage
    B --> D[Matrix Cracking]:::damage
    B --> E[Fiber Breakage]:::damage
    B --> F[Hole or Notch]:::damage
    B --> G[Sandwich Core Damage]:::damage

    C --> H{Choose Repair Type}
    D --> H
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H

    H --> I[External Bonded Patch]:::repair
    H --> J[Flush Scarf Repair]:::repair
    H --> K[Stepped Lap Repair]:::repair
    H --> L[Bolted Patch Repair]:::repair
    H --> M[Sandwich Repair]:::repair

    I --> N{One Sided Access}
    J --> O{Two Sided Access}
    K --> P{Two Sided Thick Laminate}
    L --> Q{Field Repair Possible}
    M --> R{Honeycomb Core}

    N --> S[Apply Patch and Cure]:::process
    O --> S
    P --> S
    Q --> S
    R --> S

    S --> T[NDT Inspection]:::check
    T --> U{Acceptance Criteria Met}
    U -->|Yes| V[Return to Service]:::ok
    U -->|No| W[Re-repair or Reject]:::fail

    classDef root fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#0f172a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef damage fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#92400e,color:#000000
    classDef repair fill:#10b981,stroke:#065f46,color:#ffffff
    classDef process fill:#3b82f6,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    classDef check fill:#8b5cf6,stroke:#4c1d95,color:#ffffff
    classDef ok fill:#22c55e,stroke:#14532d,color:#ffffff
    classDef fail fill:#ef4444,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Load Transfer Mechanism in Bonded Repairs

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Applied Tensile Load P]:::load --> B[Parent Laminate Section 1]:::parent
    B --> C[Damaged Zone: Zero Load Carrying]:::dam
    C --> D[Parent Laminate Section 2]:::parent
    D --> E[Reaction Support]:::sup

    B -.->|Adhesive Shear Transfer| F[Bonded Composite Patch]:::patch
    F -.->|Shear Lag| C
    F -.->|Restored Load Path| D

    style A fill:#0ea5e9,stroke:#0c4a6e,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#fde68a,stroke:#92400e,color:#000000
    style C fill:#fca5a5,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#000000
    style D fill:#fde68a,stroke:#92400e,color:#000000
    style E fill:#0ea5e9,stroke:#0c4a6e,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#86efac,stroke:#14532d,color:#000000
```

## 4.3 Textual Schematic — Scarf Repair Geometry (For Examination Diagrams)

```
        ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
        │  PARENT LAMINATE (UNDAMAGED)            │
        │ ═══════════════════════════════════════ │
        │   ┌─────────────────────────────────┐   │  ↑ Thickness (t)
        │   │  SCARFED CAVITY (taper 1:60)   │   │  │
        │   │  ░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░  │   │  ↓
        │   │  SCARF PATCH (matching taper)   │   │
        │   └─────────────────────────────────┘   │
        │ ═══════════════════════════════════════ │
        └─────────────────────────────────────────┘
        ←──────  Overlap Length L  ──────→

   Scarf Angle: θ = arctan(t/L)
   Example: t = 2 mm, L = 60 mm → θ ≈ 1.91°
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Repair Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[New Damage on Composite Part]:::start --> Q1{Is it primary or secondary structure?}
    Q1 -->|Primary| Q2{Two-sided access available?}
    Q1 -->|Secondary| Q3{Field deployable?}

    Q2 -->|Yes| Q4[Laminate thickness?]
    Q2 -->|No| Q5[External Bonded Patch or Bolted]

    Q4 -->|Thin 6mm| Scarf[Flush Scarf Repair - Best Efficiency]
    Q4 -->|Thick 6mm| Step[Stepped Lap Repair]

    Q3 -->|Yes| Bolt[Bolted Patch Repair]
    Q3 -->|No| Ext[External Bonded Patch]

    Scarf --> Done[Apply Repair and NDT Verify]:::done
    Step --> Done
    Ext --> Done
    Bolt --> Done
    Q5 --> Done

    classDef start fill:#1e293b,stroke:#0f172a,color:#ffffff
    classDef done fill:#22c55e,stroke:#14532d,color:#ffffff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Tip for KTU Exam:**
> If the question says *"the part is on a flying aircraft with one-sided access only"* → your answer MUST be **external bonded patch or bolted repair** — never scarf (which requires panel removal).

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – June 2023]
**Q: List any three basic types of composite repair and state one benefit of each.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **External Bonded Patch Repair** — Benefit: Can be performed with **one-sided access** in the field without removing the part.
2. **Flush Scarf Repair** — Benefit: Achieves the **highest patch efficiency (90–100%)** and restores the original aerodynamic profile.
3. **Bolted Patch Repair** — Benefit: **Reversible and field-deployable**, with excellent inspectability using standard NDT.

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]
**Q: What is a scarf repair? Why is the scarf angle kept very small (typically 1°–3°)?**

**Model Answer:**

A **scarf repair** is a flush-type composite repair where the damaged laminate is removed by machining a **low-angle tapered cavity**, and a matching tapered patch is bonded in.

The scarf angle is kept **very small (1°–3°)** to:
- **Increase the overlap length** $L$ for a given thickness, which reduces the peak shear stress at the interface (per Volkersen shear-lag).
- **Minimize peel stress** at the patch termination.
- **Maximize the patch efficiency** by spreading the load transfer gradually.

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Classify the **basic types of composite repair** with neat sketches. Discuss the **load transfer mechanism** in a bonded scarf repair.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A CFRP patch (width $w = 40 \text{ mm}$, thickness $t_{p} = 1.5 \text{ mm}$) is bonded onto a parent laminate using a film adhesive of shear strength $\tau_{a} = 30 \text{ MPa}$. A tensile load of $P = 18{,}000 \text{ N}$ is applied. Calculate:
1. The **minimum overlap length** required.
2. The **patch axial stress**.
3. Comment on the suitability of this repair if the parent laminate ultimate strength is $2000 \text{ MPa}$.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Classification and Load Transfer Mechanism:**

The basic types of composite repair are:

1. **External Bonded Patch Repair** — patch on the outside surface, one-sided access, load transfer via adhesive shear, limited by peel stresses.

2. **Flush Scarf Repair** — tapered cavity is cut and a matching tapered patch is bonded, giving flush aerodynamic profile and in-plane shear load transfer.

3. **Stepped-Lap Repair** — the parent laminate is removed in discrete steps and the patch is interlocked ply-by-ply, suited to thick laminates.

4. **Bolted / Mechanical Repair** — patch is fastened using rivets/bolts, load transfer via fastener bearing and friction, ideal for field repair.

5. **Sandwich Repair** — for honeycomb core panels, involves core plug replacement and face-sheet scarf repair.

**Load Transfer in Bonded Scarf Repair:**

The applied tensile load $P$ flows through the parent laminate, encounters the damaged zone (which cannot carry load), and is **diverted through the patch** across the overlap length $L$ via **interfacial shear** in the adhesive layer. The scarf's gradual taper ensures that the shear stress $\tau(x)$ is low and uniformly distributed, with peak stresses only at the very ends of the overlap. This is described mathematically by the **Volkersen shear-lag** equation:

$$
\tau(x) = \dfrac{P \omega}{2 w} \cdot \dfrac{\cosh(\omega x)}{\sinh(\omega L/2)}
$$

Because $\theta$ is small, $L$ is large → $\tau_{\text{peak}}$ is small → repair is efficient.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(1) Minimum Overlap Length:**

$$
L_{\text{min}} = \dfrac{P}{w \cdot \tau_{a}} = \dfrac{18{,}000}{40 \times 30} = \dfrac{18{,}000}{1200} = 15 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\boxed{L_{\text{min}} = 15 \text{ mm}}
$$

(Design recommendation: use $L_{\text{design}} = 4 \times 15 = 60 \text{ mm}$.)

**(2) Patch Axial Stress:**

$$
\sigma_{p} = \dfrac{P}{w \cdot t_{p}} = \dfrac{18{,}000}{40 \times 1.5} = \dfrac{18{,}000}{60} = 300 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
\boxed{\sigma_{p} = 300 \text{ MPa}}
$$

**(3) Suitability Comment:**

The patch axial stress of $300 \text{ MPa}$ is much less than the parent ultimate strength of $2000 \text{ MPa}$. Considering the **typical CFRP allowable** (≈ $0.4 \times \text{UTS} = 800 \text{ MPa}$ with knockdown factors), the patch is **safe under static load**. However, **environmental (hot/wet) and fatigue knockdown factors** of $1.5$–$2.0$ must be applied for flight certification, which would still leave sufficient margin.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **stepped-lap repair** in detail. How does it differ from a continuous scarf repair, and when would you prefer it?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare **bonded and bolted composite repairs** in a tabular form covering at least **six technical parameters**. Give one example of an in-service application for each.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Stepped-Lap Repair:**

The **stepped-lap repair** (also called the **stair-step** or **double-scarf** repair) is a hybrid technique where the parent laminate is **stepped back ply-by-ply** (or in groups of 2–3 plies) and the patch is laid up in matching steps to interlock with the parent.

**Procedure:**
1. The damaged zone is identified by NDT (ultrasonic C-scan).
2. The damaged material is removed in **discrete steps**, each step typically 5–15 mm long.
3. Each parent ply (or ply group) is exposed → provides a "stop" for the patch ply.
4. The patch is built up in matching stepped layers using **prepreg** or **wet layup**.
5. A film adhesive is interleaved at each step interface.
6. Cure is done under vacuum + autoclave (or oven for secondary structure).

**Difference from Continuous Scarf Repair:**

| Aspect | Scarf Repair | Stepped-Lap Repair |
|---|---|---|
| Profile | Smooth taper (1°–3°) | Discrete steps (5–15 mm each) |
| Tooling | CNC precision router | Simpler step-cutting tool |
| Thickness suitability | All thicknesses | Best for thick laminates (>6 mm) |
| Patch efficiency | 90–100% | 80–90% |
| Stress concentration | Lowest | Slightly higher at step risers |

**When to Prefer Stepped-Lap:**
- For **thick laminates** (e.g., 10–25 mm) where a continuous scarf becomes impractical due to machining time and ply alignment.
- When **moderate patch efficiency (80–90%)** is acceptable.
- When a **flush aerodynamic profile** is required but a full CNC scarf is not feasible.

---

**(b) Comparison Table — Bonded vs. Bolted Repair:**

| Parameter | Bonded Repair | Bolted Repair |
|---|---|---|
| **Load transfer mechanism** | Adhesive shear (interfacial) | Fastener bearing + friction |
| **Tooling required** | Autoclave, vacuum bag, heat source | Drill, torque wrench |
| **Field deployable** | No (usually) | Yes |
| **Reversibility** | Not reversible | Reversible |
| **Patch efficiency** | 80–100% | 60–75% |
| **Weight penalty** | Low (no fasteners) | Higher (fasteners + doubler) |
| **NDT inspectability** | Ultrasonic, thermography | Visual + ultrasonic |
| **Drilling required** | No | Yes (introduces new damage) |
| **Galvanic corrosion** | No | Possible (needs sealant) |
| **Fatigue performance** | Excellent (no holes) | Limited by bearing fatigue |
| **Repair time** | Long (cure cycle) | Short |
| **Example application** | Boeing 787 composite skin scarf repair per SRM | In-field repair of a composite rotor blade using bolted doubler |

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Conflating "scarf" and "stepped-lap" repairs** — students often use the terms interchangeably. The examiner will deduct **2 marks** if you do not clearly distinguish the smooth taper vs. discrete steps.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the design safety factor** — when calculating overlap length, always state that the *minimum* $L$ must be multiplied by a safety factor of **3–4** for in-service use. Writing only the minimum value without the safety factor loses **1 mark**.
>
> 3. **Writing $\sigma = P / A$ for bonded joint shear** — students often confuse adhesive shear with patch axial stress. Remember:
>    - $\tau_{\text{adhesive}} = P / (wL)$
>    - $\sigma_{\text{patch}} = P / (w \, t_{p})$
>
> 4. **Recommending scarf repair for one-sided access** — this is **impossible** in practice. Always check access first.
>
> 5. **Ignoring hot/wet and fatigue knockdown factors** — in aerospace, the allowable strength is typically **40–60% of UTS** after knockdowns. Stating that "$\sigma = 300 \text{ MPa}$ is safe because UTS = $2000 \text{ MPa}$" without applying knockdowns loses **1–2 marks**.
>
> 6. **Skipping the NDT verification step** — every KTU model answer should end with "verify by ultrasonic C-scan or thermography" to get full marks.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🔑 High-Yield Revision Points for PEPOT744 Module 4:**
>
> ☐ **5 basic types of composite repair:**
> - External bonded patch
> - Flush scarf
> - Stepped-lap
> - Bolted / mechanical
> - Sandwich repair
>
> ☐ **Scarf angle** is **1°–3°** (very small) for high-efficiency repairs.
>
> ☐ **Patch efficiency (η)** ordering: Scarf (90–100%) > Stepped-lap (80–90%) > External patch (60–80%) > Bolted (60–75%).
>
> ☐ **Volkersen shear-lag** governs load transfer in bonded repairs; non-uniform shear distribution peaks at joint ends.
>
> ☐ **Design overlap length** is typically **$4 \times L_{\text{min}}$** for safety.
>
> ☐ **Bonded repair** = high efficiency, no holes, autoclave needed.
>
> ☐ **Bolted repair** = field-deployable, reversible, but introduces new stress concentrations.
>
> ☐ **Sandwich repair** requires both core plug replacement + face sheet scarf.
>
> ☐ **Decision rule:** Primary structure + 2-sided access → Scarf. Secondary + 1-sided → External patch or bolted.
>
> ☐ **NDT methods** for repair verification: ultrasonic C-scan, thermography, coin-tap, X-ray CT.
>
> ☐ **Key formula memory:**
> - $\tau_{\text{avg}} = P / (wL)$
> - $L_{\text{min}} = P / (w \, \tau_{a})$
> - $\sigma_{p} = P / (w \, t_{p})$
> - $\eta = (\sigma_{\text{repaired}} / \sigma_{\text{pristine}}) \times 100\%$
>
> ☐ **Real-world examples to quote in exam:**
> - Boeing 787 → scarf repair per AMTAS / SRM
> - F-35 Lightning II → external patch on composite skin
> - Pipeline composite wrap repair (Clock Spring, T.D. Williamson)
> - CFRP strengthening of civil bridges
>
> ☐ **Always apply** hot/wet and fatigue **knockdown factors** when justifying design.
